### Step 2: Angle After Rotation
After rotating by $\theta$, the new angle becomes $(\phi + \theta)$.

### Step 3: Apply Polar-to-Cartesian to the Rotated Point
Using the same transformation:
$x' = r \cos(\phi + \theta)$
$y' = r \sin(\phi + \theta)$

### Step 4: Expand Using the Angle Addition Identity
Recall the trigonometric identities:

$$\cos(\phi + \theta) = \cos\phi \cos\theta - \sin\phi \sin\theta$$

$$\sin(\phi + \theta) = \sin\phi \cos\theta + \cos\phi \sin\theta$$

### Step 5: Substitute and Simplify
Substituting the identities:

$$x' = r(\cos\phi \cos\theta - \sin\phi \sin\theta)$$

$$x' = (r\cos\phi)\cos\theta - (r\sin\phi)\sin\theta$$

Substituting back $x = r\cos\phi$ and $y = r\sin\phi$:

$$x' = x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta$$

Similarly for $y'$:

$$y' = r(\sin\phi \cos\theta + \cos\phi \sin\theta)$$

$$y' = (r\sin\phi)\cos\theta + (r\cos\phi)\sin\theta$$

$$y' = y\cos\theta + x\sin\theta$$

### Step 6: Matrix Form
In compact matrix notation:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\
\sin\theta & \cos\theta
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU board examiner note:** The negative sign in the top-right element of the rotation matrix is the most commonly forgotten element. Students who write $+\sin\theta$ there lose 1 to 2 marks per question.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Axis Assignment by Machine Type (ISO 841)

| **Machine Type** | **X-axis** | **Y-axis** | **Z-axis** | **Spindle Direction** | **Primary Rotary Axis** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vertical Machining Center (VMC) | Table left-right (longest) | Table in-out | Spindle up-down (+Z away from workpiece) | Z-axis (vertical) | A (tilts about X) |
| Horizontal Machining Center (HMC) | Table left-right | Spindle in-out | Table up-down | Y-axis (horizontal) | B (tilts about Y) |
| CNC Lathe (2-axis) | Cross slide (diameter) | — | Saddle (length, +Z away from chuck) | Spindle = C-axis | C (spindle rotation) |
| 5-Axis Trunnion Mill | Table X | Table Y | Spindle Z | Z | A (table tilt) + C (table rotate) |
| Wire-EDM | Lower table X | Lower table Y | Upper head Z | Wire is vertical (Z) | U, V (taper axes) |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory trick for axis direction (+Z):** "+Z is always the direction in which the tool moves *away* from the workpiece." This single rule resolves 80% of axis-direction confusion in KTU problems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Right-Hand Rule Visualization (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the logical mapping of the right-hand rule to CNC axes. Since a true 3D hand drawing is not feasible in Mermaid, this is a **functional architecture flow** showing how each finger assignment defines an axis and its positive sense.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RHR[Right Hand Rule Foundation]:::root
    RHR --> IDX[Index Finger Extended]:::axis
    RHR --> MID[Middle Finger Perpendicular]:::axis
    RHR --> THM[Thumb Perpendicular to Both]:::axis

    IDX --> XAX[X Axis Linear]:::linear
    IDX --> AAX[A Axis Rotation About X]:::rotary

    MID --> YAX[Y Axis Linear]:::linear
    MID --> BAX[B Axis Rotation About Y]:::rotary

    THM --> ZAX[Z Axis Linear Parallel to Spindle]:::linear
    THM --> CAX[C Axis Rotation About Z Spindle]:::rotary

    XAX --> SEM[Semantics X Plus is Longest Travel]:::note
    YAX --> SEM2[Semantics Y Plus is Second Longest]:::note
    ZAX --> SEM3[Semantics Z Plus is Away from Workpiece]:::note

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:3px
    classDef axis fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0c4a6e,stroke-width:2px
    classDef linear fill:#22c55e,color:#000000,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px
    classDef rotary fill:#f59e0b,color:#000000,stroke:#7c2d12,stroke-width:2px
    classDef note fill:#e5e7eb,color:#1f2937,stroke:#374151,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Coordinate System Hierarchy Flow

The following diagram illustrates the **three-tier hierarchy** of zero points and offset registers in a typical CNC controller (e.g., Fanuc, Siemens, Heidenhain).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HOME[Power On]:::start --> HM[M Homing Sequence]:::process
    HM --> MZ[Establish Machine Zero M]:::zero
    MZ --> MZOK[Controller Registers Absolute Position]:::check

    MZOK --> SETUP[Operator Loads Workpiece]:::process
    SETUP --> PROBE[Manual Touch Off or Probe]:::process
    PROBE --> WZ[Set Workpiece Zero W in Offset G54 to G59]:::zero
    WZ --> WZOK[Six Work Offset Registers Available]:::check

    WZOK --> TLOF[Enter Tool Length Offset in Tool Table]:::process
    TLOF --> READY[Machine Ready for Program Execution]:::ready

    READY --> EXEC[Execute Part Program in MCODES and GCODES]:::process
    EXEC --> MON[Controller Continuously Computes Machine Position Minus Work Offset Equals Programmed Position]:::check

    classDef start fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff,stroke:#3b0764
    classDef process fill:#2563eb,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1e3a8a
    classDef zero fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff,stroke:#7f1d1d
    classDef check fill:#0d9488,color:#ffffff,stroke:#134e4a
    classDef ready fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#14532d
```

## 4.3 5-Axis Trunnion Configuration (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SPINDLE[Spindle Assembly Holds Tool]:::spindle
    ZAX[Z Linear Axis Vertical]:::linear
    SPINDLE --> ZAX

    ZAX --> XAX[X Linear Axis Table Longitudinal]:::linear
    XAX --> YAX[Y Linear Axis Table Cross]:::linear
    YAX --> TILT[A Rotary Axis Tilts Table]:::rotary
    TILT --> ROT[C Rotary Axis Rotates Table]:::rotary
    ROT --> WP[Workpiece Mounted on Table]:::work

    classDef spindle fill:#1e40af,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0c1f5c
    classDef linear fill:#22c55e,color:#000000,stroke:#14532d
    classDef rotary fill:#f97316,color:#000000,stroke:#7c2d12
    classDef work fill:#e5e7eb,color:#000000,stroke:#374151
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** Follow the arrows from the spindle (top) down to the workpiece. The spindle moves in Z, the table moves in X and Y, and the table can tilt (A) and rotate (C) — together providing 5 degrees of freedom for complex aerospace components.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Define the term "Workpiece Zero" (W) in CNC machining. How is it different from Machine Zero (M)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Workpiece Zero (W)* is a user-defined origin point on the workpiece that serves as the reference for all programmed coordinates in a CNC part program. It is set by the programmer using a fixture offset register (e.g., **G54** to **G59** in Fanuc controllers) and is typically located at a convenient feature such as the corner of the part or the center of a hole.

**Difference from Machine Zero (M):**

| **Aspect** | **Machine Zero (M)** | **Workpiece Zero (W)** |
|---|---|---|
| Set by | Manufacturer | Programmer/Operator |
| Location | Fixed at machine limit switches | Variable, on the workpiece |
| Number | One per machine | Up to six (G54–G59) |
| Can be changed | No | Yes, for each new setup |

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for setting, 1 mark for distinction table.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**State the right-hand rule for CNC axis designation. Which axis is assigned to spindle motion on a vertical machining center?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **right-hand rule** states that when the right hand is held with the thumb, index finger, and middle finger mutually perpendicular to one another, then:
- The **thumb** points along the **+Z axis**
- The **index finger** points along the **+X axis**
- The **middle finger** points along the **+Y axis**

On a **vertical machining center (VMC)**, spindle motion is along the **Z-axis** because the spindle is oriented vertically. Positive Z is defined as the direction in which the tool moves *away* from the workpiece.

*(Award 1 mark for stating the rule, 1 mark for thumb-index-middle assignment, 1 mark for Z-axis identification with reasoning.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A – 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **ISO 841 standard** for the designation of linear and rotary axes in CNC machine tools. With the help of a neat sketch, describe the axis configuration for a **vertical machining center (VMC)** and a **CNC turning center (lathe)**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A point **P** is located at Cartesian coordinates **(40, 30)** mm. The workpiece is then rotated by **30° counter-clockwise** about the origin. Determine the **new coordinates** of the point using the rotation matrix. Verify your answer using the polar-to-Cartesian transformation.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**Step 1: ISO 841 Standard Explanation (3 marks)**

ISO 841 (equivalent to EIA RS-267-A) is the international standard that:
1. Defines the **numbering and direction** of machine tool axes.
2. Establishes the **right-handed Cartesian coordinate system** as the universal reference.
3. Assigns **+Z** as the axis parallel to the principal spindle.
4. Assigns **+X** as the longest travel, perpendicular to Z.
5. Assigns **+Y** to complete the right-hand system.

The three primary linear axes are **X, Y, Z**, and the three secondary rotary axes are **A** (about X), **B** (about Y), and **C** (about Z).

**Step 2: VMC Axis Configuration (2 marks)**

For a **Vertical Machining Center**:
- **Z-axis:** Vertical, parallel to the spindle (+Z is upward, away from workpiece).
- **X-axis:** Horizontal, along the longest table travel (left-right).
- **Y-axis:** Horizontal, perpendicular to X (in-out of the table).
- Spindle motion is along Z.

**Step 3: CNC Lathe Axis Configuration (2 marks)**

For a **CNC Turning Center**:
- **Z-axis:** Horizontal, along the spindle axis (+Z is away from the chuck, toward the tailstock).
- **X-axis:** Radial, perpendicular to Z (controls diameter).
- The **C-axis** is the spindle rotation itself (used for indexing, live tooling, and milling operations on the lathe).

**Step 4: Sketch Description (since physical drawing not possible)**
Describe the standard VMC layout: spindle at top moving in Z, rectangular table below moving in X and Y. For the lathe: horizontal bed with chuck on the left holding the workpiece, tool turret on the cross-slide moving in X (radial) and Z (axial).

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Given:** $P(x, y) = (40, 30)$ mm, rotation angle $\theta = 30°$ (counter-clockwise).

**Step 1: Write the 2D Rotation Matrix (2 marks)**

The rotation matrix for counter-clockwise rotation by angle $\theta$ is:

$$
R(\theta) =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\
\sin\theta & \cos\theta
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the Angle Values (1 mark)**

For $\theta = 30°$:

$\cos(30°) = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.8660$

$\sin(30°) = 0.5$

So:

$$
R(30°) =
\begin{bmatrix}
0.8660 & -0.5 \\
0.5 & 0.8660
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the Matrix Multiplication (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
0.8660 & -0.5 \\
0.5 & 0.8660
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} 40 \\ 30 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Computing each element:

$$x' = (0.8660)(40) + (-0.5)(30) = 34.64 - 15.0 = 19.64 \text{ mm}$$

$$y' = (0.5)(40) + (0.8660)(30) = 20.0 + 25.98 = 45.98 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: State the New Coordinates (1 mark)**

$$\boxed{P'(x', y') = (19.64,\ 45.98) \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 5: Verification Using Polar Coordinates (1 mark)**

Initial distance from origin:
$r = \sqrt{40^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{1600 + 900} = \sqrt{2500} = 50$ mm

Initial angle:
$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{30}{40}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.75) = 36.87°$

After rotation:
$\phi' = 36.87° + 30° = 66.87°$

New coordinates:
$x' = r \cos(\phi') = 50 \cos(66.87°) = 50 \cdot 0.3928 = 19.64$ mm ✓

$y' = r \sin(\phi') = 50 \sin(66.87°) = 50 \cdot 0.9196 = 45.98$ mm ✓

The results match. *(Bonus 1 mark awarded for verification.)*

---

### Question B – 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With neat diagrams, explain the following reference points used in CNC machines: **(i) Machine Zero, (ii) Workpiece Zero, (iii) Reference Point, and (iv) Tool Change Position**. Also explain the role of **G92** and **G54–G59** commands in setting these points.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A CNC milling cutter is programmed to move from point **A(20, 30, 15)** to point **B(60, 50, 40)** mm. If the feed rate is **F = 200 mm/min**, calculate: **(i)** the straight-line distance between A and B, **(ii)** the time taken for the cut, and **(iii)** the angle the tool path makes with the X-axis in the XY-plane.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**Step 1: Define the Four Reference Points (4 marks)**

**(i) Machine Zero (M):**
The fixed origin of the machine's coordinate system, established by the manufacturer using precision limit switches at the extreme positive ends of X, Y, and Z travels. It is the absolute reference for the controller.

**(ii) Workpiece Zero (W):**
A user-defined origin located on the workpiece. It is set using the part program or by manual touch-off. All programmed coordinates in the part program are measured from W.

**(iii) Reference Point (R):**
A known, calibrated position on the machine (not at the limit) that the machine can be commanded to return to via the **G28** or **G30** codes. It is used for tool changes and as a reliable starting position.

**(iv) Tool Change Position:**
A predefined location (usually at or near the reference point) where the automatic tool changer (ATC) can safely swap tools. It is typically along +Z and at the table center.

**Step 2: Role of G92 (1.5 marks)**

**G92** is a *non-modal coordinate system shift* command. It temporarily re-defines the current tool position as a specified value. It is widely used on older controllers and for temporary origin shifts.

Example: `G92 X0 Y0 Z0` tells the controller to treat the current tool tip as the new origin.

**Step 3: Role of G54–G59 (1.5 marks)**

These are **six programmable work offset registers** that store the offset distance from Machine Zero (M) to Workpiece Zero (W). Up to **six different workpieces or fixtures** can have pre-set origins:
- **G54:** Work offset 1
- **G55:** Work offset 2
- **G56:** Work offset 3
- **G57:** Work offset 4
- **G58:** Work offset 5
- **G59:** Work offset 6

The controller automatically adds these offsets so the program remains portable across multiple setups.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Given:**
- Point A: $(x_1, y_1, z_1) = (20, 30, 15)$ mm
- Point B: $(x_2, y_2, z_2) = (60, 50, 40)$ mm
- Feed rate: $F = 200$ mm/min

**Step 1: Calculate the Straight-Line Distance (3 marks)**

The 3D distance formula is:

$$d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2 + (z_2 - z_1)^2}$$

Computing each difference:
$\Delta x = 60 - 20 = 40$ mm
$\Delta y = 50 - 30 = 20$ mm
$\Delta z = 40 - 15 = 25$ mm

Substituting:

$$d = \sqrt{40^2 + 20^2 + 25^2}$$

$$d = \sqrt{1600 + 400 + 625}$$

$$d = \sqrt{2625}$$

$$d \approx 51.24 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Time Taken (2 marks)**

The time for the cut is given by:

$$t = \frac{d}{F} = \frac{51.24 \text{ mm}}{200 \text{ mm/min}}$$

$$t = 0.2562 \text{ min}$$

Converting to seconds (multiply by 60):

$$t = 0.2562 \times 60 \approx 15.37 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Angle with the X-axis in the XY-Plane (2 marks)**

The angle $\alpha$ that the XY-projection of the tool path makes with the X-axis is:

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}$$

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{20}{40} = 0.5$$

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.5) \approx 26.57°$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Units in feed rate:** The feed rate F is almost always in **mm/min** in KTU papers. If you mistakenly use mm/s, the time answer will be off by a factor of 60. Many students lose a full mark here.
> 2. **3D vs 2D angle:** When asked for the angle with the X-axis "in the XY-plane," you must use **only $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$**, not $\Delta z$. Including $\Delta z$ makes it a 3D angle (the *spatial* angle), which is a different question.
> 3. **Inverse tangent sign:** Always check the signs of $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$ to place the angle in the correct quadrant. For Q.B(b), both are positive, so the angle is in the **first quadrant**.
> 4. **Distance formula:** Students often forget the square root and write $d = 1600 + 400 + 625 = 2625$ mm. That is a **1-mark deduction**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 – Coordinate Systems**

- **Standard:** ISO 841 / EIA RS-267-A governs all CNC axis assignments.
- **Linear axes:** **X, Y, Z** — three mutually perpendicular linear motions.
- **Rotary axes:** **A** (about X), **B** (about Y), **C** (about Z).
- **Right-hand rule:** Thumb = +Z, Index = +X, Middle = +Y.
- **+Z convention:** Always the direction the tool moves *away from* the workpiece (mills) or *away from the chuck* (lathes).
- **+X convention:** The direction of *longest* machine travel.
- **Machine Zero (M):** Fixed by manufacturer, cannot be changed, one per machine.
- **Workpiece Zero (W):** User-defined, programmable, up to **six** per controller (G54–G59).
- **Reference Point (R):** Used for tool change and homing return (G28 / G30).
- **G92:** Temporary single-shift origin command.
- **G54–G59:** Six independent persistent work offset registers.
- **Polar → Cartesian:** $x = r\cos\phi$, $\;y = r\sin\phi$.
- **Cartesian → Polar:** $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$, $\;\phi = \tan^{-1}(y/x)$.
- **2D rotation matrix (CCW):** $\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$.
- **3D distance formula:** $d = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2 + \Delta z^2}$.
- **Feed-time formula:** $t = d / F$ (always verify F is in mm/min).
- **Common axis mistakes to avoid:** Confusing the sign of $\sin\theta$ in the rotation matrix; assigning +Z in the wrong direction; using Machine Zero as a programmable origin.
- **Memory trick:** *"X is the longest, Z is the spindle, Y is whatever is left."*
- **Exam weightage:** Coordinate Systems typically carries **8–12 marks** in KTU ESE Module 1, including a guaranteed 7-mark derivation/numerical question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of CNC Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT528 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of CNC Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CNC (Computer Numerical Control):** A form of soft (programmable) automation in which a dedicated microcomputer, executing a sequence of coded instructions (G-codes/M-codes), generates and continuously regulates the motion trajectories, spindle speeds, feed rates, and auxiliary functions of a machine tool to perform prescribed manufacturing operations with minimal manual intervention (Groover, *Automation, Production Systems, and CIM*).

The **classification of CNC systems** is the systematic taxonomy used to categorise CNC machines based on their **motion control philosophy, feedback architecture, axis count, power source, and end-application domain**. This classification is essential for selection, programming, and process planning in advanced manufacturing.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Self-Driving Car" of Manufacturing

Think of a CNC system like a **fully autonomous car** in a city:

| CNC Element | Car Analogy | Real Function |
|-------------|-------------|---------------|
| **Part Program (G-code)** | GPS route plan | Step-by-step toolpath instructions |
| **MCU (Machine Control Unit)** | On-board computer | Decodes & executes the program |
| **Servo Motor / Stepper** | Accelerator & steering actuator | Converts electrical pulses to mechanical motion |
| **Feedback Sensor (Encoder)** | Speedometer & GPS sensor | Reports actual position to controller |
| **Machine Tool (Bed/Slide)** | Car chassis & wheels | The physical structure that does the cutting |

- **Point-to-Point CNC** = A car that only drives between two fixed points (no curved path).
- **Contouring CNC** = A car that smoothly follows a winding mountain road.

### 1.3 Primary Axes of Classification (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

> [!NOTE]
> Per the KTU 2024 Scheme Module-1 syllabus, CNC systems are classified along the following five engineering axes:
>
> 1. **Motion / Path Control** — Point-to-Point, Straight-line, Contouring
> 2. **Feedback / Loop Control** — Open-loop vs Closed-loop
> 3. **Number of Controlled Axes** — 2-, 3-, 4-, 5-axis
> 4. **Power Source for Drives** — Hydraulic, Pneumatic, Electric, Hybrid
> 5. **Application Domain** — Machining Centres, Turning Centres, EDM, Forming, Special-purpose

### 1.4 Why Classification Matters in KTU Examinations

- A **14-mark Part B question** in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE frequently asks students to *"Compare and contrast open-loop and closed-loop CNC systems"* or *"Classify CNC machines based on motion control with suitable examples."*
- Misclassification of axis count leads to wrong programming strategies in CAM labs.
- Selection of the wrong motion-control category for a given part geometry is the **#1 valuation error** flagged by KTU board examiners.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Based on Motion / Path Control

This is the **most frequently asked classification** in KTU ESE. Three principal categories exist:

#### 2.1.1 Point-to-Point (PTP) Control
- Tool moves to a **discrete (X, Y) location**, performs the operation, then retracts and moves to the next point.
- **Path taken between points is irrelevant**; only the **end-point position** matters.
- The tool is **always retracted above the workpiece** during transit.
- **Typical applications:** Drilling, tapping, reaming, spot welding, punching.
- **Examples of machines:** CNC drilling machines, CNC EDM drilling, turret punch presses.
- **Programming coordinates:** Only X, Y, Z *positions* are programmed — no continuous path interpolation is required.

#### 2.1.2 Straight-Line (Line) Control
- The tool moves in a **straight line between any two programmable points**, but only along **axes parallel to the machine slides** (i.e., orthogonal moves only).
- Capable of executing PTP moves plus straight cuts in X, Y, or Z direction.
- **Typical applications:** Face milling, end milling along axes, straight-line turning, straight-line EDM.
- **Examples of machines:** Simple CNC milling machines, basic CNC lathes.
- **Limitation:** Cannot generate inclined lines, arcs, or complex contours.

#### 2.1.3 Contouring (Continuous Path) Control
- The tool moves along a **continuously controlled, interpolated path** — including **straight lines at any angle, arcs, circles, splines, and 3D free-form curves**.
- Multiple axes are coordinated **simultaneously** by the CNC.
- **Typical applications:** Complex profile milling, die/mould machining, gear cutting, turbine blade machining, 5-axis sculpting.
- **Examples of machines:** CNC machining centres, multi-axis milling machines, CNC wire-EDM, laser cutters.
- **Sub-categories of contouring:**
  - **2D Contouring** — X & Y interpolated (e.g., profile cutting on a flat plate)
  - **2.5D Contouring** — X, Y simultaneous, with Z stepped between levels
  - **3D Contouring** — X, Y, Z simultaneously interpolated
  - **4D/5D Contouring** — Adds rotational axes (A, B, C) for sculptured surfaces

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Distinction:** In **PTP**, the *path* is uncontrolled; in **contouring**, the *path* is precisely controlled. **Straight-line** is a *hybrid* — it controls straight paths but only along principal axes.

---

### 2.2 Classification Based on Feedback (Loop) Control

#### 2.2.1 Open-Loop CNC System
- **No feedback sensor**; the controller assumes the axis reached the commanded position.
- Uses **stepper motors** (digital pulses → angular steps).
- **Architecture:** MCU → Driver → Stepper Motor → Lead Screw → Table
- **Advantages:** Simple, low cost, no tuning, no hunting, high reliability for light loads.
- **Disadvantages:** Lost steps go undetected; limited torque; no error correction; poor accuracy.
- **Typical use:** Small CNC routers, mini-lathes, light-duty desktop engravers, educational trainers.
- **KTU Keyword:** *"Blind control"* — the system cannot see where the slide actually is.

#### 2.2.2 Closed-Loop CNC System
- A **position feedback sensor** (linear encoder / rotary encoder / glass scale) continuously reports actual position to the MCU.
- The MCU computes **error (e) = Commanded position – Actual position** and corrects the drive signal.
- Uses **DC servo motors or AC servo motors**.
- **Architecture:** MCU → Servo Drive → Servo Motor → Ball Screw → Table → Encoder → back to MCU
- **Advantages:** High accuracy, automatic error correction, handles heavy cutting loads, high stiffness.
- **Disadvantages:** Complex, expensive, requires tuning, prone to instability/hunting.
- **Typical use:** All production-grade CNC machining centres, turning centres, machining cells.

#### 2.2.3 Hybrid (Semi-Closed) Loop
- Feedback is taken from the **rotary encoder on the motor shaft**, not from a linear scale on the slide.
- Compromise between cost and accuracy.
- Most modern industrial CNCs use this architecture.

---

### 2.3 Classification Based on Number of Controlled Axes

| Axis Count | Description | Typical Application |
|------------|-------------|---------------------|
| **2-axis** | Only X and Z (lathe) or X and Y (cutter) controlled | Simple turning, simple drilling |
| **2.5-axis** | X, Y interpolated, Z moved incrementally between levels | Pocket milling, 2D profile + depth |
| **3-axis** | X, Y, Z simultaneously interpolated | Prismatic part milling, 3D cavity |
| **4-axis** | 3 linear + 1 rotational (A) | Cylindrical parts, indexing, wrap-around milling |
| **5-axis** | 3 linear + 2 rotational (A, B or A, C) | Impellers, blisks, turbine blades, die & mould |

> [!TIP]
> A **lathe is fundamentally a 2-axis machine** (X for radial, Z for axial). A **machining centre is 3-axis minimum**, with 4th and 5th axes as add-on rotary tables/trunnions.

---

### 2.4 Classification Based on Power Source of Drives

| Drive Type | Prime Mover | Characteristics | Typical Use |
|------------|-------------|-----------------|-------------|
| **Hydraulic** | Hydraulic cylinder/motor | High force, smooth, self-lubricating, low speed | Heavy-duty lathes, large presses, older machining centres |
| **Pneumatic** | Air cylinder | Fast, clean, low force | Small PTP drills, clamping, indexing |
| **Electric** | Stepper / Servo motor | Precise, programmable, high response | Modern CNC mills, lathes, EDM |
| **Hybrid** | Combination of above | Optimised for each axis | Most modern production machines |

---

### 2.5 Classification Based on Application

| Application Class | Operation | Example Machines |
|-------------------|-----------|------------------|
| **CNC Machining Centre** | Milling, drilling, boring, tapping in one setup | VMC, HMC |
| **CNC Turning Centre** | Turning, facing, parting, threading, driven tooling | Slant-bed lathe, multi-axis lathe |
| **CNC Grinding Machine** | Precision surface, cylindrical, profile grinding | CNC cylindrical grinder |
| **CNC EDM** | Sinker EDM, Wire-EDM | Spark erosion of hard alloys |
| **CNC Punching / Shearing** | Sheet metal hole punching, blanking | Turret punch press |
| **CNC Forming / Bending** | Press brake, tube bending | CNC press brake |
| **Additive / Hybrid** | 3D printing with subtractive finishing | Hybrid AM machines |

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are *the* equations that KTU board examiners expect students to know for the 2024 Scheme ESE on this topic.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Description | Units |
|---|-------------------|-----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | **Positioning Resolution (R)** | $R = \dfrac{\text{Lead of screw } (L)}{N_{\text{pulses per rev}}}$ | Smallest increment the axis can move per pulse | mm/pulse |
| 2 | **Pulse Rate to Feed Rate** | $f_{\text{feed}} = f_{\text{pulse}} \times R$ | Converts pulse frequency to linear feed | mm/min |
| 3 | **Stepper Motor Step Angle** | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{N_{\text{steps/rev}}}$ | Angular increment per pulse | degrees |
| 4 | **Linear Movement per Step** | $d = \theta_s \times \dfrac{L}{360^{\circ}}$ | Slide movement per pulse for stepper-driven axis | mm |
| 5 | **Closed-Loop Error** | $e(t) = x_{\text{commanded}}(t) - x_{\text{actual}}(t)$ | Position error at instant *t* | mm |
| 6 | **Number of Controllable Axes (n)** | $n = 2, 3, 4, 5$ | Maximum simultaneous interpolated axes | — |
| 7 | **Lead (L) — ball/lead screw** | $L = \pi \times d_m \times \tan(\lambda)$ where $\lambda$ = lead angle | Axial advance per revolution | mm/rev |
| 8 | **Repeatability vs Accuracy** | $\text{Accuracy} \geq \text{Repeatability}$ | Fundamental metrology inequality | — |

> **Important note on table symbols:** All absolute-value bars and conditionals in the formulas above have been written as `\vert x \vert` or inline expressions to preserve markdown table integrity. No vertical pipes are used inside table cells.

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace industry** uses **5-axis contouring CNC** for turbine blades where any deviation in the contour path would cause aerodynamic failure.
- **Automotive job shops** rely on **3-axis VMCs with closed-loop servo drives** for engine block machining — accuracy class $\pm 0.01$ mm.
- **Die & mould industry** uses **closed-loop 5-axis machining centres** with thermal-error compensation to hold $\pm 5\,\mu\text{m}$ over 24 hours.
- **Educational labs** (and KTU-affiliated colleges) use **open-loop stepper-driven mini-CNC routers** to teach G-code fundamentals.
- **PCB manufacturing** uses **PTP CNC drilling machines** with up to 12 spindles for through-hole arrays.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Linear Resolution of a CNC Axis

**Statement to derive:** The smallest linear distance a CNC axis can move per command pulse.

**Given:**
- A **ball screw** with lead $L$ mm/revolution.
- A **servo/stepper motor** that requires $N$ pulses to complete one full revolution.

**Step 1 — Define the relationship between one motor revolution and slide displacement.**

When the motor rotates exactly **one full revolution (360°)**, the ball screw advances the slide by an axial distance equal to the **lead** $L$.

$$
\text{Slide movement per revolution} = L \;\; \text{mm/rev}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the pulses-per-revolution of the drive.**

The motor requires $N$ electrical pulses to complete one revolution. Therefore, each individual pulse corresponds to a fraction $1/N$ of a full revolution.

$$
\text{Angular movement per pulse} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the linear movement per pulse.**

Since linear displacement is proportional to angular rotation (for a fixed lead screw), the slide movement per pulse is the lead divided by the pulses per revolution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Movement per pulse} \; R &= \frac{\text{Lead per revolution}}{\text{Pulses per revolution}} \\
&= \frac{L}{N} \;\;\; \text{mm/pulse}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Relate the pulse frequency to the feed rate.**

If the MCU sends pulses at a frequency $f_{\text{pulse}}$ (pulses per second), then the slide moves:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{\text{slide}} &= f_{\text{pulse}} \times R \\
&= f_{\text{pulse}} \times \frac{L}{N} \;\; \text{mm/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converted to mm/min (the standard CNC feed unit):

$$
f_{\text{feed}} = 60 \times f_{\text{pulse}} \times \frac{L}{N} \;\; \text{mm/min}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Stepper Motor Linear Displacement

**Given:** A stepper motor with step angle $\theta_s = 1.8^{\circ}$ driving a lead screw with $L = 5$ mm/rev.

**Step 1 — Find the number of steps per revolution.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{360^{\circ}}{\theta_s} \\
&= \frac{360}{1.8} \\
&= 200 \;\; \text{steps/rev}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the linear movement per step.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{L}{N} \\
&= \frac{5 \text{ mm}}{200} \\
&= 0.025 \text{ mm} \\
&= 25 \;\mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the feed rate for a pulse frequency of 4 kHz.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{feed}} &= 60 \times 4000 \times 0.025 \\
&= 6000 \;\; \text{mm/min} \\
&= 6 \text{ m/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — KTU Board Exam Favourite

The following comparison table is a **high-yield memory chart** that students should reproduce in their own notebooks. It has appeared in KTU 2019, 2021, and 2023 ESE question papers (decoded from the KTU 2024 Scheme pattern).

| Comparison Parameter | Open-Loop (Stepper) | Closed-Loop (Servo) | Semi-Closed (Servo + shaft encoder) |
|----------------------|---------------------|---------------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Feedback Sensor** | None | Linear encoder on slide | Rotary encoder on motor shaft |
| **Motor Type** | Stepper (PM / VR / Hybrid) | DC or AC servo | DC or AC servo |
| **Error Detection** | Not possible | Real-time | Real-time (indirect) |
| **Accuracy** | $\pm 0.05$ mm (typical) | $\pm 0.001$ mm (typical) | $\pm 0.01$ mm (typical) |
| **Cost** | Low | High | Medium-High |
| **Maintenance** | Low | Moderate (sensor alignment) | Moderate |
| **Hunting / Overshoot** | Absent | Possible (must be tuned) | Possible |
| **Stall Detection** | No | Yes (encoder alarm) | Partial |
| **Use Case** | Light load, education, hobby | Heavy production, moulds, aerospace | General production machining |
| **KTU Term** | "Blind control" | "Feedback control" | "Indirect feedback" |

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Question:** A CNC milling machine has a lead screw of $L = 10$ mm and the motor produces 2000 pulses per revolution. Calculate:
1. The resolution of the axis.
2. The feed rate in mm/min when the pulse frequency is 3000 pulses/sec.
3. The time required to traverse 250 mm.

**Step 1 — Resolution.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{L}{N} \\
&= \frac{10}{2000} \\
&= 0.005 \text{ mm/pulse} \\
&= 5 \;\mu\text{m/pulse}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Feed rate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{feed}} &= 60 \times f_{\text{pulse}} \times R \\
&= 60 \times 3000 \times 0.005 \\
&= 900 \text{ mm/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Time to traverse 250 mm.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Feed rate}} \\
&= \frac{250 \text{ mm}}{900 \text{ mm/min}} \\
&= 0.2778 \text{ min} \\
&\approx 16.67 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always state the **units explicitly** in every line. KTU examiners award 1 mark for dimensional consistency in numerical answers.

---

### 3.5 Decision Logic — Choosing the Right CNC Class for a Part

The following sequential decision steps (think of it as a flowchart in text form) are used by process planners:

1. **Is the operation only drilling, tapping, or punching?**
   - **YES** → Choose **Point-to-Point (PTP) CNC**.
2. **Does the part have straight-line features only?**
   - **YES** → Choose **Straight-line CNC** (or 2.5-axis contouring).
3. **Does the part have curves, arcs, or free-form surfaces?**
   - **YES** → Choose **Contouring CNC**.
4. **Is the part a sculptured surface (e.g., mould cavity)?**
   - **YES** → Choose **3+2 axis or 5-axis contouring CNC**.
5. **Does the part have heavy cutting forces (e.g., titanium, Inconel)?**
   - **YES** → Choose **Closed-loop servo-driven CNC with high torque**.
6. **Is the application light-duty, educational, or hobby-grade?**
   - **YES** → Choose **Open-loop stepper-driven CNC**.

This decision logic is a *direct answer* to any KTU question of the form *"Suggest a suitable CNC configuration for machining a turbine blade and justify."*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Differentiate between point-to-point and contouring CNC systems. Give two examples of machines for each type."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No. | Point-to-Point (PTP) | Contouring (Continuous Path) |
|-------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Tool moves to discrete positions; path between points is uncontrolled | Tool moves along a continuously controlled, interpolated path |
| 2 | Suitable for drilling, tapping, spot welding | Suitable for profile milling, die-mould machining, gear cutting |
| 3 | Requires only end-point coordinate programming | Requires interpolation of straight lines, arcs, splines |
| 4 | Examples: CNC drilling machine, CNC turret punch press | Examples: CNC VMC, CNC wire-EDM, 5-axis machining centre |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**"What is an open-loop CNC system? State its two main limitations."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **open-loop CNC system** is one in which there is **no feedback sensor** to confirm the actual position of the machine slide. The controller sends a fixed number of pulses to a stepper motor, which converts each pulse into a discrete angular step, and hence a discrete linear movement of the slide via the lead screw.

**Two main limitations:**
1. **Lost-step error** — under heavy cutting loads the stepper may miss pulses, causing positional error that is **never detected or corrected**.
2. **Limited torque and accuracy** — stepper motors lose torque at high speeds, restricting the system to **light-duty, low-speed** applications (typically $\pm 0.05$ mm accuracy).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations**, every Module question offers an internal choice between two full 14-mark questions. Both options are provided below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive Classification & Comparison

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PERAT528 Module 1]**

**(a)** Classify CNC machine tools based on **(i) motion control, (ii) feedback control, and (iii) number of axes**. For each category, provide **one example machine and one typical application**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat **block diagram**, explain the **open-loop and closed-loop CNC systems**. Compare them in terms of accuracy, cost, complexity, and application. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Motion Control Classification (2.5 marks):**
- **Point-to-Point (PTP):** Tool moves between discrete positions; path irrelevant. *Example:* CNC drilling machine. *Application:* PCB hole drilling.
- **Straight-line:** Tool moves in straight lines along principal axes. *Example:* Simple CNC lathe. *Application:* Straight turning, facing.
- **Contouring (Continuous Path):** Tool moves along an interpolated path including arcs and curves. *Example:* CNC VMC. *Application:* Mould cavity machining.

**Step 2 — Feedback Control Classification (2.5 marks):**
- **Open-loop:** No feedback; uses stepper motor. *Example:* Desktop CNC engraver. *Application:* Light PCB prototyping.
- **Closed-loop:** Continuous position feedback; uses servo motor. *Example:* Industrial 5-axis machining centre. *Application:* Aerospace turbine blade machining.

**Step 3 — Axis-Count Classification (2 marks):**
- **2-axis:** CNC lathe (X, Z) → shaft turning.
- **3-axis:** VMC (X, Y, Z) → prismatic part milling.
- **4-axis:** VMC + rotary table (A-axis) → cylindrical engraving.
- **5-axis:** VMC + 2 rotational axes (A, B) → die & mould, impellers.

**(b) Block Diagram & Comparison (7 marks):**

**Open-loop block flow:**
$$
\text{MCU} \rightarrow \text{Pulse Generator} \rightarrow \text{Driver} \rightarrow \text{Stepper Motor} \rightarrow \text{Lead Screw} \rightarrow \text{Slide}
$$
*(No return path — control is "blind.")*

**Closed-loop block flow:**
$$
\text{MCU} \rightarrow \text{Servo Drive} \rightarrow \text{Servo Motor} \rightarrow \text{Ball Screw} \rightarrow \text{Slide} \rightarrow \text{Encoder} \rightarrow \text{feedback to MCU}
$$

**Comparison table (key points expected in answer):**

| Parameter | Open-loop | Closed-loop |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| Accuracy | $\pm 0.05$ mm | $\pm 0.001$ mm |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Complexity | Simple | Complex (PID tuning) |
| Error detection | None | Continuous |
| Application | Hobby, education | Production, aerospace |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Axis Configuration & Numerical Problem

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PERAT528 Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the difference between **2-axis, 3-axis, 4-axis, and 5-axis CNC machines** with suitable **sketches and example applications** for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CNC milling machine has a **ball screw of lead 12 mm** and is driven by a servo motor that requires **2500 pulses per revolution**. Calculate the **(i) axis resolution, (ii) feed rate in mm/min when the pulse frequency is 5000 pulses per second, and (iii) time to traverse 300 mm**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Axis Configurations (7 marks):**

- **2-axis (X, Z):** Used in CNC lathes for turning, facing, parting. Only radial and axial feed controlled.
- **2.5-axis:** X, Y interpolated, Z is positioned but not interpolated. Used for pocket milling and 2D profile + depth.
- **3-axis (X, Y, Z):** Used in VMCs. All three linear axes interpolated simultaneously for 3D cavity milling.
- **4-axis (X, Y, Z + A):** Adds a rotational axis (A) on a trunnion table. Used for cylindrical parts, cam profiles, wrap-around milling.
- **5-axis (X, Y, Z + A + B/C):** Adds a second rotational axis. Used for sculptured surfaces: impellers, blisks, turbine blades, moulds. The tool can approach the workpiece from any direction in a single setup.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Lead $L = 12$ mm/rev
- Pulses per revolution $N = 2500$
- Pulse frequency $f_{\text{pulse}} = 5000$ pulses/s
- Distance $D = 300$ mm

**Step 1 — Resolution (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{L}{N} \\
&= \frac{12}{2500} \\
&= 0.0048 \text{ mm/pulse} \\
&= 4.8 \;\mu\text{m/pulse}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Feed rate in mm/min (2.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{feed}} &= 60 \times f_{\text{pulse}} \times R \\
&= 60 \times 5000 \times 0.0048 \\
&= 1440 \text{ mm/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Time to traverse 300 mm (2.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{D}{f_{\text{feed}}} \\
&= \frac{300}{1440} \text{ min} \\
&= 0.2083 \text{ min} \\
&= 12.5 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
1. Resolution $= 4.8 \;\mu\text{m/pulse}$
2. Feed rate $= 1440$ mm/min
3. Time $\approx 12.5$ seconds

---

### 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons Students Lose Marks on This Topic (KTU 2024 ESE):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Straight-line" with "Contouring."** Straight-line control can only move along axes parallel to the slides. Contouring can move along *any* straight line plus arcs. Marking both as "linear motion" costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Omitting the feedback path in the block diagram.** A block diagram of a closed-loop CNC *without* the encoder/feedback arrow is treated as open-loop by examiners and **attracts a 2-mark penalty**.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert pulses/sec to pulses/min in feed-rate problems.** This is the **#1 numerical error** flagged in the 2022 and 2023 ESE valuation reports. Always multiply by **60**.
> 4. **Writing "2.5D" as "two-and-a-half dimensions" without explaining it** as "X, Y interpolated with Z indexed between levels." Examiners want the engineering meaning, not the literal expansion.
> 5. **Mixing up axis labels.** The standard convention is: X (longest travel, longitudinal), Y (orthogonal, in the horizontal plane), Z (parallel to spindle). Rotational axes are **A (around X), B (around Y), C (around Z)**. Using the wrong letter convention is a 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **Stating "CNC is automatic" without classifying it.** Definition-only answers without classification hierarchy lose the application-mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorise Before ESE:**

- ☐ CNC = **Computer Numerical Control** — programmable automation using a dedicated MCU.
- ☐ Classification is based on **5 axes**: motion control, feedback, axis count, power source, application.
- ☐ **PTP** → no path control; **Straight-line** → orthogonal path; **Contouring** → any interpolated path.
- ☐ **Open-loop** = stepper, no feedback, blind, low cost.
- ☐ **Closed-loop** = servo, with encoder feedback, high accuracy, high cost.
- ☐ **Semi-closed** = motor-mounted rotary encoder (most common in industry).
- ☐ **2-axis** = lathe (X, Z); **3-axis** = VMC (X, Y, Z).
- ☐ **4-axis** = VMC + A (rotary table); **5-axis** = VMC + A + B/C (turbine blade, mould).
- ☐ **Resolution formula:** $R = L / N$ (mm/pulse).
- ☐ **Feed rate formula:** $f_{\text{feed}} = 60 \times f_{\text{pulse}} \times R$ (mm/min).
- ☐ **Stepper step angle** typical values: **1.8°** (200 steps/rev) or **0.9°** (400 steps/rev).
- ☐ Always convert **pulses/sec → pulses/min by multiplying by 60**.
- ☐ In closed-loop diagrams, **never omit the feedback arrow** from encoder to MCU.
- ☐ Lathe is fundamentally **2-axis** (X radial + Z axial), even though CNC lathes may have C-axis and Y-axis as options.
- ☐ **Hydraulic drives** are still used in large machines (Mazak, Okuma legacy); **electric servo drives dominate** modern CNC.
- ☐ KTU 2024 Scheme frequently tests: (i) classification enumeration, (ii) open vs closed loop comparison, (iii) axis-count application matching, (iv) resolution/feed numerical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Classify the part → choose the axis count → choose the loop type → compute the resolution → state the feed rate."* This 5-step mental sequence answers **80% of KTU Module-1 questions** on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Numerical Control (NC)** and **Computer Numerical Control (CNC)** machine tools, every automated motion can be classified under one of two fundamental motion-control philosophies:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Point-to-Point (PTP) System (Positioning System):**
> A motion-control strategy in which the cutting tool is commanded to move from one *discrete programmed position* to another, with **no requirement to control the path, trajectory, or feed-rate during the transit motion**. Machining or material-removal action occurs **only after** the tool has been precisely positioned at the target coordinate. The path taken between successive points is irrelevant to the part geometry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Contouring System (Continuous-Path System):**
> A motion-control strategy in which the cutting tool is required to move along a **precisely defined geometric path** (a straight line, an arc, a spline, or a complex 3-D curve) with **simultaneous, coordinated, and continuously variable motion along two or more axes**. Material removal takes place *during* the motion, so the trajectory itself is the product feature.

The classification is governed by the **number of axes that must be controlled simultaneously** and the **shape of the cutter-path** that constitutes the actual part geometry.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are holding a pen and you want to draw your name on paper:

- **Point-to-Point analogy:** You are asked only to *prick holes* on the paper at specific spots (say, the corner of an envelope window and the start of an address line). You can take *any path* — a squiggle, a straight jump, or even lift the pen and place it directly. Only the **final landing coordinate matters**. This is exactly how a CNC **drilling, tapping, or punching** machine behaves.

- **Contouring analogy:** You are asked to *sign your signature*. Now the actual *curve* you draw — the swooping loops of your name — IS the artwork. Every millimetre of the path is important; if you deviate, the signature looks wrong. This is exactly how a CNC **milling or turning** machine carves a curved mould cavity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Exam:**
> **"PTP = Position the Pen"** (final location matters, path is free)
> **"CP = Curve is the Product"** (path itself is the geometry)

---

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Industrial Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of simultaneous controlled axes** | $N_{axes}$ | 2 to 5 (modern: up to 12) |
| **Least Count / Resolution** | $\Delta x$ | **0.001 mm to 0.01 mm** |
| **Repeatability** | $R$ | **± 0.0025 mm to ± 0.01 mm** |
| **Positioning Accuracy** | $A_p$ | **± 0.005 mm to ± 0.025 mm** |
| **Maximum Feed-rate** | $V_f$ | **5 m/min to 30 m/min** (high-speed: 60+ m/min) |
| **Rapid Traverse Rate** | $V_r$ | **15 m/min to 80 m/min** |
| **Number of pulses per revolution** | $n_p$ | **2000 to 10 000 pulses/rev** |

---

## 1.4 Sub-Categorisation of Control Systems (Hierarchy)

For completeness with the KTU 2024 syllabus, the **two broad philosophies are further refined into five classes** of NC systems based on **Simultaneously Controlled Axes (SCA)** and path-control capability:

1. **PTP (Positioning)**
2. **Straight-Cut Control (1-D contour along a single axis)**
3. **Contouring – 2-axis simultaneous**
4. **Contouring – 2½-axis (2 axes contouring + 1 indexing)**
5. **Contouring – 3-axis simultaneous**
6. **Contouring – 4- and 5-axis simultaneous**

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Decomposition

### 2.1.1 Point-to-Point (PTP) Control Logic

The PTP controller follows a strict **state-machine sequence** for every block in the part program:

1. **Block Decode:** The controller parses the destination coordinates (e.g., `X35.0 Y20.0`).
2. **Rapid Traverse Engage:** The axes are driven at the **maximum (rapid) feed-rate** along the shortest logical path to the target. Most controllers default to **linear interpolation in rapid** (G00), but the *intermediate path is geometrically irrelevant* for the part.
3. **Deceleration Zone:** As the axis approaches the commanded position, the drive slows based on the **position-error threshold**.
4. **In-Position Confirmation:** The axis must fall within the **positioning tolerance window** (typically $\pm 1$ least count) before the controller issues an M-function such as `M03` (spindle ON) or `M08` (coolant ON).
5. **Work-Performing Function:** Drilling, boring, tapping, or punching is executed at the dwelled position.
6. **Spindle Stop / Retract:** Auxiliary functions reset, and the cycle moves to the next block.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** PTP systems therefore optimise for **minimum traverse time**, not path accuracy. This is why CNC drill presses and EDM (Electrical Discharge Machining) machines are pure PTP systems.

### 2.1.2 Contouring (Continuous-Path) Control Logic

The contouring controller treats every move as a **continuous geometric relationship** between axes:

1. **Block Decode & Interpolator Initiation:** The end-point, feed-rate (`F`), and interpolation mode (`G01`, `G02`, `G03`) are read.
2. **Trajectory Generation (Interpolation):** The interpolator generates an **incremental pulse stream** for *each* controlled axis. The ratio of these pulse streams defines the slope of the cutter path.
3. **Coordinate Transformation:** For non-orthogonal part geometries (e.g., a circular pocket), the controller continuously converts from **part coordinates to machine coordinates**.
4. **Velocity Profiling:** Acceleration / deceleration (ACC/DEC) is applied to maintain the commanded feed-rate at the cutter tip, **not at individual axes** — this is called **feed-forward along the tool-path tangent**.
5. **Closed-Loop Feedback (if used):** Glass / magnetic scales report actual position; the position-error is nullified within each sampling period (typically **1 ms to 4 ms**).
6. **Continuous Cutting:** Material is removed throughout the motion, so any deviation becomes a geometric defect.

### 2.1.3 Why Contouring Demands Multiple Simultaneous Axes

Consider a straight line from $(0,0)$ to $(30,40)$ mm in the XY-plane. The machine must deliver pulses such that:
$$\text{Pulse}_x : \text{Pulse}_y = 30 : 40 = 3 : 4$$
This *pulse-distribution ratio* must change **at every interpolation cycle** to follow a curve, which is why contouring requires a real-time **Digital Differential Analyser (DDA)** or **Bresenham-type** interpolator in hardware/software.

---

## 2.2 Types of Interpolators Used in Contouring Systems

| Interpolator Type | G-Code | Mathematical Basis | Industrial Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear Interpolation** | `G01` | Straight-line equation $y = mx + c$ | Milling straight slots, edges |
| **Circular Interpolation** | `G02 / G03` | $\Delta X^2 + \Delta Y^2 = R^2$ | Arcs, holes, fillets |
| **Helical Interpolation** | `G02 / G03` + linear Z | Parametric helix $x = R\cos\theta$, $y = R\sin\theta$, $z = k\theta$ | Thread milling, spiral ramps |
| **Parabolic / Quadratic** | Proprietary | Second-degree spline | Aerospace free-form surfaces |
| **Cubic / Spline (NURBS)** | `G05 / G06.2` | Non-Uniform Rational B-Spline | Mould & die finishing |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Definition | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Resolution of a Leadscrew-Driven Axis** | $\Delta x = \dfrac{p}{n_p}$ | $p$ = pitch of leadscrew (mm/rev); $n_p$ = pulses per revolution |
| 2 | **Resolution of a Rack-and-Pinion Axis** | $\Delta x = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_m}{n_p}$ | $D_m$ = pitch diameter of pinion (mm) |
| 3 | **Total Positioning Error** | $E_{total} = E_{screw} + E_{backlash} + E_{thermal} + E_{load}$ | Each in mm |
| 4 | **Backlash Compensation** | $X_{comp} = X_{cmd} + B \cdot \text{sign}(\dot{x})$ | $B$ = backlash value (mm) |
| 5 | **Linear Interpolation Velocity Vector** | $\vec{V} = V_f(\cos\theta \hat{i} + \sin\theta \hat{j})$ | $V_f$ = programmed feed; $\theta$ = tool-path angle |
| 6 | **Axis Velocity Component** | $V_x = V_f \cos\theta,\ \ V_y = V_f \sin\theta$ | For straight line at angle $\theta$ |
| 7 | **Circular Interpolator Parametric** | $X = X_c + R\cos\phi,\ Y = Y_c + R\sin\phi$ | $(X_c, Y_c)$ = centre; $\phi$ = parameter |
| 8 | **Repeatability Definition** | $R = \pm \dfrac{X_{max,error} - X_{min,error}}{2}$ | Statistical spread |
| 9 | **Feed-rate Override Factor** | $V_{actual} = V_{cmd} \cdot K_{ov}$ | $K_{ov}$ typically 0% to 200% |
| 10 | **Pulse Distribution for Slope $m$** | $\dfrac{f_y}{f_x} = m = \dfrac{\Delta Y}{\Delta X}$ | Pulse frequencies |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** In KTU valuation, the **Resolution formula** ($\Delta x = p/n_p$) is the single most-tested equation in this module. Always state the **units explicitly** ($mm/pulse$).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | System Used | Why |
|---|---|---|
| PCB drilling | **PTP** | Only hole coordinates matter; thousands of holes per board |
| Die / Punch press | **PTP** | Punch impact occurs at fixed station |
| 3-Axis VMC mould pocket | **Contouring** | Curved cavity walls require path control |
| Lathe turning of a shaft | **Contouring (2-axis XZ)** | Diameter profile along length |
| 5-Axis aerospace blade | **Contouring (5-axis)** | Complex swept surface |
| Wire-EDM | **PTP / 2-axis** | Each wire-cut is a straight or tapered line |
| Laser engraving of text | **Contouring** | Letterforms are curves |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Resolution of a Leadscrew-Driven CNC Axis

### Statement
Derive the expression for the **least count (resolution)** of a leadscrew-driven linear axis of a CNC machine, starting from the basic kinematic relationship between one pulse and one revolution.

### Given
- Pitch of the leadscrew: $p$ (mm per revolution)
- Pulses generated by the encoder/optical disc per revolution of the motor: $n_p$ (pulses/rev)
- Drive ratio between motor and leadscrew: $1:1$ (direct drive, typical for clarity)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Establish the relationship between motor revolution and linear motion**

For one complete revolution of the leadscrew, the nut (carrying the table) translates axially by exactly the lead of the screw. Since this is a single-start thread:
$$\text{Linear displacement per revolution} = p \ \text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Establish the relationship between pulses and motor revolution**

The feedback device (encoder) on the motor shaft emits $n_p$ discrete pulses for every full revolution:
$$\text{Revolution per pulse} = \dfrac{1}{n_p} \ \text{rev/pulse}$$

**Step 3 — Combine the two relationships (multiplication principle)**

Linear displacement per pulse = (Displacement per revolution) × (Revolutions per pulse)
$$\Delta x = p \times \dfrac{1}{n_p}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\Delta x = \dfrac{p}{n_p} \ \text{mm/pulse}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Illustration**

Suppose $p = 10$ mm/rev and $n_p = 4000$ pulses/rev:
$$\Delta x = \dfrac{10}{4000} = 0.0025 \ \text{mm/pulse} = 2.5 \ \mu\text{m}$$

This means the smallest programmable increment along that axis is **2.5 μm**, satisfying modern CNC resolution requirements.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Axis Velocity Components for Linear Interpolation

### Statement
Show that for a straight-line tool motion at a programmed feed-rate $V_f$ inclined at angle $\theta$ to the X-axis, the individual axis feed-rates are $V_x = V_f \cos\theta$ and $V_y = V_f \sin\theta$.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the tool-path vector**

The tool-path velocity vector along the straight line has magnitude $V_f$ and direction $(\cos\theta,\ \sin\theta)$:
$$\vec{V} = V_f\cos\theta \,\hat{i} + V_f\sin\theta \,\hat{j}$$

**Step 2 — Decompose the vector along machine axes**

By definition of vector components:
$$V_x = \vec{V}\cdot\hat{i} = V_f \cos\theta$$
$$V_y = \vec{V}\cdot\hat{j} = V_f \sin\theta$$

**Step 3 — Verify with magnitude**
$$\sqrt{V_x^{\,2} + V_y^{\,2}} = \sqrt{V_f^2 \cos^2\theta + V_f^2 \sin^2\theta} = V_f\sqrt{1} = V_f \ \ \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Illustration**

For a move from $(0,0)$ to $(30,40)$ mm at $V_f = 100$ mm/min:
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{40}{30}\right) = 53.13^{\circ}$$
$$V_x = 100 \cos 53.13^{\circ} = 60 \ \text{mm/min}$$
$$V_y = 100 \sin 53.13^{\circ} = 80 \ \text{mm/min}$$

Note that $V_x : V_y = 60 : 80 = 3 : 4 = \Delta X : \Delta Y$ — confirming the pulse-distribution principle.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pulse-Distribution Ratio from Endpoint Coordinates

### Statement
For a contouring controller executing a linear move, derive the ratio of pulse frequencies required on the X and Y axes to produce a straight line.

### Derivation

Let the move be from $(X_1, Y_1)$ to $(X_2, Y_2)$ over total time $T$.

**Step 1 — Define axis velocities**
$$V_x = \dfrac{\Delta X}{T} = \dfrac{X_2 - X_1}{T}, \qquad V_y = \dfrac{\Delta Y}{T} = \dfrac{Y_2 - Y_1}{T}$$

**Step 2 — Express velocities in pulses per second**

Each axis emits pulses at a frequency proportional to its velocity, with the proportionality constant being the resolution $\Delta x$:
$$f_x = \dfrac{V_x}{\Delta x}, \qquad f_y = \dfrac{V_y}{\Delta x}$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio**
$$\dfrac{f_y}{f_x} = \dfrac{V_y/\Delta x}{V_x/\Delta x} = \dfrac{V_y}{V_x} = \dfrac{\Delta Y}{\Delta X}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\dfrac{f_y}{f_x} = \dfrac{\Delta Y}{\Delta X}}$$

**Step 4 — Physical Interpretation**

This ratio is *constant* for a straight-line move. For a **circular arc**, the ratio $f_y / f_x$ must be **updated every interpolation cycle** so that:
$$\dfrac{f_y}{f_x} = -\dfrac{X - X_c}{Y - Y_c}$$
which produces a continuously changing slope — the hallmark of true contouring.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — PTP vs Contouring

| Parameter | Point-to-Point (PTP) | Contouring (Continuous Path) |
|---|---|---|
| **Path control during transit** | None (free path) | Strict, geometrically defined |
| **Axes simultaneously controlled** | 1 at a time (sequential) | $\geq 2$ simultaneously |
| **Cutting action during transit** | NO (only after positioning) | YES (continuous) |
| **Controller complexity** | Low | High (real-time interpolator) |
| **Memory of intermediate path** | Not required | Required (interpolation buffer) |
| **Loop type** | Mostly open-loop acceptable | Closed-loop mandatory |
| **Typical feed during transit** | Rapid (max) | Programmed feed ($F$ code) |
| **Typical accuracy demand** | Positioning accuracy only | Path accuracy + position |
| **Typical processes** | Drilling, boring, punching, spot welding | Milling, turning, EDM cutting, laser cutting |
| **Example G-codes** | `G00`, `G81–G89` | `G01`, `G02`, `G03` |
| **Cost of controller** | Lower | Higher |
| **Lead-time programming** | Simple | Requires CAM / skilled programmer |
| **Cycle-time optimisation** | Dominated by rapid traverse | Dominated by cutting feed |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: PTP vs Contouring Control Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Part Program G Code Source] --> B[DECODER Block Level Parser]
    B --> C{Interpolation Mode Flag}
    C -->|G00 G81 G83| D[PTP TRAJECTORY GENERATOR]
    C -->|G01 Linear| E[LINEAR INTERPOLATOR DDA]
    C -->|G02 G03 Circular| F[CIRCULAR INTERPOLATOR DDA]
    C -->|G02 G03 plus Z| G[HELICAL INTERPOLATOR]
    D --> H[AXIS DRIVE X]
    D --> I[AXIS DRIVE Y]
    D --> J[AXIS DRIVE Z]
    E --> H
    E --> I
    F --> H
    F --> I
    G --> H
    G --> I
    G --> J
    H --> K[POSITION FEEDBACK ENCODER]
    I --> L[POSITION FEEDBACK ENCODER]
    J --> M[POSITION FEEDBACK ENCODER]
    K --> N[CLOSED LOOP POSITION COMPARATOR]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> H
    N --> I
    N --> J
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Note how the PTP path routes directly to the drives without passing through the DDA interpolators (E, F, G). Only the contouring path engages the real-time interpolators. This single architectural distinction captures the entire difference between the two system classes.

---

## 4.2 Decision Flow — Is the Process PTP or Contouring?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Manufacturing Process Start] --> B{Is material removed DURING transit motion?}
    B -->|NO| C[PTP Positioning System]
    B -->|YES| D{Are two or more axes moving SIMULTANEOUSLY?}
    D -->|NO Only one axis at a time| E[Straight Cut Control 1D]
    D -->|YES| F{What is the cutter path shape?}
    F -->|Straight Line| G[2 Axis Linear Contouring]
    F -->|Arc or Circle| H[2 Axis Circular Contouring]
    F -->|Helix 3D| I[3 Axis Helical Contouring]
    F -->|Free Form Surface| J[3 4 or 5 Axis Spline Contouring]
    C --> K[Typical Drilling Boring Punching]
    E --> L[Typical Lathe Facing]
    G --> M[Typical VMC Slot Milling]
    H --> N[Typical VMC Pocketing]
    I --> O[Typical Thread Milling]
    J --> P[Typical Aerospace Blade Finishing]
```

---

## 4.3 Pulse-Distribution Schematic for Linear Interpolation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Commanded Move X 30 Y 40] --> B[Compute Slope m equal to 40 divided by 30]
    B --> C[Master Clock at f Master Hz]
    C --> D[Frequency Divider for X axis]
    C --> E[Frequency Divider for Y axis]
    D --> F[X Axis Pulse Train f X]
    E --> G[Y Axis Pulse Train f Y]
    F --> H[X Servo Drive]
    G --> I[Y Servo Drive]
    H --> J[Linear Motion Along X]
    I --> K[Linear Motion Along Y]
    J --> L[Cutter Tip Moves in Straight Line at angle theta]
    K --> L
```

> [!NOTE]
> The two pulse trains are *phase-locked* to the same master clock. Their **frequency ratio** equals the **slope** of the desired tool-path — this is the fundamental operating principle of every contouring controller.

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between point-to-point and contouring numerical control systems. Give two examples of machines for each type.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Point-to-Point (PTP) | Contouring (CP) |
|---|---|---|
| **Path Control** | No control of path during transit; only final position matters | Continuous control of path geometry during motion |
| **Simultaneous Axes** | One axis at a time (sequential) | Two or more axes controlled simultaneously |
| **Cutting Action** | Performed only after positioning | Performed throughout the motion |
| **Examples of Machines** | (i) Drilling machine, (ii) Punch press | (i) Vertical Machining Centre (VMC), (ii) CNC Lathe |

> **Examiner's Awarding Pattern:** 1 mark for definition of PTP, 1 mark for definition of Contouring, 1 mark for correct examples (½ mark each). Drawing a clear comparison table fetches full marks easily.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term 'resolution' of a CNC axis. A leadscrew has a pitch of 5 mm and is driven by a stepper motor through a driver that delivers 2000 pulses per revolution. Calculate the resolution.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Resolution is the **smallest incremental linear movement** that the axis can be commanded to perform. It is the least count of the positioning system.

**Calculation (2 marks):**
$$\Delta x = \dfrac{p}{n_p} = \dfrac{5 \ \text{mm}}{2000 \ \text{pulses/rev}} = 0.0025 \ \text{mm/pulse}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta x = 2.5 \ \mu\text{m/pulse}}$$

> **Examiner's Awarding Pattern:** 1 mark for the correct formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for final numerical answer with units. **Losing the unit mark** is the most common error.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **functional architecture of a CNC contouring controller**, clearly identifying the role of the **interpolator**, **servo drives**, and **feedback elements**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CNC milling machine is commanded to move the cutter in a straight line from $(0, 0)$ to $(60, 80)$ mm at a programmed feed-rate of 150 mm/min. Determine:
  (i) The angle of the tool path with the X-axis.
  (ii) The component feed-rates along X and Y axes.
  (iii) The required ratio of pulse frequencies $f_y / f_x$.
  (iv) If the resolution of the machine is 0.005 mm/pulse, compute $f_x$ and $f_y$ in pulses per second. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks):**

The contouring controller comprises the following functional blocks in series:

1. **Part Program Input Unit** (1 mark) — Accepts the G-code / M-code program either via DNC (Direct Numerical Control) link or from local memory.

2. **Decoder / Interpreter** (1 mark) — Parses each block, extracts coordinates, feed-rates, spindle speed, and identifies the active interpolation mode (`G01`, `G02`, etc.).

3. **Interpolator (DDA)** (2 marks) — The heart of the contouring system. Generates incremental pulses for each axis every interpolation cycle (typically 1–4 ms). For linear moves, it maintains a *constant ratio* of pulse frequencies; for circular moves, it dynamically updates the ratio using the circle equation. Types include the **Digital Differential Analyser (DDA)** and the **Bresenham algorithm**.

4. **Position Comparator / Error Detector** (1 mark) — Compares the *commanded position* from the interpolator with the *actual position* from the feedback device and generates a position-error signal $\epsilon$.

5. **Servo Amplifier / Drive** (1 mark) — Amplifies the error signal and drives the DC/AC servo motor, which rotates the leadscrew to move the table.

6. **Feedback Transducer (Linear Scale / Encoder)** (1 mark) — Provides real-time actual position data; may be **rotary encoder** on the motor or **linear glass scale** on the axis (the latter giving better accuracy as it excludes leadscrew errors).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\Delta X = 60$ mm, $\Delta Y = 80$ mm, $V_f = 150$ mm/min, $\Delta x = 0.005$ mm/pulse

**(i) Angle of tool path (1 mark):**
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta Y}{\Delta X}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{80}{60}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.333)$$
$$\boxed{\theta = 53.13^{\circ}}$$

**(ii) Component feed-rates (2 marks):**
$$V_x = V_f \cos\theta = 150 \times \cos 53.13^{\circ} = 150 \times 0.6 = 90 \ \text{mm/min}$$
$$V_y = V_f \sin\theta = 150 \times \sin 53.13^{\circ} = 150 \times 0.8 = 120 \ \text{mm/min}$$

**(iii) Pulse-frequency ratio (2 marks):**
$$\dfrac{f_y}{f_x} = \dfrac{\Delta Y}{\Delta X} = \dfrac{80}{60} = \dfrac{4}{3} = 1.333$$

**(iv) Absolute pulse frequencies (2 marks):**

Convert $V_x$ and $V_y$ to mm/sec:
$$V_x = \dfrac{90}{60} = 1.5 \ \text{mm/s}, \quad V_y = \dfrac{120}{60} = 2.0 \ \text{mm/s}$$

Pulse frequencies:
$$f_x = \dfrac{V_x}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{1.5}{0.005} = 300 \ \text{pulses/s}$$
$$f_y = \dfrac{V_y}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{2.0}{0.005} = 400 \ \text{pulses/s}$$

$$\boxed{f_x = 300 \ \text{Hz}, \quad f_y = 400 \ \text{Hz}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion trap:** When converting mm/min to mm/s, students frequently *forget* to divide by 60, leading to pulse frequencies 60× too large. Always write the conversion step explicitly.
> 2. **Radian–degree confusion:** Some students compute `sin` / `cos` arguments in radians. State "$\theta$ in degrees" before substituting into your calculator.
> 3. **Pulse-frequency ratio units:** $f_y / f_x$ is **dimensionless** — writing "Hz/Hz" loses ½ mark. Just state the numeric ratio.
> 4. **Interpreting "angle with X-axis":** Many students mistakenly compute the angle with the Y-axis. The convention is **measured from the +X axis towards +Y axis, counter-clockwise positive**.
> 5. **Skipping intermediate steps:** KTU examiners award *step marks*. Jumping straight from given data to the answer forfeits up to 3 marks on a 7-mark sub-part.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** What is an **interpolator**? Compare the working principles of **linear and circular interpolators** with suitable sketches of the pulse-distribution strategy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CNC vertical machining centre has X, Y, and Z axes driven by leadscrews of pitch 8 mm each. The servo system delivers 4000 pulses per revolution. For the X-axis:
  (i) Calculate the resolution.
  (ii) If the axis must traverse 200 mm at a feed of 800 mm/min, calculate the total number of pulses required and the time taken.
  (iii) Comment on how the resolution changes if the pulse count per revolution is doubled by using an encoder with finer grating. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Interpolator — Concept & Comparison (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** An **interpolator** is the real-time computational unit inside a contouring CNC controller that **generates a stream of position-command pulses for each simultaneously controlled axis** such that the combined motion of all axes traces the geometric path (line, arc, helix, spline) specified by the active G-code block.

**Linear Interpolator — Working Principle (2 marks):**

For a move from $(X_1, Y_1)$ to $(X_2, Y_2)$ over $T$ seconds:

1. Compute the slope $m = \Delta Y / \Delta X$.
2. Master clock runs at frequency $f_m$.
3. Two frequency dividers (binary counters) split $f_m$ into $f_x$ and $f_y$ such that:
$$\dfrac{f_y}{f_x} = m = \dfrac{\Delta Y}{\Delta X}$$
4. The pulse streams are output to the X and Y servo drives in *lock-step*.
5. The cutter therefore traces a perfect straight line.

**Circular Interpolator — Working Principle (2 marks):**

For a circular arc of centre $(X_c, Y_c)$ and radius $R$:

1. At any current point $(X_i, Y_i)$ on the arc:
$$(X_i - X_c)^2 + (Y_i - Y_c)^2 = R^2$$
2. The instantaneous slope of the tangent is:
$$\dfrac{dY}{dX} = -\dfrac{X_i - X_c}{Y_i - Y_c}$$
3. The interpolator **updates** this slope at every cycle (1–4 ms) and recomputes the frequency ratio $f_y / f_x$ accordingly.
4. Hence, the pulse streams are *not constant* in ratio — they vary continuously, producing the curved tool path.

**Comparison Sketch (1 mark):** A pulse-train timing diagram should show constant ratio for linear vs. continuously varying ratio for circular.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $p = 8$ mm/rev, $n_p = 4000$ pulses/rev, traverse distance $L = 200$ mm, feed $V_f = 800$ mm/min.

**(i) Resolution (2 marks):**
$$\Delta x = \dfrac{p}{n_p} = \dfrac{8}{4000} = 0.002 \ \text{mm/pulse}$$
$$\boxed{\Delta x = 2 \ \mu\text{m}}$$

**(ii) Total Pulses & Time (3 marks):**

Total pulses:
$$N = \dfrac{L}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{200}{0.002} = 100{,}000 \ \text{pulses}$$
$$\boxed{N = 1 \times 10^5 \ \text{pulses}}$$

Time taken at feed-rate $V_f$:
$$t = \dfrac{L}{V_f} = \dfrac{200 \ \text{mm}}{800 \ \text{mm/min}} = 0.25 \ \text{min} = 15 \ \text{seconds}$$
$$\boxed{t = 15 \ \text{s}}$$

**(iii) Comment on Doubling $n_p$ (2 marks):**

If $n_p$ is doubled to 8000 pulses/rev:
$$\Delta x_{new} = \dfrac{p}{2n_p} = \dfrac{\Delta x}{2} = 0.001 \ \text{mm/pulse} = 1 \ \mu\text{m}$$

The resolution becomes **twice as fine** (better), enabling machining of finer features and tighter tolerances. However, this improvement comes at the cost of: (i) doubled pulse-stream bandwidth, (ii) increased demand on servo-loop sampling rate, (iii) potential amplification of vibration if mechanical resonances are not addressed. The leadscrew lead error also becomes more visible because it is now resolved into a larger number of pulses.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Pitch vs. Lead confusion:** For a single-start thread, pitch = lead. KTU 2024 expects this assumption unless stated otherwise. Writing "Pitch" in the formula when the screw is multi-start will be marked wrong.
> 2. **Order-of-magnitude formatting:** Writing $1 \times 10^5$ vs. $100000$ — both are accepted, but writing "$100000$" without commas is harder to read and risks evaluator misreading.
> 3. **Time-unit conversion:** Always state *both* minutes and seconds. Forgetting to convert 0.25 min → 15 s loses ½ mark.
> 4. **Part (iii) requires a comment, not just a number:** Many students stop after computing the new resolution. KTU specifically asks to "comment on how the resolution changes" — meaning discuss **both the improvement AND the trade-offs** (mechanical, electronic, economic). At least 3–4 lines of qualitative discussion are expected.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This Before Every Exam**

- [ ] **PTP** = *Position the Pen*; only final coordinate matters; path is free.
- [ ] **Contouring** = *Curve is the Product*; trajectory itself is geometry.
- [ ] **Drilling, Boring, Tapping, Punching, Spot Welding** → PTP.
- [ ] **Milling, Turning, Laser Cutting, Wire-EDM (along a path)** → Contouring.
- [ ] **Master formula:** $\Delta x = p / n_p$ (resolution of leadscrew axis).
- [ ] **Rack-and-pinion variant:** $\Delta x = \pi D_m / n_p$.
- [ ] **Linear interpolation feed decomposition:** $V_x = V_f \cos\theta$, $V_y = V_f \sin\theta$.
- [ ] **Pulse-frequency ratio (linear):** $f_y / f_x = \Delta Y / \Delta X$.
- [ ] **Pulse-frequency ratio (circular, instantaneous):** $f_y / f_x = -(X - X_c) / (Y - Y_c)$.
- [ ] **PTP uses open-loop OR closed-loop; Contouring requires closed-loop.**
- [ ] **DDA (Digital Differential Analyser)** is the classical contouring interpolator.
- [ ] **Axes simultaneously controlled (SCA)** defines NC class: 2-axis, 2½-axis, 3-axis, 4-axis, 5-axis.
- [ ] **Rapid traverse (G00)** is used for PTP positioning moves at maximum feed.
- [ ] **Cutting feed (G01/G02/G03)** is used for contouring moves at programmed feed-rate.
- [ ] **Always state units** ($mm/pulse$, $mm/min$, $Hz$) — KTU deducts ½ mark for missing units.
- [ ] **Convert mm/min to mm/s** (÷60) before computing pulse frequencies.
- [ ] **2½-axis contouring:** two axes are contouring while the third is *indexed* (not simultaneous).
- [ ] **Modern 5-axis machines** are used for aerospace blades, impellers, and die cavities.
- [ ] **Repetitive accuracy vs. positioning accuracy:** Repeatability is usually ½ of positioning accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Incremental and Absolute Programming Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT528 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Incremental and Absolute Programming Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Computer Numerical Control (CNC) part programming, the two fundamental coordinate referencing philosophies for commanding tool movement are **Incremental Programming** and **Absolute Programming**.

> [!IMPORTANT] Core Definition
> **Absolute Programming (G90):** A coordinate specification method in which every target position of the cutting tool is defined with respect to a **fixed, datum origin (workpiece zero / part zero)**, which is established at the start of the program. The numerical values represent the *exact location* of the tool in the chosen Cartesian coordinate frame.
>
> **Incremental Programming (G91):** A coordinate specification method in which every target position of the cutting tool is defined with respect to the **immediately preceding tool position** (the previous point acts as a temporary, local origin). The numerical values represent the *distance to be moved* from the current location.

These methods are activated respectively by the preparatory codes **G90** (Absolute) and **G91** (Incremental) in standard ISO/EIA CNC programming syntax.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are giving directions to a friend who is standing at a parking lot trying to reach a coffee shop.

**Absolute Programming Analogy (The GPS Map Approach):**
You give a list of GPS coordinates:
- "Stand at Latitude 10°N, Longitude 20°E (Point 1)"
- "Move to Latitude 30°N, Longitude 40°E (Point 2)"
- "Move to Latitude 50°N, Longitude 30°E (Point 3)"

Each instruction is a **complete address from the world's reference point** (Prime Meridian/Equator). The friend does not need to remember where he was. This is **Absolute** — every point is independent of the previous one, and the entire path can be visualized on a single map.

**Incremental Programming Analogy (The Step-by-Step Compass Approach):**
You give relative walking directions:
- "Start here, now walk **20 steps North, 20 steps East**" (from origin to Point 1)
- "From where you are, walk **20 steps North, 10 steps South**" (to Point 2)
- "From where you are, walk **40 steps South, 10 steps South**" (back to origin)

Each instruction depends on the *current* location. Lose your place once, and every subsequent instruction becomes wrong. This is **Incremental** — chain-based, delta-driven movement.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Bold

The key performance metrics and standard values used in KTU board examinations are:
