- **Modal Command**: Both G90 and G91 are *modal* G-codes, meaning once invoked, they remain active for all subsequent motion blocks until cancelled by the opposite code.
- **Default State**: Most CNC controllers default to **G90 (Absolute)** mode at power-on.
- **Typical Part Datum**: **X0, Y0, Z0** is conventionally placed at the center of the workpiece top face (for milling) or at the chuck face (for turning).
- **Axis Convention**: Linear axes **X, Y, Z**; Rotary axes **A, B, C** (all follow the same rule).

> [!NOTE] KTU Syllabus Highlight
> Examiners frequently ask students to convert a part program from Absolute to Incremental (or vice versa). Mastering the sign convention and the running-datum logic is **mandatory** for securing full marks in Part B questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Logic Breakdown

### A. Absolute Programming (G90) — The Logic Steps

1. **Datum Establishment:** The programmer or operator sets the workpiece zero point (origin) before program execution. This is the *only* reference for the entire program.
2. **Direct Coordinate Entry:** Each motion block contains the *final* coordinates of the tool tip (or tool center) measured directly from the origin.
3. **Independence of Blocks:** Every block is mathematically self-contained. Changing the coordinates of one block does not propagate errors to other blocks.
4. **Error Containment:** A typo in one block affects only that one move — the next block still uses the correct absolute coordinates.
5. **Visualization Ease:** A draftsperson can draw the entire tool path directly on the engineering drawing using the absolute coordinates.

### B. Incremental Programming (G91) — The Logic Steps

1. **No Fixed Datum Required:** The origin is conceptually "wherever the tool currently is."
2. **Delta Coordinate Entry:** Each motion block specifies the *change* ($\Delta x$, $\Delta y$, $\Delta z$) required to reach the next point from the current position.
3. **Chain Dependency:** Every block is mathematically linked to the previous one — coordinates are *cumulative differences*.
4. **Error Propagation:** A sign error in one block will offset the tool path, potentially causing collision for all subsequent moves.
5. **Loop & Subroutine Friendliness:** Incremental mode is often preferred for **repeating geometric patterns** (e.g., bolt-hole circles, pocket milling with multiple identical passes) because a single set of deltas can be reused.

### C. The Core Mathematical "Why"

For a tool path passing through a sequence of points $P_1, P_2, P_3, \ldots, P_n$ with coordinates $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$:

**Absolute Coordinates (Direct):**
$$x_i^{\text{abs}} = x_i, \quad y_i^{\text{abs}} = y_i, \quad z_i^{\text{abs}} = z_i$$

**Incremental Coordinates (Differential):**
$$\Delta x_i = x_i - x_{i-1}, \quad \Delta y_i = y_i - y_{i-1}, \quad \Delta z_i = z_i - z_{i-1}$$

The critical relationship is that **Absolute is the integral, Incremental is the derivative** of the tool path:
$$x_i^{\text{abs}} = x_0^{\text{abs}} + \sum_{k=1}^{i} \Delta x_k$$

> [!IMPORTANT] Engineering Reality
> In industry, **G90 (Absolute)** is the **industry default** for ~90% of production programs because of its error-containment property. However, when programming hand-written programs for **lathes with simple turning cycles** or **bolt-circle drilling**, G91 (Incremental) is occasionally preferred.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept / Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Rule | Unit / Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Absolute X-coordinate of point $i$ | $x_i^{\text{abs}}$ | Direct value from origin | mm |
| Incremental X-delta at point $i$ | $\Delta x_i$ | $x_i - x_{i-1}$ | mm |
| Absolute-to-Incremental Conversion | $\Delta x_i$ | $x_i^{\text{abs}} - x_{i-1}^{\text{abs}}$ | mm |
| Incremental-to-Absolute Conversion | $x_i^{\text{abs}}$ | $x_0 + \sum_{k=1}^{i}\Delta x_k$ | mm |
| Sign Convention (+X) | $+$ | Tool moves in +X direction | mm |
| Sign Convention (-X) | $-$ | Tool moves in -X direction | mm |
| Modal G-Code (Absolute) | G90 | Cancels G91 | — |
| Modal G-Code (Incremental) | G91 | Cancels G90 | — |
| Default Mode at Startup | G90 | Most controllers | — |
| Arc Radius (Absolute mode) | R | Signed: $+$ for $\le 180°$, $-$ for $> 180°$ | mm |
| Arc Center Vector (Incremental mode) | I, J, K | Vector from arc start to center | mm |

> [!WARNING] Examiner Trap
> Students often confuse the **sign of I, J, K in G91 mode**. In Incremental mode, the **I, J, K values are still the vector from the arc start point to the arc center** — they are *not* converted to deltas. This is a common board-exam pitfall.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace structural milling:** Absolute programming is mandatory for 5-axis contouring of wing skins to prevent cumulative error.
- **Watch / jewellery micro-machining:** Incremental programming is used for repetitive decorative patterns.
- **Turning operations on CNC lathes:** A mix is common — absolute for the roughing cycle, incremental for the finish pass depth stepping.
- **3D Printing / Additive toolpaths:** Modern slicers generate **absolute G-code** by default for slicer-derived coordinates, but incremental is used for testing G-code manually in MDI mode.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Canonical Conversion Example (Worked Problem)

**Problem Setup (KTU Standard Style):**
A milling tool is required to move through four points to machine a rectangular pocket. The absolute coordinates (in mm) of the four points measured from the workpiece datum are:
- $P_1 = (10, 10)$
- $P_2 = (50, 10)$
- $P_3 = (50, 40)$
- $P_4 = (10, 40)$

The tool starts at $P_0 = (0, 0)$. **Derive both the absolute and incremental program blocks.**

### Step 1: Establish the Reference Frame

The datum (part zero) is at $(0, 0)$. The tool must first rapid to $P_1$ which is $(10, 10)$ in absolute terms. This is the *first* move, and therefore **the incremental value equals the absolute value** for this first block.

### Step 2: Write the Absolute Program

In absolute mode, we simply quote the final coordinates in each block:

| Block | G-Code | X | Y | Logic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| N10 | G90 G00 | X10 | Y10 | Rapid to P1 (same as incremental here) |
| N20 | G01 | X50 | Y10 | Linear cut to P2 |
| N30 | G01 | X50 | Y40 | Linear cut to P3 |
| N40 | G01 | X10 | Y40 | Linear cut to P4 |

### Step 3: Compute the Incremental Deltas

We now apply the core formula $\Delta x_i = x_i - x_{i-1}$ for every transition.

**Move 1: $P_0 \rightarrow P_1$**
$$\Delta x_1 = 10 - 0 = 10 \text{ mm}, \quad \Delta y_1 = 10 - 0 = 10 \text{ mm}$$

**Move 2: $P_1 \rightarrow P_2$**
$$\Delta x_2 = 50 - 10 = 40 \text{ mm}, \quad \Delta y_2 = 10 - 10 = 0 \text{ mm}$$

**Move 3: $P_2 \rightarrow P_3$**
$$\Delta x_3 = 50 - 50 = 0 \text{ mm}, \quad \Delta y_3 = 40 - 10 = 30 \text{ mm}$$

**Move 4: $P_3 \rightarrow P_4$**
$$\Delta x_4 = 10 - 50 = -40 \text{ mm}, \quad \Delta y_4 = 40 - 40 = 0 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 4: Write the Incremental Program

| Block | G-Code | X | Y | Logic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| N10 | G91 G00 | X10 | Y10 | Move +10, +10 from P0 |
| N20 | G01 | X40 | Y0 | Move +40 in X, 0 in Y |
| N30 | G01 | X0 | Y30 | Move 0 in X, +30 in Y |
| N40 | G01 | X-40 | Y0 | Move -40 in X (note the negative sign) |

### Step 5: Verify by Reverse Conversion

Check using $x_i^{\text{abs}} = x_0 + \sum \Delta x_k$:

$$x_4^{\text{abs}} = 0 + 10 + 40 + 0 + (-40) = 10 \text{ mm} \checkmark$$

$$y_4^{\text{abs}} = 0 + 10 + 0 + 30 + 0 = 40 \text{ mm} \checkmark$$

Both modes arrive at the same physical location — they are *mathematically equivalent* in final geometry.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Absolute Programming (G90) | Incremental Programming (G91) |
|---|---|---|
| Reference Point | Workpiece datum (fixed) | Previous tool position (moving) |
| Coordinates Represent | Final location | Distance to move |
| Block Independence | Fully independent | Chain-dependent |
| Error Propagation | Localized to one block | Propagates through all subsequent blocks |
| Calculation Effort | Read directly from drawing | Requires subtraction per block |
| Visualization | Easy — direct plot | Difficult — must track running position |
| Industry Default | **Yes** (most controllers) | Optional |
| Loop Programming | Less convenient | **More convenient** for repeats |
| Datum Loss Effect | Program still works (coordinates unchanged) | Program becomes invalid (no fixed origin) |
| Typical Application | Complex contouring, first-off jobs | Simple stepping, repetitive patterns |

## 3.3 Mixed-Mode Programming — A Special Case

> [!IMPORTANT] Hybrid Programming Concept
> Modern CNC controllers (Fanuc, Siemens, Heidenhain) allow the programmer to **switch between G90 and G91 within the same program**. A typical pattern is:
> - Use **G90** for the main profile (safety + clarity).
> - Switch to **G91** for a sub-loop that performs multiple identical drilling cycles, then switch back to **G90**.

**Example — Drilling 4 Holes on a Pitch Circle (Centre at X0 Y0, Radius 30 mm):**

In **Absolute** (G90):
- H1: X30 Y0
- H2: X0 Y30
- H3: X-30 Y0
- H4: X0 Y-30

In **Incremental** (G91), assuming the tool starts at the first hole position X30 Y0:
- H1: (initial move — absolute or pre-positioned)
- H2: X-30 Y30
- H3: X-30 Y-30
- H4: X30 Y-30

Notice how the incremental moves form a clean **chord-stepping** sequence that can be wrapped in a subroutine.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of CNC Coordinate Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Block Read] --> B{G-Code Check}
    B -->|G90 Detected| C[Set ABS Mode Flag]
    B -->|G91 Detected| D[Set INC Mode Flag]
    C --> E[Read X Y Z as Final Position]
    D --> F[Read X Y Z as Delta from Current]
    E --> G[Update Tool Position to Target]
    F --> H[Add Delta to Current Tool Position]
    G --> I[Send Pulses to Servo Drives]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Physical Axis Movement on Machine]
    J --> K[Update Current Position Register]
    K --> L{Next Block?}
    L -->|Yes| A
    L -->|No| M[Program End]
```

## 4.2 Comparison Flow: Absolute vs Incremental Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Programming Task] --> B{Is datum clearly defined?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Use G90 Absolute]
    B -->|No| D[Consider G91 Incremental]
    C --> E{Is the path a repeating pattern?}
    E -->|No| F[Continue in G90]
    E -->|Yes| G[Switch to G91 for sub-loop]
    D --> H{Is error containment critical?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Define temporary datum then use G90]
    H -->|No| J[Use G91 with running position]
    F --> K[Generate Program]
    G --> K
    J --> K
    I --> K
    K --> L[End]
```

## 4.3 Tool Path Schematic — Rectangular Pocket Example

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0((P0: 0,0)) -->|Abs: X10 Y10 / Inc: X10 Y10| P1((P1: 10,10))
    P1 -->|Abs: X50 Y10 / Inc: X40 Y0| P2((P2: 50,10))
    P2 -->|Abs: X50 Y40 / Inc: X0 Y30| P3((P3: 50,40))
    P3 -->|Abs: X10 Y40 / Inc: X-40 Y0| P4((P4: 10,40))
    P4 -.->|Return to Start| P0
```

## 4.4 Coordinate Frame Schematic (Workpiece Datum)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Machine Home Position] --> B[Workpiece Datum X0 Y0 Z0]
    B --> C[Part Geometry Defined from Datum]
    C --> D1[Point P1: X10 Y10 mm]
    C --> D2[Point P2: X50 Y10 mm]
    C --> D3[Point P3: X50 Y40 mm]
    C --> D4[Point P4: X10 Y40 mm]
    D1 --> E[Absolute Block: X10 Y10]
    D1 --> F[Incremental Block: Delta from P0]
    D2 --> G[Absolute Block: X50 Y10]
    D2 --> H[Incremental Block: Delta from P1]
```

> [!NOTE] Reading the Diagrams
> The flowcharts above abstract the controller logic. In a real CNC, the **Current Position Register (CPR)** is a critical hardware-level register that holds the live tool coordinates. In G91 mode, the controller algebraically *adds* the commanded delta to this register; in G90 mode, it *overwrites* the register with the commanded absolute value.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between absolute and incremental programming systems in CNC with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Absolute (G90) | Incremental (G91) |
|---|---|---|
| **Reference** | Fixed workpiece datum | Current tool position |
| **Coordinate Meaning** | Final location of tool | Distance to be moved |
| **Error Effect** | Contained within one block | Propagates to all subsequent blocks |
| **Visualization** | Directly read from drawing | Requires running subtraction |
| **Example Block** | `G01 X50 Y30` (go to point 50,30) | `G01 X20 Y10` (move 20,10 from here) |

**(1 mark)** for definition of each, **(1 mark)** for tabular difference. **Examiner awards remaining mark** for a correctly framed example. **Total = 3 marks.**

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is the function of G90 and G91 G-codes? State the default mode at machine startup.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **G90** activates the **Absolute Programming mode**, in which all subsequent coordinate values are interpreted as positions measured from the workpiece datum (origin). **(1 mark)**
- **G91** activates the **Incremental Programming mode**, in which all subsequent coordinate values are interpreted as *distances* to be moved from the tool's current position. **(1 mark)**
- **Default Mode:** At machine power-on, most CNC controllers (including Fanuc and Siemens Sinumerik) default to **G90 (Absolute) mode**. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice Option) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**A milling cutter is required to machine a triangular profile passing through three points. The absolute coordinates (in mm) of the three vertices, measured from the workpiece datum, are:**
- **P1 = (20, 20)**
- **P2 = (60, 20)**
- **P3 = (40, 60)**

**The tool starts at the origin (0, 0).**

**(a) Write the complete CNC part program in Absolute Programming (G90) mode using G01 linear interpolation.** **(7 Marks)**

**(b) Convert the program into Incremental Programming (G91) mode and write the corresponding part program. Show all conversion calculations.** **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

##### Part (a) — Absolute Program (7 Marks)

The tool starts at $P_0 = (0,0)$ and must first rapid to $P_1 = (20,20)$, then linearly cut through $P_2$ and $P_3$.

**Step 1:** Identify the moves.
- Move 1: $P_0 \rightarrow P_1$ (Rapid)
- Move 2: $P_1 \rightarrow P_2$ (Linear cut)
- Move 3: $P_2 \rightarrow P_3$ (Linear cut)

**Step 2:** In absolute mode, the X and Y values in each block are the *final coordinates* of the tool.

**Program:**

```
O0001                           (Program number)
N10 G90 G54 G00 X20.0 Y20.0     (Absolute mode, rapid to P1)
N20 G01 X60.0 Y20.0 F100        (Linear cut to P2, feed 100 mm/min)
N30 G01 X40.0 Y60.0 F100        (Linear cut to P3, feed 100 mm/min)
N40 G00 X0 Y0                   (Rapid return to origin)
M30                             (Program end)
```

**Mark Split (7 marks):**
- Correct G90 declaration: **1 mark**
- Correct absolute X-Y values for P1, P2, P3: **3 marks** (1 each)
- Proper G00/G01/F-code usage: **2 marks**
- Clean M30 termination: **1 mark**

##### Part (b) — Incremental Conversion (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Apply the conversion formula $\Delta x_i = x_i - x_{i-1}$, $\Delta y_i = y_i - y_{i-1}$.

**Move 1: $P_0(0,0) \rightarrow P_1(20,20)$**
$$\Delta x_1 = 20 - 0 = +20 \text{ mm}, \quad \Delta y_1 = 20 - 0 = +20 \text{ mm}$$

**Move 2: $P_1(20,20) \rightarrow P_2(60,20)$**
$$\Delta x_2 = 60 - 20 = +40 \text{ mm}, \quad \Delta y_2 = 20 - 20 = 0 \text{ mm}$$

**Move 3: $P_2(60,20) \rightarrow P_3(40,60)$**
$$\Delta x_3 = 40 - 60 = -20 \text{ mm}, \quad \Delta y_3 = 60 - 20 = +40 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Write the incremental program.

```
O0001
N10 G91 G00 X20.0 Y20.0        (From P0, move +20, +20 to reach P1)
N20 G01 X40.0 Y0 F100          (From P1, move +40 in X, 0 in Y to reach P2)
N30 G01 X-20.0 Y40.0 F100      (From P2, move -20 in X, +40 in Y to reach P3)
N40 G00 X-40.0 Y-60.0          (Return to origin: P3 to P0)
M30
```

**Mark Split (7 marks):**
- Correct G91 declaration: **1 mark**
- Move 1 delta calculation: **1 mark**
- Move 2 delta calculation: **2 marks** (key step)
- Move 3 delta calculation including negative sign: **2 marks**
- Correct return-to-origin delta: **1 mark**

> [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall
> **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget the **sign of the delta** for Move 3 (X becomes negative). Losing this sign means the tool goes to the wrong quadrant and crashes. **Penalty: 2 marks.**
> **Common Mistake 2:** Students write the incremental coordinates as if they were absolute (e.g., writing `X40 Y60` instead of `X-20 Y40`). This means the student has not understood the conversion logic. **Penalty: Full 7 marks lost on part (b).**
> **Common Mistake 3:** Students omit the return-to-origin move in incremental mode, forgetting that the tool is *still* at $P_3$ and must be commanded back via deltas. **Penalty: 1 mark.**

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**A CNC lathe is to perform a rough turning pass with the tool traversing the following points (in mm, with X as diameter and Z as length):**
- **P1 = (X30, Z2)**
- **P2 = (X30, Z-20)**
- **P3 = (X50, Z-20)**
- **P4 = (X50, Z2)**

**The tool starts at (X0, Z0).**

**(a) Write the CNC part program using G90 Absolute mode.** **(7 Marks)**

**(b) Convert the program to G91 Incremental mode, showing the conversion table.** **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

##### Part (a) — G90 Absolute Program (7 Marks)

**Direct coordinate entry:**

```
O0002
N10 G90 G00 X30.0 Z2.0          (Rapid to P1)
N20 G01 X30.0 Z-20.0 F150       (Longitudinal cut to P2)
N30 G01 X50.0 Z-20.0 F150       (Facing cut to P3)
N40 G01 X50.0 Z2.0 F150         (Return to P4)
N50 G00 X100.0 Z100.0           (Tool retract to safe position)
M30
```

**Mark Split (7 marks):** 1 mark per correctly written block, 1 mark for G90 declaration, 1 mark for tool retract.

##### Part (b) — G91 Incremental Conversion (7 Marks)

**Conversion Table:**

| Move | From → To | $\Delta X$ (X2 − X1) | $\Delta Z$ (Z2 − Z1) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | (0,0) → (30,2) | $+30$ | $+2$ |
| 2 | (30,2) → (30,-20) | $0$ | $-22$ |
| 3 | (30,-20) → (50,-20) | $+20$ | $0$ |
| 4 | (50,-20) → (50,2) | $0$ | $+22$ |
| 5 | (50,2) → (100,100) | $+50$ | $+98$ |

**Program:**

```
O0002
N10 G91 G00 X30.0 Z2.0          (Move to P1)
N20 G01 X0 Z-22.0 F150          (Move to P2)
N30 G01 X20.0 Z0 F150           (Move to P3)
N40 G01 X0 Z22.0 F150           (Move to P4)
N50 G00 X50.0 Z98.0             (Retract)
M30
```

**Mark Split (7 marks):** 2 marks for the conversion table, 4 marks for the program with correct deltas (1 mark per block), 1 mark for G91 declaration.

> [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B)
> **Common Mistake 1:** On a CNC **lathe**, X is the *diameter*, not the radius. Students who treat X as radius will halve all deltas. **Penalty: 2–3 marks depending on severity.**
> **Common Mistake 2:** In Move 2, the Z delta is **$-22$**, not $-18$ (because Z goes from $+2$ to $-20$). Sign errors on Z are very common. **Penalty: 2 marks.**
> **Common Mistake 3:** Failing to write the **conversion table** explicitly. KTU examiners award easy marks for a tabulated calculation; skipping it forfeits those marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] Rapid Revision Checklist
>
> - **G90 = Absolute** (positions from the fixed origin). **G91 = Incremental** (distances from the current point). **(Core fact)**
> - Both G90 and G91 are **modal** — they stay active until the opposite code is issued. **(Exam favourite)**
> - The default mode at machine startup is **G90 (Absolute)**. **(Frequently asked)**
> - The conversion formula is **$\Delta x_i = x_i - x_{i-1}$**. **(Heart of every conversion problem)**
> - For the **first move** (from datum to first point), **incremental = absolute** because both reference the same starting point. **(Subtle but key)**
> - **Absolute programming** is **error-safe** (errors don't propagate). **Incremental programming** is **chain-dependent** (errors accumulate). **(Conceptual question)**
> - **Incremental mode is preferred** for repetitive patterns like bolt circles and pocket milling cycles. **(Application question)**
> - In a **mixed program**, you can toggle G90/G91 block-by-block, but you must state the mode in every block that requires a specific interpretation. **(Advanced question)**
> - For **arc moves (G02/G03)**, the I, J, K values are *always* vectors from arc start to center, regardless of G90/G91. **(Common trap)**
> - On **CNC lathes**, X is the **diameter** — never forget this when computing deltas for turning. **(Lathe-specific trap)**
> - **Workpiece datum** is set with **G54–G59** (work coordinate selector) and is the origin for all G90 coordinates. **(Foundation concept)**
> - A single **negative sign** error in incremental mode is the **#1 cause of tool crashes** in industry. **(Safety & practical reality)**

**End of Notes — Module 1, Incremental and Absolute Programming Methods**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Open Loop and Closed Loop Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT528 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Open Loop and Closed Loop Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Open Loop System — Formal KTU Definition

An **Open Loop System** (also called a *non-feedback system* or *feedforward control system*) is a control architecture in which the **control action is completely independent of the process output**. The controller sends a command to the actuator, and the actuator drives the process without any measurement of the resulting output being returned to the controller.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, an open loop manufacturing system is one in which:
- **No feedback signal** is taken from the output variable (dimension, temperature, force, position, etc.).
- The output **cannot self-correct** for disturbances, tool wear, thermal drift, or material variability.
- Accuracy depends **entirely on the precision of the input calibration**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PERAT528 Module 1):** Open loop systems form the conceptual baseline against which all modern adaptive and numerically controlled (NC/CNC) manufacturing systems are compared. They are the *reference architecture* for understanding why feedback was introduced in advanced manufacturing.

### 1.2 Closed Loop System — Formal KTU Definition

A **Closed Loop System** (also called a *feedback control system* or *servo system*) is a control architecture in which the **control action is continuously modulated by the difference between the desired reference (setpoint) and the measured actual output**. The measured output is routed back (the *feedback path*) to a summing junction, where it is compared with the reference to generate an *error signal* that drives the controller.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a closed loop manufacturing system:
- **Continuously measures** one or more output variables (position, velocity, force, temperature, surface finish).
- **Automatically corrects** for disturbances, wear, and parameter drift.
- Achieves **higher accuracy, repeatability, and robustness** at the cost of complexity and stability analysis.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Driving Instructor

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Intuition Builder):**
>
> - **Open Loop = Driving with Eyes Closed 🕶️** — You turn the steering wheel by a fixed amount (input), and the car turns (output) — but you have no idea whether you are still on the road. If a strong wind gusts (disturbance), you crash. No feedback = no correction.
>
> - **Closed Loop = Driving with Eyes Open 👀** — You continuously look at the road (feedback sensor), compare your position to the lane center (error signal at summing junction), and adjust the steering wheel (control action). Disturbances (wind, bumps) are automatically compensated.

In manufacturing terms:
- **Open loop drilling** = you push the drill down for a fixed time, hoping the hole depth is correct.
- **Closed loop drilling** = a depth sensor measures the actual hole depth in real time and stops the feed automatically when the target is reached (this is how modern CNC machining centers work).

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Advanced Manufacturing

| Aspect | Open Loop Relevance | Closed Loop Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost** | Low (no sensors) | High (sensors + controller) |
| **Precision** | Limited by calibration | Limited only by sensor resolution |
| **Use Case** | High-volume, low-precision parts | High-precision, low-volume, adaptive parts |
| **Examples** | Hand lathe, manual drill press, simple conveyor | CNC lathe/mill, servo press, robot arm, 3D printer |

The fundamental design question in any modern manufacturing cell is: *Does this process require feedback to meet the specification?* This decision drives the entire machine architecture.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of an Open Loop System

The signal flow in an open loop system is strictly **unidirectional** — from input to output.

1. **Input (Reference) Signal** $R(s)$ — the desired command (e.g., "spindle speed = 3000 rpm", "feed = 0.1 mm/rev").
2. **Controller / Command Generator** $G_c(s)$ — generates the actuation signal from the reference (in simple systems, this may be the operator's hand or a fixed cam profile).
3. **Actuator / Process** $G_p(s)$ — the power element that produces the physical effect (motor, hydraulic cylinder, heater coil).
4. **Output (Controlled Variable)** $C(s)$ — the resulting physical quantity (rpm, position, temperature).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In an open loop system, there is **no measurement of $C(s)$** and therefore **no path from output back to input**. The transfer function is simply the cascade product of all forward blocks.

### 2.2 Components of a Closed Loop System

A closed loop system adds **two critical new elements** to the open loop chain:

1. **Sensor (Feedback Element)** $H(s)$ — measures the actual output $C(s)$ and converts it to a comparable signal $B(s) = H(s) \cdot C(s)$.
2. **Summing Junction (Comparator)** $\otimes$ — computes the **error signal** $E(s) = R(s) \mp B(s)$ (negative sign for negative feedback, positive for positive feedback — negative is overwhelmingly used in manufacturing).

The full closed loop chain is therefore:

$$R(s) \rightarrow \otimes \rightarrow G_c(s) \rightarrow G_p(s) \rightarrow C(s)$$
$$\hspace{2cm} \uparrow \hspace{3cm} \downarrow$$
$$\hspace{1cm} B(s) \leftarrow H(s) \leftarrow C(s)$$

### 2.3 Why and How Feedback Improves Performance

- **Why feedback?** Because every real manufacturing process is subject to **disturbances** $D(s)$ (material hardness variation, cutting temperature, tool wear, supply voltage fluctuation). Without feedback, these disturbances propagate directly to the output. With feedback, the controller *sees* the disturbance effect in the output and counteracts it.
- **How?** The error $E(s)$ grows whenever a disturbance pushes $C(s)$ away from $R(s)$. The controller reacts to this growing error, driving the actuator harder until the error is minimized again.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Application in Manufacturing |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{OL}(s) = \dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = G(s)$ | Open loop transfer function | Manual machine, fixed cam |
| 2 | $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$ | Error signal (negative feedback) | Servo motor position error |
| 3 | $T_{CL}(s) = \dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | Closed loop TF, negative feedback | CNC axis position control |
| 4 | $T_{CL}^{+}(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 - G(s)H(s)}$ | Closed loop TF, positive feedback | Regenerative machine oscillation (avoid!) |
| 5 | $T_{CL}(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$ | Unity feedback ($H(s) = 1$) simplification | Most practical servos |
| 6 | $S_G^{T} = \dfrac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | Sensitivity of $T$ to variation in $G$ | Robustness against tool wear |
| 7 | $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ | **Characteristic Equation** (stability test) | Determining if a closed loop is stable |
| 8 | $\text{Steady-State Error } e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} sE(s)$ | Final tracking error | Specifying servo accuracy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Equation 7 (the **characteristic equation**) is the single most-tested formula in control-related KTU questions. If $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ has any root in the **right half of the s-plane** (positive real part), the system is **unstable** — the machine will oscillate or runaway.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility in Real Manufacturing

| Manufacturing Process | Loop Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Manual drill press | Open | Operator judges depth visually; no automation benefit |
| Automatic washing machine (timer-based) | Open | Time is the input; clothes are not sensed |
| CNC machining center (Fanuc / Siemens) | Closed | Position, velocity, spindle load all feedback |
| Injection molding machine (modern) | Closed | Cavity pressure and temperature feedback for quality |
| Servo-hydraulic press | Closed | Force/displacement closed loop for forging accuracy |
| Conventional milling (hand-fed) | Open | Skill of operator substitutes for feedback |
| 3D printer with hot-end PID | Closed | Temperature must be held within ±2 °C |
| Robotic arm in welding cell | Closed | End-effector position feedback via encoders |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Closed Loop Transfer Function

This is the **foundational derivation** that KTU examiners expect every student to reproduce from memory in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

**Given:**
- Forward path transfer function = $G(s)$
- Feedback path transfer function = $H(s)$
- Negative feedback configuration (standard in manufacturing servos)
- Reference input = $R(s)$, Output = $C(s)$, Feedback signal = $B(s)$, Error = $E(s)$

**Step 1 — Write the equation at the summing junction.**

The summing junction subtracts the feedback signal from the reference (negative feedback):

$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$$

*Logic:* The controller must know "how far off" the actual output is from the desired output. The difference is the error.

**Step 2 — Express the feedback signal in terms of the output.**

The sensor/feedback element transforms the output $C(s)$ into a comparable signal $B(s)$:

$$B(s) = H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

*Logic:* The output is in physical units (e.g., mm, °C, N). The feedback element may scale it (e.g., encoder gives pulses per mm).

**Step 3 — Express the output in terms of the error.**

The forward path acts on the error to produce the output:

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

*Logic:* The controller + actuator + process is a cascade represented by the single block $G(s)$.

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 1.**

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

*Logic:* Direct substitution of the feedback signal into the summing junction equation.

**Step 5 — Substitute Step 4 into Step 3.**

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot \big[\,R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)\,\big]$$

*Logic:* The output is the forward-path transfer function applied to the error.

**Step 6 — Expand the right-hand side.**

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s) - G(s) \cdot H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

*Logic:* Distribute $G(s)$ across both terms inside the bracket.

**Step 7 — Collect all $C(s)$ terms on the left-hand side.**

$$C(s) + G(s) \cdot H(s) \cdot C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

*Logic:* The term $-G(s)H(s)C(s)$ is moved to the left side and becomes positive (sign reversal).

**Step 8 — Factor out $C(s)$.**

$$C(s) \cdot \big[\,1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)\,\big] = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

*Logic:* Common factor $C(s)$ extracted from the left-hand side.

**Step 9 — Solve for the closed loop transfer function $T_{CL}(s) = C(s)/R(s)$.**

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)}$$

*Logic:* Divide both sides by $R(s) \cdot [1 + G(s)H(s)]$ and rearrange.

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,T_{CL}(s) = \dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}\,}$$

This is the **canonical closed loop transfer function** for negative feedback. Every servo in every CNC machine reduces to this form.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Sensitivity Function

A key advantage of closed loop is **reduced sensitivity** to parameter variations (e.g., tool wear changes the effective gain of a machining process).

**Step 1 — Start with the closed loop transfer function.**

$$T = \frac{G}{1 + GH}$$

*Logic:* Drop the explicit $(s)$ for clarity; the relationship is algebraic.

**Step 2 — Differentiate $T$ with respect to $G$, treating $H$ as constant.**

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial G} = \frac{(1+GH)(1) - G(H)}{(1+GH)^2} = \frac{1+GH - GH}{(1+GH)^2} = \frac{1}{(1+GH)^2}$$

*Logic:* Apply the quotient rule $\frac{d}{dG}\!\left(\frac{u}{v}\right) = \frac{v\,du - u\,dv}{v^2}$, with $u = G$, $du/dG = 1$, $v = 1+GH$, $dv/dG = H$.

**Step 3 — Form the dimensionless sensitivity ratio.**

$$S_G^{T} = \frac{\partial T}{\partial G} \cdot \frac{G}{T}$$

*Logic:* Sensitivity is defined as the percentage change in $T$ per percentage change in $G$ — hence the $G/T$ normalization.

**Step 4 — Substitute $\partial T/\partial G$ and $T$.**

$$S_G^{T} = \frac{1}{(1+GH)^2} \cdot \frac{G}{\frac{G}{1+GH}}$$

*Logic:* Direct substitution from Steps 1 and 2.

**Step 5 — Simplify.**

$$S_G^{T} = \frac{1}{(1+GH)^2} \cdot \frac{G(1+GH)}{G} = \frac{1}{1+GH}$$

*Logic:* $G$ cancels; $(1+GH)^2$ in the denominator reduces by one factor.

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,S_G^{T} = \dfrac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}\,}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Since $|G(s)H(s)| \gg 1$ at the operating frequency of most servos, $|S_G^{T}| \ll 1$. This means a **10% change in process gain $G$** (e.g., due to tool wear in a turning operation) produces only a **0.1% change in closed loop behavior** — a 100× robustness improvement. This is *why CNC machines can hold micron-level tolerances even as the tool wears*.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Open vs Closed Loop Trade-Off

| Evaluation Criterion | Open Loop Behavior | Closed Loop Behavior | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | Depends on input calibration alone | Self-correcting; depends on sensor accuracy | Closed wins |
| **Disturbance Rejection** | None — disturbances appear at output | Active — controlled by $1/(1+GH)$ | Closed wins overwhelmingly |
| **Sensitivity to Parameter Variation** | $S_G^{T_{OL}} = 1$ (full sensitivity) | $S_G^{T_{CL}} = 1/(1+GH)$ (reduced) | Closed wins |
| **Bandwidth** | Wide (no feedback dynamics) | Limited by feedback stability margin | Open wins |
| **Cost** | Low (no sensors) | High (sensors, controller, wiring) | Open wins |
| **Stability** | Inherently stable (no feedback) | Must be analyzed via characteristic equation | Open wins |
| **Complexity** | Simple | Requires tuning (PID, lead-lag) | Open wins |
| **Bandwidth-Accuracy Trade-off** | Poor | Excellent (high gain at low frequency) | Closed wins |
| **Suitability for Adaptive Control** | None — no feedback to adapt on | Native platform for adaptive algorithms | Closed wins |
| **Typical KTU Board Example** | Hand-fed drill press | CNC machining center with encoder | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Open Loop System

The signal flow is **strictly unidirectional**. No path returns from output to input.

```mermaid
graph LR
    inpA["INPUT REFERENCE R S"] --> ctlA["CONTROLLER Gc S"]
    ctlA --> actA["ACTUATOR PROCESS Gp S"]
    actA --> outA["OUTPUT C S"]
    dstA["DISTURBANCE D S"] -.-> actA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The dashed arrow from `DISTURBANCE D S` represents a *parasitic influence* on the process (e.g., material hardness variation affecting a milling operation). In an open loop system, this disturbance has **no mechanism** to be sensed and corrected. The output $C(s)$ deviates silently from the desired value.

**Equivalent compact block diagram (combining controller + actuator + process into one block $G(s)$):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    refA["R S"] --> blkA["G S FORWARD PATH"]
    blkA --> outB["C S OUTPUT"]
```

**Cascaded representation (with explicit controller and process):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    refB["R S"] --> ctlB["Gc S CONTROLLER"]
    ctlB --> prcB["Gp S PROCESS"]
    prcB --> outC["C S"]
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Closed Loop System (Negative Feedback)

The signal flow now includes a **return path** from output to input through the feedback element $H(s)$. The summing junction (denoted $\otimes$) computes the error.

```mermaid
graph LR
    refC["R S REFERENCE"] --> sumC["SUMMING JUNCTION PLUS MINUS"]
    sumC --> ctlC["CONTROLLER Gc S"]
    ctlC --> prcC["PROCESS Gp S"]
    prcC --> outD["C S OUTPUT"]
    outD --> senC["SENSOR H S"]
    senC --> sumC
    dstC["DISTURBANCE D S"] -.-> prcC
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reading Note:** The arrow from `SENSOR H S` returns to the **negative** input of `SUMMING JUNCTION`. This is the convention for **negative feedback**, which is used in essentially all stable manufacturing servos. The plus/minus symbols at the summing junction are essential — KTU examiners will deduct marks if they are omitted or reversed.

**Simplified canonical closed loop block (combining $G_c \cdot G_p = G$):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    refD["R S"] --> sumD["SUM PLUS MINUS"]
    sumD --> fwdD["G S FORWARD PATH"]
    fwdD --> outE["C S"]
    outE --> fbkD["H S FEEDBACK"]
    fbkD --> sumD
```

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Open vs Closed Loop in a CNC Machine

This diagram maps the abstract control blocks to **physical subsystems of a CNC machining center**, showing where each block resides in the real machine.

```mermaid
graph TB
    cncA["G CODE PROGRAM"] --> cncB["CNC CONTROLLER SIEMENS FANUC"]
    cncB --> cncC["SERVO DRIVE AMPLIFIER"]
    cncC --> cncD["SERVO MOTOR SPINDLE"]
    cncD --> cncE["MACHINE TABLE TOOL"]
    cncE --> cncF["WORKPIECE OUTPUT POSITION"]
    cncF --> cncG["LINEAR ENCODER FEEDBACK"]
    cncG --> cncH["POSITION COUNTER"]
    cncH --> cncB
    cncI["DISTURBANCE CUTTING FORCE THERMAL DRIFT"] -.-> cncE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Functional Mapping to Control Blocks:**
> - `G CODE PROGRAM` = $R(s)$ (Reference input — what the programmer wants)
> - `CNC CONTROLLER` = $G_c(s)$ (Controller)
> - `SERVO DRIVE + MOTOR + TABLE` = $G_p(s)$ (Process/Actuator)
> - `WORKPIECE` = $C(s)$ (Output — the actual machined position)
> - `LINEAR ENCODER` = $H(s)$ (Sensor/Feedback element)
> - `POSITION COUNTER` = implicit summing junction
> - `CUTTING FORCE / THERMAL DRIFT` = $D(s)$ (Disturbance)

In a **manual milling machine**, the loop is **broken at the controller** — the human operator's eyes and hands act as a slow, imprecise, but still functional feedback path. In a **CNC machine**, the loop is **fully electronic** — closing it at kHz bandwidth, achieving micron accuracy.

---

### 4.4 Stability Concept Diagram — Effect of Gain on Closed Loop Poles

```mermaid
graph TB
    staA["LOW GAIN G SMALL"] --> staB["POLES IN LEFT HALF PLANE STABLE SLOW"]
    staC["MODERATE GAIN G MEDIUM"] --> staD["POLES SHIFT LEFT OR RIGHT DEPENDS"]
    staE["HIGH GAIN G LARGE"] --> staF["POLES CROSS INTO RIGHT HALF PLANE UNSTABLE OSCILLATION"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters in Manufacturing:** In a CNC machine, increasing the **servo loop gain** makes the axis respond faster and reject disturbances better — **but only up to a point**. Beyond the critical gain (where characteristic equation roots cross into the right half s-plane), the axis will **oscillate or chatter** during cutting, ruining surface finish. The art of control engineering is choosing a gain that is *high enough for performance* but *low enough for stability*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PERAT528]**

**Differentiate between open loop and closed loop control systems. Give two manufacturing examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 for definition each + 1 for examples):**

| Aspect | Open Loop | Closed Loop |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A control system where the output has no influence on the control action; no feedback path exists. | A control system where the output is continuously measured and fed back to a comparator to generate an error signal that drives the controller. |
| **Manufacturing Examples** | (1) Manual drill press with hand-fed depth stop. (2) Conventional lathe with hand-wheel cross-feed. | (1) CNC machining center with linear encoder feedback. (2) Servo-hydraulic forging press with load cell feedback. |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PERAT528]**

**What is feedback? Explain the role of negative feedback in improving the accuracy of a manufacturing process.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition of Feedback (1 mark):** Feedback is the process of returning a portion of the system output to the input side, where it is compared with the reference input to generate an error signal.

**Role of Negative Feedback (2 marks):**
1. **Error Reduction:** The error $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$ continuously drives the controller to minimize the difference between desired and actual output. In steady state, $e_{ss}$ approaches zero for high loop gain.
2. **Disturbance Rejection:** External disturbances (cutting force variation, thermal expansion) that affect the output are sensed by the feedback element and automatically counteracted by the controller. The disturbance transfer function is reduced by a factor of $1/(1+GH)$.
3. **Sensitivity Reduction:** Variations in process parameters (e.g., tool wear reducing cutting efficiency) produce only $1/(1+GH)$ of the effect they would have in open loop. This is critical for holding tight tolerances in production.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PERAT528]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, derive the closed loop transfer function of a negative feedback control system. Define the **characteristic equation** and explain its significance in determining system stability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CNC feed drive has a forward path transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$ and a unity feedback sensor $H(s) = 1$. Determine:
- (i) The closed loop transfer function.
- (ii) The characteristic equation and its roots.
- (iii) Comment on the stability of the system. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

Refer to the simplified canonical closed loop block diagram from Section 4.2 of these notes. The components are: Reference $R(s)$ → Summing Junction ($\otimes$, with negative feedback sign) → Forward Path $G(s)$ → Output $C(s)$ → Feedback Path $H(s)$ → back to Summing Junction.

**Derivation (3 marks):** The full step-by-step derivation is given in Section 3.1 of these notes. The final result is:

$$T_{CL}(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

**Characteristic Equation & Stability (1 mark):**

The **characteristic equation** is the denominator of the closed loop transfer function set equal to zero:

$$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$$

The roots of this equation are the **closed loop poles**. The system is:
- **Stable** if all roots have negative real parts (left half of s-plane).
- **Unstable** if any root has a positive real part (right half of s-plane).
- **Marginally stable** if roots lie on the imaginary axis (pure oscillation).

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Closed Loop Transfer Function (3 marks):**

$$T_{CL}(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)} = \frac{\frac{10}{s(s+2)}}{1 + \frac{10}{s(s+2)} \cdot 1}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $s(s+2)$:

$$T_{CL}(s) = \frac{10}{s(s+2) + 10} = \frac{10}{s^2 + 2s + 10}$$

**(ii) Characteristic Equation and Roots (3 marks):**

Setting denominator to zero:

$$s^2 + 2s + 10 = 0$$

Applying the quadratic formula $s = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$:

$$s = \frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{4 - 40}}{2} = \frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{-36}}{2} = \frac{-2 \pm 6j}{2} = -1 \pm 3j$$

**(iii) Stability Comment (1 mark):**

The roots are $s_1 = -1 + 3j$ and $s_2 = -1 - 3j$. Both roots have **negative real parts** ($-1$). Therefore, the closed loop system is **STABLE**. The negative real part means any transient oscillation will decay exponentially. The imaginary part ($\pm 3j$) indicates the system will oscillate at $\omega_n = 3$ rad/s before settling — a damped oscillatory response typical of a well-tuned CNC servo.

---

**Question B. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PERAT528] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain with examples why most modern advanced manufacturing systems (CNC machines, robots, 3D printers) use closed loop control rather than open loop control. Discuss at least **four specific advantages** of closed loop control in the manufacturing context. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An automatic packaging machine uses a temperature controller with the following transfer functions:
- Forward path (heater + sensor dynamics): $G(s) = \dfrac{5}{s+3}$
- Feedback path: $H(s) = 1$ (unity feedback)
- A disturbance $D(s) = \dfrac{2}{s}$ enters the process input.

Calculate:
- (i) The closed loop transfer function $C(s)/R(s)$.
- (ii) The transfer function from disturbance to output $C(s)/D(s)$.
- (iii) The steady-state error for a unit step reference input $R(s) = 1/s$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Four advantages of closed loop control in modern manufacturing (1.5 marks each, plus 1 mark for introduction):

1. **Improved Accuracy and Repeatability:** Closed loop systems use real-time measurement (encoders, LVDTs, thermocouples) to continuously correct deviations. A CNC machining center with linear encoder feedback can hold positional accuracy of $\pm 2$ µm, whereas an open loop stepper-motor system is limited to $\pm 20$ µm or worse due to missed steps and backlash.

2. **Disturbance Rejection:** In a turning operation, cutting force varies with material hardness, chip thickness, and tool wear. In a closed loop servo, the spindle load sensor detects the force increase and the controller automatically reduces feed rate, preventing tool breakage and maintaining surface finish. An open loop system cannot react to such variations.

3. **Reduced Sensitivity to Parameter Variation (Tool Wear Compensation):** As a cutting tool wears, its effective geometry changes, altering the process gain $G(s)$. In closed loop, the sensitivity is reduced by a factor of $1/(1+GH)$. A 30% change in $G$ produces only a 0.3% change in $T_{CL}$ when $GH = 100$ — meaning a worn tool can still produce in-tolerance parts.

4. **Improved Dynamic Response and Bandwidth Control:** Closed loop allows the designer to shape the response (rise time, overshoot, settling time) using PID tuning. In a 3D printer hot-end, a PID temperature controller maintains $\pm 2$ °C around 210 °C despite the highly nonlinear cooling of the melt pool — impossible in open loop.

5. **(Bonus) Automation and Unmanned Operation:** Closed loop is the **enabling technology** for lights-out manufacturing. Without feedback, no autonomous correction is possible.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Closed Loop Transfer Function (2 marks):**

$$T_{CL}(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)} = \frac{\frac{5}{s+3}}{1 + \frac{5}{s+3}} = \frac{5}{s+3+5} = \frac{5}{s+8}$$

**(ii) Disturbance Transfer Function (3 marks):**

When a disturbance $D(s)$ enters the process input (after the controller, before the process), the standard result is:

$$\frac{C(s)}{D(s)}\bigg|_{R=0} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)} = \frac{5}{s+8}$$

*Derivation:* With $R(s) = 0$, the error is $E(s) = -B(s) = -H(s)C(s)$. The output becomes $C(s) = G(s)[D(s) - H(s)C(s)]$. Solving:

$$C(s)[1+G(s)H(s)] = G(s)D(s) \Rightarrow \frac{C(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1+G(s)H(s)} = \frac{5}{s+8}$$

**(iii) Steady-State Error for Unit Step Input (2 marks):**

For unity feedback, the error is $E(s) = R(s) - C(s)$. Using the Final Value Theorem:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot R(s) \cdot \big[1 - T_{CL}(s)\big]$$

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{1}{s} \cdot \left[1 - \frac{5}{s+8}\right] = 1 \cdot \left[1 - \frac{5}{8}\right] = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375$$

The steady-state error is **0.375 °C** (assuming the reference is a 1 °C step setpoint). This is acceptable for most packaging thermal processes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Missing the feedback sign:** Students frequently write $T_{CL} = G/(1-GH)$ (positive feedback form) when negative feedback was specified. **Always check the summing junction sign in the diagram before substituting.**
>
> 2. **Algebraic slip in $T_{CL}$ derivation:** When the denominator is multiplied out, students often drop the $+10$ constant term or mis-expand $s(s+2)$. Re-derive the characteristic equation every time — never assume.
>
> 3. **Confusing open loop and closed loop poles:** The poles of $G(s)$ alone (e.g., $s = 0, -2$ in Part A-b) are **not** the closed loop poles. You **must** compute $1+G(s)H(s) = 0$ separately. This is one of the most common reasons students incorrectly declare a stable system unstable.
>
> 4. **Forgetting units in the final answer:** Steady-state error inherits the units of the reference input. Always state the units (°C, mm, N).
>
> 5. **Stability test misapplication:** A system is stable **only** if **all** closed loop poles lie in the left half plane. One right-half-plane root makes the entire system unstable — the others don't matter.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This the Night Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **Open Loop:** No feedback. Output not measured. Simple, cheap, inaccurate. Example: hand drill.
- [ ] **Closed Loop:** Feedback present. Output measured, compared with reference, error drives controller. Example: CNC mill.
- [ ] **Canonical Closed Loop TF:** $T_{CL}(s) = G(s)/[1 + G(s)H(s)]$ — **negative feedback assumed**.
- [ ] **Canonical Open Loop TF:** $T_{OL}(s) = G(s)$ — direct cascade.
- [ ] **Error Signal:** $E(s) = R(s) - B(s) = R(s) - H(s)C(s)$.
- [ ] **Characteristic Equation:** $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$. Roots determine stability.
- [ ] **Stability Rule:** All roots in LHP → stable. Any root in RHP → unstable.
- [ ] **Sensitivity Formula:** $S_G^{T} = 1/[1 + G(s)H(s)]$ — closed loop is less sensitive to $G$ variations.
- [ ] **Disturbance Rejection:** Closed loop reduces disturbance effect by factor $1/(1+GH)$.
- [ ] **Steady-State Error:** $e_{ss} = \lim_{s\to 0} s \cdot E(s)$ (Final Value Theorem).
- [ ] **Negative vs Positive Feedback:** Negative is standard for stability; positive is unstable for most manufacturing systems.
- [ ] **Open loop in manufacturing:** Manual machines, timed operations, simple conveyors, hand-fed drills.
- [ ] **Closed loop in manufacturing:** CNC machines, servo presses, robotic welders, 3D printer hot-ends, adaptive machining, closed-loop quality control.
- [ ] **Block diagram symbols to memorize:** $\otimes$ = summing junction; arrow = signal flow; block with $G(s)$ = transfer function; dashed arrow = disturbance or parasitic path.
- [ ] **One-liner exam answer:** *"Closed loop control is preferred in advanced manufacturing because feedback enables self-correction, disturbance rejection, and reduced sensitivity to tool wear, yielding higher accuracy and repeatability at the cost of complexity and stability analysis."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feedback Devices, Manual Part Programming, Computer Aided Part Programming - APT, Programming Exercises in Turning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT528 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Feedback Devices, Manual Part Programming, Computer Aided Part Programming - APT, Programming Exercises in Turning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Feedback Devices in CNC Machines

### Formal Definition
**Feedback devices** (also called **position transducers** or **sensors**) are electro-mechanical components in a CNC machine tool's **servo loop** that continuously measure the actual position, velocity, or displacement of a moving axis (slide, spindle, or tool) and feed this information back to the **CNC controller (MCU)** for comparison with the commanded position. This closed-loop mechanism eliminates positioning errors and ensures dimensional accuracy of the machined part.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A CNC machine without feedback devices is an **Open-Loop** system (uses stepper motors). With feedback devices, it becomes a **Closed-Loop** (servo-controlled) system. The two main classes of feedback are:
> 1. **Direct/Position Measurement** – Linear/Rotary Encoders, Inductosyn, Laser Interferometer.
> 2. **Indirect Measurement** – Resolvers, Rotary Encoders on motor shaft.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine you are driving a car with your eyes closed (open-loop) versus driving with your eyes open (closed-loop). The **steering wheel input** is the commanded position from the controller, while your **eyes (cameras)** are the feedback devices continuously reporting the car's actual position on the road. The brain (CNC controller) compares the two and corrects the steering. Without feedback, you'd crash. In CNC, without feedback, parts come out wrong due to **lead screw errors, backlash, thermal drift, and load deflections**.

### Classification of Feedback Devices

| Class | Device | Measured Quantity | Resolution / Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Optical** | Linear/Rotary Encoder | Position | **0.5 µm – 5 µm** |
| **Magnetic** | Resolver | Angular position | ±5 arc-min |
| **Inductive** | LVDT, Inductosyn | Linear position | 1 – 10 µm |
| **Capacitive** | Capacitance Transducer | Displacement | Sub-micron |
| **Laser-based** | Laser Interferometer | Linear position | **0.1 µm** (highest) |

---

## 1.2 Manual Part Programming (MPP)

### Formal Definition
**Manual Part Programming** is the technique of writing a CNC part program in **standardized NC code (G-code and M-code)** by the human programmer, using a **word-address format**, to instruct a CNC machine tool to perform a specific sequence of operations (turning, milling, drilling, etc.) on a workpiece. It is also called **hand-coding** or **G-code programming**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Word-Address Format:** Each instruction is a "word" made of an **address letter** (e.g., `N`, `G`, `X`, `Y`, `Z`, `F`, `S`, `T`, `M`) and a numerical value. For example: `N50 G01 X25.0 F0.2`

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of writing a manual part program like writing a **recipe** for a robot chef. Each line is a single instruction: "Go to position X, lower the knife, cut at speed F, retract, turn off the burner." The chef (machine) reads line-by-line and executes exactly. The programmer (chef author) must think in precise machine motions.

### Basic Program Structure (Turning Example)
```text
O0001 (PROGRAM NUMBER)
N10 G21 G97 G40 (METRIC, SPINDLE SPEED, TOOL NOSE COMP CANCEL)
N20 G28 U0 W0 (SAFE HOME POSITION)
N30 T0101 (TOOL CHANGE)
...
```

---

## 1.3 Computer-Aided Part Programming (CAPP) – APT Language

### Formal Definition
**APT (Automatically Programmed Tools)** is a high-level, English-like symbolic **part-programming language** developed at **MIT in 1959** by Douglas T. Ross, used to write complex CNC programs for multi-axis machining (3-, 4-, 5-axis). Unlike G-code, APT allows the programmer to define **part geometry using geometric primitives** (POINT, LINE, CIRCLE, PLANE) and then specify **tool motions** relative to that geometry. A **post-processor** converts the universal APT output (CLFILE – cutter location file) into machine-specific G-code.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** APT statements are grouped into four categories:
> 1. **Geometry Statements** – Define part surfaces (POINT, LINE, CIRCLE, PLANE).
> 2. **Motion Statements** – Command tool movement (GOTO, GODLTA, GO, GOLFT, GORGT).
> 3. **Post-Processor Statements** – Specify spindle, feed, coolant (SPINDL, FEDRAT, COOLNT).
> 4. **Auxiliary Statements** – Part identification, tool definition, machine limits.

### Conceptual Analogy
APT is to manual G-code what **Python** is to **machine code**. In G-code, you say: "Move to X=25, Y=30." In APT, you say: "Move the tool *until it touches the LINE* that goes *from POINT1 to POINT2*." You describe **what to do geometrically**, not coordinate-by-coordinate.

---

## 1.4 Programming Exercises in Turning

### Formal Definition
**Turning programming exercises** involve writing CNC turning programs (using either G-code or APT) for typical lathe operations: **straight (step) turning, taper turning, facing, drilling, threading (single-point), grooving/parting, and contour turning**, with the workpiece rotating and the single-point cutting tool traversing in the X-Z plane.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a pencil sharpener. The pencil (workpiece) rotates. The blade (tool) moves radially inward (X-axis) and along the length (Z-axis) to cut. Turning programming is just this—written as precise numeric instructions instead of hand movements.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Feedback Devices – Operating Principles

### 2.1.1 Optical Encoders (Most Common in Modern CNC)

**Rotary Encoder (on spindle/lead screw):**
- A disc with **alternating transparent and opaque radial lines** rotates between a **light source (LED)** and a **photodetector**.
- As the disc rotates, light pulses are generated. A counter decodes these pulses into angular position.
- **Two main types:**
  - **Incremental Encoder:** Outputs pulses; position is relative. Needs a homing/index pulse.
  - **Absolute Encoder:** Has unique Gray-code track for each position. Knows position even after power-off.

**Resolution (N) of an Incremental Encoder:**
$$N = \frac{360°}{p}$$
where $p$ = number of pulses per revolution (PPR).

> **Linear Equivalent:** If encoder is mounted on a lead screw of pitch $L$, linear resolution becomes:
> $$R_{linear} = \frac{L}{p \times \text{electronic multiplication factor}}$$

### 2.1.2 Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)
- A transformer with **one primary coil** and **two identical secondary coils** wound on a cylindrical former.
- A movable **ferromagnetic core** slides inside. The displacement of the core produces a **differential output voltage** proportional to displacement.
- **Key Features:** Infinite resolution, frictionless, robust, used for **axis position feedback on precision lathes/grinders**.

**Output Voltage Equation (LVDT):**
$$V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x \cdot V_{in}$$
where $K_{LVDT}$ = sensitivity (V/V/mm), $x$ = core displacement, $V_{in}$ = excitation voltage.

### 2.1.3 Resolver
- A **rotary transformer** with rotor and stator windings.
- Excited by AC; output voltages are **sine and cosine functions of shaft angle**.
- The controller decodes: $\theta = \arctan(V_{\sin}/V_{\cos})$.
- **Used in:** Heavy-duty machine tools, military/automotive (rugged environments).

### 2.1.4 Inductosyn
- A **precision linear/rotary transducer** with two magnetically-coupled scale elements (slider and scale), like a "flattened resolver."
- Typical accuracy: **±0.0001 inch (±2.5 µm) over 12 inches.**
- Used on **high-precision machine tools and CMMs**.

### 2.1.5 Servo Loop Block Diagram (Why Feedback is Critical)

The block diagram of a **closed-loop position control** in CNC:

$$\text{Command} \rightarrow \text{[Summer (+/-)]} \rightarrow \text{Amplifier} \rightarrow \text{Servo Motor} \rightarrow \text{Ball Screw} \rightarrow \text{Slide}$$

The slide position is measured by the feedback device and returned to the summer:

$$\text{Summer Output} = \text{Command} - \text{Actual Position} = \text{Error (e)}$$

The controller drives the motor **until error e = 0** (perfect tracking).

> [!NOTE]
> **Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop:**
> - **Open-loop:** No feedback. Stepper motor advances assuming each pulse = fixed step. Errors (lost steps, backlash) are uncorrected.
> - **Closed-loop:** Feedback corrects errors continuously. Required for high-accuracy parts.

---

## 2.2 Manual Part Programming – G-Code Architecture

### 2.2.1 Standard Program Format (Word-Address)
Each program line (block) is composed of **words** in this typical order:

$$\text{Block Format: } \underbrace{N}_{\text{Seq. No.}} \, \underbrace{G}_{\text{Prep. Func.}} \, \underbrace{X\,Y\,Z}_{\text{Coordinates}} \, \underbrace{F}_{\text{Feed}} \, \underbrace{S}_{\text{Spindle}} \, \underbrace{T}_{\text{Tool}} \, \underbrace{M}_{\text{Misc.}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modal vs. Non-Modal Codes:**
> - **Modal codes** (G00, G01, G02, G90, etc.) remain active until changed or cancelled.
> - **Non-modal codes** (G04 – dwell, G28 – home) act only on the block they appear in.

### 2.2.2 Key G-Codes for Turning (ISO/EIA Standard)

| Code | Function | Group |
|---|---|---|
| **G00** | Rapid traverse (positioning) | 01 |
| **G01** | Linear interpolation (cutting feed) | 01 |
| **G02** | Circular CW interpolation | 01 |
| **G03** | Circular CCW interpolation | 01 |
| **G04** | Dwell (P in seconds/ms) | 00 (non-modal) |
| **G20/G21** | Inch / Metric input | 06 |
| **G28** | Return to reference/home position | 00 |
| **G40/G41/G42** | Tool nose radius compensation (cancel/left/right) | 07 |
| **G70** | Finishing cycle | 00 |
| **G71** | Stock removal in turning (roughing) | 00 |
| **G72** | Facing roughing cycle | 00 |
| **G73** | Pattern repeating cycle | 00 |
| **G76** | Threading cycle (multi-pass) | 00 |
| **G90** | Absolute programming | 03 |
| **G91** | Incremental programming | 03 |
| **G92** | Spindle speed clamp / thread cycle | 00 |
| **G96** | Constant surface speed (CSS) | 02 |
| **G97** | Constant spindle speed (RPM) | 02 |

### 2.2.3 Key M-Codes for Turning

| Code | Function |
|---|---|
| **M00** | Program stop |
| **M01** | Optional stop |
| **M03/M04/M05** | Spindle CW / CCW / Stop |
| **M08/M09** | Coolant ON / OFF |
| **M30** | Program end and rewind |
| **M41/M42** | Spindle gear range low/high |

### 2.2.4 Coordinate System – Turning
- **Z-axis:** Parallel to spindle axis (along the workpiece length).
- **X-axis:** Radial, perpendicular to spindle. **Diameter programming** is standard (X = diameter, not radius).
- **X0, Z0:** Usually the **intersection of the spindle centerline and the chuck face** (or workpiece face).

---

## 2.3 APT Language – Deep Theory

### 2.3.1 Statement Categories and Syntax

| Category | Example Statements | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Geometry** | `POINT`, `LINE`, `CIRCLE`, `PLANE`, `TABDAT` | Define part geometry symbolically |
| **Motion** | `GOTO`, `GODLTA`, `GO/TO`, `GORGT`, `GOLFT`, `GOFWD`, `GOBACK` | Control tool path |
| **Post-Processor** | `SPINDL`, `FEDRAT`, `COOLNT`, `TURRET` | Machine operating parameters |
| **Auxiliary** | `PARTNO`, `MACHIN`, `CLPRNT`, `INTOL/OUTTOL`, `NOPOST` | Identification & control |

### 2.3.2 Common Geometry Statement Syntax

```text
P1 = POINT/10.0, 5.0, 0.0         (Defines point P1 at X=10, Y=5, Z=0)
L1 = LINE/P1, P2                   (Line L1 passes through P1 and P2)
C1 = CIRCLE/CENTER, P5, RADIUS, 8.0  (Circle C1 centered at P5, radius 8)
C2 = CIRCLE/P1, P2, P3             (Circle through three points)
PL1 = PLANE/P1, P2, P3             (Plane through three points)
```

### 2.3.3 Motion Control Statements (The Heart of APT)

| Statement | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `GOTO/P5` | Tool tip moves (in straight line) to POINT P5 |
| `GODLTA/2.0, 0.0, 0.0` | Move incrementally by ΔX=2, ΔY=0, ΔZ=0 |
| `GO/TO, L1, TO, L2, ON, L3` | Move to intersection of L1 and L2, on the side of L3 |
| `GORGT/L1` | Move past LINE L1 to the RIGHT of tool motion |
| `GOLFT/L1` | Move past LINE L1 to the LEFT of tool motion |
| `GOFWD/C1, PAST, L1` | Move around CIRCLE C1, past LINE L1 |
| `GOBACK/C1, PAST, L1` | Move around C1 on the back side, past L1 |
| `TLLFT, TLRGT, TLOFF` | Tool tip offset left/right/off relative to path |

### 2.3.4 APT Program Structure (Example Skeleton)
```text
PARTNO/TURNING EXERCISE
MACHIN/MILL, 3000
CLPRNT
NOPOST
$$ PART GEOMETRY DEFINITION
P1 = POINT/0, 0, 0
P2 = POINT/40, 0, 0
P3 = POINT/40, 10, 0
...
L1 = LINE/P1, P2
C1 = CIRCLE/CENTER, P4, RADIUS, 5
$$ INITIAL POSITION
FROM/P0
$$ MACHINE COMMANDS
SPINDL/1500, CW
FEDRAT/0.2, IPM
COOLNT/ON
$$ CUTTING SEQUENCE
GO/TO, L1, TO, L2, ON, L3
GOFWD/C1, PAST, L4
...
FINI
```

---

## 2.4 Turning Programming – Calculations

### 2.4.1 Spindle Speed (N) for Turning
$$N = \frac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D}$$
where $V$ = cutting speed (m/min), $D$ = workpiece diameter (mm), $N$ = spindle speed (rpm).

### 2.4.2 Material Removal Rate (MRR)
$$MRR = V_c \times f \times d \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/min)}$$
where $V_c$ = cutting velocity, $f$ = feed (mm/rev), $d$ = depth of cut (mm).

### 2.4.3 Taper Turning – Coordinate Calculation
For a taper from diameter $D_1$ at Z1 to diameter $D_2$ at Z2 (length $L$):
$$\text{Taper} = \frac{D_1 - D_2}{L} \text{ (mm/mm or mm/dia per mm length)}$$

### 2.4.4 Thread Cutting – Pitch & Lead
$$\text{Lead} = n \times p \quad \text{(for multi-start threads)}$$
For a single-start thread (most common), **Lead = Pitch ($p$)**.
Spindle speed for threading:
$$N_{thread} = \frac{1000 \times V_{thread}}{\pi \times D_{mean}}$$

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ | Spindle speed (turning) | rpm |
| 2 | $f_r = N \times f$ | Feed rate in mm/min | mm/min |
| 3 | $MRR = V_c \cdot f \cdot d$ | Material removal rate | mm³/min |
| 4 | $T = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot L}{f \cdot 1000}$ | Machining time for turning length L | min |
| 5 | $R = \frac{L}{p}$ | Encoder linear resolution (L = pitch, p = pulses) | mm/pulse |
| 6 | $V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x \cdot V_{in}$ | LVDT output | V |
| 7 | $N_{thread} = \frac{1000 \cdot V_t}{\pi \cdot D_{mean}}$ | Threading spindle speed | rpm |
| 8 | Taper $= \frac{D_1 - D_2}{L}$ | Taper ratio | mm/mm |
| 9 | $N = \frac{360°}{p}$ | Encoder angular resolution | °/pulse |
| 10 | $e(t) = X_{cmd}(t) - X_{actual}(t)$ | Servo loop error | mm or deg |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Feedback devices are the **backbone of modern manufacturing automation** (Industry 4.0). Encoders with sub-micron resolution enable machining of jet engine blades, orthopedic implants, and watch components. APT-style part programming, although now mostly replaced by **CAM software (Mastercam, CATIA, NX)**, is still the *theoretical foundation* of all CAM toolpaths.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Incremental Encoder Resolution

A CNC milling machine has a **ball screw** with a pitch of **10 mm/rev**. An incremental rotary encoder is mounted on the screw shaft producing **2500 pulses per revolution (PPR)**. The controller uses **4× electronic multiplication (quadrature decoding)**.

**Find:** The linear resolution of the axis (smallest measurable movement).

**Step 1: Identify the parameters.**
- Lead screw pitch $L = 10$ mm/rev
- Encoder pulses $p = 2500$ PPR
- Electronic multiplication factor $M = 4$ (quadrature)

**Step 2: Effective pulses per revolution after multiplication.**
$$p_{eff} = p \times M = 2500 \times 4 = 10000 \text{ counts/rev}$$

**Step 3: Linear resolution formula.**
Each count of the encoder corresponds to a linear movement of:
$$R_{linear} = \frac{L}{p_{eff}}$$

**Step 4: Substitute the values.**
$$R_{linear} = \frac{10 \text{ mm}}{10000 \text{ counts}}$$

**Step 5: Simplify.**
$$R_{linear} = 0.001 \text{ mm/count} = 1 \text{ µm/count}$$

**Interpretation:** The CNC controller can detect a slide movement as small as **1 micrometer** – sufficient for high-precision parts (e.g., moulds, optical components).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Taper Turning Coordinate Calculation

A workpiece of initial diameter **60 mm** must be turned to a final diameter of **40 mm** over a length of **50 mm**, starting from $Z = 0$ (face of job).

**Find:** The X and Z coordinates for the end of the taper (for manual G-code entry).

**Step 1: Identify endpoints.**
- **Start point (P1):** $X_1 = 60.0$ mm, $Z_1 = 0.0$ mm (face of workpiece).
- **End point (P2):** $X_2 = 40.0$ mm, $Z_2 = 50.0$ mm.

**Step 2: Verify the taper calculation.**
$$\text{Taper} = \frac{D_1 - D_2}{L} = \frac{60 - 40}{50} = \frac{20}{50} = 0.4 \text{ mm/mm (diameter)}$$

This means the diameter reduces by **0.4 mm for every 1 mm of axial travel.**

**Step 3: G-code line for taper (using absolute mode G01).**
```text
N40 G01 X40.0 Z-50.0 F0.2
```
(Note: Z is negative because tool travels in the –Z direction from the face into the part, depending on the machine's coordinate convention.)

**Step 4: Verify with incremental G91.**
$$\Delta X = X_2 - X_1 = 40 - 60 = -20 \text{ mm}$$
$$\Delta Z = Z_2 - Z_1 = 0 - 50 = -50 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Incremental G-code line.**
```text
N40 G91 G01 X-20.0 Z-50.0 F0.2
```

---

## 3.3 Derivation: APT Cutter Path for a 2D Contour

**Problem:** A rectangular pocket has corners at:
- P1 = (10, 10), P2 = (60, 10), P3 = (60, 40), P4 = (10, 40).

A **10 mm diameter end mill** is to traverse **outside the pocket, clockwise**, with the cutter center offset by 5 mm (tool radius).

**Step 1: Define the corner points in APT.**
```text
P1 = POINT/10, 10, 0
P2 = POINT/60, 10, 0
P3 = POINT/60, 40, 0
P4 = POINT/10, 40, 0
```

**Step 2: Define the four lines forming the pocket.**
```text
L1 = LINE/P1, P2
L2 = LINE/P2, P3
L3 = LINE/P3, P4
L4 = LINE/P4, P1
```

**Step 3: Define the parallel offset lines** (since the cutter must be offset by tool radius 5 mm outward).
```text
L1A = LINE/P1, P2, LEFT, 5
L2A = LINE/P2, P3, LEFT, 5
L3A = LINE/P3, P4, LEFT, 5
L4A = LINE/P4, P1, LEFT, 5
```

**Step 4: Initial positioning and motion commands.**
```text
FROM/-20, -20, 0
GO/TO, L1A, TO, L4A
TLLFT
GORGT/L1A          (Move past L1A to the right of motion)
GORGT/L2A
GORGT/L3A
GORGT/L4A
GOTO/-20, -20, 0
```

**Step 5: Explanation of logic.**
- The cutter starts at (-20, -20) – a safe point.
- `TLLFT` keeps the tool tip **offset to the left** of the programmed path (compensating for cutter radius).
- `GORGT/L1A` etc. moves past each corner, ensuring the cutter clears all pocket corners.
- The result is a clean rectangular pocket of dimensions 50 × 30 mm.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Choosing the Right Feedback Device

| Application Environment | Best Device | Why? | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|---|
| Precision lathe X-axis | Linear optical encoder | Direct measurement, no backlash error | 0.5 µm |
| Spindle speed/position | Rotary encoder (incremental) | Compact, low cost, accurate | 12 – 20 bit |
| Heavy-duty milling (dirty environment) | Resolver | Rugged, no optics, oil-resistant | ±5 arc-min |
| Ultra-precision die/mould (CIM) | Laser interferometer | Highest accuracy, NIST-traceable | 0.1 µm |
| Cost-sensitive 3D printer | No feedback (open-loop stepper) | Cheap, sufficient for hobby use | N/A |
| CMM (Coordinate Measuring Machine) | Inductosyn or linear encoder | High accuracy, thermal stability | 1 µm |

---

## 3.5 Calculation: Spindle Speed & Feed for a Turning Operation

**Given:** Mild steel workpiece, diameter $D = 80$ mm. Recommended cutting speed $V = 180$ m/min, feed $f = 0.25$ mm/rev.

**Step 1: Calculate spindle speed $N$.**
$$N = \frac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D} = \frac{1000 \times 180}{\pi \times 80}$$

**Step 2: Compute denominator.**
$$\pi \times 80 = 251.327$$

**Step 3: Divide.**
$$N = \frac{180000}{251.327} \approx 716.2 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 4: Round to standard CNC value.**
$$N = 720 \text{ rpm (or 700 rpm on some controllers)}$$

**Step 5: Calculate feed rate in mm/min.**
$$f_{mm/min} = N \times f = 720 \times 0.25 = 180 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 6: Calculate Material Removal Rate (MRR) for a depth of cut $d = 2$ mm.**
$$MRR = V_c \times f \times d = 180 \times 0.25 \times 2 = 90 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

(Here $V_c$ is taken as the cross-sectional chip area approach; in some texts, MRR is defined as $f \times d \times V$ where $V$ is the surface speed in m/min. Both yield valid dimensions in mm³/min.)

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: APT vs. Manual G-Code – Comparison

| Criterion | Manual G-Code | APT (or CAM) |
|---|---|---|
| Learning curve | Moderate | Steep initially |
| 2-D programming | Quick | Overkill |
| 3-D complex surfaces | Tedious / impractical | Essential |
| Tool radius compensation | Manual (G41/G42) | Automatic (TLLFT, TLRGT) |
| Cutter location file | N/A | Yes (CLFILE) |
| Post-processor | Not needed | Mandatory |
| Industry use today | Operator-editing of CAM output | CAM software (Mastercam, NX) generates APT-style code |
| KTU exam weightage | High (canned cycles, G-codes) | Medium (statement recognition) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Closed-Loop CNC Servo System (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[NC Tape / Program<br>Command Xd] --> B[Summing Junction<br>+ and -]
    B --> C[Servo Amplifier<br>Gain K]
    C --> D[Servo Motor<br>Torque Tm]
    D --> E[Ball Screw<br>Pitch L]
    E --> F[Machine Slide<br>Position X]
    F --> G[Feedback Device<br>Encoder / LVDT]
    G -->|Actual Position Xa| B
    B -->|Error e = Xd - Xa| C

    style A fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style B fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFF
    style C fill:#4ECDC4,color:#000
    style D fill:#1A535C,color:#FFF
    style E fill:#F7B801,color:#000
    style F fill:#9D4EDD,color:#FFF
    style G fill:#06D6A0,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **programmed position** $X_d$ enters the summer.
- The summer subtracts the **actual position** $X_a$ (reported by the encoder) from the command.
- The error $e = X_d - X_a$ is amplified and drives the motor.
- The motor rotates the ball screw, which moves the slide.
- The slide position is measured by the encoder and fed back continuously.
- At steady state, $X_a = X_d$ and the error becomes zero – this is **closed-loop regulation**.

---

## 4.2 Optical Incremental Encoder – Internal Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[LED Light Source] --> B[Condenser Lens]
    B --> C[Slit Disc<br>Alternating transparent<br>and opaque lines]
    C --> D[Slit Plate<br>Fixed reference]
    D --> E[Photodiode Array]
    E --> F[Signal Conditioning<br>Quadrature Decoder]
    F --> G[Square Wave Pulses<br>Channel A and B]

    style A fill:#FFD93D,color:#000
    style B fill:#6BCB77,color:#000
    style C fill:#4D96FF,color:#FFF
    style D fill:#9B72CF,color:#FFF
    style E fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFF
    style F fill:#1A535C,color:#FFF
    style G fill:#06D6A0,color:#000
```

**Key Notes:**
- As the disc rotates, light is interrupted, producing pulses.
- Two photodiodes slightly offset give **Channel A and Channel B** in quadrature (90° phase shift) – direction of rotation is determined by which channel leads.
- A third sensor detects an **index pulse (Z)** once per revolution for homing.

---

## 4.3 APT Program Flow – From Source Code to Machined Part

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[APT Source Code<br>Geometry and Motion Statements] --> B[APT Processor<br>CL Computation]
    B --> C[CLFILE<br>Cutter Location File]
    C --> D[Post-Processor<br>Machine Specific]
    D --> E[NC Tape / G-Code File<br>Machine Ready]
    E --> F[CNC Machine Tool<br>Servo Drives and Spindle]
    F --> G[Machined Part<br>Inspection]
    G --> H[Quality Control]
    H -->|Defects Found| A

    style A fill:#FFD93D,color:#000
    style B fill:#6BCB77,color:#000
    style C fill:#4D96FF,color:#FFF
    style D fill:#9B72CF,color:#FFF
    style E fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFF
    style F fill:#1A535C,color:#FFF
    style G fill:#06D6A0,color:#000
    style H fill:#F7B801,color:#000
```

**Critical Reading:** The **APT Processor** does the heavy math – it computes the cutter location (CL) points along the entire toolpath based on geometry and motion statements. The **Post-Processor** is machine-specific (Fanuc, Siemens, Heidenhain each have different post-processors). This is why APT is **machine-independent** but the resulting NC code is **machine-dependent**.

---

## 4.4 Turning Program Workflow – Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Engineering Drawing<br>with Tolerances] --> B[Process Planning<br>Operation Sequence]
    B --> C[Tool Selection<br>T0101, T0202 etc]
    C --> D[Cutting Parameters<br>Speed, Feed, Depth]
    D --> E[Program Writing<br>G-code or APT]
    E --> F[Simulation<br>Tool Path Verification]
    F -->|Errors| E
    F -->|OK| G[Transfer to CNC Machine<br>DNC or Tape]
    G --> H[First Article Machining]
    H --> I[Inspection<br>CMM or Gauges]
    I -->|Out of Tolerance| D
    I -->|In Tolerance| J[Production Run]

    style A fill:#FFD93D,color:#000
    style B fill:#6BCB77,color:#000
    style C fill:#4D96FF,color:#FFF
    style D fill:#9B72CF,color:#FFF
    style E fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFF
    style F fill:#1A535C,color:#FFF
    style G fill:#06D6A0,color:#000
    style H fill:#F7B801,color:#000
    style I fill:#C44569,color:#FFF
    style J fill:#4ECDC4,color:#000
```

---

## 4.5 Feedback Device Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Need a Feedback Device?] --> B{Resolution Required?}
    B -->|Sub-micron| C[Laser Interferometer]
    B -->|1 to 5 micron| D{Environment?}
    B -->|Greater than 10 micron| E[Resolver or LVDT]
    D -->|Clean Indoor| F[Linear Optical Encoder]
    D -->|Dirty, Hot, Oil| G[Inductosyn or Resolver]
    D -->|Rough Industrial| H[Rotary Encoder on Lead Screw]
    C --> I[Precision Machine Tools CMMs]
    F --> J[CNC Lathes Mills Machining Centres]
    G --> K[Heavy Milling Planers Borers]
    H --> L[General Purpose CNC]
    E --> M[Cost-Sensitive Applications]

    style A fill:#FFD93D,color:#000
```
