A first-order estimate is obtained by assuming a single spherical abrasive particle accelerated by the *turbulent drag force* of the water jet along the focusing tube length $L$:

$$
F_{drag} = C_D \, A_p \, \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_w (V_j - V_a)^{2}
$$

**Step 1 — Newton's second law** for the particle of mass $m_p$:

$$
m_p \dfrac{dV_a}{dt} = C_D A_p \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_w (V_j - V_a)^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate along the focusing tube** length $L$ with $V_a(0) = 0$. Solving (analytically messy, but approximated empirically):

$$
V_a \approx V_j \left(1 - e^{-\beta L}\right)
$$

where $\beta$ is a dimensionless drag-coupling constant depending on $d_a$, $L$, and $d_n$.

**Step 3 — Empirical simplification** (Hashish, 1991): For $L/d_n > 50$ (typical industrial focusing tubes), the exponential term is negligible, and:

$$
V_a \approx (0.6 \text{ to } 0.8)\,V_j
$$

**Step 4 — Example:** For $V_j = 700$ m/s, $V_a \approx 0.7 \times 700 = 490$ m/s.

### 3.3 Derivation of MRR Power-Law Correlation

Start from dimensional analysis. The relevant variables and their dimensions are:

| Variable | Symbol | Dimension |
|---|---|---|
| MRR | Q | $L^{3} T^{-1}$ |
| Pressure | P | $M L^{-1} T^{-2}$ |
| Abrasive Flow Rate | ṁₐ | $M T^{-1}$ |
| Traverse Speed | V_c | $L T^{-1}$ |
| Nozzle Diameter | d_n | L |
| Abrasive Size | d_a | L |
| Workpiece Hardness | H | $M L^{-1} T^{-2}$ |

By Buckingham's $\pi$-theorem, the MRR can be reduced to a power-product:

$$
Q = K \cdot P^{a} \cdot \dot{m}_a^{b} \cdot V_c^{-c} \cdot d_n^{d} \cdot d_a^{e} \cdot H^{-f}
$$

**Step 1 — Logarithmic linearization** (the standard regression technique):

$$
\ln Q = \ln K + a \ln P + b \ln \dot{m}_a - c \ln V_c + d \ln d_n + e \ln d_a - f \ln H
$$

**Step 2 — Multi-linear regression on experimental data** yields (typical for mild steel, Hashish-style):

$$
Q = 0.034 \, P^{0.62} \, \dot{m}_a^{0.45} \, V_c^{-0.85} \, d_n^{1.10} \quad [\text{mm}^{3}/\text{min}]
$$

with $P$ in MPa, ṁₐ in g/s, $V_c$ in mm/min, $d_n$ in mm.

**Step 3 — Sensitivity check:** Doubling pressure multiplies MRR by $2^{0.62} \approx 1.54$. Doubling traverse speed *reduces* MRR by a factor of $2^{0.85} \approx 1.80$ — a very strong inverse relation, which is why AWJM is usually *feed-limited* not pressure-limited.

### 3.4 Kerf Width Geometry Derivation

The jet diverges after exiting the focusing tube at a half-angle $\alpha$ (typically 0.5°–1.5°). The kerf is the *truncated cone* impression:

$$
K_w = d_n + 2 \cdot SOD \cdot \tan(\alpha)
$$

**Step 1 — For $d_n = 1.0$ mm, SOD = 4 mm, $\alpha = 1°$:**

$$
K_w = 1.0 + 2 \times 4 \times \tan(1°) = 1.0 + 8 \times 0.01745 = 1.0 + 0.140 = 1.14 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The minimum kerf (top of cut) ≈ $d_n$. The maximum kerf (bottom of cut) is wider due to jet divergence and lag. This is why AWJM cuts are *trapezoidal* in cross-section, not rectangular.

### 3.5 Surface Roughness (R_a) Correlation

A widely cited empirical correlation is:

$$
R_a = C_1 \cdot V_c^{C_2}
$$

where $C_1, C_2$ depend on material. For mild steel: $C_1 \approx 0.08$, $C_2 \approx 0.45$ (with $R_a$ in μm, $V_c$ in mm/min).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an AWJM System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Intensifier Pump] --> A2[High Pressure Water Line]
    A2 --> A3[Sapphire Orifice]
    A3 --> A4[Mixing Chamber]
    B1[Abrasive Hopper] --> B2[Dosing Valve]
    B2 --> A4
    A4 --> A5[Focusing Tube]
    A5 --> A6[Workpiece Table XY CNC]
    A6 --> A7[Catcher Tank]
    A7 --> A8[Sludge Filtration Unit]
    A8 --> A9[Recycled Water Reservoir]
    A9 --> A1
    C1[Pressure Transducer] -.-> A1
    C2[CNC Controller] -.-> A6
    C3[Vibration Isolators] -.-> A1
    C4[Catcher Tank Pneumatic Damper] -.-> A7
```

**Node-by-Node Functional Description:**

- **Intensifier Pump** — Converts low-pressure hydraulic input (~20 MPa) into ultra-high pressure (up to 400 MPa) via a dual-acting plunger.
- **Sapphire Orifice** — A precision-drilled jewel (synthetic sapphire, ID ≈ 0.25 mm) that converts pressure energy to kinetic energy.
- **Mixing Chamber** — Where the high-velocity water jet *entrains* abrasive via a venturi effect (low-pressure zone at the abrasive inlet).
- **Focusing Tube** — A tungsten carbide (WC) or boron carbide (B₄C) cylindrical tube (ID 0.6–1.5 mm, length 50–100 mm) that straightens and accelerates the abrasive.
- **Workpiece Table (XY CNC)** — A 3- or 5-axis gantry that moves the jet (or workpiece) at programmed traverse speed $V_c$.
- **Catcher Tank** — A water-filled chamber that decelerates the spent jet, suppressing noise (≈ 100 dB → < 75 dB) and capturing debris.
- **Sludge Filtration Unit** — Separates garnet and metal swarf from water; garnet is non-recyclable but water is recycled.
- **Vibration Isolators** — Decouple the intensifier's ~3 Hz pulsations from the cutting head to maintain jet coherence.
- **CNC Controller** — Runs G-code paths and synchronizes $V_c$ with jet pressure.

### 4.2 Cross-Sectional View of the Nozzle Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    N1[Water Inlet 300 MPa] --> N2[Orifice Sapphire dia 0.25 mm]
    N2 --> N3[Mixing Chamber]
    N3 --> N4[Abrasive Inlet Venturi]
    N4 --> N5[Focusing Tube WC ID 0.8 mm]
    N5 --> N6[Exit Jet Vj 700 m/s]
    N6 --> N7[Workpiece]
    N8[Stand Off Distance 3 mm] -.-> N6
```

### 4.3 Material Removal Mechanism Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Ductile Metals] --> R1[Micro ploughing]
    M2[Brittle Ceramics] --> R2[Micro chipping]
    M3[Composites] --> R3[Delamination plus cutting]
    M4[Polymers] --> R4[Thermal softening plus erosion]
    R1 --> Z[Material Removal Zone]
    R2 --> Z
    R3 --> Z
    R4 --> Z
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition Type)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Module-2]**
*Define Abrasive Water Jet Machining (AWJM). List any four advantages of AWJM over conventional machining processes.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Abrasive Water Jet Machining (AWJM) is a non-traditional, cold-type material removal process in which a high-velocity jet of water carrying abrasive particles (usually garnet) is directed at the workpiece. The kinetic energy of the abrasive particles erodes the material through micro-chipping and micro-ploughing.

**Four Advantages:**
1. **No Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)** — Process is cold, hence no thermal damage, residual stress, or metallurgical transformation.
2. **Versatility** — Can cut any material irrespective of hardness (metals, ceramics, composites, stone, glass).
3. **High Flexibility** — Easily programmable CNC path; complex 2D/3D contours without tool change.
4. **Environment-friendly** — No chemical coolant, no toxic fumes; water is recyclable.
5. *(Bonus point)* Negligible tool wear — only the focusing tube wears.

*(Any four — 3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for advantages)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is the role of the focusing tube in an AWJM setup? Why is tungsten carbide (WC) the preferred material for its manufacture?*

**Model Answer:**

> **Role of Focusing Tube:**
> - The focusing tube is a precision cylindrical nozzle of ID 0.6–1.5 mm placed immediately downstream of the mixing chamber.
> - Its function is to **align, accelerate, and collimate** the abrasive-laden water jet before it exits and strikes the workpiece.
> - It also acts as a *bearing surface* where momentum from the high-velocity water is transferred to the abrasive particles via drag.

> **Why Tungsten Carbide?**
> 1. **Extreme hardness (~1800 HV)** — resists erosion by garnet particles.
> 2. **High fracture toughness** — withstands internal hydraulic shock and abrasive impact.
> 3. **Dimensional stability** — maintains tight bore tolerances (μm-level) under thermal and mechanical cycling.
> 4. **Longer tool life** — typically 40–200 hours of continuous cutting, which is economically viable.

*(3 marks: 1.5 marks for role + 1.5 marks for WC justification)*

---

### **PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Module-2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of an **Abrasive Water Jet Machining (AWJM)** system. Describe the functions of any six major components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 50 mm thick mild-steel plate is to be cut using AWJM. The water jet pressure is **300 MPa**, abrasive flow rate is **0.5 kg/min**, and traverse speed is **150 mm/min**. Given the empirical constants $K = 0.034$, $a = 0.62$, $b = 0.45$, $c = 0.85$, $d = 1.10$ for the MRR equation $MRR = K \cdot P^{a} \cdot \dot{m}_a^{b} \cdot V_c^{-c} \cdot d_n^{d}$ with $d_n = 1.0$ mm, calculate:
(i) The MRR in mm³/min.
(ii) The time required to complete a 1.5 m long cut.
(iii) The kerf width, if the jet divergence half-angle is 1° and stand-off distance is 4 mm. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Working Principle of AWJM (Block Diagram):**

*(Refer to SECTION 4.1 for the architectural diagram. In the exam, draw a rectangular block diagram showing: Water Inlet → Intensifier Pump → HP Line → Sapphire Orifice → Mixing Chamber (with abrasive inlet) → Focusing Tube → Workpiece → Catcher Tank → Filtration → Recycle.)*

**Six Major Components and Their Functions:**

1. **Intensifier Pump:** Generates ultra-high pressure (200–400 MPa) water by reciprocating a piston driven by a hydraulic actuator. Pressure is accumulated in an accumulator.
2. **Sapphire Orifice:** Converts pressure energy of water into kinetic energy; produces a coherent, hair-thin water jet (ID ≈ 0.25 mm).
3. **Abrasive Hopper & Dosing System:** Stores garnet abrasive and meters it into the mixing chamber at a controlled flow rate (0.2–1.0 kg/min).
4. **Mixing Chamber:** The venturi region where abrasive is entrained by the low-pressure zone created by the high-velocity water jet.
5. **Focusing Tube:** Accelerates the abrasive particles (to 60–80 % of jet velocity) and collimates the jet. Made of tungsten carbide or boron carbide.
6. **Catcher Tank:** A water-filled chamber beneath the workpiece that absorbs the residual jet energy, dampens noise, and traps debris. Includes a sludge removal system.
7. **CNC X-Y Work Table:** Moves the workpiece (or nozzle) along a programmed path at a precise traverse speed.

*(7 marks: 2 for diagram + 5 for component explanation — pick any 6 well-described)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
- P = 300 MPa
- ṁₐ = 0.5 kg/min = **500 g/min** (to match empirical constants' unit assumption)

> *Note for examiners:* The empirical constants in Hashish-style regressions often take ṁₐ in g/s. Converting: 0.5 kg/min = **8.33 g/s**. The student must perform unit conversion. The model below shows both interpretations.

- V_c = 150 mm/min
- d_n = 1.0 mm

**(i) MRR Calculation:**

Using the power-law formula:

$$
MRR = 0.034 \times P^{0.62} \times \dot{m}_a^{0.45} \times V_c^{-0.85} \times d_n^{1.10}
$$

Substituting (with ṁₐ in **g/s = 8.33** for SI-consistent form, or equivalently 500 g/min — we use 500 g/min for direct substitution):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P^{0.62} &= 300^{0.62} \\
&= \exp(0.62 \times \ln 300) \\
&= \exp(0.62 \times 5.7038) \\
&= \exp(3.5363) \\
&\approx 34.35
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_a^{0.45} &= 500^{0.45} \\
&= \exp(0.45 \times \ln 500) \\
&= \exp(0.45 \times 6.2146) \\
&= \exp(2.7966) \\
&\approx 16.38
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_c^{-0.85} &= 150^{-0.85} \\
&= \exp(-0.85 \times \ln 150) \\
&= \exp(-0.85 \times 5.0106) \\
&= \exp(-4.2590) \\
&\approx 0.01409
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
d_n^{1.10} = 1.0^{1.10} = 1.0
$$

Now multiply all factors:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MRR &= 0.034 \times 34.35 \times 16.38 \times 0.01409 \times 1.0 \\
&= 0.034 \times 34.35 \times 0.2308 \\
&= 0.034 \times 7.928 \\
&\approx 0.2695 \text{ mm}^{3}/\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **MRR ≈ 0.27 mm³/min**

*(Note: This value is low because the constants above are illustrative. In real industrial cuts, MRR is hundreds of mm³/min. The methodology, however, is what earns marks.)*

**(ii) Time for 1.5 m Cut:**

The volume of material removed = traverse path length × kerf width × plate thickness. The MRR equation already gives volume/time, so the volume removed is:

$$
V_{cut} = L \times K_w \times t = 1500 \text{ mm} \times 1.14 \text{ mm} \times 50 \text{ mm} = 85{,}500 \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

$$
t = \dfrac{V_{cut}}{MRR} = \dfrac{85{,}500}{0.27} \approx 316{,}667 \text{ min} \approx 5{,}278 \text{ hours}
$$

> **Time ≈ 5,278 hours (≈ 220 days continuous)**

*(Clearly, the 0.27 mm³/min figure is non-industrial. A more realistic MRR is 200 mm³/min, giving t ≈ 7 hours — which is industrially sensible. The discrepancy arises from the *chosen* empirical constants; exam-mark scheme accepts any consistent computation.)*

**(iii) Kerf Width Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_w &= d_n + 2 \times SOD \times \tan(\alpha) \\
&= 1.0 + 2 \times 4 \times \tan(1°) \\
&= 1.0 + 8 \times 0.01745 \\
&= 1.0 + 0.1396 \\
&\approx 1.14 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Kerf Width ≈ 1.14 mm**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Module-2 Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the **material removal mechanism** in AWJM for (i) ductile metals, (ii) brittle ceramics, and (iii) fibre-reinforced composites. Compare AWJM with **WJM** and **AJM** in a tabular format. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An AWJM machine operates at 380 MPa pressure through a 0.3 mm sapphire orifice. Calculate:
(i) The water jet exit velocity, $V_j$.
(ii) The abrasive particle velocity, assuming $V_a = 0.75 \, V_j$.
(iii) The kinetic power of the water jet, given water density $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³ and volumetric flow $Q_w = 2.5$ L/min.
(iv) Comment on whether the jet is subsonic, sonic, or supersonic. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Material Removal Mechanisms:**

**(i) Ductile Metals (e.g., mild steel, aluminium, titanium):**
- Removal occurs via **micro-ploughing** — the abrasive particle slides along the surface, displacing material as a small chip.
- At higher pressures, this transitions to **micro-cutting** (similar to a single-point tool on a micro-scale).
- A small fraction of brittle fracture also occurs at the exit edges of the cut.

**(ii) Brittle Ceramics (e.g., Al₂O₃, SiC, glass):**
- Removal occurs via **micro-chipping and lateral cracking** — the abrasive particle's impact exceeds the material's fracture toughness, generating a Hertzian cone crack and lateral crack that removes a chip.
- The fracture threshold pressure is given approximately by $P_c \propto K_{IC}^{2}/H^{1.5}$.

**(iii) Fibre-Reinforced Composites (e.g., CFRP, GFRP):**
- A **hybrid mode** — fibre cutting (micro-chipping for carbon/glass fibres) plus matrix erosion (micro-ploughing for epoxy).
- A specific problem is **delamination** between plies; this is minimized by using a *low-pressure* jet with *fine abrasive* and *high traverse speed*.

**Comparison Table: AWJM vs WJM vs AJM**

| Feature | WJM (Pure Water) | AJM (Abrasive Air) | AWJM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carrier fluid | Water | Air (gas) | Water |
| Pressure | 100–400 MPa | 0.2–0.8 MPa | 100–400 MPa |
| Velocity | 600–900 m/s | ~200 m/s | 600–900 m/s (jet), 400–700 m/s (abrasive) |
| Abrasive | None | Yes (Al₂O₃) | Yes (Garnet) |
| Cut depth (steel) | 1–50 mm | < 1 mm | 50–300 mm |
| Surface finish | Better than AWJM | Moderate | Moderate (R_a 1–6 μm) |
| Material range | Soft only | Soft, brittle | All materials |
| HAZ | None | None | None |
| Cost | High pressure pump | Cheap | Most expensive |
| Application | Paper, food | Cleaning, deburring | Heavy industrial cutting |

*(7 marks: 3 for mechanisms + 4 for table)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Given:** P = 380 MPa = $3.8 \times 10^{8}$ Pa, $d_n = 0.3$ mm, $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³, $Q_w = 2.5$ L/min.

**(i) Water Jet Exit Velocity:**

$$
V_j = \sqrt{\dfrac{2P}{\rho_w}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 3.8 \times 10^{8}}{1000}} = \sqrt{7.6 \times 10^{5}} \approx 871.8 \text{ m/s}
$$

> **V_j ≈ 872 m/s**

**(ii) Abrasive Particle Velocity:**

$$
V_a = 0.75 \times V_j = 0.75 \times 871.8 = 653.85 \text{ m/s}
$$

> **V_a ≈ 654 m/s**

**(iii) Kinetic Power of the Water Jet:**

Mass flow rate:

$$
\dot{m}_w = \rho_w \times Q_w = 1000 \times \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 1000 \times 4.167 \times 10^{-5} = 0.0417 \text{ kg/s}
$$

Kinetic power:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_k &= \tfrac{1}{2} \dot{m}_w V_j^{2} \\
&= 0.5 \times 0.0417 \times (871.8)^{2} \\
&= 0.5 \times 0.0417 \times 760{,}036 \\
&= 0.5 \times 31{,}693.5 \\
&\approx 15{,}847 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **P_k ≈ 15.85 kW**

**(iv) Mach Number Comment:**

Speed of sound in air at room temperature, $a = 343$ m/s.

$$
M = \dfrac{V_j}{a} = \dfrac{871.8}{343} \approx 2.54
$$

> Since M > 1, the jet is **supersonic (Mach 2.54)**. In practice, the jet rapidly decelerates due to air drag and breaks up into droplets within a few centimeters, but at the exit plane it is supersonic.

*(7 marks: 2 + 1.5 + 2.5 + 1 = 7)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors** — ṁₐ from kg/min to g/s is a *classic* trap. Half the marks lost on Part B are here. Always state conversions explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the Discharge Coefficient** — Real jet velocity is $V_j = C_d \sqrt{2P/\rho_w}$ with $C_d \approx 0.85$. If your calculated $V_j$ exceeds 1000 m/s, you forgot $C_d$.
> 3. **Confusing AWJM with AJM** — AJM uses *compressed air* and is limited to ~1 mm depth. AWJM uses *water* and goes 100+ mm deep. This is a 2-mark direct penalty.
> 4. **Ignoring the Kerf Geometry** — The cut is *trapezoidal*, not rectangular. Many students compute kerf = $d_n$ only — wrong.
> 5. **Not Labelling Diagrams** — A block diagram without labelled components and arrows loses 2 of the 3 diagram marks.
> 6. **Mixing up Sapphire Orifice vs Focusing Tube** — Orifice = pure water jet formation (downstream of pump). Focusing tube = abrasive acceleration (downstream of mixing chamber). Order matters.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — AWJM (Module 2)**
>
> - AWJM is a **cold, non-contact, non-traditional** process using a high-pressure water jet as the carrier for abrasive particles (garnet).
> - Energy chain: **Hydraulic → Kinetic → Material Removal**.
> - Typical operating pressure: **100–400 MPa**; jet velocity up to **~900 m/s (Mach 2.6)**.
> - Abrasive velocity ≈ **0.6 – 0.8 × V_j** (Hashish empirical coupling).
> - **Key components:** Intensifier pump → Sapphire orifice → Mixing chamber → Focusing tube (WC/B₄C) → Catcher tank.
> - **Most important formula:** $V_j = \sqrt{2P/\rho_w}$ — memorize for sure.
> - **MRR power-law:** Strong function of $P$, ṁₐ, $d_n$; inverse of $V_c$.
> - **Kerf width:** $K_w = d_n + 2\,SOD\,\tan(\alpha)$ — top of cut = $d_n$, bottom wider.
> - **No HAZ, no residual stress, no tool wear on workpiece** — main advantages.
> - **Best for:** Aerospace composites, titanium, ceramics, stone — multi-material versatility.
> - **Limitations:** High capital cost, noise (mitigated by catcher tank), high abrasive consumption, surface striations.
> - **Comparison must-know:** WJM (no abrasive, soft materials) vs AJM (air carrier, shallow cuts) vs AWJM (water carrier, deep cuts, all materials).
> - **Examiner's favourite traps:** unit conversion, $C_d$ correction, trapezoidal kerf, orifice vs focusing tube distinction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrochemical Machining (ECM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT528 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrochemical Machining (ECM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrochemical Machining (ECM)** is a non-traditional, **thermo-electrically neutral** material removal process in which the workpiece material is removed by **controlled anodic electrochemical dissolution** when a direct current is passed through an electrolyte flowing between a shaped cathode (tool) and the workpiece (anode), separated by a small inter-electrode gap (typically **0.05 mm to 0.80 mm**).

In other words, ECM exploits **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis** to convert metallic atoms at the workpiece surface into soluble metallic ions (or insoluble hydroxides), which are swept away by a high-velocity electrolyte stream. The cathode (tool) is *not* consumed — only the anode (workpiece) dissolves, irrespective of its hardness, strength, or toughness.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Candy Dissolving in Water" Picture**
>
> Imagine you place a sugar cube in still water — it dissolves *very slowly*. Now, imagine you stir the water rapidly and blow warm air over the surface — the sugar dissolves much faster because the dissolved layer is continuously swept away, exposing fresh surface.
>
> ECM works exactly the same way:
> - The **sugar cube** = the **workpiece (anode)**.
> - The **water** = the **electrolyte (e.g., NaCl solution)**.
> - The **stirring + warm air** = the **electric current + flowing electrolyte** that whisks dissolved metal ions away.
> - The "shape" of the dissolved sugar matches the *negative* of the "shape" of the mold (tool), just like a chocolate mould that takes the negative impression of the cavity.
>
> The metal is *not* melted, *not* vaporized, *not* mechanically cut — it is **electrochemically eaten away** one atom at a time, with zero thermal damage to the surface.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Faraday's Constant (F)** = **96,487 C/mol** (≈ 96,500 C/mol for board exams)
> - **Operating Voltage (V)** = **5 V to 25 V** DC
> - **Operating Current Density** = **20 A/cm² to 200 A/cm²**
> - **Inter-Electrode Gap (IEG)** = **0.05 mm to 0.80 mm**
> - **Electrolyte Velocity** = **10 m/s to 60 m/s** (to flush sludge and dissipate heat)
> - **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** — typically expressed in **mm³/min** or **cm³/min**

### 1.4 Why ECM is a "Non-Traditional" Process

| Aspect | Conventional Machining | ECM |
|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Mechanical (shear) | Electrochemical |
| Tool wear | Significant | Negligible (cathode is not consumed) |
| Surface integrity | Residual stress, burrs | Stress-free, burr-free |
| Hardness dependency | Hard materials difficult | Hardness-independent |
| HAZ (Heat Affected Zone) | Yes (in thermal processes) | **None** (cold process) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Workpiece is connected to the positive terminal** of a DC power supply → it becomes the **anode**.
2. **Tool (cathode) is connected to the negative terminal** → it remains the **cathode**.
3. A **conductive electrolyte** (commonly NaCl, NaNO₃, or NaBr) is pumped under high pressure through the narrow gap between tool and workpiece.
4. When DC voltage is applied, an **electrochemical cell** is formed. At the anode surface, metal atoms lose electrons and dissolve as metal ions:
   $$M \rightarrow M^{Z+} + Z e^{-}$$
5. At the cathode, a complementary reduction reaction occurs (typically hydrogen evolution):
   $$2H_2O + 2e^{-} \rightarrow H_2\uparrow + 2OH^{-}$$
6. The metallic ions combine with hydroxyl ions (or stay dissolved) and are **flushed away** by the flowing electrolyte, taking the heat and reaction products with them.
7. The tool is **mechanically fed** slowly toward the workpiece at a rate that exactly matches the workpiece's dissolution rate — this maintains a constant equilibrium gap and produces the desired cavity shape (a *mirror-image* of the tool).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Exams:** The shape produced on the workpiece is the **negative replica** of the cathode. If the tool has a convex profile, the workpiece gets a concave cavity, and vice versa.

### 2.2 Why & How of Each Step (Intuitive Layer)

- **Why DC and not AC?** Because the dissolution reaction must be unidirectional — AC would alternately dissolve the tool and workpiece.
- **Why flowing electrolyte?** (a) To carry away sludge and dissolved ions, (b) to dissipate Joule heat, (c) to maintain uniform concentration and temperature.
- **Why low voltage (<25 V)?** Higher voltages cause sparking, gas evolution, and loss of dimensional control — ECM must stay in the *electrochemical regime*, not the *discharge regime*.
- **Why the cathode does not dissolve?** Because the cathode reaction is *reduction* (gain of electrons), not oxidation. The tool is electrochemically protected.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All the following formulas are **directly tested in KTU ESE** — memorize the variables, units, and the conditions of use.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Volume MRR (ideal) | $MRR = \dfrac{A \cdot I}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | $\text{cm}^3/\text{s}$ | 100% current efficiency assumed |
| 2 | Volume MRR (with $\eta$) | $MRR = \dfrac{A \cdot I \cdot \eta}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | $\text{cm}^3/\text{s}$ | $\eta$ = current efficiency (decimal) |
| 3 | Specific MRR (SMRR) | $SMRR = \dfrac{A}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | $\text{cm}^3/(\text{A}\cdot\text{s})$ | Volume per coulomb of charge |
| 4 | Mass MRR (Faraday's 1st Law) | $m = \dfrac{A \cdot I \cdot t \cdot \eta}{Z \cdot F}$ | grams | Direct mass form |
| 5 | Equilibrium Feed Rate | $f = \dfrac{A \cdot I \cdot \eta}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A_w}$ | $\text{mm}/\text{min}$ | $A_w$ = workpiece cross-section |
| 6 | Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{MRR}$ | $\text{W}\cdot\text{s}/\text{cm}^3$ | Lower SEC ⇒ more efficient |
| 7 | Hydrogen gas evolved (Faraday's 2nd Law) | $V_{H_2} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{2F} \times 22{,}400$ | mL (at STP) | Per Faraday's equivalent volume |
| 8 | Atomic wt. from Faraday's Law | $A = \dfrac{Z \cdot F \cdot m}{I \cdot t}$ | g/mol | For unknown alloy composition |

> **Variable Legend:**
> - $A$ = atomic weight of workpiece (g/mol) — for alloys, use equivalent atomic weight
> - $I$ = current (Amperes)
> - $Z$ = valency (number of electrons transferred per atom)
> - $F$ = **96,487 C/mol**
> - $\rho$ = density of workpiece (g/cm³)
> - $\eta$ = current efficiency (dimensionless, 0.85 to 0.95 in practice)
> - $V$ = applied voltage
> - $t$ = time (seconds)
> - $A_w$ = cross-sectional area of cut (mm²)
> - $m$ = mass removed (grams)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Machining of **turbine blades, blisks, and blings** made of nickel super-alloys (Inconel, Waspaloy) — these are *impossible* to machine conventionally due to extreme hardness.
- **Die & Mould Making:** Production of forging/casting dies with complex internal contours.
- **Medical Devices:** Fine machining of **surgical implants** (titanium, Co-Cr alloys) with burr-free finish.
- **Automotive:** Machining of valve guides, connecting rod big-end bores.
- **Defence:** Shaped-charge liner machining, missile body components.
- **Electronics:** Drilling of **micro-holes** (down to **75 µm** diameter) in printed circuit boards.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental MRR Equation (Faraday's First Law applied to ECM)

**Starting Point — Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis (1833):**
"The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte."

Mathematically:
$$m \propto Q$$
$$m = Z' \cdot Q$$
where $Z'$ is the *electrochemical equivalent* (mass liberated per coulomb).

**Step 1 — Express $Z'$ in terms of atomic weight, valency, and Faraday's constant.**

By definition, when **1 Faraday (96,487 C)** of charge is passed, **1 gram-equivalent** of substance is liberated. A gram-equivalent has mass:
$$E = \frac{A}{Z}$$

So the electrochemical equivalent is:
$$Z' = \frac{E}{F} = \frac{A}{Z \cdot F}$$

This gives:
$$m = \frac{A \cdot Q}{Z \cdot F} = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{Z \cdot F}$$

**Step 2 — Convert mass to volume using density $\rho$.**

$$V_{removed} = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to time to get Volume MRR (cm³/s).**

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = MRR = \frac{A \cdot I}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce current efficiency $\eta$.**

In real ECM, not all current contributes to metal dissolution (some goes into gas evolution, side reactions). So:
$$MRR_{actual} = \eta \cdot \frac{A \cdot I}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 5 — Practical unit conversion (cm³/s → mm³/min).**

Multiply by $1000 \times 60 = 60{,}000$:
$$MRR \text{[mm}^3/\text{min]} = 60{,}000 \times \frac{A \cdot I \cdot \eta}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Dimensional check** (verifies formula correctness):
> $$\frac{[\text{g/mol}] \cdot [\text{A}]}{[\text{dimensionless}] \cdot [\text{C/mol}] \cdot [\text{g/cm}^3]} = \frac{\text{g} \cdot \text{C}}{\text{mol} \cdot \text{C} \cdot \text{g}/\text{cm}^3} = \text{cm}^3/\text{s} \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Examples (Step-by-Step, Examiner-Grade)

---

#### **Example 1 — Iron (Fe) MRR Calculation**

**Given:**
- Workpiece material = Iron (Fe)
- $A = 55.85$ g/mol
- $Z = 2$ (Fe dissolves as Fe²⁺)
- $\rho = 7.86$ g/cm³
- $I = 1000$ A
- $\eta = 0.90$ (90%)

**Find:** MRR in cm³/s and mm³/min

**Step-by-step Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
MRR &= \frac{A \cdot I \cdot \eta}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho} \\
&= \frac{55.85 \times 1000 \times 0.90}{2 \times 96{,}487 \times 7.86} \\
&= \frac{50{,}265}{1{,}516{,}854.84} \\
&= 0.03314 \;\text{cm}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Convert to mm³/min:**
$$\begin{aligned}
MRR &= 0.03314 \times 60{,}000 \\
&= 1988.4 \;\text{mm}^3/\text{min} \\
&\approx 1.988 \;\text{cm}^3/\text{min}
\end{aligned}$$

---

#### **Example 2 — Specific Material Removal Rate (SMRR) for Aluminium**

**Given:**
- Aluminium: $A = 26.98$ g/mol, $Z = 3$, $\rho = 2.70$ g/cm³

**Step-by-step Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
SMRR &= \frac{A}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho} \\
&= \frac{26.98}{3 \times 96{,}487 \times 2.70} \\
&= \frac{26.98}{781{,}544.7} \\
&= 3.453 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{cm}^3/(\text{A}\cdot\text{s})
\end{aligned}$$

**In more convenient form (mm³/min per 1000 A):**
$$SMRR = 3.453 \times 10^{-5} \times 60 \times 1000 = 2.072 \;\text{mm}^3/(\text{min}\cdot\text{A}) \times 1000$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When a question gives current in Amperes and asks for MRR, **always work in SI/CGS units** for the formula, then convert at the end. Most KTU mistakes happen because students mix mm³ with cm³.

---

#### **Example 3 — Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) Calculation**

**Given:**
- Workpiece = Aluminium (Al), $A = 26.98$ g/mol, $Z = 3$, $\rho = 2.70$ g/cm³
- $I = 500$ A, $V = 12$ V, $\eta = 0.90$

**Step 1 — Compute MRR first.**
$$\begin{aligned}
MRR &= \frac{26.98 \times 500 \times 0.90}{3 \times 96{,}487 \times 2.70} \\
&= \frac{12{,}141}{781{,}544.7} \\
&= 0.01553 \;\text{cm}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the SEC formula.**
$$\begin{aligned}
SEC &= \frac{V \cdot I}{MRR} \\
&= \frac{12 \times 500}{0.01553} \\
&= \frac{6000}{0.01553} \\
&= 386{,}349 \;\text{W}\cdot\text{s}/\text{cm}^3 \\
&\approx 3.86 \times 10^{5} \;\text{J}/\text{cm}^3 \\
&\approx 107.3 \;\text{W}\cdot\text{h}/\text{cm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> ECM typically has a *very low* SEC compared to EDM (which is 10× to 100× higher), but the *volumetric* efficiency is poor because the dissolution is governed by Faraday's law — a fundamental physical limit.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Influence of Process Parameters on MRR

| Parameter | Effect on MRR | Mechanism | KTU Exam Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Current ($I$) | ↑ MRR (linear) | More electrons ⇒ more ions | Directly proportional |
| ↑ Valency ($Z$) | ↓ MRR (inverse) | Multi-electron reactions need more charge | Watch for $Z$ in alloys |
| ↑ Density ($\rho$) | ↓ MRR (inverse) | Denser metal ⇒ less volume per gram | Compare Fe vs. Al |
| ↑ Atomic weight ($A$) | ↑ MRR (linear) | Heavier atoms ⇒ more mass per mole | Subtle but important |
| ↑ Electrolyte flow | Stabilizes MRR | Prevents local concentration drop | No direct formula effect |
| ↑ Gap (IEG) | ↓ MRR (roughly) | Higher resistance ⇒ lower current | Ohm's law dependency |
| ↑ Voltage | Saturates MRR | Beyond back-EMF, no further gain | Has *upper* threshold |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an ECM System

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below is a **functional flow representation** of an industrial ECM setup. In actual KTU exams, the *sketch of the tool-workpiece-gap-electrolyte flow loop* is the single most frequently asked 2-mark/4-mark diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PS[DC POWER SUPPLY 5V to 25V]:::ps
    POS[POSITIVE TERMINAL ANODE]:::an
    NEG[NEGATIVE TERMINAL CATHODE TOOL]:::ca
    WP[WORKPIECE ANODE METAL DISSOLUTION M to MZ PLUS PLUS Ze]:::wp
    TL[SHAPED CATHODIC TOOL]:::tl
    GAP[SMALL INTER ELECTRODE GAP 0.05 TO 0.80 MM]:::gap
    EL[ELECTROLYTE FLOW NACL OR NANO3]:::el
    PUMP[HIGH PRESSURE PUMP 10 TO 60 M PER S]:::pump
    FILT[FILTER REMOVES SLUDGE AND HEAT]:::filt
    TANK[ELECTROLYTE RESERVOIR TANK]:::tank
    COOL[COOLER AND pH CONTROLLER]:::cool
    H2[H2 GAS EVOLUTION AT CATHODE]:::gas

    PS --> POS
    POS --> WP
    PS --> NEG
    NEG --> TL
    WP --- GAP
    GAP --- TL
    EL --> GAP
    PUMP --> EL
    TANK --> PUMP
    FILT --> TANK
    COOL --> TANK
    GAP --> H2
    GAP --> FILT

    classDef ps fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef an fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef ca fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef wp fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef tl fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef gap fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef el fill:#00CED1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pump fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef filt fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tank fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cool fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef gas fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Functional Process Flow (Sequential Stages of ECM)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[STAGE 1 DC POWER ON]:::s1 --> B[STAGE 2 ELECTROLYTE FLOW STARTS]:::s2
    B --> C[STAGE 3 TOOL ADVANCES TOWARD WORKPIECE]:::s3
    C --> D[STAGE 4 ANODIC DISSOLUTION M to MZ PLUS PLUS Ze]:::s4
    D --> E[STAGE 5 HYDROGEN GAS EVOLVED AT CATHODE]:::s5
    E --> F[STAGE 6 SLUDGE FLUSHED BY ELECTROLYTE]:::s6
    F --> G[STAGE 7 TOOL FEED BALANCES DISSOLUTION RATE]:::s7
    G --> H[STAGE 8 DESIRED SHAPE OBTAINED]:::s8

    classDef s1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef s2 fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef s3 fill:#FFEBCD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef s4 fill:#FF7F50,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef s5 fill:#F0FFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef s6 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef s7 fill:#7FFFD4,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef s8 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Textual Schematic — Tool, Workpiece, and Gap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual sketch (to be drawn in exam):** A *cathode tool* (typically the negative of the desired shape) is held at a precise gap from the *anode workpiece*. Electrolyte enters at high velocity, flows radially across the gap, and exits carrying dissolved metal hydroxides. The cathode is mechanically fed at the equilibrium feed rate $f$.

**Layered cross-section of ECM gap (top to bottom):**

1. **Tool (Cathode)** — top layer, advancing downward at feed rate $f$.
2. **Electrolyte inlet manifold** — distributed at multiple ports.
3. **Bulk electrolyte flow** — turbulent, high-velocity.
4. **Inter-electrode gap (IEG)** — region of intense current density; this is where the metal dissolution occurs.
5. **Workpiece (Anode)** — bottom layer, dissolving upward at MRR.
6. **Electrolyte exit** — carries dissolved metal ions + hydrogen gas + heat.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**State Faraday's First and Second Laws of Electrolysis. How are they applied in Electrochemical Machining?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Faraday's First Law:** The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte. $m = Z' \cdot Q = \dfrac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{Z \cdot F}$
- **Faraday's Second Law:** When the same quantity of electricity is passed through different electrolytes in series, the masses of substances liberated are proportional to their chemical equivalent weights.
- **Application in ECM:** The workpiece is dissolved (at the anode) at a rate directly determined by the current passed. By controlling current, the material removal rate can be precisely controlled — this is the fundamental basis of ECM.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**List any three advantages and two limitations of ECM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. **No tool wear** — the cathode is not consumed, so tool geometry remains constant.
2. **No thermal damage** — purely electrochemical dissolution produces a stress-free, burr-free surface.
3. **Hardness-independent** — can machine materials harder than the tool itself (e.g., Inconel, tungsten carbide, ceramics with conductive binder).

**Limitations:**
1. Restricted to **electrically conductive materials** only (cannot machine ceramics, plastics, rubber).
2. **High capital cost** and need for precise electrolyte filtration, temperature, and pH control systems.

---

### 📕 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) Explain the working principle of Electrochemical Machining (ECM) with a neat labelled schematic. Discuss the role of the electrolyte and state the important process parameters. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Working Principle (3 marks):**

ECM is based on the principle of **anodic electrochemical dissolution** governed by Faraday's laws. The workpiece is connected as the **anode** and the shaped tool as the **cathode** of a low-voltage (5–25 V), high-current (up to 10,000 A) DC power supply. A conductive electrolyte (commonly **NaCl 15–20%** or **NaNO₃ 20–30%**) is pumped at high pressure (10–60 m/s) through the small inter-electrode gap (0.05–0.80 mm). When current flows, the metal atoms at the anode lose electrons and dissolve as metal ions: $M \rightarrow M^{Z+} + Z e^{-}$. These ions are flushed away by the electrolyte. The tool is mechanically advanced at a rate equal to the dissolution rate to maintain a constant gap and obtain a *negative replica* of the tool shape on the workpiece.

**Role of Electrolyte (2 marks):**
- Acts as the **current-carrying medium** between the electrodes.
- **Removes** dissolved metal ions, hydrogen gas, and Joule heat from the machining zone.
- **NaCl** is preferred for ferrous alloys (gives high conductivity, aggressive dissolution).
- **NaNO₃** is preferred for **passivating metals** like stainless steel (forms a protective oxide that improves accuracy).

**Key Process Parameters (2 marks):**
1. **Applied voltage** (5–25 V DC)
2. **Current density** (20–200 A/cm²)
3. **Inter-electrode gap** (0.05–0.80 mm)
4. **Electrolyte flow rate and temperature**
5. **Feed rate** of the tool
6. **Tool geometry and material**

---

**(b) In an ECM operation, the workpiece is iron (Fe) with $A = 55.85$ g/mol, $Z = 2$, and $\rho = 7.86$ g/cm³. If the current supplied is 1500 A and the current efficiency is 92%, calculate: (i) the volume MRR in cm³/s, (ii) the mass MRR in g/s, and (iii) the volume MRR in mm³/min. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $A = 55.85$ g/mol, $Z = 2$, $\rho = 7.86$ g/cm³, $I = 1500$ A, $\eta = 0.92$, $F = 96{,}487$ C/mol.

**(i) Volume MRR in cm³/s:**

$$\begin{aligned}
MRR_v &= \frac{A \cdot I \cdot \eta}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho} \\
&= \frac{55.85 \times 1500 \times 0.92}{2 \times 96{,}487 \times 7.86} \\
&= \frac{77{,}073}{1{,}516{,}854.84} \\
&= 0.05081 \;\text{cm}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Mass MRR in g/s:**

$$\begin{aligned}
MRR_m &= \frac{A \cdot I \cdot \eta}{Z \cdot F} \\
&= \frac{55.85 \times 1500 \times 0.92}{2 \times 96{,}487} \\
&= \frac{77{,}073}{192{,}974} \\
&= 0.3994 \;\text{g}/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Volume MRR in mm³/min:**

$$\begin{aligned}
MRR_v &= 0.05081 \times 60{,}000 \\
&= 3048.6 \;\text{mm}^3/\text{min} \\
&\approx 3.05 \;\text{cm}^3/\text{min}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to multiply by $\eta$ → loses 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Unit conversion error (cm³/s to mm³/min) — must multiply by 60,000, not 1000. → loses 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Using $Z = 3$ for Fe. **Iron dissolves as Fe²⁺, not Fe³⁺** in ECM under typical conditions. → loses 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Mixing up atomic weight with mass number. → loses 1 mark.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) Discuss the process parameters that affect the Material Removal Rate (MRR) and surface finish in ECM. Explain the concept of equilibrium gap and feed rate. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Parameters affecting MRR (4 marks):**

1. **Current ($I$):** MRR is *directly proportional* to current (Faraday's 1st Law). Higher current ⇒ more electrons ⇒ more metal ions removed per second.
2. **Valency of the metal ($Z$):** MRR is *inversely proportional* to $Z$. A metal dissolving as $M^2+$ needs fewer electrons per atom than $M^3+$, hence gives a higher MRR for the same current.
3. **Atomic weight and density:** MRR is proportional to $A/\rho$ — heavier, less-dense metals have higher volumetric MRR.
4. **Current efficiency ($\eta$):** Depends on the metal-electrolyte combination. NaCl gives higher efficiency but poorer surface; NaNO₃ gives lower efficiency but better surface (passivation effect).
5. **Electrolyte concentration and flow rate:** Insufficient flow leads to localized concentration drop and uneven MRR (stray-cut). Higher flow stabilizes MRR.
6. **Inter-electrode gap (IEG):** Smaller gap ⇒ higher current density (lower electrolyte resistance) ⇒ higher MRR. But too small a gap causes sparking and short-circuiting.

**Parameters affecting Surface Finish (2 marks):**

1. **Current density uniformity** — controlled by tool geometry and electrolyte flow distribution.
2. **Passivation tendency** of the metal-electrolyte pair (NaNO₃ gives smoother finish on stainless steel).
3. **Electrolyte purity** — contamination causes pitting and stray machining.

**Equilibrium Gap and Feed Rate (1 mark):**

The **equilibrium gap** is the steady-state inter-electrode distance at which the *rate of metal dissolution* equals the *rate of tool feed*. At this point, the gap is constant and dimensional accuracy is achieved. The tool feed rate $f$ is calculated as:
$$f = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot \eta}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A_w}$$
where $A_w$ is the cross-sectional area being cut.

---

**(b) Calculate the specific material removal rate (SMRR) and specific energy consumption (SEC) for machining Aluminium ($A = 26.98$ g/mol, $Z = 3$, $\rho = 2.70$ g/cm³) at a current of 800 A, voltage of 15 V, and current efficiency of 90%. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $A = 26.98$ g/mol, $Z = 3$, $\rho = 2.70$ g/cm³, $I = 800$ A, $V = 15$ V, $\eta = 0.90$, $F = 96{,}487$ C/mol.

**Step 1 — Compute SMRR (cm³ per A·s):**

$$\begin{aligned}
SMRR &= \frac{A}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho} \\
&= \frac{26.98}{3 \times 96{,}487 \times 2.70} \\
&= \frac{26.98}{781{,}544.7} \\
&= 3.453 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{cm}^3/(\text{A}\cdot\text{s})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Volume MRR (cm³/s):**

$$\begin{aligned}
MRR_v &= SMRR \times I \times \eta \\
&= 3.453 \times 10^{-5} \times 800 \times 0.90 \\
&= 0.02486 \;\text{cm}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Specific Energy Consumption (W·s/cm³ or J/cm³):**

$$\begin{aligned}
SEC &= \frac{V \cdot I}{MRR_v} \\
&= \frac{15 \times 800}{0.02486} \\
&= \frac{12{,}000}{0.02486} \\
&= 482{,}703 \;\text{J}/\text{cm}^3 \\
&\approx 4.83 \times 10^{5} \;\text{J}/\text{cm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kWh/cm³ (optional but useful):**

$$SEC = \frac{482{,}703}{3.6 \times 10^{6}} = 0.1341 \;\text{kWh}/\text{cm}^3$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget $\eta$ in MRR calculation → loses 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Using $Z = 2$ for Al — **Aluminium is trivalent ($Z = 3$) in ECM.** → loses 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing **SMRR** with **MRR** — SMRR is per unit current (cm³/A·s), not absolute (cm³/s). → loses 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Not including $\eta$ in $SEC$ calculation (it cancels in the standard $V \cdot I / MRR$ form, but be careful if a non-ideal MRR is used).

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Points — Pin These to Your Memory Before Walking into the ESE Hall:**

- ✅ ECM is a **cold, non-thermal, non-mechanical** material removal process.
- ✅ **Workpiece = Anode (+)**; **Tool = Cathode (−)**. The tool does *not* dissolve.
- ✅ **Electrolyte:** NaCl (aggressive, high MRR) or NaNO₃ (passivating, better surface).
- ✅ **Operating Voltage:** 5–25 V DC. **Current:** up to 10,000 A.
- ✅ **Inter-electrode gap:** 0.05–0.80 mm — must be controlled precisely.
- ✅ **Key formula:** $MRR = \dfrac{A \cdot I \cdot \eta}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ — always quote this in the form *with* $\eta$ to score full marks.
- ✅ **SMRR** = $A / (Z \cdot F \cdot \rho)$ — characteristic property of the *material*, independent of current.
- ✅ **SEC** = $V \cdot I / MRR$ — useful for comparing process efficiencies.
- ✅ **Equilibrium gap** = the gap at which dissolution rate = tool feed rate.
- ✅ **Workpiece shape** = *negative replica* of the tool.
- ✅ **MRR ∝ Current (linear)** and **MRR ∝ 1/Z (inverse)** — common board-exam question.
- ✅ **No tool wear, no HAZ, no residual stress** — primary advantages.
- ✅ **Limitations:** only conductive materials, high equipment cost, electrolyte maintenance.
- ✅ **Common valencies to remember:** Fe → 2, Al → 3, Cu → 2, Ni → 2, Ti → 4, W → 6, Cr → 3.
- ✅ **Faraday's Constant** = **96,487 C/mol** (board exam value).
- ✅ **For alloys**, use **equivalent atomic weight**: $A_{eq} = \sum (w_i / A_i)^{-1}$ and **equivalent valency**.
- ✅ **Stray-cut (overcut)** is a common defect — caused by fringing of current lines; can be minimized by side-insulation of the cathode and proper gap control.
- ✅ **Unit conversion trap:** cm³/s → mm³/min requires **× 60,000**, not × 1000.

> [!NOTE]
> **Most Frequent Board Question Format (Past 5 Years):**
> 1. *Define ECM and explain its principle with a sketch.* (5–7 marks)
> 2. *Derive the MRR equation based on Faraday's law.* (5–7 marks)
> 3. *Calculate MRR / SMRR / SEC for a given material and current.* (7 marks numerical)
> 4. *Compare ECM with EDM.* (often appears as a 7-mark question)

— **End of ECM Module Notes — Good luck for your KTU ESE!** 🎓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electro Discharge Machining (EDM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT528 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING PROCESSES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Electro Discharge Machining (EDM) ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition **Electro Discharge Machining (EDM)** is a non-conventional, electro-thermal material removal process in which the desired shape is obtained by eroding the workpiece material through a series of rapidly recurring, precisely controlled, discrete **electrical discharges (sparks)** generated between a tool electrode (cathode) and a conductive workpiece (anode), both immersed in a dielectric fluid, with no direct physical contact between them. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** EDM is a **thermo-electric process**, not a mechanical one. Material removal occurs due to **melting and vaporization** caused by extremely high localized temperatures (typically **8,000 °C to 12,000 °C**) at the spark point, NOT due to abrasion or cutting forces. > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology to Remember:** > - **Tool Electrode (Cathode, –ve):** Shapes the cavity; usually made of copper, graphite, or brass. > - **Workpiece (Anode, +ve):** The conductive material to be machined. > - **Dielectric Fluid:** Insulating liquid (EDM oil, kerosene, or deionized water) that becomes conductive only when ionized. > - **Spark Gap (Overcut):** The small distance (typically **0.01 mm to 0.05 mm**) maintained between tool and workpiece. --- ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine trying to sculpt a hard rock (the workpiece) without ever touching it with a hammer. Instead, you use a magnifying glass to focus sunlight onto tiny spots until those spots **explode into vapor**. If you move that focused "heat bullet" around following a pattern, you slowly carve out the shape you want. That is exactly what EDM does — but instead of sunlight, it uses a **controlled lightning bolt** (the spark) generated inside a liquid bath. | Everyday Thing | EDM Equivalent | |---|---| | Lightning in a thunderstorm | Discrete spark between tool and workpiece | | Insulation of air (no spark) | Role of the dielectric fluid (insulates until voltage threshold is crossed) | | Burning a hole in paper with focused sunlight | Melting/vaporization of a tiny crater on the workpiece | | Rain washing away the ash | Dielectric flushing the eroded debris away | | Camera flashbulb (very short, intense) | A single EDM pulse (microseconds long) | > [!TIP] > **Why the dielectric fluid?** If the gap were filled with air, the spark would keep going once started (arc). The dielectric **quenches** (extinguishes) each spark instantly so a fresh spark can form at the next pulse. This is the genius of EDM — controlled, repeated, *discrete* sparks. --- ### Key Physical Constants & Engineering Metrics (Bold-Faced for Exams) - **Spark Temperature:** **8,000 °C to 12,000 °C** (≈ 2× surface temp of the sun) - **Spark Duration (Pulse On-Time, T_on):** **1 μs to 2000 μs** - **Spark Gap (Working Gap):** **0.01 mm to 0.05 mm** - **Open Circuit Voltage:** **50 V to 400 V** - **Peak Current:** **0.5 A to 500 A** - **Dielectric Strength Required:** **10⁶ V/m to 10⁷ V/m** - **Material Removal Rate (MRR):** **2 mm³/min to 400 mm³/min** (typical range) ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### Step-by-Step Working Principle The EDM process follows a continuous, repeating cycle. Each cycle consists of **four phases**: #### Phase 1 — Dielectric Ionization (Initiation) - The servo system maintains a small gap (a few microns) between the tool and workpiece. - A high voltage (open-circuit voltage) is applied across this gap. - The electric field intensity **E = V/d** in the gap reaches the **dielectric breakdown strength** of the fluid (typically **10⁶ V/m to 10⁷ V/m**). - Free electrons (always present in the fluid) get accelerated, collide with fluid molecules, and knock out more electrons → **avalanche ionization**. > [!NOTE] > The exact electric field needed is $E = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{d_{gap}}$, where $V_{oc}$ is the open-circuit voltage and $d_{gap}$ is the spark gap. When $E \ge E_{breakdown}$, the fluid turns from insulator to conductor instantaneously. #### Phase 2 — Spark Discharge (Material Erosion) - A narrow conductive **plasma channel** forms between the tool and workpiece. - Current rushes through this channel, producing extremely high, localized Joule heating. - Temperatures soar to **8,000–12,000 °C**. - A small volume of workpiece material at the spark point **melts and vaporizes** — a tiny crater is formed. - A small amount of tool material is also vaporized (this is called **tool wear**). #### Phase 3 — Pulse Off (Debris Flushing) - The voltage is switched OFF for a brief period (T_off, typically **2× to 10× T_on**). - The plasma channel collapses (dielectric de-ionizes and regains insulating property). - The dielectric fluid is flushed through the gap (either by external pump or through-tool flushing) and **carries away the vaporized debris** (re-solidified metal particles) and any gas bubbles. - The gap is now "clean" and ready for the next spark. #### Phase 4 — Servo Feed & Repetition - The servo system senses the gap impedance and **feeds the tool downward** to maintain the constant gap distance. - The next voltage pulse arrives, and the cycle repeats thousands of times per second. --- ### Why EDM? (Real-World Engineering Utility) | Industry | Typical Application | |---|---| | **Die & Mold Making** | Sintered carbide dies, forging dies, plastic injection molds (deep, complex cavities) | | **Aerospace** | Turbine blade cooling holes, EDM-drilled super-alloy components | | **Automotive** | Precision gears, fuel injection nozzles, prototype parts | | **Medical** | Orthopedic implants, surgical instruments in titanium alloys | | **Electronics** | Micro-EDM for printed circuit board micro-holes, wire EDM for micro-features | > [!IMPORTANT] > **EDM can machine ANY electrically conductive material** regardless of its hardness (hardness is **not** a factor). It is the only practical method to machine **tungsten carbide, titanium, Inconel, and other super-alloys** to intricate shapes. --- ### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following formulas are **frequently asked** in KTU 2024 scheme exams. Memorize the variables, units, and the relationships — questions are often numerical substitution problems. | # | Formula | Description | Key Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V_{spark} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot d_c^{\,2} \cdot h_c$ | Volume of material removed per single spark (crater volume) | $d_c$ = crater diameter (mm), $h_c$ = crater depth (mm), $V_{spark}$ in mm³ | | 2 | $MRR = V_{spark} \cdot f$ | Material Removal Rate per minute | $f$ = spark frequency (sparks/sec), MRR in mm³/min | | 3 | $T_{on}$ and $T_{off}$ | Pulse ON-time and OFF-time (μs) — controls crater size | $T_{on}$ ≈ 1–2000 μs | | 4 | $E_{pulse} = V \cdot I \cdot T_{on}$ | Energy released in one pulse (Joules) | V = voltage (V), I = current (A), T_on in seconds | | 5 | $TWR = \dfrac{V_{tool\,wear}}{V_{workpiece\,removed}}$ | **Tool Wear Ratio** (dimensionless, often expressed as %) | Lower TWR is better; ranges 0.1% to 50% depending on electrode material | | 6 | $RWR = \dfrac{V_{recast}}{V_{removed}}$ | **Recast White Layer Ratio** | Indicates heat-affected layer fraction | | 7 | $SR = C \cdot I^{a} \cdot T_{on}^{b}$ | Empirical **Surface Roughness** relation | C, a, b are empirical constants; a ≈ 1, b ≈ 0.3–0.5 | | 8 | $OC = \dfrac{d_h - d_e}{2}$ | **Overcut** (radial gap) | $d_h$ = hole diameter, $d_e$ = electrode diameter | | 9 | $E_{specific} = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot T_{on}}{V_{spark}}$ | **Specific Energy** consumed per unit volume removed (J/mm³) | Indicates process efficiency | | 10 | $f = \dfrac{1}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$ | Spark frequency | T_on, T_off in seconds | > [!TIP] > **Absolute value safety:** Wherever you see \vert x \vert in textbook derivations, treat it as the modulus (magnitude). This is critical for the gap voltage $V_{oc} = \vert V_{tool} - V_{work} \vert$ where one is +ve and the other –ve. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### Derivation 1: Volumetric Material Removal Rate (MRR) Per Spark The shape of an EDM crater is, to a first approximation, an oblate hemispheroid. For an engineering approximation, we model the crater as a **hemisphere**. #### Step 1 — Volume of a Single Crater (Hemisphere Model) The volume of a hemisphere of diameter $d_c$ and depth $h_c$ is approximated as: $$ V_{spark} \approx \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot d_c^{\,2} \cdot h_c $$ > **Reasoning:** A full sphere of diameter $d_c$ has volume $\tfrac{\pi}{6} d_c^{\,3}$. A hemisphere has half that, $\tfrac{\pi}{12} d_c^{\,3}$. But EDM craters are *flatter* than hemispheres — they are shallow disks. Empirically, the disk approximation with depth $h_c$ (typically $0.1 d_c$ to $0.5 d_c$) gives a more accurate estimate: $V_{spark} = (\pi/4) d_c^{\,2} h_c$. #### Step 2 — Expressing Crater Dimensions in Terms of Process Parameters Empirically (from extensive EDM research by Snoeys and others): $$ d_c \;\propto\; I^{1/4} \cdot T_{on}^{\,1/2} $$ $$ h_c \;\propto\; I^{1/2} \cdot T_{on}^{\,1/2} $$ where: - $I$ = peak discharge current (A) - $T_{on}$ = pulse on-time (μs) > **Why this proportionality?** Higher current delivers more energy per pulse → bigger crater. Longer pulse allows more heat conduction → deeper crater. #### Step 3 — Substituting into the Volume Equation $$ V_{spark} \;\propto\; \left(I^{1/4} T_{on}^{\,1/2}\right)^{2} \cdot \left(I^{1/2} T_{on}^{\,1/2}\right) $$ $$ V_{spark} \;\propto\; I^{1/2} \cdot T_{on} \cdot I^{1/2} \cdot T_{on}^{\,1/2} $$ $$ V_{spark} \;\propto\; I \cdot T_{on}^{\,3/2} $$ > **Verification by dimensions:** I (Amperes) × $T_{on}^{3/2}$ (μs^{1.5}) is consistent with the empirical observation that MRR is approximately linear in current and super-linear in pulse-on time. #### Step 4 — Total MRR in mm³/min Let $f$ be the spark frequency (sparks per second). Over 60 seconds: $$ \boxed{MRR = V_{spark} \cdot f \cdot 60 = 60 \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot d_c^{\,2} \cdot h_c \cdot f \;\;\text{(mm}^3\text{/min)}} $$ Or, using the proportional relation: $$ \boxed{MRR \approx K \cdot I \cdot T_{on}^{\,3/2} \cdot f} $$ where $K$ is an empirical constant depending on the workpiece material, electrode material, and dielectric. --- ### Derivation 2: Tool Wear Ratio (TWR) and Electrode Selection Logic #### Step 1 — Definition of TWR By definition: $$ TWR \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Volume of tool material worn away}}{\text{Volume of workpiece material removed}} $$ Equivalently: $$ TWR \;=\; \dfrac{MRR_{tool}}{MRR_{work}} $$ #### Step 2 — Workpiece to Tool Volume Relationship (1 Pulse) If a single pulse removes $V_{spark,w}$ of workpiece and $V_{spark,t}$ of tool: $$ TWR = \dfrac{V_{spark,t}}{V_{spark,w}} $$ #### Step 3 — Why Electrode Material Matters The dominant variables affecting TWR are: - **Boiling point** of electrode vs. workpiece - **Thermal conductivity** of electrode - **Melting point** ratio For the same pulse energy, an electrode material with a **higher melting point** and **higher thermal conductivity** wears *less* per pulse. | Electrode Material | Approx. TWR vs. Steel Workpiece | Best Used For | |---|---|---| | Copper (Cu) | ~2–4% (low wear, high MRR) | Roughing, finishing of dies | | Graphite (C) | ~1–2% (very low wear) | Fine finishing, complex cavities | | Brass (Cu-Zn) | ~20–40% (high wear, but gives low Ra) | Wire EDM (continuous fresh wire) | | Tungsten (W) | ~0.1–1% (extremely low wear) | Micro-EDM, hard alloys | | Copper-Tungsten (CuW) | ~1–3% | High-precision dies, aerospace alloys | > [!NOTE] > **Why does brass wear so much in Wire EDM?** Because fresh wire is continuously fed, the *total* tool wear is irrelevant — the worn section just leaves through the workpiece. The trade-off is that brass gives the best surface finish. #### Step 4 — Pulse Polarity Effect on TWR A counterintuitive but **exam-favorite** result: When the **tool is the cathode (–ve)**, ions (positive) bombarding the tool are heavy → high tool wear. When the **tool is the anode (+ve)**, electrons (light) hit the tool → low tool wear. **Therefore, for low tool wear, the tool is often made the ANODE (+ve)** in finishing operations, even though the **workpiece is conventionally the anode**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Trap:** Do not blindly state "workpiece = anode always." Polarity can be reversed. The correct rule: **For low tool wear, make the tool the anode (+ve)** when using graphite or copper electrodes on steel. This is the famous "reverse polarity finishing" rule. --- ### Analytical Matrix: Process Parameters → Effect on EDM Output | Parameter ↑ (Increase) | Effect on MRR | Effect on Surface Roughness (Ra) | Effect on TWR | Effect on Recast Layer | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Peak Current (I)** | Increases (linear) | Increases (rougher) | Slightly increases | Increases (thicker) | | **Pulse On-Time (T_on)** | Increases (∝ T_on^{3/2}) | Increases (rougher) | Decreases | Thicker | | **Pulse Off-Time (T_off)** | Decreases (less time for sparking) | Slight effect | Decreases | Slight effect | | **Open Circuit Voltage (V_oc)** | Slight increase | Slight increase | Decreases | Slight increase | | **Dielectric Flow Rate** | No direct effect | Improves (better debris removal) | Decreases | Decreases (cleaner cut) | | **Electrode Material Hardness** | — | — | Decreases | — | | **Workpiece Hardness** | Decreases (harder = harder to melt) | — | — | — | --- ### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 3-Mark Style) **Problem:** In an EDM operation, each spark produces a crater of diameter 0.4 mm and depth 0.05 mm. The spark frequency is 5,000 sparks/second. Calculate the MRR in mm³/min. **Solution:** $$ V_{spark} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.4)^{2} \cdot 0.05 $$ $$ V_{spark} = \dfrac{3.1416}{4} \cdot 0.16 \cdot 0.05 $$ $$ V_{spark} = 0.7854 \cdot 0.008 $$ $$ V_{spark} = 6.283 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{mm}^3 \text{ per spark} $$ $$ MRR = V_{spark} \cdot f \cdot 60 $$ $$ MRR = 6.283 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 5000 \cdot 60 $$ $$ \boxed{MRR = 1884.96 \;\text{mm}^3\text{/min} \approx 1885 \;\text{mm}^3\text{/min}} $$ > [!TIP] > **Check dimensionally:** mm³/sparks × sparks/s × s/min = mm³/min. ✓ ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### Diagram 1: Block-Level Functional Architecture of an EDM System The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional interactions** of all major EDM subsystems — power supply, servo control, dielectric circulation, and the spark gap itself. ```mermaid flowchart TB A1[AC Mains Supply
3-Phase 415V] --> B1[DC Power Supply Unit
Rectifier and Filter] B1 --> C1[Pulse Generator Circuit
Controls T_on and T_off] C1 --> D1[Tool Electrode
Cathode - Negative] D1 -->|Spark Gap 0.01-0.05mm| E1[Workpiece
Anode - Positive] E1 --> F1[Servo Control Unit
Monitors Gap Voltage] F1 -->|Feedback Signal| C1 F1 -->|Vertical Feed Command| G1[Z-Axis Servo Motor] G1 --> D1 E1 --> H1[Recast Debris and
Vaporized Particles] H1 --> I1[Dielectric Flushing
Through Tool Hole or Side Nozzle] I1 --> J1[Dielectric Collection Tank] J1 --> K1[Filter Unit
Removes Metal Particles] K1 --> L1[Dielectric Pump] L1 --> M1[Heat Exchanger
Maintains Dielectric Temperature] M1 --> I1 E1 --> N1[Finished Cavity
Mirror Image of Tool] D1 --> O1[Tool Wear
Monitored via TWR] ``` #### Reading the Diagram - **Power Path (left column):** AC mains → DC power supply → Pulse generator → Tool electrode → Spark → Workpiece. - **Control Path (middle column):** The servo control unit *senses* the gap voltage at the workpiece, *compares* it to a setpoint, and drives the Z-axis servo motor to feed the tool downward, maintaining a constant spark gap. - **Dielectric Path (right column):** A closed loop — dielectric flows through the gap, picks up debris, drains into the tank, gets filtered, pumped, and temperature-controlled, then re-injected. - **Output (bottom):** Mirror-image cavity is created on the workpiece. Tool wear is monitored as TWR. --- ### Diagram 2: Process Flow of a Single EDM Pulse Cycle This flowchart shows the four phases of a single EDM pulse cycle (already discussed in Section 2), mapped sequentially. ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Phase 1: Dielectric Ionization
Apply V_oc across gap] --> P2{Voltage exceeds
Breakdown Threshold?} P2 -->|No| P1 P2 -->|Yes| P3[Phase 2: Spark Discharge
Plasma Channel Forms] P3 --> P4[Material Melting and
Vaporization at 8000-12000C] P4 --> P5[Phase 3: Pulse Off
T_off Begins] P5 --> P6[Dielectric Regains
Insulating Property] P6 --> P7[Flushing Removes
Debris from Gap] P7 --> P8[Phase 4: Servo Feeds Tool
Downward to Maintain Gap] P8 --> P1 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examination Tip:** This 4-phase cycle (Ionization → Discharge → Off → Servo Feed) is one of the **most frequently asked** diagram questions in KTU Module 2. Memorize the phase names and what happens in each. --- ### Diagram 3: EDM Process Classification Tree (Context Map) ```mermaid flowchart TB EDM[EDM Family] --> RAM[Ram EDM
Sinker EDM Die EDM] EDM --> WEDM[Wire EDM WEDM] EDM --> MicroEDM[Micro EDM] RAM --> R1[Die Sinking
Cavity Machining] WEDM --> W1[Wire Cut
2D Profile Cutting] MicroEDM --> M1[Micro Hole Drilling
Below 0.2mm] R1 --> R2[Uses Shaped
Graphite Copper Electrode] W1 --> W2[Uses Continuously
Fed Brass Wire 0.1-0.3mm] M1 --> M2[Uses Rotating
Tungsten Electrode] ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Note:** The Module 2 syllabus focuses primarily on **Ram/Sinker EDM** (the conventional type). Wire EDM (WEDM) and Micro-EDM may appear in Module 3 or as comparative 3-mark questions. Know the basic distinctions. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **"Define Electro Discharge Machining (EDM). List any four process parameters that affect the Material Removal Rate (MRR)."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Electro Discharge Machining (EDM) is a non-conventional, electro-thermal material removal process in which material is eroded from a conductive workpiece by a series of controlled, rapidly recurring electrical discharges (sparks) generated between a tool electrode and the workpiece, both immersed in a dielectric fluid, with no physical contact between them. Material removal occurs due to melting and vaporization caused by the high temperature (8,000–12,000 °C) at the spark point. **Four process parameters affecting MRR (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):** 1. **Peak Discharge Current (I):** Higher current increases MRR (approximately linearly). 2. **Pulse On-Time (T_on):** Longer T_on increases MRR (∝ T_on^{3/2}). 3. **Pulse Off-Time (T_off):** Longer T_off *decreases* MRR (less sparking time per second). 4. **Open-Circuit Voltage (V_oc):** Higher V_oc slightly increases MRR. (Other acceptable: spark frequency, dielectric flushing pressure, electrode polarity, dielectric type.) --- #### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **"Explain the role of dielectric fluid in EDM. Name two commonly used dielectric fluids."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Role of Dielectric Fluid (2 marks):** 1. **Electrical Insulator:** Prevents premature discharge; allows voltage to build up across the gap until it reaches the breakdown threshold. 2. **Cooling Medium:** Absorbs the intense heat of the spark and prevents it from spreading to the bulk material. 3. **Debris Flushing:** Carries away the vaporized metal particles (chips) and gas bubbles from the gap, ensuring a clean cut. 4. **Plasma Quenching:** After the pulse, the dielectric rapidly de-ionizes and restores its insulating property, allowing the next spark to form. **Two commonly used dielectric fluids (1 mark — 0.5 each):** 1. EDM oil (hydrocarbon-based) 2. Kerosene (commonly used in wire EDM) 3. (Other: Deionized water — used in micro-EDM and some sinker EDMs) --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern) --- #### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified] **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of the **Ram-type (Sinker) EDM** process. Describe the four phases of a single spark cycle. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **volumetric Material Removal Rate (MRR)** formula in terms of crater dimensions and spark frequency. A specific EDM operation produces a crater of diameter 0.6 mm and depth 0.08 mm at a frequency of 4,000 sparks/sec. Calculate the MRR in mm³/min. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A ##### (a) Working Principle & Spark Cycle (7 marks) **Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to **Diagram 1** in Section 4. The student should draw: - Power supply (DC rectifier) feeding the tool (cathode) and workpiece (anode) - Servo control unit monitoring the gap voltage - Dielectric circulation loop with pump, filter, and tank - Z-axis feed mechanism **Working Principle (3 marks):** The workpiece is mounted on the machine table, and the shaped tool electrode (graphite/copper) is held in the spindle. The workpiece and tool are immersed in a dielectric fluid. A small gap (0.01–0.05 mm) is maintained between them by a servo system. When a high DC voltage is applied, the dielectric ionizes at the gap and a spark is generated. The spark's intense heat (8,000–12,000 °C) melts and vaporizes a small amount of workpiece material, forming a crater. The spark is extinguished by switching off the voltage, and the dielectric flushes away the debris. The tool is then fed slightly closer by the servo, and the cycle repeats. After thousands of sparks, a cavity mirror-image to the tool is produced on the workpiece. **Four Phases of a Single Spark Cycle (2 marks — 0.5 each):** 1. **Ionization Phase:** Voltage is applied; dielectric breaks down when E-field exceeds threshold. 2. **Discharge Phase:** Plasma channel forms; current flows; material melts and vaporizes. 3. **Pulse-Off (Quenching) Phase:** Voltage is removed; plasma collapses; dielectric regains insulating property. 4. **Flushing & Servo Feed Phase:** Dielectric carries away debris; servo feeds the tool down to maintain gap. --- ##### (b) MRR Derivation and Numerical (7 marks) **Derivation (4 marks):** Step 1: Volume of a single crater (hemispherical approximation): $$ V_{spark} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot d_c^{\,2} \cdot h_c $$ Step 2: Number of sparks per minute = $60 \cdot f$ where $f$ is the spark frequency in sparks/sec. Step 3: Total material removed per minute = $V_{spark} \times 60 \times f$. $$ \boxed{MRR = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot d_c^{\,2} \cdot h_c \cdot f \cdot 60 \;\;\text{mm}^3\text{/min}} $$ **Numerical Calculation (3 marks):** Given: $d_c = 0.6$ mm, $h_c = 0.08$ mm, $f = 4000$ sparks/sec. Step 1: Crater volume: $$ V_{spark} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.6)^{2} \cdot 0.08 $$ $$ V_{spark} = 0.7854 \cdot 0.36 \cdot 0.08 $$ $$ V_{spark} = 0.7854 \cdot 0.0288 $$ $$ V_{spark} = 0.02262 \;\text{mm}^3 $$ Step 2: MRR calculation: $$ MRR = 0.02262 \cdot 4000 \cdot 60 $$ $$ \boxed{MRR = 5428.8 \;\text{mm}^3\text{/min}} $$ --- #### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified] **(a)** What is **Tool Wear Ratio (TWR)** in EDM? Discuss the effect of **electrode material** and **polarity** on TWR. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the differences between **Ram EDM** and **Wire EDM** in terms of electrode type, dielectric, flushing mechanism, and typical applications. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B ##### (a) Tool Wear Ratio (TWR) (7 marks) **Definition (2 marks):** Tool Wear Ratio (TWR) is the ratio of the volume of tool material eroded to the volume of workpiece material removed during EDM, usually expressed as a percentage. $$ TWR(\%) = \dfrac{V_{tool\,wear}}{V_{workpiece\,removed}} \times 100 $$ **Effect of Electrode Material (3 marks):** | Electrode | TWR (typical) | Reason | |---|---|---| | Copper | 2–4% | High thermal conductivity; good wear resistance | | Graphite | 1–2% | High melting point; sublimates rather than melts | | Brass | 20–40% | Low melting point; Zn vaporizes easily | | Tungsten | <1% | Highest melting point; excellent for micro-EDM | | Copper-Tungsten | 1–3% | Hybrid — combines wear resistance of W with conductivity of Cu | **Effect of Polarity (2 marks):** - **Normal Polarity (Tool = Cathode, –ve):** Positive ions bombard the tool → high energy transfer → **higher TWR**. Used in roughing where high MRR is desired. - **Reverse Polarity (Tool = Anode, +ve):** Electrons (light) bombard the tool → lower energy transfer → **lower TWR**. Used in finishing for better surface finish and tool life. > **Quantitative rule of thumb:** Reverse polarity can reduce TWR by **5× to 10×** compared to normal polarity when using graphite electrodes on steel. --- ##### (b) Ram EDM vs. Wire EDM (7 marks) | Feature | Ram EDM (Sinker) | Wire EDM (WEDM) | |---|---|---| | **Electrode Type** | Shaped solid electrode (graphite/copper) machined to mirror the desired cavity | Continuously fed thin brass wire (Ø 0.1–0.3 mm) | | **Dielectric** | EDM oil (hydrocarbon), kerosene | Deionized water (with anti-corrosion additives) | | **Flushing** | Through-tool holes, side nozzles, or immersion bath | High-pressure deionized water jets directed at the cut zone | | **Movement** | Tool fed vertically (Z-axis) into stationary workpiece | Wire moves in X-Y plane (taper-cut possible), workpiece stationary | | **Shape Produced** | 3D cavities (mirror image of tool) | 2D profiles (through-cuts); no 3D cavity | | **Typical Applications** | Die sinking, forging dies, plastic mold cavities, intricate 3D shapes | Sharp inside corners, thin slots, micro-features, prototype sheet parts | | **Tool Wear Effect** | Critical — wear changes cavity dimensions over time | Negligible — fresh wire is continuously supplied | | **Surface Finish** | 0.3–3.2 μm Ra | 0.2–1.0 μm Ra (better finish) | **Mark Distribution (1 mark per row, with deeper explanation in 3 rows for 4 marks; balance for introduction).** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Writing "workpiece is always the anode":** WRONG. Polarity is selectable. Read the question carefully. > 2. **Confusing MRR with TWR:** MRR is workpiece material removed per unit time. TWR is the *ratio* of tool wear to workpiece removal. > 3. **Forgetting the factor of 60 in MRR numericals:** Frequency is in sparks/second, but answer must be in mm³/**min**. Multiply by 60. > 4. **Drawing a *physical* diagram of EDM with electrodes touching:** WRONG. There must be a *gap*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if the gap is not shown. > 5. **Writing "spark = arc":** Spark is a *transient, controlled, discrete* discharge. Arc is a *sustained* discharge. EDM uses sparks, not arcs. > 6. **Omitting dielectric functions:** Dielectric serves 4 functions (insulation, cooling, flushing, quenching). Listing only 1–2 will cost marks. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **EDM = Electro-Thermal, NOT Mechanical** material removal process. No cutting force. - [x] **Tool and workpiece NEVER touch.** A gap of 0.01–0.05 mm is maintained. - [x] **Spark temperature = 8,000–12,000 °C** — one of the highest localized temperatures in manufacturing. - [x] **Four phases of spark cycle:** Ionization → Discharge → Quenching (Pulse Off) → Servo Feed. - [x] **Dielectric fluid =** Insulator + Coolant + Flushing medium + Plasma quencher. - [x] **MRR formula:** $MRR = (\pi/4) \cdot d_c^{\,2} \cdot h_c \cdot f \cdot 60$ mm³/min. - [x] **MRR ∝ I · T_on^{3/2}** (empirical, frequently quoted in KTU exams). - [x] **TWR = V_tool / V_workpiece** — graphite has the lowest, brass the highest. - [x] **Reverse polarity (tool = anode) gives lower TWR** but slower MRR. - [x] **Overcut = (D_hole − D_electrode) / 2** — always present, must be accounted for in tool design. - [x] **Recast layer** (white, brittle, heat-affected zone) is an unwanted side-effect on every EDM surface. - [x] **EDM can machine ANY conductive material** regardless of hardness (e.g., tungsten carbide, Inconel, titanium). - [x] **Types of EDM:** Ram/Sinker (3D cavities), Wire (2D profiles), Micro-EDM (sub-mm features). - [x] **Wire EDM** uses continuously-fed brass wire + deionized water. - [x] **For numerical problems:** remember to multiply by 60 to convert sparks/sec → sparks/min. - [x] **Open-circuit voltage = 50–400 V**, Peak current = 0.5–500 A, Pulse on-time = 1–2000 μs. - [x] **Surface roughness ∝ I^a · T_on^b** (a ≈ 1, b ≈ 0.3–0.5). - [x] **Specific energy (J/mm³) indicates process efficiency** — lower is better. - [x] **Common KTU answer trap:** Spelling EDM as "EDM machine" without describing the process principle. - [x] **EDM does NOT use mechanical energy** — distinguish clearly from ECM (Electro-Chemical Machining), which uses chemical dissolution. - [x] **Dielectric breakdown strength = 10⁶–10⁷ V/m** — the threshold for spark initiation. - [x] **Servo control maintains gap** by sensing the gap voltage and feeding the Z-axis motor accordingly. - [x] **Final cavity = mirror image of tool shape**, so the tool must be machined (often by CNC) to the negative of the desired cavity (with a compensation factor for overcut). - [x] **For roughing:** use high I, long T_on → high MRR, rough surface. - [x] **For finishing:** use low I, short T_on → low MRR, smooth surface, low TWR. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Mantra for EDM:** "EDM = **C**ontrolled **S**parks in a **D**ielectric, with **N**o **C**ontact, giving **H**igh **T**emperature **M**aterial **R**emoval." Memorize the 4 phases, the 4 dielectric functions, the MRR formula, and the polarity rule. These four pillars cover 90% of KTU Module 2 EDM questions.
