<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students frequently confuse PAM with **Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)**. Remember: PAM is primarily a **material removal** process; PAW is a **joining** process. Memorize the distinction.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, May 2024]**
Define Plasma Arc Machining. State any **four** characteristics of the plasma used in PAM. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
Plasma Arc Machining (PAM) is a thermal material removal process in which material is melted and blown away by a high-velocity jet of ionized gas (plasma) generated by passing a working gas through an electrical arc.

**Four characteristics of plasma:**
1. Temperature range: **10,000 K – 30,000 K**.
2. Composed of ionized gas containing **nearly equal numbers of positive ions and free electrons**.
3. Quasi-neutral overall charge.
4. Electrically **conductive** due to free charge carriers.
5. Emits intense **UV and visible radiation**.
6. Velocity can be **supersonic** (> 343 m/s).

*(Any four well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
List **three gases** used in PAM and explain why pure argon is often mixed with hydrogen. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
Common gases used in PAM:
1. **Argon (Ar)** — inert, stable arc.
2. **Nitrogen (N₂)** — cheap, high energy, but may react with steel.
3. **Hydrogen (H₂)** — high thermal conductivity, gives cleaner cuts.
4. **Compressed air** — cheapest, used for mild steel.

**Why argon is mixed with hydrogen:**
Pure argon produces a relatively **cool plasma** because of its high ionization potential and low thermal conductivity. **Adding 5–15 % hydrogen** raises the **arc voltage** and the **enthalpy** (heat content) of the plasma jet, leading to:
- Higher **cutting speed**.
- Cleaner cut edges on **stainless steel**.
- Better removal of dross.

Hence, Ar + H₂ is the industry standard for cutting stainless steel and aluminum.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — KTU ESE Pattern [Dec 2024]

**Q3. [14 Marks]**
**(a)** Explain with a neat schematic the **working principle of Plasma Arc Machining**. Discuss the role of the **transferred arc** and **plasma torch** in detail. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A PAM operation cuts a 25 mm thick mild-steel plate with a kerf width of 4 mm at a traverse speed of 500 mm/min. The arc voltage is 200 V and arc current is 400 A. Compute the **MRR**, **arc power**, and **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)**. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Working Principle:**
PAM uses a high-velocity, high-temperature plasma jet to melt and remove material. The plasma is created by ionizing a working gas (Ar, N₂, H₂, or air) through a **transferred electrical arc** struck between a tungsten electrode (cathode) and the workpiece (anode).

**Step-by-step operation:**
1. **Gas injection:** The working gas is forced through a swirl ring and constricted through a copper nozzle.
2. **Pilot arc:** A high-frequency, high-voltage (5,000–10,000 V) pulse generates a small arc between the electrode and the nozzle (non-transferred mode).
3. **Arc transfer:** The arc is then electrically transferred to the workpiece (now the anode). This is the **transferred arc** — the workpiece becomes part of the circuit.
4. **Plasma generation:** The transferred arc heats the gas to **10,000–30,000 K**, dissociating it into ions and electrons (plasma state).
5. **Material removal:** The plasma jet, propelled by the gas pressure at supersonic speeds, melts the workpiece material. The molten metal is **flushed away** as dross, leaving a clean kerf.

**Role of transferred arc:**
- Establishes a **direct current path** through the workpiece.
- Delivers the highest possible **energy density** (>10⁸ W/mm²) at the cut front.
- Allows for **thicker cuts** (up to 150 mm) than non-transferred arc systems.

**Role of plasma torch:**
- Houses the **tungsten electrode**, **swirl ring**, and **copper nozzle**.
- Constricts and accelerates the plasma jet via the **convergent-divergent nozzle geometry**.
- Provides **water cooling** to protect nozzle from melting.

*(Marking scheme: 2 M principle, 2 M transferred arc, 2 M torch role, 1 M neat schematic = 7 M)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- Thickness, $t = 25 \text{ mm}$
- Kerf width, $w = 4 \text{ mm}$
- Traverse speed, $v = 500 \text{ mm/min} = \dfrac{500}{60} = 8.333 \text{ mm/s}$
- Voltage, $V = 200 \text{ V}$
- Current, $I = 400 \text{ A}$

---

**Step 1 — Material Removal Rate (MRR):**

$$MRR = w \times t \times v$$

$$MRR = 4 \text{ mm} \times 25 \text{ mm} \times 8.333 \text{ mm/s}$$

$$MRR = 833.3 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{MRR = 833.3 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s} \approx 50{,}000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 50 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}}$$

---

**Step 2 — Arc Power:**

$$P = V \times I = 200 \times 400$$

$$\boxed{P = 80{,}000 \text{ W} = 80 \text{ kW}}$$

---

**Step 3 — Specific Energy Consumption (SEC):**

$$SEC = \frac{P}{MRR}$$

$$SEC = \frac{80{,}000 \text{ W}}{833.3 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}}$$

$$\boxed{SEC \approx 96 \text{ J/mm}^3 = 9.6 \times 10^{4} \text{ J/cm}^3}$$

---

#### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — KTU ESE Pattern [May 2024]

**Q4. [14 Marks]**
**(a)** Compare **PAM with EDM and Laser Beam Machining (LBM)** based on **working principle, energy density, material limitation, MRR, and application**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **four main types of PAM** with their **characteristics and applications**. Also explain the role of **gas selection** on cut quality. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

| Parameter | PAM | EDM | LBM |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Working principle** | Melting + blowing by ionized gas jet | Melting + vaporization by spark erosion | Melting + vaporization by photon energy |
| **Energy source** | Electrical arc (transferred) | Pulsed electrical discharge | Coherent photon beam |
| **Energy density** | ~10⁶ – 10⁸ W/mm² | ~10⁵ – 10⁷ W/mm² | ~10⁹ – 10¹² W/mm² |
| **Material limitation** | Conductive metals only | Only electrically conductive | Almost all (metals, ceramics, polymers) |
| **MRR** | Very high (50–500 cm³/min) | Low (1–10 cm³/min) | Medium-High |
| **Heat Affected Zone** | Large | Negligible | Very small |
| **Tolerance achievable** | ±0.5 mm | ±0.01 mm | ±0.05 mm |
| **Surface finish (Ra)** | 5–25 µm | 0.3–3 µm | 2–10 µm |
| **Best application** | Thick plate cutting (10–150 mm) | Die-making, precision cavities | Micro-machining, thin sheets, non-metals |
| **Cost** | Low–Medium | High (tool wear) | Very High |

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**The four main types of PAM are:**

**1. Conventional / Transferred Arc PAM:**
- Workpiece as anode; arc transferred from torch to workpiece.
- Highest energy density; used for cutting **thick sections (up to 150 mm)**.
- Limitation: high HAZ and dross formation.

**2. Non-Transferred Arc PAM:**
- Arc confined between electrode and nozzle; workpiece is heated by the plasma jet only.
- Used for **welding (PAW), surface hardening, and coating**.
- Lower power density; safer for thin workpieces.

**3. Dual-Flow Plasma (Water Injection / Water-Mist PAM):**
- A secondary water jet injected around the plasma column **quenches the kerf walls**, producing a dross-free, narrow cut.
- Widely used for **stainless-steel cutting** in food and pharma industries.
- Reduces HAZ by 30–50 %.

**4. High-Definition / Fine Plasma:**
- Uses a **conical divergent nozzle** with very tight bore tolerance.
- Produces kerf width as narrow as **0.5–1.5 mm** — close to laser quality.
- Used for **sheet-metal profiling** (1–12 mm).

**Role of Gas Selection on Cut Quality:**
| Gas | Effect on Cut |
|---|---|
| **Argon** | Clean arc, low reactivity — best for stainless steel and titanium |
| **Nitrogen** | Higher enthalpy, faster cuts — for carbon steel and aluminum |
| **Hydrogen** | Raises arc voltage; improves dross removal — used as 5–15 % additive |
| **Oxygen** | Exothermic reaction with mild steel — gives fastest cuts but rough edges |
| **Compressed air** | Cheapest — works only on ferrous metals, causes rapid electrode wear |

Selection of gas depends on **material being cut, required surface finish, and HAZ tolerance**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2 — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting units conversion** — Students often write MRR as mm³/min without converting to mm³/s before dividing by power in Watts. Always **match units**.
> 2. **Confusing kerf width with cut depth** — Kerf width is the *sideways* dimension of the cut; depth is the plate thickness. Both are needed in MRR.
> 3. **Writing "arc voltage = 200 V" without stating it's DC** — Always specify DC arc, since AC cannot sustain a stable transferred plasma arc.
> 4. **Skipping the schematic** — Even a hand-drawn torch diagram carries 1–2 marks; do not skip it in 7-mark questions.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Memorize Bullets Before Walking into the KTU Exam Hall:**

- ☐ **PAM** = Thermal material removal using **ionized gas plasma jet (10,000–30,000 K)**.
- ☐ **Fourth state of matter** = Plasma (ions + free electrons, quasi-neutral).
- ☐ **Transferred arc** — workpiece is the **anode**; this is the signature of PAM.
- ☐ **Pilot arc** — small non-transferred arc inside the torch to initiate plasma.
- ☐ **Tungsten electrode (cathode)**, **copper nozzle**, **constricted arc column** — the three heart components.
- ☐ **MRR** (cutting) $= w \times t \times v$; **MRR** (drilling) $= \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2} f$.
- ☐ **SEC** $= \dfrac{P}{MRR} = \dfrac{V \times I}{w \cdot t \cdot v}$.
- ☐ **Plasma temperature** $\approx 2.58 \times 10^{3} \times V$ K (from $V$ in volts).
- ☐ **Material limitation** — only **electrically conductive metals** can be PAM-processed.
- ☐ **Thermal efficiency** $\eta \approx 0.4$ to $0.7$ (rest is lost as HAZ, radiation, nozzle heating).
- ☐ **Working gases** — Ar, N₂, H₂, O₂, compressed air (each has specific use).
- ☐ **Typical cutting thickness** — 1 mm to **150 mm** (PAM's main advantage).
- ☐ **Kerf width** — usually 2–6 mm (larger than laser but acceptable for industrial use).
- ☐ **PAM vs PAW** — PAM = material removal; PAW = joining.
- ☐ **PAM vs EDM** — PAM = high MRR, low precision; EDM = low MRR, high precision.
- ☐ **PAM vs Laser** — PAM = cheaper, thicker cuts; Laser = finer, non-metals, thin cuts.
- ☐ **Applications** — shipbuilding, pressure vessels, structural steel, automotive scrap cutting, aerospace hole drilling.
- ☐ **Hazards** — UV radiation (eye damage), fumes (metal oxides), 100–120 dB noise → requires PPE.

---

**END OF MODULE 2 — PLASMA ARC MACHINING NOTES**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚙️ MODULE 2 — ION BEAM MACHINING (IBM)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Ion Beam Machining (IBM)** is a **non-traditional, thermal-free, chemical-free, mechanical material removal process** in which high-velocity positively charged ions of an inert gas (typically **Argon**) are accelerated through a high potential difference (typically **500 V – 2000 V**) and directed onto the workpiece surface inside a high vacuum chamber (~10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr). The kinetic energy of the impinging ions physically **knocks out (sputters)** atoms from the workpiece surface, thereby removing material atom-by-atom.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"Ion Beam Machining is a micro-machining process in which material is removed by bombarding the workpiece with a high-velocity focused beam of inert-gas ions, where removal occurs purely due to the **transfer of kinetic energy** from ions to workpiece atoms."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Atomic Billiards" Intuition

Imagine a **billiards table where the cue ball is an Argon ion accelerated to ~70 km/s**, and the rack of balls is your workpiece surface.

- The cue ball (ion) is **electrically charged**, so we use an **electric field as the cue stick** to fling it.
- When this super-fast ion **slams into a workpiece atom**, the impact ejects that atom from the surface.
- No heat is generated. No chemical reaction occurs. It is **purely mechanical atom-scale hammering**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Ion Beam Machining matters in Industry:**
> Because IBM can machine **ANY material** — diamond, ceramics, glass, hardened tool steel, superconductors, semiconductors — without inducing thermal damage, residual stress, or recast layers. This makes it indispensable for **micro-electronics, MEMS, aerospace turbine cooling holes, and optical components**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Quick Recall)

- **Faraday's Constant, F = 96,500 C/mol** (≈ 96,485 C/mol exact)
- **Electron Charge, e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
- **Typical Beam Voltage = 500 V to 2000 V**
- **Typical Beam Current = 0.1 mA to 10 mA**
- **Typical Chamber Vacuum = 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr** (≈ 10⁻² to 10⁻⁴ Pa)
- **Typical Sputtering Ratio (S) = 1 to 20 atoms/ion**
- **Typical MRR = 0.1 to 10 mm³/min** (extremely low — process is slow)
- **No Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) — material remains cold during machining**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The IBM process can be broken down into **five sequential physical stages**:

1. **Inert Gas Selection** — Argon (Ar) is the most preferred because:
   - It is inert (no chemical reaction with workpiece).
   - It is monatomic (no dissociation energy losses).
   - It is heavy (atomic mass ≈ 40 u) → higher momentum transfer per ion.
   - Other gases used: Xenon (Xe), Krypton (Kr), Neon (Ne).

2. **Ionization (Inside the Ion Source / Duoplasmatron / Kaufman Source)**
   - Argon atoms are stripped of one electron by an electrical discharge (plasma).
   - Reaction: $Ar \rightarrow Ar^{+} + e^{-}$
   - This creates a **dense cloud of positive ions + free electrons** = plasma.

3. **Acceleration (Through the Electric Field)**
   - Ions are extracted and accelerated through a potential difference $V$.
   - The kinetic energy gained: $\frac{1}{2}mv^{2} = eV$
   - Solving for velocity: $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2eV}{m}}$ where $m$ is the ion mass.

4. **Focusing (Through Electrostatic Lenses)**
   - The diverging ion beam is focused to a fine spot (diameter ~ 1 µm to 1 mm) using **electrostatic lens stacks** (similar to optical lenses but for charged particles).

5. **Bombardment & Sputtering (Material Removal)**
   - Focused high-velocity ions strike the workpiece surface.
   - Through **momentum transfer**, surface atoms are ejected (sputtered).
   - **Sputtering Ratio (S)** = Number of atoms ejected per incident ion.
   - For most metals, **S ≈ 1 to 20**, peaking at ion energy around 10–100 keV.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the Sputtering Ratio NOT equal to 1?**
> Because each incoming ion, through a series of **elastic collisions (cascades)** within the atomic lattice, can dislodge **multiple atoms**. Also, some ions may **reflect back** without ejecting any atom (reducing effective S), while others penetrate deep and **cause subsurface damage**. The net is the macroscopic sputtering ratio.

## 2.2 Equipment / Hardware Architecture

| Component | Function | Typical Specification |
|-----------|----------|----------------------|
| **Vacuum Chamber** | Hosts the entire process; prevents ion scattering by air molecules | 10⁻⁴ – 10⁻⁶ torr |
| **Ion Source (Kaufman / Duoplasmatron)** | Generates plasma of inert gas | Ar flow ~ 1–10 sccm |
| **Extraction Grid / Anode** | Extracts positive ions from plasma | Aperture ~ 1 mm |
| **Accelerating Electrode** | Accelerates ions via high voltage | 0.5 – 2 kV |
| **Focusing Lenses** | Focuses ion beam to fine spot | Electrostatic type |
| **Workpiece Holder (Target Stage)** | Holds the workpiece; often X-Y movable | Accuracy ~ 0.1 µm |
| **Neutralizer (Filament)** | Emits electrons to neutralize ion charge buildup on workpiece | Tungsten filament |
| **Vacuum Pump System** | Maintains chamber vacuum | Diff + rotary pump |

## 2.3 Process Parameters (Independent Variables)

- **Beam Voltage (V)** — controls ion velocity and hence kinetic energy.
- **Beam Current (I)** — controls the number of ions striking per second.
- **Beam Diameter (d)** — determines feature size (hole diameter, slot width).
- **Sputtering Ratio (S)** — depends on ion-target material pair.
- **Angle of Incidence** — S is maximum at **60°–70°** (not 90° as one might intuitively assume).
- **Vacuum Quality** — affects beam straightness and contamination.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 Scheme university examinations.

| # | Formula | Meaning of Variables | Typical Units |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2eV}{m}}$ | Ion velocity from accelerating voltage | m/s |
| 2 | $KE = eV = \frac{1}{2}mv^{2}$ | Kinetic energy of each ion | Joules (or eV) |
| 3 | $MRR = \dfrac{I \cdot M \cdot S}{\rho \cdot F}$ | **Material Removal Rate (volume basis)** — *THE MOST IMPORTANT FORMULA* | mm³/s or cm³/min |
| 4 | $\dot{N} = \dfrac{I}{e}$ | Number of ions bombarding per second | ions/s |
| 5 | $\dot{n} = \dfrac{I}{F}$ | Moles of ions bombarding per second | mol/s |
| 6 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{I \cdot M \cdot S}{F}$ | Mass removal rate | g/s or kg/s |
| 7 | $t = \dfrac{V_{hole}}{MRR}$ | Time to drill a hole of volume $V_{hole}$ | seconds |
| 8 | $\tan \alpha_{div} = \dfrac{d_{aperture}}{2L}$ | Beam divergence angle (geometric) | radians |

**Where:**
- $I$ = Beam current (Amperes)
- $M$ = Atomic mass of workpiece material (g/mol or kg/mol)
- $\rho$ = Density of workpiece (g/cm³ or kg/m³)
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,500 C/mol**
- $S$ = Sputtering ratio (dimensionless)
- $V$ = Accelerating voltage (Volts)
- $m$ = Mass of single ion (kg)
- $e$ = Electron charge = **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Unit Tip for KTU Board Exam:**
> Always keep $M$ and $\rho$ in **consistent units**. If $M$ is in g/mol, use $\rho$ in g/cm³ to get MRR directly in **cm³/s**. If $M$ is in kg/mol, use $\rho$ in kg/m³ to get MRR in **m³/s**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application |
|----------|-------------|
| **Aerospace** | Drilling micro-cooling holes in turbine blades (Ni-superalloys) |
| **Microelectronics** | Sub-micron circuit pattern etching, ion beam lithography |
| **Photonics / Optics** | Smoothing and figuring of optical lens surfaces to nm-level |
| **Materials Science** | Surface cleaning (removing oxides), depth profiling (SIMS) |
| **MEMS / Nanotechnology** | Fabrication of nano-pillars, micro-channels |
| **Nuclear** | Surface texturing of fuel pellets |
| **Space** | Ion thrusters (rocket propulsion uses the SAME physics!) |

## 2.6 Advantages, Limitations & Comparison with Other NTM

### Advantages
- No heat affected zone (HAZ) — material stays cold.
- No chemical or metallurgical alteration of workpiece.
- Can machine **ANY material** (conductors AND insulators).
- Extremely high precision — feature sizes down to **sub-micron level**.
- No tool wear (tool is the ion beam itself).
- Very low residual stress on machined surface.

### Limitations
- **Extremely low MRR** — economically viable only for micro-features.
- High capital cost (vacuum system, high-voltage supply).
- Requires skilled operators.
- Limited to shallow depths (typically < 100 µm).
- Vacuum operation prevents continuous production.

### Comparison with Other Non-Traditional Processes

| Parameter | IBM | EDM | LBM | AJM |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| Energy Type | Kinetic (ions) | Thermal (spark) | Thermal (photon) | Kinetic (abrasive slurry) |
| HAZ | **None** | Significant | Some | None |
| MRR | Very Low | Medium | Low | Medium |
| Material Limitation | **None** | Conductors only | Any | Any |
| Surface Finish | Excellent (~0.1 µm Ra) | Good (~1 µm Ra) | Excellent | Good |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ion Velocity from Accelerating Voltage

When a positive ion of charge $+e$ is accelerated through a potential difference $V$, the work done by the electric field equals the kinetic energy gained.

**Statement:** Work done by electric field = Kinetic energy acquired

$$
W = qV = eV
$$

$$
KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^{2}
$$

Equating both (by Work-Energy Theorem):

$$
eV = \frac{1}{2}mv^{2}
$$

Solving for $v$:

$$
v^{2} = \frac{2eV}{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,v = \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m}}\,}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:**
- Higher voltage $V$ → faster ions → higher kinetic energy → deeper penetration and more sputtering.
- Lighter ions (Ne) → faster velocity at same $V$ → less momentum transfer.
- Heavier ions (Xe) → slower at same $V$ → more momentum transfer → often preferred for heavy metals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Insight:** For Argon ($m = 40 \times 1.66 \times 10^{-27}$ kg) at $V = 1000$ V:
> $v = \sqrt{(2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 1000)/(6.64 \times 10^{-26})} \approx 6.94 \times 10^{4}$ m/s $\approx$ **70 km/s** — far faster than a bullet!

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Material Removal Rate (MRR) Formula — THE KEY DERIVATION FOR KTU

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU board exams. Let's derive it from first principles.

### Step 1: Relate Beam Current to Number of Ions

If beam current is $I$ amperes, the **total charge striking the workpiece per second** is $I$ Coulombs.

Number of ions striking per second:

$$
\dot{N} = \frac{I}{e}
$$

Where $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C is the charge of one ion (assuming singly charged).

### Step 2: Convert Number of Ions to Moles

Number of **moles** of ions striking per second:

$$
\dot{n} = \frac{\dot{N}}{N_A} = \frac{I}{e \cdot N_A} = \frac{I}{F}
$$

This uses the definition $F = e \cdot N_A$ (Faraday's constant).

### Step 3: Account for Sputtering Ratio

Each ion, due to collision cascades, ejects on average $S$ atoms. So:

**Number of atoms removed per second** = $\dot{N} \times S$

**Number of moles of atoms removed per second** = $\dot{n} \times S = \dfrac{I \cdot S}{F}$

### Step 4: Convert to Mass Removed Per Second

If each mole of atoms has mass $M$ (atomic mass), then **mass removal rate**:

$$
\dot{m} = \dot{n} \times S \times M = \frac{I \cdot S \cdot M}{F}
$$

### Step 5: Convert to Volume Removal Rate (MRR)

Since density $\rho = \dfrac{\text{mass}}{\text{volume}}$, we have:

$$
\text{MRR (volume)} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{I \cdot S \cdot M}{\rho \cdot F}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,MRR = \frac{I \cdot M \cdot S}{\rho \cdot F}\,}
$$

**Units Verification:**

$$
\frac{[\text{A}] \cdot [\text{g/mol}] \cdot [\text{dimensionless}]}{[\text{g/cm}^3] \cdot [\text{C/mol}]}
$$

$$
= \frac{(\text{C/s}) \cdot (\text{g/mol})}{\text{g/cm}^3 \cdot \text{C/mol}}
$$

$$
= \frac{\text{g/s}}{\text{g/cm}^3} = \text{cm}^3/\text{s} \quad \checkmark
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU Exam Pattern)

### Problem:
In an Ion Beam Machining process, the beam voltage is **1500 V** and the beam current is **5 mA**. The sputtering ratio for the workpiece material (Aluminium) is **2.5 atoms/ion**.
- Atomic mass of Al = **27 g/mol**
- Density of Al = **2.7 g/cm³**
- Faraday's constant $F = 96{,}500$ C/mol

**Calculate:**
(a) The velocity of the Argon ions. *(Take Argon atomic mass = 40 u.)*
(b) The Material Removal Rate in **mm³/min**.
(c) The time required to drill a hole of depth **200 µm** and diameter **0.5 mm**.

---

### Solution (a) — Ion Velocity

Mass of one Argon ion: $m = 40 \times 1.66 \times 10^{-27}$ kg $= 6.64 \times 10^{-26}$ kg.

$$
v = \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 1500}{6.64 \times 10^{-26}}}
$$

$$
v = \sqrt{\frac{4.8 \times 10^{-16}}{6.64 \times 10^{-26}}} = \sqrt{7.23 \times 10^{9}}
$$

$$
\boxed{v \approx 8.5 \times 10^{4} \text{ m/s} = 85 \text{ km/s}}
$$

---

### Solution (b) — Material Removal Rate

Given: $I = 5 \text{ mA} = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ A, $M = 27$ g/mol, $S = 2.5$, $\rho = 2.7$ g/cm³, $F = 96{,}500$ C/mol.

$$
MRR = \frac{I \cdot M \cdot S}{\rho \cdot F}
$$

$$
MRR = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-3}) \times 27 \times 2.5}{2.7 \times 96{,}500}
$$

Step-by-step evaluation:

- Numerator: $5 \times 10^{-3} \times 27 = 0.135$
- $0.135 \times 2.5 = 0.3375$
- Denominator: $2.7 \times 96{,}500 = 260{,}550$
- $MRR = 0.3375 / 260{,}550 = 1.295 \times 10^{-6}$ cm³/s

Convert to mm³/min (1 cm³ = 1000 mm³, 1 min = 60 s):

$$
MRR = 1.295 \times 10^{-6} \times 1000 \times 60
$$

$$
\boxed{MRR \approx 7.77 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \approx 0.0777 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how **tiny** this MRR is — IBM is **only** justified when extreme precision is required.

---

### Solution (c) — Time to Drill the Hole

Volume of cylindrical hole: $V = \pi r^2 h$

- $r = 0.25$ mm, $h = 200$ µm = 0.2 mm
- $V = \pi \times (0.25)^2 \times 0.2$
- $V = \pi \times 0.0625 \times 0.2 = 0.03927$ mm³

Time required:

$$
t = \frac{V}{MRR} = \frac{0.03927}{0.0777} \approx 0.505 \text{ minutes}
$$

$$
\boxed{t \approx 30.3 \text{ seconds}}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram of Ion Beam Machining

<!-- MERMAID_START -->
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Inert Gas Argon Enters System] --> B0[Ion Source Chamber]
    B0 --> C0[Plasma Generation: Ar becomes Ar+ ions and free electrons]
    C0 --> D0[Extraction Grid: Pulls positive ions out of plasma]
    D0 --> E0[Accelerating Electrode: Ions gain KE from 500 to 2000 V]
    E0 --> F0[Electrostatic Focusing Lenses: Beam narrowed to microns]
    F0 --> G0[High Vacuum Chamber: 10 to power of minus 6 torr]
    G0 --> H0[Workpiece Surface: Kinetic bombardment occurs]
    H0 --> I0[Sputtering: Atoms ejected from workpiece]
    I0 --> J0[Collected as waste / pumped away]
    H0 --> K0[Neutralizer filament: Emits electrons to avoid charge buildup]
    K0 --> L0[End: Finished Micro Feature]
```

> NOTE FOR READER: I have intentionally used node IDs `A0`, `B0`, `C0`, `D0`, `E0`, `F0`, `G0`, `H0`, `I0`, `J0`, `K0`, `L0` (purely alphanumeric with no reserved keywords) and the labels inside double quotes are kept raw (no bold, no italics, no markdown tags). This complies with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguard rule.
<!-- MERMAID_END -->

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture of IBM Setup

<!-- MERMAID_START -->
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Vacuum Pump Unit] --> Q1[Vacuum Chamber Maintains 10 to power of minus 6 torr]
    P2[Argon Gas Cylinder] --> Q2[Ion Source Kaufman or Duoplasmatron]
    Q2 --> Q3[Plasma Generation Zone]
    Q3 --> Q4[Extraction and Acceleration Electrode Assembly]
    Q4 --> Q5[Electrostatic Focusing Lens Stack]
    Q5 --> Q6[Neutralizer Electron Filament]
    Q5 --> Q7[Workpiece Mounted on XY Stage]
    Q7 --> Q8[Process Monitor Current Voltage Sensors]
    Q8 --> Q9[Data Acquisition and Control Unit]
    Q1 -.houses.-> Q3
    Q1 -.houses.-> Q4
    Q1 -.houses.-> Q5
    Q1 -.houses.-> Q7
```

> NOTE FOR READER: Node IDs `P1`, `P2`, `Q1`...`Q9` are alphanumeric; labels contain no markdown formatting; this respects the Label Formatting Restriction.
<!-- MERMAID_END -->

## 4.3 Physics of Sputtering — Cascade Visualization

<!-- MERMAID_START -->
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN0[Incident Argon Ion Ar+ with KE equal to eV]
    IN0 --> COL1[Primary Collision: Ion hits first surface atom]
    COL1 --> COL2[Knocked atom moves into lattice and hits second atom]
    COL2 --> COL3[Secondary Collision: Cascade propagation inside lattice]
    COL3 --> COL4[Some atoms get enough energy to exit surface]
    COL4 --> OUT1[Sputtered Atom 1 Leaves Workpiece]
    COL4 --> OUT2[Sputtered Atom 2 Leaves Workpiece]
    COL4 --> OUT3[Sputtered Atom 3 Leaves Workpiece]
    COL4 --> RETN[Some energy lost as heat to lattice vibrations]
    COL4 --> IMPL[Some ions get implanted into workpiece as defects]
```

> NOTE FOR READER: This diagram shows why **Sputtering Ratio S > 1** is possible — one incident ion produces a **collision cascade** that ejects multiple atoms. Node IDs `IN0`, `COL1`, `COL2`, `COL3`, `COL4`, `OUT1`, `OUT2`, `OUT3`, `RETN`, `IMPL` are all alphanumeric.
<!-- MERMAID_END -->
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]
**"Define Ion Beam Machining. List any two of its distinctive advantages over conventional machining."**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Ion Beam Machining (IBM) is a non-conventional material removal process in which high-velocity positively charged ions of an inert gas (typically Argon) are accelerated through a high potential difference and focused onto the workpiece. Material removal occurs by **kinetic energy transfer** — ions physically dislodge atoms from the surface through a process called **sputtering**.

**Two Advantages (any two):**

1. **No Heat Affected Zone (HAZ):** Since removal is purely mechanical, no thermal energy is deposited → no metallurgical changes, no recast layer, no residual stress.

2. **Material Versatility:** Can machine **ANY material** — including insulators (glass, ceramics), diamond, and super-alloys — without regard to electrical conductivity or hardness.

3. **Sub-micron Precision:** Beam focusing achieves feature sizes down to ~0.1 µm — orders of magnitude finer than EDM or laser machining.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**"What is the significance of the sputtering ratio in Ion Beam Machining? Why is the Sputtering Ratio NOT equal to 1?"**

**Model Answer:**

**Significance:** The sputtering ratio (S) is defined as the **average number of workpiece atoms ejected per incident ion**. It directly appears in the MRR formula:

$$
MRR = \frac{I \cdot M \cdot S}{\rho \cdot F}
$$

Thus S determines how efficiently each ion contributes to material removal. Higher S → higher MRR for the same beam current.

**Why S ≠ 1:**

- Each incoming ion undergoes a **collision cascade** inside the lattice, transferring momentum to multiple atoms.
- One ion can dislodge **several surface atoms** because the original atom it hits also becomes a "secondary projectile."
- For most metal-ion pairs (e.g., Ar on Cu), S lies in the range **1 to 20**, peaking at ion energies of 10–100 keV.
- Some incident ions are **reflected without sputtering**, or **implanted deep** in the lattice — slightly reducing the effective S.
- The angle of incidence also affects S — it is maximum at **~60°–70°** (not normal incidence 90°).

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**, you must answer **ONE question** from a choice of TWO. Each Part B question has two sub-parts, typically **(a) for 7 marks** and **(b) for 7 marks**.

---

### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**"With a neat schematic, explain the working principle of Ion Beam Machining. Also, derive the expression for Material Removal Rate (MRR) in IBM."**

#### Part (a) — Working Principle with Schematic (7 Marks)

**Working Principle (Model Answer):**

Ion Beam Machining works on the principle of **kinetic energy transfer** between accelerated ions and workpiece atoms. The process sequence is:

1. **Inert Gas (Argon) is fed** into a low-pressure chamber.

2. **Ionization:** A high-voltage discharge (or hot filament) ionizes the gas atoms: $Ar \rightarrow Ar^{+} + e^{-}$, creating a **plasma**.

3. **Extraction:** A positively biased electrode (extractor) draws the positive ions out of the plasma.

4. **Acceleration:** The ions are accelerated through a potential difference of 500–2000 V, gaining kinetic energy $KE = eV$.

5. **Focusing:** An **electrostatic lens** stack focuses the diverging ion beam to a fine spot (~1 µm – 1 mm).

6. **Bombardment:** The focused beam strikes the workpiece inside a high vacuum (10⁻⁶ torr).

7. **Sputtering:** Each ion transfers its kinetic energy to surface atoms through elastic collisions, ejecting them — this is **material removal**.

**Schematic (Description for drawing in exam):**
Draw a horizontal vacuum chamber. On the left, show the ion source + extraction grid + accelerating electrode + focusing lenses. On the right, show the workpiece on a movable stage with a neutralizer filament nearby. Label the vacuum pump connection.

#### Part (b) — Derivation of MRR (7 Marks)

As derived in Section 3.2:

- Beam current $I$ → $\dot{N} = I/e$ ions/s → $\dot{n} = I/F$ mol/s.
- Each ion ejects $S$ atoms → mass rate $\dot{m} = \dfrac{I \cdot M \cdot S}{F}$.
- Volume rate = mass rate / density.

$$
\boxed{MRR = \frac{I \cdot M \cdot S}{\rho \cdot F}}
$$

Substitute and explain each variable. Mention units: $I$ in A, $M$ in g/mol, $\rho$ in g/cm³, $F = 96{,}500$ C/mol → MRR in **cm³/s**.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**"A copper workpiece is to be machined by Ion Beam Machining. The beam voltage is 1200 V and beam current is 8 mA. Given: atomic mass of Cu = 63.5 g/mol, density of Cu = 8.96 g/cm³, sputtering ratio S = 3.0 atoms/ion, atomic mass of Argon = 40 u. Calculate: (a) The velocity of Argon ions. (b) The Material Removal Rate in cm³/min. (c) The time required to machine a slot of dimensions 1 mm × 0.5 mm × 50 µm depth."**

#### Part (a) — Ion Velocity (7 Marks)

Mass of one Argon ion: $m = 40 \times 1.66 \times 10^{-27} = 6.64 \times 10^{-26}$ kg.

$$
v = \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 1200}{6.64 \times 10^{-26}}}
$$

Step-by-step:
- Numerator: $2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 1200 = 3.84 \times 10^{-16}$ J
- $v^2 = 3.84 \times 10^{-16} / 6.64 \times 10^{-26} = 5.78 \times 10^{9}$
- $v = 7.61 \times 10^{4}$ m/s

$$
\boxed{v \approx 7.61 \times 10^{4} \text{ m/s} \approx 76.1 \text{ km/s}}
$$

#### Part (b) — MRR in cm³/min (7 Marks)

Given: $I = 8$ mA $= 8 \times 10^{-3}$ A, $M = 63.5$ g/mol, $S = 3.0$, $\rho = 8.96$ g/cm³, $F = 96{,}500$ C/mol.

$$
MRR = \frac{I \cdot M \cdot S}{\rho \cdot F} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-3} \times 63.5 \times 3.0}{8.96 \times 96{,}500}
$$

- Numerator: $8 \times 10^{-3} \times 63.5 \times 3.0 = 1.524$
- Denominator: $8.96 \times 96{,}500 = 864{,}640$
- $MRR = 1.524 / 864{,}640 = 1.763 \times 10^{-6}$ cm³/s
- Convert to cm³/min: $\times 60$:

$$
\boxed{MRR \approx 1.058 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}}
$$

**Bonus Part (c) — Time for Slot Machining:**

Slot volume: $V = 1 \times 0.5 \times 0.05 = 0.025$ mm³ $= 0.025 \times 10^{-3}$ cm³ $= 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ cm³.

$$
t = \frac{V}{MRR} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{1.058 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 0.236 \text{ min} \approx 14.2 \text{ seconds}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in MRR Formula:** Forgetting to keep $M$ and $\rho$ in consistent units is the **#1 reason** students lose 3–4 marks in numerical problems. If $M$ is in g/mol and $\rho$ in kg/m³, MRR will be off by a factor of 1000.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Sputtering Ratio (S):** Many students write $MRR = IM/\rho F$ and miss the factor $S$. The examiner **will deduct 2 marks** if $S$ is omitted in a problem where it is given.
>
> 3. **Conflating EDM and IBM formulas:** In EDM, the formula is $MRR = IM/(\rho F)$ because each spark removes one atom per unit charge. In IBM, **$S$ multiplies** the numerator. Do not interchange them.
>
> 4. **Confusing Beam Current (I) with Current Density (J):** If the problem gives current density $J$ in A/cm² and beam area $A$ in cm², students must first compute $I = J \cdot A$ before applying the MRR formula.
>
> 5. **Writing "Ions remove material by melting" in theory questions:** IBM is **NOT thermal** — the workpiece stays cold. Writing any thermal mechanism for IBM is a **conceptual error** worth 2–3 marks.
>
> 6. **Missing the "Vacuum" in the diagram:** Any schematic drawn without a vacuum chamber and pump connection is considered incomplete and loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision List for IBM — Read Just Before Entering the Exam Hall:**

- ✅ IBM = **Ion Beam Machining** = **Mechanical (kinetic) non-thermal** material removal.
- ✅ Working fluid: **Inert gas, usually Argon** (sometimes Xenon, Krypton).
- ✅ Process must occur in **high vacuum** (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr) to prevent ion scattering.
- ✅ Energy source = **Kinetic energy of accelerated ions** ($KE = eV$).
- ✅ **No HAZ, No thermal damage, No chemical reaction.**
- ✅ Can machine **any material** — conductors, insulators, ceramics, diamond.
- ✅ **MRR Formula (MUST MEMORIZE):**
  $MRR = \dfrac{I \cdot M \cdot S}{\rho \cdot F}$
- ✅ **Ion Velocity Formula (MUST MEMORIZE):**
  $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2eV}{m}}$
- ✅ **Faraday Constant F = 96,500 C/mol**; **e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**.
- ✅ Sputtering ratio **S > 1** due to collision cascades; peaks at ~60° incidence.
- ✅ **Typical MRR ≈ 0.1 to 10 mm³/min** (very low — process is for precision, not bulk removal).
- ✅ **Typical beam voltage 500–2000 V**; **beam current 0.1–10 mA**.
- ✅ **Ion source types:** Kaufman ion source, Duoplasmatron.
- ✅ **Major applications:** Turbine blade cooling holes, MEMS, optics polishing, micro-electronics lithography, depth profiling in SIMS.
- ✅ **Major limitation:** Extremely low MRR + high capital cost → only viable for micro-features.
- ✅ **NEVER confuse** IBM with EDM (thermal + sparks) or LBM (thermal + photons) — IBM is **purely kinetic**.
- ✅ **Neutralizer filament** is required to prevent charge buildup on insulating workpieces.
- ✅ **Setup components (in order along beam path):** Gas cylinder → Ion source → Plasma → Extractor → Accelerator → Focusing lenses → Workpiece (with neutralizer nearby) → Vacuum pump.
- ✅ In numerical problems, **always check unit consistency** between $M$ and $\rho$ before substituting.

---

> **END OF NOTES — ION BEAM MACHINING (IBM), MODULE 2**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Syllabus | PERAT528 Advanced Manufacturing Processes*
