# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]
**Explain the term "Elastic Emission" in the context of EEM. Why is the term "elastic" used?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The term "Elastic Emission" refers to the mechanism by which individual atoms are ejected from the workpiece surface when struck by a high-velocity abrasive particle. The collision is termed "elastic" because:
(i) The workpiece responds elastically during the very short impact duration (~ps), so no plastic deformation or heat-affected zone is created (1 mark).
(ii) The particle itself rebounds elastically with almost no energy loss (1 mark).
(iii) No chemical reaction, phase change, or thermal effect is involved — only mechanical kinetic energy transfer (1 mark).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]
**List any four advantages of Elastic Emission Machining over conventional grinding.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — any four):**
1. Atomic-level material removal → no subsurface damage (1 mark).
2. No thermal effect → no heat-affected zone, no residual stress (1 mark).
3. Achievable surface finish Ra < 1 nm (mirror finish) (1 mark).
4. Suitable for ultra-brittle materials (silicon, glass, ceramics) where conventional grinding causes cracks.

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **forget to state the binding energy threshold condition** explicitly. Always write: *"If KE of particle ≥ binding energy of workpiece atom, atomic ejection occurs."* (–1 mark if omitted).
> - Do **not** confuse EEM with sandblasting or abrasive jet machining. The examiner looks for the keywords **atomic-level, elastic, no thermal/chemical effect** (–2 marks if missing).
> - Numerical problems: Always show **unit conversions** (e.g., eV → J, µm → m). Missing units = –1 to –2 marks.
> - When asked to "explain working," draw/describe the **nozzle acceleration stage** explicitly. Many students skip this (–1 mark).

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper]

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, describe the working principle of Elastic Emission Machining. Clearly explain the role of the converging nozzle and the atomic ejection mechanism. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the threshold velocity equation for atomic ejection in EEM. A silicon workpiece ($E_b = 4.6 \,\text{eV}$) is processed using alumina particles ($d_p = 1 \,\mu m$, $\rho_p = 3970 \,\text{kg/m³}$). Calculate the threshold velocity and comment on whether a nozzle exit velocity of 300 m/s is sufficient. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Working Principle (7 marks):**

EEM removes material at the atomic level using accelerated abrasive particles (3 marks for description):

- **Setup:** Fine abrasive powder (ZrO₂, SiC, Al₂O₃) is mixed with a carrier fluid (gas or deionized water) in a mixing chamber.
- **Nozzle Role:** The slurry is forced through a precision converging nozzle. By the **Bernoulli principle**, pressure energy converts to kinetic energy, accelerating particles to 200–1000 m/s.
- **Free Flight:** Particles travel across a precisely controlled gap (1–10 mm) and strike the workpiece surface normally.
- **Atomic Ejection:** On impact, kinetic energy transfers to surface atoms. If KE ≥ atomic binding energy $E_b$, individual atoms are ejected one by one — producing atomic-scale surface smoothing.
- **Collection:** Spent slurry is sucked away via a hood and the abrasive is recycled.
- **Surface finish:** Achieved roughness Ra < 1 nm, with zero thermal, chemical, or subsurface damage.

**(2 marks reserved for neat schematic / labeled diagram description** — nozzle, mixing chamber, workpiece stage, suction, control loop).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert binding energy to Joules:**

$$E_b = 4.6 \,\text{eV} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \,\text{J/eV} = 7.36 \times 10^{-19} \,\text{J}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate particle mass:**

$$m_p = \rho_p \cdot \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} = 3970 \times \frac{\pi \times (1 \times 10^{-6})^3}{6}$$

$$m_p = 3970 \times 5.236 \times 10^{-19} = 2.079 \times 10^{-15} \,\text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Threshold velocity:**

$$v_{th} = \sqrt{\frac{2 E_b}{m_p}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 7.36 \times 10^{-19}}{2.079 \times 10^{-15}}}$$

$$v_{th} = \sqrt{7.080 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.0266 \,\text{m/s} \approx 2.66 \,\text{cm/s}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison:**

Since $v = 300 \,\text{m/s} \gg v_{th} = 0.0266 \,\text{m/s}$, the impact energy is **~11,000 times** the threshold. The process will readily remove material atom by atom.

**Engineering comment:** While 300 m/s is far above threshold, in practice the velocity must be **optimized** — too high causes multi-atom ejection clusters, defeating the atomic-scale finish. Typical optimized velocities are 200–600 m/s.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Compare Elastic Emission Machining with Conventional Grinding and Chemical-Mechanical Polishing (CMP). Highlight situations where EEM is the preferred choice. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The exit velocity of particles from an EEM nozzle is given by $v = \sqrt{2\Delta P / \rho_f}$. For nitrogen gas ($\rho_f = 14 \,\text{kg/m³}$) at a pressure drop of 2 MPa, compute (i) the exit velocity, and (ii) the kinetic energy per particle for $d_p = 0.8 \,\mu m$ zirconia particles ($\rho_p = 5680 \,\text{kg/m³}$). State whether this KE exceeds the binding energy of silicon ($E_b = 4.6 \,\text{eV}$). **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Aspect | EEM | Conventional Grinding | CMP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Atomic elastic ejection | Abrasive fracture + micro-chip | Chemical dissolution + abrasive |
| Damage | **None** | Subsurface cracks | Some micro-scratches |
| Heat | **None** | Significant | Mild (exothermic) |
| Surface Ra | **< 1 nm** | 0.1 – 10 µm | 0.5 – 2 nm |
| Material | Brittle/optical | Metals, all materials | Metals, semiconductors |

**EEM is preferred for:**
1. **Single-crystal silicon wafers** for advanced semiconductor nodes (sub-nm finish required).
2. **Optical components** for high-power lasers / EUV lithography where any subsurface damage is unacceptable.
3. **Advanced ceramics** (Si₃N₄, ZrO₂) used in medical implants — no chemical contamination desired.
4. **Substrate cleaning / atomic-level deburring** where ultra-clean surfaces are mandatory.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Exit velocity:**

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_f}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 2 \times 10^6}{14}} = \sqrt{2.857 \times 10^5} \approx 534.5 \,\text{m/s}$$

**(ii) KE per particle — first compute particle mass:**

$$m_p = \rho_p \cdot \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} = 5680 \times \frac{\pi \times (0.8 \times 10^{-6})^3}{6}$$

$$m_p = 5680 \times 2.681 \times 10^{-19} = 1.523 \times 10^{-15} \,\text{kg}$$

**Kinetic energy:**

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} m_p v^2 = 0.5 \times 1.523 \times 10^{-15} \times (534.5)^2$$

$$KE = 0.5 \times 1.523 \times 10^{-15} \times 2.857 \times 10^5 = 2.176 \times 10^{-10} \,\text{J}$$

**Convert KE to eV:**

$$KE = \frac{2.176 \times 10^{-10}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.36 \times 10^9 \,\text{eV} = 1.36 \,\text{GeV}$$

**Comparison with binding energy:**

$$KE = 1.36 \times 10^9 \,\text{eV} \gg E_b = 4.6 \,\text{eV}$$

$$\boxed{\text{KE exceeds binding energy by a factor of} \sim 3 \times 10^8 \text{ — material removal is assured.}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Although the KE per particle is enormous compared to atomic binding energy, the *fractional energy transferred to a single atom* during the brief collision is small (typically 1–10%) because of momentum and mass mismatch between particle and atom. This is precisely why only **one atom at a time** is ejected, giving EEM its atomic-scale precision.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **EEM = atomic-level elastic ejection** of workpiece atoms by accelerated abrasive particles.
- ✅ Material is removed **one atom at a time** — no chips, no cracks, no heat.
- ✅ Process requires **kinetic energy ≥ atomic binding energy**: $v_{th} = \sqrt{2E_b/m_p}$.
- ✅ Exit velocity governed by nozzle pressure: $v = \sqrt{2\Delta P/\rho_f}$.
- ✅ Typical abrasives: ZrO₂, SiC, Al₂O₃, CeO₂; typical carriers: N₂, He, deionized water.
- ✅ Achievable roughness: **Ra < 1 nm** (sub-nanometer mirror finish).
- ✅ Extremely low MRR → process is **finishing-only**, never bulk material removal.
- ✅ Applications: **silicon wafers, optical mirrors, EUV components, ceramics**.
- ✅ Developed by **Y. Mori et al. (Riken, Japan)** in the late 1980s.
- ✅ Nozzle-workpiece gap is critical: too small → nozzle erosion; too large → particle deceleration.
- ✅ **Distinguish from sandblasting**: EEM = atomic ejection; sandblasting = brittle fracture.
- ✅ Always mention the **four zero-effects** in your answers: zero thermal, zero chemical, zero plastic, zero subsurface damage.
- ✅ For numericals, **unit conversion** (eV → J, µm → m) is mandatory to avoid losing marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ion Beam Machining (IBM) - Nano Fabrication using CVD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT528 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ion Beam Machining (IBM) - Nano Fabrication using CVD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Ion Beam Machining (IBM)

**Formal Definition:**
Ion Beam Machining (IBM) is a non-traditional, thermal-free, atom-scale material removal process in which high-velocity positively charged ions (typically inert gases like Argon, Xenon, or reactive gases like Oxygen) are accelerated electrostatically to energies of **0.5 keV to 5 keV** and directed onto a workpiece inside a high vacuum environment (typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$ torr). Material is removed by *physical sputtering* — momentum transfer from energetic ions to surface atoms — without any chemical reaction or thermal melting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> IBM is the *only* nanomachining process capable of *atom-by-atom* material removal with sub-nanometer accuracy. It is classified as a **Mechanical-Energy-Based Non-Conventional Process** in the KTU PERAT528 module classification.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Imagine a *sandblaster*, but instead of throwing visible sand grains, you fire **invisible, electrically-charged atomic bullets** at a surface. Each bullet is a single ionized atom accelerated to extreme speed. When these bullets hit the workpiece, they physically knock off surface atoms (one layer at a time) through pure mechanical impact, similar to how a billiard ball knocks another ball on a billiards table. The vacuum chamber ensures the ions travel in a straight line without colliding with air molecules.

### 1.2 Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) for Nano-Fabrication

**Formal Definition:**
Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) is a thin-film deposition technique in which volatile precursor gases react chemically (either thermally or via plasma activation) on or near a heated substrate to form a solid nanoscale film, while the by-products are removed via gas flow. In **nano-fabrication**, CVD is the principal method for synthesizing carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene, silicon nanowires, and other 1D/2D nanostructures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU 2024):**
> CVD involves the **chemical reaction** of a volatile precursor on a substrate surface to produce a *non-volatile* solid deposit, in contrast to Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD) which is purely physical.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Think of CVD like **frost forming on a cold window** on a winter morning, but in reverse — instead of water vapor turning into ice, special chemical vapors (like methane, silane) pass over a hot wafer, decompose, and "freeze" into an atomically thin solid layer on the surface. Whatever does not stick is carried away as exhaust gas. By carefully choosing the gases and the temperature, you can "grow" structures as small as a single atom layer tall (graphene) or tubes only 1 nanometer wide (carbon nanotubes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why IBM + CVD are studied together (KTU context):**
> In modern nano-fabrication, **IBM is used for patterning/milling** (removing material with atomic precision) while **CVD is used for depositing/growing** the nano-structures. Together, they form the *top-down + bottom-up* hybrid nano-manufacturing toolkit.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Ion Beam Machining — Operating Principle

The process operates on the principle of **Physical Sputtering**, governed by the following sequential logic:

1. **Ion Generation:** A neutral gas (commonly Ar) is introduced into a chamber at low pressure. High-energy electrons (from a hot filament or plasma discharge) collide with gas atoms, stripping outer electrons to create positive ions (Ar⁺).
2. **Ion Acceleration:** A potential difference $V$ applied between the anode (ion source) and the extraction/cathode grid imparts kinetic energy to the ions:
   
   $$KE_{ion} = qV$$

   where $q$ is the ionic charge ($+1e$ for single ionization) and $V$ is the accelerating voltage.

3. **Beam Collimation & Focusing:** Electrostatic lenses (Einzel lenses) and magnetic focusing coils shape the divergent ion beam into a parallel, fine-diameter beam (as small as **10 nm to 1 µm**).
4. **Vacuum Environment:** A vacuum of $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-6}$ torr prevents ion scattering by residual gas molecules, ensuring the beam reaches the workpiece with full energy.
5. **Sputtering Impact:** When the energetic ion strikes the workpiece surface, momentum transfer to surface atoms ejects them. The number of ejected atoms per incident ion is the **Sputtering Yield (S)**.
6. **Mask/Pattern Control:** Material is removed only where the beam strikes; a metallic mask protects other regions, enabling patterned micro/nano features.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does sputtering occur?**
> Sputtering is purely a **momentum-transfer (kinematic) process**, NOT thermal. The ion's kinetic energy (not heat) physically dislodges atoms. This is why IBM produces *no heat-affected zone (HAZ)* — a major advantage over laser or EDM machining.

**Key Process Parameters:**

- Accelerating Voltage: **0.5 to 5 kV** (low-energy IBM) or up to **50 kV** (Focused Ion Beam – FIB)
- Beam Current: $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-6}$ A
- Vacuum Pressure: **$10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$ torr**
- Beam Diameter: as small as **5 nm** (for FIB systems)
- Sputtering Yield (Ar on Si): ~**0.5 to 2 atoms/ion** at 1 keV

### 2.2 Material Removal Rate in IBM

The MRR is derived from the sputtering yield and ion flux:

$$MRR = \frac{n \cdot S \cdot M}{N_A \cdot \rho}$$

where:
- $n$ = number of incident ions per second (= $\frac{I}{q}$, where $I$ = beam current)
- $S$ = sputtering yield (atoms removed per incident ion)
- $M$ = atomic mass of the workpiece material
- $N_A$ = Avogadro's number ($6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹)
- $\rho$ = density of the workpiece

Equivalently, the volumetric removal rate can be written as:

$$MRR_{vol} = \frac{I \cdot S \cdot M}{q \cdot N_A \cdot \rho}$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---------------------|------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Kinetic Energy of Ion | $KE = qV$ | eV or J | $q$ = charge, $V$ = voltage |
| 2 | Ion Flux | $\dot{N} = I / q$ | ions/s | Beam current / charge |
| 3 | Sputtering Yield | $S = \frac{\text{atoms ejected}}{\text{incident ion}}$ | dimensionless | 0.1 – 10 typically |
| 4 | Material Removal Rate (mass) | $\dot{m} = \dot{N} \cdot S \cdot m_{atom}$ | kg/s | |
| 5 | Material Removal Rate (volume) | $MRR_v = \frac{I \cdot S \cdot M}{q \cdot N_A \cdot \rho}$ | mm³/s | KTU board-favorite |
| 6 | Depth of Cut | $z = \frac{MRR_v \cdot t}{A}$ | mm | $t$ = time, $A$ = area |
| 7 | Mean Free Path of Ion | $\lambda = \frac{kT}{\sqrt{2} \pi d^2 p}$ | m | Must be $>$ chamber length |
| 8 | CVD Growth Rate (mass transport limited) | $G_r = \frac{h_g (C_g - C_s)}{\rho \cdot N_{Av}} \cdot M$ | µm/min | $h_g$ = mass-transfer coefficient |
| 9 | CVD Film Thickness | $t_f = G_r \cdot \tau$ | µm | $\tau$ = deposition time |
| 10 | Arrhenius Growth Rate (surface-reaction limited) | $G_r = K_0 \exp(-E_a / RT)$ | µm/min | $E_a$ = activation energy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** IBM is used to fabricate **X-ray mirrors, optical gratings, micro-fluidic channels, micro-electro-mechanical systems (MEMS), and most importantly — TEM/STEM sample preparation** (Focused Ion Beam, or FIB, is a derivative of IBM). CVD is the *workhorse* of the semiconductor industry, used to grow silicon dioxide, polysilicon, silicon nitride, epitaxial silicon, and modern 2D materials.

### 2.4 Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) — Operating Principle

1. **Gas Transport:** Precursor gases (e.g., SiH₄, CH₄, WF₆) enter the reactor via mass-flow controllers.
2. **Convection & Diffusion:** Gases move to the substrate through the boundary layer.
3. **Adsorption:** Reactant molecules adsorb onto the heated substrate surface.
4. **Surface Reaction:** Chemical decomposition/reaction occurs on the surface (e.g., $SiH_4 \rightarrow Si + 2H_2$).
5. **Nucleation & Film Growth:** The solid product nucleates and grows layer-by-layer.
6. **Desorption & Byproduct Removal:** Gaseous by-products (e.g., H₂, HCl) desorb and are exhausted.

> [!NOTE]
> **CVD in Nano-fabrication (KTU focus):**
> - **Carbon Nanotubes (CNTs):** Catalytic CVD using $C_2H_2$ or $CH_4$ on Fe/Mo/Co nanoparticles at **700–900 °C**
> - **Graphene:** LPCVD on Cu foils using $CH_4 + H_2$ at **1000 °C**
> - **Silicon Nanowires:** Vapor-Liquid-Solid (VLS) CVD using SiH₄ + Au catalyst at **500–600 °C**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Material Removal Rate in Ion Beam Machining

**Step 1 — Define the ion flux:**

Let $I$ be the ion beam current (in Amperes) and $q$ the ionic charge ($1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C for a singly-charged ion). The number of ions striking the workpiece per second is:

$$\dot{N} = \frac{I}{q} \quad \text{[ions/second]}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the sputtering yield:**

Each incident ion ejects $S$ atoms. The number of atoms removed per second is:

$$\dot{N}_{atoms} = S \cdot \dot{N} = \frac{S \cdot I}{q}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to mass rate:**

If $m_{atom}$ is the mass of one atom (= $M/N_A$ where $M$ is molar mass and $N_A$ is Avogadro's number), then the mass removal rate is:

$$\dot{m} = \dot{N}_{atoms} \cdot m_{atom} = \frac{S \cdot I}{q} \cdot \frac{M}{N_A}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to volumetric removal rate:**

Dividing by density $\rho$ of the workpiece:

$$MRR_v = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{S \cdot I \cdot M}{q \cdot N_A \cdot \rho}$$

**Final Compact Form:**

$$\boxed{MRR_v = \frac{S \cdot I \cdot M}{q \cdot N_A \cdot \rho}}$$

### 3.2 Worked Example (Typical KTU 2024 Numerical)

> **Problem:**
> An IBM process uses a beam of Ar⁺ ions with a current of $0.5$ mA at an accelerating voltage of $3$ kV. The sputtering yield for Ar⁺ on silicon is **0.8 atoms/ion**. Calculate the **material removal rate (mm³/s)** for silicon.
> Given: $M_{Si} = 28.09$ g/mol, $\rho_{Si} = 2.33$ g/cm³, $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹, $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C.

**Solution — Step 1: Identify values**

$$I = 0.5 \text{ mA} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}, \quad S = 0.8, \quad M = 28.09 \text{ g/mol}, \quad \rho = 2.33 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the MRR formula**

$$MRR_v = \frac{S \cdot I \cdot M}{q \cdot N_A \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 3: Numerator calculation**

$$S \cdot I \cdot M = 0.8 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 28.09 = 1.1236 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 4: Denominator calculation**

$$q \cdot N_A \cdot \rho = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 2.33 = 2.245 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 5: Final ratio (units: cm³/s → convert to mm³/s by multiplying by $10^3$)**

$$\begin{aligned}
MRR_v &= \frac{1.1236 \times 10^{-2}}{2.245 \times 10^{5}} \text{ cm}^3/\text{s} \\
&= 5.00 \times 10^{-8} \text{ cm}^3/\text{s} \\
&= 5.00 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

> **MRR ≈ 5 × 10⁻⁵ mm³/s — extremely low, which confirms IBM is used for *precision*, not bulk removal.**

### 3.3 Derivation of CVD Film Thickness from Growth Rate

For a **mass-transport-limited** CVD process, the growth rate is given by:

$$G_r = \frac{h_g (C_g - C_s)}{\rho / M}$$

The film thickness after deposition time $\tau$ is:

$$t_f = G_r \cdot \tau = \frac{h_g (C_g - C_s) M \tau}{\rho}$$

For a **surface-reaction-limited** regime, the Arrhenius expression dominates:

$$G_r = K_0 \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

Therefore, film thickness becomes:

$$t_f = K_0 \cdot \tau \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Plotting $\ln(t_f)$ vs $1/T$ gives a straight line with slope $= -E_a/R$, allowing experimental determination of activation energy (a frequent KTU lab question).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of an Ion Beam Machining Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Gas Inlet Ar or Xe] --> B[Ion Source Chamber]
    B --> C[Filament and Anode Ionization]
    C --> D[Accelerating Electrodes 0.5 to 5 kV]
    D --> E[Electrostatic Lenses Einzel Lens]
    E --> F[Beam Collimation and Focusing]
    F --> G[Vacuum Chamber 10 to the minus 5 torr]
    G --> H[Workpiece on XY Stage with Mask]
    H --> I[Material Ejection by Sputtering]
    I --> J[Exhaust and Pump System]
    K[Vacuum Pump] --> G
    L[Power Supply 0 to 5 kV] --> D
    M[Controller and Monitor] --> H
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram of a CVD System for Nano-Fabrication

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GasDelivery["Gas Delivery System"]
        P1[Precursor 1 e.g. CH4] --> MFC1[Mass Flow Controller 1]
        P2[Precursor 2 e.g. H2] --> MFC2[Mass Flow Controller 2]
        P3[Carrier Gas e.g. Ar] --> MFC3[Mass Flow Controller 3]
    end
    MFC1 --> MIX[Gas Mixing Manifold]
    MFC2 --> MIX
    MFC3 --> MIX
    MIX --> REACTOR[Heated CVD Reactor 700 to 1000 C]
    REACTOR --> SUB[Heated Substrate with Catalyst Nanoparticles]
    SUB --> NANO[Nano Structure Growth CNT Graphene Nanowire]
    NANO --> EXHAUST[Byproduct Exhaust H2 HCl]
    EXHAUST --> PUMP[Vacuum Pump]
    HEATER[External Heater and Temperature Controller] --> REACTOR
```

### 4.3 Process Flow — Hybrid IBM + CVD Nano-Fabrication

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1 Substrate Cleaning Si Wafer] --> B[Step 2 CVD Deposition of Catalyst Film 1 to 5 nm]
    B --> C[Step 3 IBM Patterning to Define Catalyst Islands]
    C --> D[Step 4 CVD Nano Structure Growth on Patterned Catalyst]
    D --> E[Step 5 Optional Final IBM Milling for Shape Correction]
    E --> F[Final Nano Device e.g. CNT FET Graphene Sensor]
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Energy and Material Flow in IBM

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    EN[Electrical Energy Input Power Supply] --> ACC[Acceleration Zone]
    GAS[Neutral Gas Atoms] --> ION[Plasma Ionization Region]
    ION --> ACC
    ACC --> BEAM[High Velocity Ion Beam]
    BEAM --> SURF[Workpiece Surface]
    SURF --> SP1[Primary Sputtered Atoms]
    SURF --> SP2[Secondary Electrons]
    SURF --> SP3[Heat Tiny Local Rise]
    SP1 --> VAC[Removed Material on Vacuum Walls]
    SP2 --> DET[Detector for Imaging]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic Note (KTU 2024):** In board exam diagrams, the *ion source*, *accelerating grid*, *focusing lens*, and *workpiece holder* must be **labeled explicitly**. Examiners specifically check for: (a) direction of ion travel (anode → workpiece), (b) location of vacuum pump, (c) identification of mask (if any).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
*Define Ion Beam Machining. List any two advantages and one limitation of IBM.*

**Model Answer:**

Ion Beam Machining (IBM) is a non-conventional material removal process in which accelerated ions (typically Ar⁺) are directed onto a workpiece under high vacuum to remove material by physical momentum transfer (sputtering).

**Advantages:**
1. Achieves **atomic-level precision** (down to sub-nm accuracy).
2. **No heat-affected zone (HAZ)** because the process is purely mechanical and non-thermal.

**Limitation:**
1. Extremely low MRR (of the order of $10^{-5}$ mm³/s), making it uneconomical for bulk material removal.

*(1 mark: definition, 1 mark: advantages, 1 mark: limitation)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supple]**
*What is CVD? Mention any two nano-materials produced by CVD.*

**Model Answer:**

Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) is a thin-film deposition process in which volatile precursor gases undergo chemical reaction on a heated substrate to form a solid, non-volatile deposit, while by-products are removed as exhaust.

**Nano-materials produced by CVD:**
1. **Carbon Nanotubes (CNTs)** — grown from CH₄/C₂H₂ on Fe/Mo catalyst nanoparticles.
2. **Graphene** — grown monolayer-by-monolayer from CH₄ on copper foil using LPCVD.

*(1 mark: definition, 2 marks: nano-materials with brief reason)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **Instruction (KTU 2024 ESE format):** *Answer any one full question. Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A(a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of Ion Beam Machining. List four applications of IBM.*

**Model Answer:**

*(Sketch — expected: schematic with ion source, accelerating electrodes, focusing lens, vacuum chamber, mask, workpiece. Award 2 marks for clean labeled diagram.)*

**Working Principle:**
1. Inert gas (Ar) is introduced into the ion source at low pressure.
2. A high voltage (0.5–5 kV) applied between the anode and extraction electrodes ionizes the gas and accelerates the positive ions to high velocity.
3. The ion beam is collimated and focused using electrostatic and/or magnetic lenses to a fine diameter.
4. The accelerated ions strike the workpiece surface (kept in vacuum at $\approx 10^{-5}$ torr) and physically dislodge surface atoms via momentum transfer — this is called **sputtering**.
5. Material is removed only where the beam strikes; a mask can be used to protect specific regions, allowing patterned machining.

**Applications of IBM (any 4 — 1 mark each, max 4 marks):**
1. Fabrication of **micro-electronic and nano-electronic devices** (Josephson junctions, X-ray masks).
2. **Focused Ion Beam (FIB) milling** for TEM/STEM specimen preparation.
3. Micro-machining of **optical components** (diffraction gratings, X-ray mirrors).
4. Fabrication of **micro-fluidic channels** and MEMS structures.
5. Nanopatterning of magnetic storage media.
6. Smoothing and figuring of **precision optical surfaces** at sub-nm level.

---

**Q.A(b). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Derive an expression for the material removal rate (MRR) in Ion Beam Machining. An IBM system uses a 1.2 mA Ar⁺ beam on an aluminum target (atomic mass 27 g/mol, density 2.7 g/cm³) with a sputtering yield of 1.2. Calculate the MRR in mm³/s. Take $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹.*

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Let $I$ be the beam current (A), $q$ be the charge of one ion (C), and $S$ be the sputtering yield (atoms/ion).

Number of ions striking the surface per second:

$$\dot{N} = \frac{I}{q} \quad \text{[ions/s]}$$

Number of atoms ejected per second:

$$\dot{N}_{atoms} = S \cdot \dot{N} = \frac{S \cdot I}{q} \quad \text{[atoms/s]}$$

Mass of one atom = $M / N_A$. Therefore, the mass removal rate:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{S \cdot I}{q} \cdot \frac{M}{N_A} \quad \text{[g/s]}$$

Dividing by density $\rho$:

$$MRR_v = \frac{S \cdot I \cdot M}{q \cdot N_A \cdot \rho}$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $I = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ A, $S = 1.2$, $M = 27$ g/mol, $\rho = 2.7$ g/cm³.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 1.2 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 27 = 3.888 \times 10^{-2} \\
\text{Denominator} &= 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 2.7 = 2.6015 \times 10^{5} \\
MRR_v &= \frac{3.888 \times 10^{-2}}{2.6015 \times 10^{5}} = 1.495 \times 10^{-7} \text{ cm}^3/\text{s} \\
&= 1.495 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mm}^3/\text{s} \\
&\approx 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**MRR ≈ 1.5 × 10⁻⁴ mm³/s**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**Q.B(a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Explain the working principle of Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) with a suitable diagram. List the major process parameters that affect the quality of CVD-deposited films.*

**Model Answer:**

*(Sketch — expected: gas cylinders, mass flow controllers, mixing chamber, heated reactor, substrate holder, exhaust. Award 2 marks for diagram.)*

**Working Principle:**
1. Volatile precursor gases (e.g., silane SiH₄, methane CH₄) are metered into a reaction chamber via mass flow controllers.
2. The gases flow over a heated substrate (held at 500–1000 °C depending on the process).
3. The precursor molecules adsorb on the substrate surface and undergo chemical decomposition or reaction (e.g., $SiH_4 \rightarrow Si + 2H_2$).
4. The non-volatile solid product deposits on the substrate, nucleating and growing into a continuous thin film.
5. Volatile by-products (H₂, HCl, etc.) desorb and are removed from the chamber by the carrier gas flow and exhaust system.

**Process Parameters (any 4 — 1 mark each, max 4 marks):**
1. **Substrate temperature** — controls reaction kinetics and film crystallinity.
2. **Chamber pressure** — APCVD (atmospheric), LPCVD (low pressure ~10–100 torr), UHVCVD (<10⁻⁶ torr).
3. **Gas flow rates & composition** — determines stoichiometry and growth rate.
4. **Reactor geometry** — affects gas flow uniformity and film thickness uniformity.
5. **Substrate material & surface preparation** — influences nucleation density and adhesion.
6. **Plasma power (in PECVD)** — lowers deposition temperature by providing external activation energy.

---

**Q.B(b). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Discuss the application of CVD in the synthesis of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and graphene. Mention the role of the catalyst, typical temperatures, and precursor gases used.*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Carbon Nanotubes (CNTs) by CVD (3.5 marks):**

- **Precursor gases:** Acetylene (C₂H₂) or methane (CH₄), often diluted with H₂ and Ar.
- **Catalyst:** Transition metal nanoparticles (Fe, Co, Mo, Ni) of size **1–10 nm**, pre-deposited on a Si/SiO₂ substrate.
- **Temperature range:** **700–900 °C** in a tube furnace.
- **Mechanism (Vapor–Liquid–Solid / VLS-like):** Hydrocarbon decomposes on the catalyst surface; carbon dissolves into the metal particle; upon supersaturation, carbon precipitates as a tubular graphene cylinder that lifts the catalyst off the substrate, growing into a multi-walled or single-walled CNT.
- **Advantages over arc discharge/laser ablation:** Better diameter control, lower defect density, scalable to wafer-level.

**2. Graphene by CVD (3.5 marks):**

- **Precursor gases:** Methane (CH₄) + Hydrogen (H₂), with Ar as carrier.
- **Catalyst/substrate:** Polycrystalline **copper (Cu)** foil (sometimes Ni).
- **Temperature:** **1000–1050 °C** in a hot-wall LPCVD reactor.
- **Mechanism:** At high temperature, CH₄ decomposes on Cu surface; carbon atoms dissolve into Cu (low solubility). On cooling, carbon segregates to the surface and forms a **monolayer of sp²-bonded carbon** (graphene).
- **Significance:** This process enables **wafer-scale continuous graphene films** that can be transferred to arbitrary substrates using PMMA-assisted wet transfer — the workhorse of modern graphene research and 2D electronics.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse IBM with FIB** — FIB is a *focused* version of IBM; both are sputtering-based, but FIB uses a Ga⁺ liquid metal ion source and a focused column. Generic IBM often uses Ar⁺ with a broader or patterned beam.
> 2. **MRR unit trap:** Always convert cm³/s to mm³/s by multiplying by $10^3$ — students frequently lose 1 mark by leaving the unit as cm³/s.
> 3. **CVD ≠ PVD:** Many students write "CVD is physical deposition" — examiners deduct 2 marks. CVD is *chemical*, PVD is *physical* (sputtering, evaporation).
> 4. **Catalyst role in CNT/graphene CVD:** Do NOT skip the catalyst. Without it, the precursor just decomposes into amorphous carbon, not structured CNTs or graphene.
> 5. **Beam current vs. ion flux:** A common numerical mistake is using $I$ (current in A) directly in the MRR formula without dividing by $q$ (charge per ion). Always show: ions/s = $I/q$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **IBM = Ion Beam Machining**, a **non-thermal, vacuum-based, momentum-transfer (sputtering) process**.
- Key parameters: Accelerating voltage (0.5–5 kV), beam current (nA–mA), vacuum (10⁻⁵ torr), sputtering yield (S).
- **MRR formula:** $MRR_v = \frac{S \cdot I \cdot M}{q \cdot N_A \cdot \rho}$ — universally applicable; commit to memory.
- Sputtering yield *S* depends on ion energy, ion mass, target material, and angle of incidence.
- **No HAZ, no recast layer, no mechanical stress** — only mechanical atom ejection.
- **CVD = Chemical Vapor Deposition**, a *chemical* thin-film process using gas-phase precursors on a heated substrate.
- Variants: **APCVD, LPCVD, PECVD, UHVCVD, MOCVD** — distinguish by pressure and activation method.
- **Nano-applications of CVD:** Carbon Nanotubes (Fe/Co catalyst, CH₄, 700–900 °C), Graphene (Cu foil, CH₄, ~1000 °C), Silicon nanowires (Au catalyst, SiH₄, 500–600 °C, VLS mechanism).
- **CVD growth rate regimes:** Mass-transport limited (high T) vs. surface-reaction limited (low T); Arrhenius relation: $G_r = K_0 \exp(-E_a/RT)$.
- **Hybrid IBM + CVD** = top-down patterning + bottom-up growth = core of modern nano-fabrication.
- Key advantages of IBM: **sub-nm precision, no thermal damage, mask-free in FIB mode**.
- Key disadvantage of IBM: **extremely low MRR, requires high vacuum and is capital-intensive**.
- Key advantage of CVD: **excellent conformality, uniform thickness over large areas, scalable**.
- Key disadvantage of CVD: **high temperatures limit substrate choice (for thermal CVD)**, toxic/pyrophoric precursors (e.g., SiH₄).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT528 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** PVD and LIGA Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD)** is a thin-film deposition process carried out under vacuum conditions (typically **10⁻³ to 10⁻⁶ Torr**) in which a target material is converted from a condensed phase (solid or liquid) into a vapor phase via purely physical mechanisms (thermal evaporation, momentum transfer, or arc discharge) and subsequently transported to a substrate where it condenses to form a thin, adherent coating.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Atomic Spray Painter" 🖌️

Imagine you want to paint a wall, but the paint particles are individual **atoms**. In PVD:
- You take a chunk of solid metal (the *target*) inside a sealed vacuum chamber.
- You energize it — either by **heating it until it boils** (thermal evaporation) or by **bombarding it with ionized gas atoms** (sputtering).
- The atoms that fly off travel in straight lines (no air molecules to bump into, thanks to vacuum) and land on your substrate, building up layer-by-layer — almost like a 3D printer that lays down a single atomic layer at a time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** PVD is a **line-of-sight** process. Atoms travel in straight trajectories. To coat complex 3D shapes uniformly, parts are often **rotated** (e.g., in fixture rotation or planetary fixturing).

### Major Variants of PVD

| Variant | Vaporization Mechanism | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermal Evaporation** | Resistive heating / e-beam | Optical coatings, metallization |
| **Sputtering** (DC/RF/Magnetron) | Ion bombardment momentum transfer | Semiconductor metallization, hard coatings |
| **Ion Plating** | Combined evaporation + ion bombardment | Adherent tool coatings (TiN, CrN) |
| **Cathodic Arc Deposition** | Vacuum arc discharge | DLC films, decorative coatings |
| **Pulsed Laser Deposition (PLD)** | Laser ablation | Multi-component oxide films |

> [!NOTE]
> The deposition temperature in PVD is generally in the range **200 °C to 500 °C**, making it compatible with **tempered tool steels** and many pre-hardened engineering substrates.

---

## 1.2 LIGA Process

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **LIGA** (German acronym: **Li**thographie — **G**alvanoformung — **A**bformung, meaning *X-ray Lithography — Electroforming — Molding*) is a micro-fabrication technology used to produce **high-aspect-ratio micro-structures** with structural heights of several hundred micrometers to millimeters and lateral feature sizes down to the sub-micron level. It employs **synchrotron X-ray radiation** (wavelength ≈ **0.2 – 0.5 nm**) to expose a thick **PMMA (polymethyl methacrylate)** resist.

### Conceptual Analogy — "Carving Sky-Scrapers in Plastic" 🏙️

Think of conventional photolithography (used in smartphone chips) as **drawing on a sheet of paper** — features are flat and shallow (a few micrometers). LIGA is like **drilling vertical mine-shafts kilometers deep** in a block of clear plastic:
1. The plastic block (**PMMA**) is so thick that normal UV light cannot penetrate it.
2. Powerful **X-rays from a synchrotron** are used instead — these have a wavelength small enough to cut clean, deep, near-vertical sidewalls through hundreds of micrometers of resist.
3. The exposed regions are chemically dissolved (**development**), leaving tall, precise negative molds.
4. These molds are filled with metal using **electroplating** to create towering micro-structures.
5. Optionally, the metal mold is used as a die for **plastic injection molding**, enabling mass production.

> [!NOTE]
> **High Aspect Ratio (HAR)** is the defining feature of LIGA: aspect ratios of **10:1 to 100:1** are routinely achieved — something conventional lithography simply cannot do.

### The Three LIGA Steps at a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Lithography (X-ray deep exposure of PMMA)** — Pattern transfer using synchrotron radiation.
> 2. **Galvanoformung (Electroforming)** — Filling the developed resist cavities with metal (typically **nickel**) by electroplating.
> 3. **Abformung (Molding)** — Using the metal mold insert for hot embossing or injection molding of polymer replicas.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 PVD — Process Theory

### 2.1.1 Thermal Evaporation

The process relies on heating the source material above its **vapor pressure threshold** ($P_v \geq 10^{-2}$ Torr). Two heating strategies are used:

- **Resistive (Filament) Evaporation:** Source held in a refractory metal boat (W, Ta, Mo). Limited to low-melting metals (Al, Au, Ag).
- **Electron Beam (E-Beam) Evaporation:** A focused electron beam (5–10 kV, ~1 A) locally melts the source. Can evaporate high-melting materials (Ti, Mo, W, ceramics).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why vacuum?** At low pressure, the **mean free path** of vapor atoms is much greater than the source-to-substrate distance, ensuring ballistic (collision-free) transport and preventing gas-phase reactions.

### 2.1.2 Sputtering

Sputtering is governed by **momentum transfer** rather than heat. A plasma (typically Ar at **5–30 mTorr**) is ignited between the target (cathode) and substrate (anode). Positively charged Ar⁺ ions accelerate toward the negatively biased target and eject target atoms through momentum exchange.

**Why and How of the Sputter Process (Step-by-Step):**
1. **Plasma Generation** — A glow discharge is initiated in inert gas (Ar) at low pressure.
2. **Ion Acceleration** — The target is biased negatively (–200 to –1000 V). Ar⁺ ions are drawn toward it.
3. **Momentum Transfer (Knock-On Cascade)** — The incoming Ar⁺ collides with surface atoms, generating a collision cascade that ejects surface atoms.
4. **Transport** — Ejected atoms traverse the vacuum with energy **1–10 eV** and land on the substrate.
5. **Nucleation & Growth** — Atoms condense, diffuse, and coalesce into a continuous thin film.

**Magnetron Sputtering Enhancement:** Magnets behind the target trap electrons in a closed-loop **E×B drift path** near the target surface, increasing plasma density by **10× to 100×** and allowing lower operating pressures and voltages.

### 2.1.3 Ion Plating

Combines evaporation (high deposition rate) with concurrent ion bombardment of the growing film. A negative bias of **–2 to –5 kV** on the substrate:
- **Sputter-cleans** the substrate before deposition (improves adhesion).
- **Densifies** the film during growth (reduces porosity).
- **Enhances diffusion** at lower bulk temperatures.

---

## 2.2 LIGA — Process Theory in Depth

### 2.2.1 X-Ray Lithography (Lithographie)

- **Resist:** PMMA (Polymethyl Methacrylate), bonded to a conductive substrate (Cu/Cr) for the subsequent electroforming step.
- **Radiation Source:** Synchrotron (electron storage ring). Wavelengths around **0.2–0.5 nm** provide the required penetration depth (several hundred μm of PMMA).
- **Mask:** Made of a thin **gold absorber pattern** on a **titanium or beryllium membrane** (mask membrane must be X-ray transparent).
- **Exposure Dose:** Typically **1–4 kJ/cm³** for PMMA.
- **Development:** Chemical dissolution in a liquid developer (e.g., **GG developer**: 60% morpholine, 20% 2-aminoethanol-1, 5% diethanolamine, 15% DI water, or simpler **xylene-based** developers) selectively removes the exposed regions.

> [!NOTE]
> The **depth-dose distribution** must remain above the development threshold throughout the resist thickness to ensure complete exposure. This is why the X-ray spectrum and resist sensitivity curve must be carefully matched.

### 2.2.2 Electroforming (Galvanoformung)

- The developed PMMA structure serves as a **negative mold**.
- The conductive seed layer (Cu or Au) underneath acts as the cathode.
- The substrate is immersed in an **electrolyte** (e.g., **nickel sulfamate bath**: Ni(SO₃NH₂)₂ with boric acid buffer, pH 3.5–4.5, T = 50–55 °C).
- Current density: **1–5 A/dm²**.
- The metal is deposited **only in the resist cavities**, growing upward.
- Process continues **until the deposit overflows the resist top**, forming a thick solid metal plate.
- The PMMA is then stripped (using solvents or plasma ashing), leaving a free-standing metal mold insert.

### 2.2.3 Molding (Abformung)

The metal mold insert is then used in one of:
- **Hot Embossing** (T ≈ T_g + 70 °C, e.g., for PMMA, COC, COP)
- **Injection Molding** (mass production of polymer replicas)
- **Reaction Injection Molding (RIM)**

This converts a single expensive synchrotron-fabricated master into **thousands to millions** of low-cost polymer micro-parts.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula required for KTU Part A and Part B numerical/derivation questions on this topic. Memorize the **symbol definitions** and the **physical units** exactly.

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Y = \dfrac{N_{\text{ejected}}}{N_{\text{incident}}}$ | **Sputtering Yield** (dimensionless) | $N_{\text{ejected}}$ = atoms sputtered, $N_{\text{incident}}$ = incident ions |
| 2 | $R = \dfrac{M \cdot N}{\rho \cdot A \cdot t}$ | **Deposition Rate** | $M$ = molar mass (kg/mol), $N$ = moles deposited, $\rho$ = density (kg/m³), $A$ = area (m²), $t$ = time (s) |
| 3 | $\phi = \dfrac{N_A \cdot P}{\sqrt{2 \pi M R T}}$ | **Incident Molar Flux** (Hertz–Knudsen) | $N_A$ = Avogadro's number, $P$ = vapor pressure (Pa), $M$ = molar mass, $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $T$ = temperature (K) |
| 4 | $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \cdot \pi \cdot d^2 \cdot P}$ | **Mean Free Path** of vapor atom | $k_B$ = 1.38×10⁻²³ J/K, $d$ = atomic diameter (m), $P$ = pressure (Pa) |
| 5 | $R_{\text{evap}} = \alpha \cdot \phi \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{2 \pi R T}}$ | **Thermal Evaporation Rate** (kg/m²·s) | $\alpha$ = sticking coefficient (≈ 0 to 1) |
| 6 | $AR = \dfrac{H}{W}$ | **Aspect Ratio** (LIGA) | $H$ = structural height, $W$ = lateral feature width |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{film}}}{\rho_{\text{bulk}}}$ | **Film Packing Density Ratio** | $\rho_{\text{film}}$ = film density, $\rho_{\text{bulk}}$ = bulk density |
| 8 | $D_{\text{exp}} = I_{\text{abs}} \cdot t_{\text{exp}}$ | **X-ray Exposure Dose** (J/cm³) | $I_{\text{abs}}$ = absorbed intensity, $t_{\text{exp}}$ = exposure time |
| 9 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | **Electroplating Mass Deposited** (Faraday's Law) | $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $M$ = atomic mass, $n$ = valence, $F$ = 96485 C/mol |
| 10 | $v_{\text{dep}} = \dfrac{I \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A}$ | **Electroplating Deposition Velocity** (m/s) | $A$ = plating area (m²) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sputtering yield values** to remember (for Ar⁺ at 500 eV, normal incidence):
> - Cu ≈ 2.4, Al ≈ 1.5, Au ≈ 2.8, Ti ≈ 0.5, W ≈ 0.6
> Yields increase with ion energy up to a peak (~10–100 keV) and then decrease due to ion implantation.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PVD Coatings on Cutting Tools:** TiN, TiAlN, CrN, and DLC coatings deposited by cathodic arc / magnetron sputtering extend tool life by **3×–10×** in turning, drilling, and milling operations.
- **LIGA Micro-Components:** Used in **MEMS accelerometers**, **micro-gear trains**, **fluidic microsystems**, **X-ray optics (zone plates)**, and **fiber-optic connectors**.
- **LIGA Cost Economy:** Synchrotron exposure is expensive (~₹ 1–10 lakh per exposure), but a single LIGA master can produce **millions** of molded replicas, making per-part cost minimal at scale.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Sputtering Yield Estimation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A magnetron sputtering system uses an Ar⁺ ion beam at **500 eV** incident normally on a copper target. If the ion current is **100 mA** and the sputtering yield of Cu at this energy is **2.4 atoms/ion**, calculate the **number of Cu atoms sputtered per second** and the **mass of copper deposited on the substrate per minute**, assuming all sputtered atoms reach a circular substrate of radius **5 cm** placed at 10 cm distance.

### Solution

**Step 1: Calculate the number of incident ions per second.**

The total charge per second is the current:
$$I = 100 \text{ mA} = 0.1 \text{ A}$$

The charge of one Ar⁺ ion (singly charged) is:
$$q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$$

Number of incident ions per second:
$$N_{\text{incident}} = \dfrac{I}{q} = \dfrac{0.1}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$N_{\text{incident}} = 6.24 \times 10^{17} \text{ ions/second}$$

**Step 2: Apply the sputtering yield to get the sputtered atom flux.**

Given yield $Y = 2.4$ atoms per incident ion:
$$N_{\text{ejected}} = Y \times N_{\text{incident}} = 2.4 \times 6.24 \times 10^{17}$$

$$N_{\text{ejected}} = 1.498 \times 10^{18} \text{ atoms/second}$$

**Step 3: Convert atomic flux to mass flux.**

The molar mass of copper is:
$$M_{\text{Cu}} = 63.55 \text{ g/mol} = 63.55 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}$$

Mass of one Cu atom:
$$m_{\text{atom}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{Cu}}}{N_A} = \dfrac{63.55 \times 10^{-3}}{6.022 \times 10^{23}}$$

$$m_{\text{atom}} = 1.055 \times 10^{-25} \text{ kg/atom}$$

Mass sputtered per second:
$$\dot{m} = N_{\text{ejected}} \times m_{\text{atom}} = 1.498 \times 10^{18} \times 1.055 \times 10^{-25}$$

$$\dot{m} = 1.581 \times 10^{-7} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 4: Compute mass deposited per minute.**

$$m_{60\text{s}} = 60 \times 1.581 \times 10^{-7} = 9.49 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg} = 9.49 \text{ mg/min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $\approx 1.50 \times 10^{18}$ atoms/second of Cu are sputtered, depositing $\approx 9.49$ mg of Cu per minute on the substrate. This is the order-of-magnitude deposition rate used in laboratory magnetron systems.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Thermal Evaporation Deposition Rate

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> Aluminum is thermally evaporated in a vacuum chamber. The vapor pressure of Al at the source temperature is **$1.0 \times 10^{-2}$ Torr**. The substrate is **0.3 m** from the source. The sticking coefficient is **0.9**. Calculate the **evaporation flux (atoms/m²·s)** and the **mean free path** of Al vapor atoms at the source temperature of **1500 K**.

**Constants provided:**
- $M_{\text{Al}} = 26.98 \text{ g/mol}$
- $d_{\text{Al}} \approx 2.86 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}$
- $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$

### Solution

**Step 1: Convert vapor pressure to SI units (Pascals).**

$$P = 1.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ Torr} \times 133.322 \text{ Pa/Torr} = 1.333 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Hertz–Knudsen incident molar flux equation.**

$$\phi = \dfrac{N_A \cdot P}{\sqrt{2 \pi M R T}}$$

Substituting values:
- $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹
- $P = 1.333$ Pa
- $M = 26.98 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- $T = 1500$ K

Compute denominator:
$$\sqrt{2 \pi M R T} = \sqrt{2 \pi \times 26.98 \times 10^{-3} \times 8.314 \times 1500}$$

$$= \sqrt{2114.6} = 45.99 \text{ (SI units: kg·m}^{-1}\text{·s}^{-1}\text{)}$$

Compute numerator:
$$N_A \cdot P = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 1.333 = 8.027 \times 10^{23}$$

Therefore:
$$\phi = \dfrac{8.027 \times 10^{23}}{45.99} = 1.745 \times 10^{22} \text{ mol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}$$

**Step 3: Apply sticking coefficient for the actual deposition rate.**

$$R_{\text{dep}} = \alpha \cdot \phi = 0.9 \times 1.745 \times 10^{22} = 1.571 \times 10^{22} \text{ mol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$

Convert to atoms/m²·s:
$$R_{\text{atoms}} = 1.571 \times 10^{22} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 9.46 \times 10^{45}$$

Wait — there is a unit error above. Let me re-evaluate.

**Correction — Step 3 (Proper Unit Conversion):**

$\phi$ in mol/(m²·s) gives the *molar* flux. To get *atomic* flux, multiply by Avogadro's number:
$$J_{\text{atoms}} = \phi \cdot N_A = 1.745 \times 10^{22} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}$$

$$J_{\text{atoms}} = 1.051 \times 10^{46} \text{ atoms/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$

That is still incorrect magnitude. The correct interpretation:

**Hertz–Knudsen flux** $\phi$ in mol/(m²·s) is:
$$\phi = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{2 \pi M R T}} \quad \text{(mol/m}^2\text{/s)}$$

Computing the bare flux (not yet multiplied by $N_A$):
$$\phi = \dfrac{1.333}{\sqrt{2 \pi \times 0.02698 \times 8.314 \times 1500}}$$

$$= \dfrac{1.333}{45.99} = 0.02898 \text{ mol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$

Converting to atoms/m²·s:
$$J = 0.02898 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 1.745 \times 10^{22} \text{ atoms/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$

With sticking coefficient $\alpha = 0.9$:
$$J_{\text{deposited}} = 0.9 \times 1.745 \times 10^{22} = 1.57 \times 10^{22} \text{ atoms/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** Atomic flux at the source ≈ $1.75 \times 10^{22}$ atoms/m²·s. Deposited flux on substrate (with $\alpha=0.9$) ≈ $1.57 \times 10^{22}$ atoms/m²·s.

**Step 4: Mean Free Path Calculation.**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \cdot \pi \cdot d^2 \cdot P}$$

$$= \dfrac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 1500}{\sqrt{2} \cdot \pi \cdot (2.86 \times 10^{-10})^2 \cdot 1.333}$$

Numerator:
$$1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 1500 = 2.07 \times 10^{-20}$$

Denominator:
$$\sqrt{2} \cdot \pi \cdot (2.86 \times 10^{-10})^2 \cdot 1.333 = 1.414 \times 3.1416 \times 8.18 \times 10^{-20} \times 1.333$$

$$= 4.443 \times 8.18 \times 10^{-20} \times 1.333 = 4.846 \times 10^{-19}$$

Therefore:
$$\lambda = \dfrac{2.07 \times 10^{-20}}{4.846 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.0427 \text{ m} = 4.27 \text{ cm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $\lambda \approx 4.27$ cm $\ll$ source–substrate distance (30 cm), the vacuum is inadequate. In practice, the system should be at **$10^{-5}$ Torr or lower** so that $\lambda$ exceeds the chamber dimensions, ensuring ballistic transport.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: LIGA Electroforming Mass & Aspect Ratio

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A LIGA electroforming step deposits nickel into a PMMA mold. The PMMA is **300 μm thick** with lateral features of **5 μm width**. The plating current is held at **0.5 A** for **6 hours** through a plating area of **10 cm²**. Calculate: (a) the **aspect ratio** of the resulting Ni structure, and (b) the **mass of Ni deposited**. Given: $M_{\text{Ni}} = 58.69$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $F = 96485$ C/mol.

### Solution

**Part (a) — Aspect Ratio:**

$$AR = \dfrac{H}{W} = \dfrac{300 \text{ μm}}{5 \text{ μm}} = 60$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is well within the **LIGA typical AR range of 10:1 to 100:1**.

**Part (b) — Mass of Nickel Deposited (Faraday's Law):**

$$m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

Total charge passed:
$$Q = I \cdot t = 0.5 \text{ A} \times (6 \times 3600) \text{ s} = 0.5 \times 21600 = 10800 \text{ C}$$

Moles of electrons:
$$n_e = \dfrac{Q}{F} = \dfrac{10800}{96485} = 0.1119 \text{ mol}$$

Moles of Ni (since n=2, Ni²⁺):
$$n_{\text{Ni}} = \dfrac{n_e}{2} = \dfrac{0.1119}{2} = 0.05597 \text{ mol}$$

Mass of Ni:
$$m_{\text{Ni}} = n_{\text{Ni}} \times M_{\text{Ni}} = 0.05597 \times 58.69 \text{ g}$$

$$m_{\text{Ni}} = 3.285 \text{ g}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answers:** (a) AR = 60, (b) $m_{\text{Ni}} = 3.29$ g.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PVD Sputtering Process — Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block represents the functional flow of a planar magnetron sputtering PVD system, which is the most commonly tested variant in KTU examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vacuum Chamber Initial Pump Down] --> B[Chamber Pressure reaches 10^-6 Torr]
    B --> C[Introduce Argon Gas Working Pressure 5 to 30 mTorr]
    C --> D[Apply Cathode Bias -300 to -1000 V DC]
    D --> E[Glow Discharge Plasma Ignition]
    E --> F[Ar+ Ions Accelerated toward Target Cathode]
    F --> G[Momentum Transfer Knock On Cascade at Target Surface]
    G --> H[Target Atoms Ejected into Vacuum]
    H --> I[Atoms Travel Ballistically to Substrate]
    I --> J[Substrate Rotation for Uniformity]
    J --> K[Nucleation and Film Growth on Substrate]
    K --> L[Final PVD Coating Thickness 1 to 5 micrometer]
    M[Magnetron Array behind Target] --> E
    N[Cryopump and Turbopump] --> A
```

## 4.2 Thermal Evaporation PVD — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Power Supply DC or AC] --> B[Heating Element W Ta or e beam gun]
    B --> C[Source Material in Crucible or hearth]
    C --> D[Atomization at High Vacuum 10^-5 to 10^-6 Torr]
    D --> E[Vapor Plume with Cosine Distribution]
    E --> F[Substrate Holder with optional rotation]
    F --> G[Quartz Crystal Monitor for rate control]
    G --> H[Shutter open close mechanism]
    H --> I[Thin Film Deposition on Substrate]
    J[Vacuum System Diffusion and Rotary pump] --> A
    K[Heater Stage 200 to 500 degree C] --> F
```

## 4.3 LIGA Process — Three-Stage Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: PMMA Sheet bonded to Conductive Substrate] --> A2[Step 1 Lithographie X ray exposure via synchrotron]
    A2 --> A3[X ray Mask defines absorber pattern on Be or Ti membrane]
    A3 --> A4[Exposed PMMA undergoes chain scission]
    A4 --> A5[Step 2 Development: GG developer dissolves exposed PMMA]
    A5 --> A6[Negative mold cavities with HAR sidewalls]
    A6 --> A7[Step 2 GalvanoforElectroforming: Ni sulfamate bath]
    A7 --> A8[Current density 1 to 5 A per square decimeter]
    A8 --> A9[Ni grows in cavities overflows to form backing plate]
    A9 --> A10[PMMA stripped by solvent or plasma ashing]
    A10 --> A11[Free standing Metal Master Insert]
    A11 --> A12[Step 3 Abformung: Hot embossing or injection molding]
    A12 --> A13[Mass produced Polymer Micro replicas]
    A13 --> A14[End: LIGA Component Ready for MEMS or Micro system]
    B1[Synchrotron Radiation Source 0.2 to 0.5 nm] --> A2
    B2[Mask Aligner with X ray beamline] --> A3
    B3[DC Power Supply for electroforming] --> A8
```

flowchart TB

    end
