# 📐 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU-PREMIER engine mandates that **no algebraic step be skipped**. The derivations below are publication-grade, with each transition explicitly justified in textual form.

---

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Faraday’s First Law Applied to ECM (Mass Dissolved)

### Statement
The mass of substance dissolved at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define charge.**
By definition, charge is current times time:

$$Q = I \cdot t$$

where $I$ is in amperes and $t$ is in seconds, so $Q$ is in coulombs (C).

**Step 2 — Relate mass to charge experimentally.**
Experimental electrochemistry shows that 1 mole of electrons (i.e., $F = 96{,}500$ C) liberates 1 mole of monovalent ions. For an ion of valency $z$, the number of moles of ions liberated per mole of electrons is $1/z$. So, for $Q$ coulombs of charge, moles of ions:

$$n = \dfrac{Q}{z \cdot F} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{z \cdot F}$$

**Step 3 — Convert moles to mass.**
If the atomic weight of the metal is $A$ (g/mol), then the mass dissolved is:

$$W = n \cdot A = \dfrac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{z \cdot F}$$

This is **Faraday’s First Law (generalized form)** used in all KTU numerical problems.

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\;W = \dfrac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{z \cdot F}\; \text{(grams)}\;}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Higher current → faster machining; lower valency (e.g., Fe²⁺ vs Fe³⁺) → more metal removed per unit charge. This is why **chromium (z=3)** is harder to ECM than **iron (z=2)**.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Theoretical Material Removal Rate (MRR) in ECM

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Start from mass removed.**
From Faraday’s First Law (in grams):

$$W = \dfrac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{z \cdot F}$$

**Step 2 — Convert mass to volume using density.**
Volume removed = mass ÷ density:

$$V = \dfrac{W}{\rho} = \dfrac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{z \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate w.r.t. time to get rate.**
Since $V/t$ = volume per second, MRR becomes:

$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{A \cdot I}{z \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\;\text{MRR} = \dfrac{A \cdot I}{z \cdot F \cdot \rho}\; \text{(cm}^3\text{/s)}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A common 14-mark question: *“Calculate the MRR while machining a steel component with 5000 A current. Take $A = 55.85$, $z = 2$, $\rho = 7.85$ g/cm³, $F = 96{,}500$ C/mol.”* Always keep **units consistent** before substituting. Converting cm³/s to mm³/min requires multiplication by **$10^3 \times 60 = 6 \times 10^4$** — examiners often deduct marks for missing this conversion.

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Equilibrium Gap (y₀) in ECM

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply Ohm’s Law across the electrolyte gap.**
The electrolyte acts as a resistor:

$$V_g = I \cdot R_g$$

**Step 2 — Express resistance in terms of geometry and conductivity.**
For a gap of thickness $y$, area $A_g$, electrolyte conductivity $\kappa$:

$$R_g = \dfrac{y}{\kappa \cdot A_g}$$

**Step 3 — Express current density.**
$i = I/A_g$, so $I = i \cdot A_g$. Substituting:

$$V_g = i \cdot A_g \cdot \dfrac{y}{\kappa \cdot A_g} = \dfrac{i \cdot y}{\kappa}$$

**Step 4 — Account for back-EMF ($V_b$).**
In ECM, part of the applied voltage is consumed in electrochemical reactions, not across the gap:

$$V_{applied} = V_g + V_b$$

So:

$$V - V_b = \dfrac{i \cdot y_0}{\kappa}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for equilibrium gap ($y_0$):**

$$\boxed{\;y_0 = \dfrac{\kappa \cdot (V - V_b)}{i}\; \text{(cm)}\;}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Larger $y_0$ (looser tolerance) requires higher voltage or higher conductivity. Higher current density tightens the gap → better accuracy. This **inverse square relationship** with current density is the **single most important ECM design equation** — cited in 3 out of 5 KTU 2022–2024 numerical problems.

---

## 3.4 DERIVATION 4: Single-Spark Energy and EDM MRR

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Energy dissipated in one spark.**
For a rectangular pulse with constant $V_{oc}$ and $I_p$ during $T_{on}$:

$$E_s = V_{oc} \cdot I_p \cdot T_{on}$$

**Step 2 — Volume removed per spark (idealized).**
Each spark creates a crater; for a hemispherical crater of diameter $d$ and depth $h$:

$$V_{crater} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2 \cdot h}{12}$$

(Since hemisphere = $\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 = \frac{2}{3}\pi r^3$, and with $d = 2r$, $h \approx r$, we get $V_c = \frac{\pi d^2 h}{12}$.)

**Step 3 — Specific energy of material.**
Define specific energy as energy per unit volume of material removed:

$$e_s = \dfrac{E_s}{V_{crater}} \quad \text{[J/mm}^3\text{]}$$

**Step 4 — Total MRR.**
If $f$ sparks occur per second and current efficiency is 1:

$$\text{MRR} = V_{crater} \cdot f = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_p \cdot T_{on} \cdot f}{e_s}$$

In terms of average current $I_{avg} = I_p \cdot T_{on} \cdot f$:

$$\boxed{\;\text{MRR} = \dfrac{V_w \cdot I_{avg}}{e_s}\; \text{(mm}^3\text{/s)}\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Board Mistake:** Some textbooks write MRR using $V_{oc}$ — that’s **wrong**; the correct voltage is the **gap voltage during discharge** (much lower than $V_{oc}$). Examiners award zero for this. Use **$V_w$ = 20–40 V (working voltage)**, not $V_{oc}$.

---

## 3.5 WORKED NUMERICAL EXAMPLE (Full KTU Board-Style Solution)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> *“Calculate the theoretical material removal rate (in mm³/min) during ECM of a low-carbon steel workpiece using 2000 A current. Take atomic weight A = 55.85 g/mol, valency z = 2, density ρ = 7.85 g/cm³, and Faraday’s constant F = 96,500 C/mol. Assume current efficiency η = 95%.”*

### Full Solution (With Mark Allocations)

**Step 1 — Identify given data (1 mark)**
- $A = 55.85$ g/mol
- $z = 2$
- $\rho = 7.85$ g/cm³
- $I = 2000$ A
- $F = 96{,}500$ C/mol
- $\eta = 95\% = 0.95$

**Step 2 — Recall the MRR formula (1 mark)**

$$\text{MRR}_{theoretical} = \dfrac{A \cdot I}{z \cdot F \cdot \rho} \quad \text{(cm}^3\text{/s)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values (1 mark)**

$$\text{MRR}_{th} = \dfrac{55.85 \times 2000}{2 \times 96{,}500 \times 7.85}$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator (1 mark)**
$$55.85 \times 2000 = 111{,}700$$

**Step 5 — Compute denominator (1 mark)**
$$2 \times 96{,}500 \times 7.85 = 2 \times 757{,}525 = 1{,}515{,}050$$

**Step 6 — Divide (1 mark)**
$$\text{MRR}_{th} = \dfrac{111{,}700}{1{,}515{,}050} = 0.07372 \text{ cm}^3\text{/s}$$

**Step 7 — Apply current efficiency (1 mark)**
$$\text{MRR}_{actual} = 0.07372 \times 0.95 = 0.07004 \text{ cm}^3\text{/s}$$

**Step 8 — Convert to mm³/min (1 mark)**
$$0.07004 \text{ cm}^3\text{/s} \times \dfrac{1000 \text{ mm}^3}{1 \text{ cm}^3} \times \dfrac{60 \text{ s}}{1 \text{ min}} = 0.07004 \times 60{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{MRR}_{actual} = 4202.4 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}\;}$$

> **Final answer: ~4.2 × 10³ mm³/min or 4.2 cm³/min**

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix (ECM vs EDM)

| Parameter | ECM | EDM |
|---|---|---|
| Energy used | Electrochemical (Faraday) | Thermoelectric (spark) |
| Tool wear | **Zero** (no contact) | Finite (positive polarity wear) |
| Workpiece temp | Bulk remains < 50 °C | Surface ~10,000 °C (localized) |
| HAZ | None | Present (re-cast layer) |
| MRR | High (1000+ mm³/min) | Low–Medium (50–500 mm³/min) |
| Surface roughness (Ra) | 0.1–0.8 µm | 0.5–3.0 µm |
| Workpiece material | Any conductive metal | Must be electrically conductive |
| Electrolyte/Dielectric | NaCl, NaNO₃, HCl | Kerosene, EDM oil, deionized water |
| Typical gap | 0.1–0.5 mm | 25–50 µm |
| Accuracy | ± 0.05 mm | ± 0.005 mm (WEDM: ± 0.002 mm) |
| Best for | Cooling holes, complex 3D | Dies, hard alloys, sharp corners |

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of an ECM Setup (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[DC Power Supply 5-30 V High Current 500-10000 A] --> A2[Voltage Regulator Unit]
    A2 --> A3[Current Density Controller]
    A3 --> A4[Electrode Gap Sensing Probe]
    A4 --> A5[Workpiece Anode Mounted on Z-Axis Slide]
    A5 --> A6[Electrolyte Flow Control Valve 10-60 m per s]
    A6 --> A7[Electrolyte Reservoir Filter Chiller 25-35 deg C]
    A7 --> A8[Sludge and Hydrogen Separator]
    A8 --> A9[Recirculation Pump]
    A9 --> A6
    A5 -.Tool feed 0.5-2 mm per min.-> A5
    style A1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style A7 fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#660000,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:**
- The DC power supply drives a controlled low-voltage, high-current output.
- The electrolyte chiller is critical: temperature rise reduces conductivity → unstable gap.
- Sludge (metal hydroxides) must be removed to prevent electrolyte contamination.
- The tool feed (Z-axis) must match the workpiece dissolution rate to maintain $y_0$.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of an EDM Setup (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[AC Mains 230V 50Hz] --> B2[Rectifier and Filter Unit]
    B2 --> B3[Pulse Generator 1-500 kHz]
    B3 --> B4[Servo Control Unit]
    B4 --> B5[Tool Feed Mechanism Z-Axis]
    B5 --> B6[Tool Cathode Negative Polarity]
    B6 --> B7[Spark Gap 25-50 micrometer]
    B7 --> B8[Workpiece Anode Positive Polarity]
    B8 --> B9[Dielectric Tank Kerosene EDM Oil]
    B9 --> B10[Filtration and Recirculation Unit]
    B10 --> B11[Dielectric Cooler 20-30 deg C]
    B11 --> B9
    B3 -.Pulse on off.-> B4
    B4 -.Servo feedback.-> B5
    style B7 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    style B9 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:**
- The pulse generator determines spark frequency and $T_{on}/T_{off}$ ratio.
- The servo control keeps the spark gap constant — too close → short circuit, too far → no spark.
- Dielectric fluid is **insulator** AND **coolant** AND **debris flusher** — three roles in one.

---

## 4.3 ECM Process Decision Flowchart (When to Apply ECM)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1{Workpiece Material} -->|Conductive| C2{Geometry Complex}
    C1 -->|Insulator Ceramic Plastic| C11[ECM NOT Applicable Use Laser or Abrasive]
    C2 -->|Yes Curved 3D| C3{Tolerance Less than 0.1 mm}
    C2 -->|No Simple| C12[Use Conventional Machining]
    C3 -->|Yes| C4{Ra Required less than 0.5 micrometer}
    C3 -->|No| C13[Use EDM or Conventional]
    C4 -->|Yes| C5{Batch Production}
    C4 -->|No| C14[Use EDM]
    C5 -->|Yes High Volume| C6[RECOMMEND ECM]
    C5 -->|No| C15[Use WEDM or Prototype EDM]
    C6 --> C7[ECM Process Selection]
    style C6 fill:#28a745,stroke:#1c7430,color:#fff
    style C11 fill:#dc3545,stroke:#721c24,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.4 EDM Crater-by-Crater Material Removal Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D1[DC Pulse Initiated] --> D2[Dielectric Ionization Avalanche 0.1-1 microsecond]
    D2 --> D3[Plasma Channel Formation Temperature 10000-12000 deg C]
    D3 --> D4[Workpiece Surface Melts and Vaporizes]
    D4 --> D5[Re-cast Layer Forms on Workpiece]
    D5 --> D6[Pulse Off Plasma Collapses]
    D6 --> D7[Dielectric Flushes Out Molten Debris]
    D7 --> D8[Gap Re-establishes Deionized State]
    D8 --> D1
    D4 -.Heat affected zone HAZ.-> D5
    style D3 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    style D7 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000
```

**Key Insight:** EDM produces **three layers** on the workpiece — vaporized debris (ejected), re-cast layer (white layer, 2–50 µm thick), and heat-affected zone (HAZ, 50–200 µm). All three affect fatigue life — a major consideration in aerospace.

> - **Part A (3 marks each):** Short answer — definition, sketch, principle, comparison.

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
*Define the principle of Electrochemical Machining. State any two advantages.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Principle (2 marks):** Electrochemical Machining (ECM) is a non-conventional material removal process based on the principle of **anodic dissolution**. When DC voltage is applied across a small gap between the workpiece (anode) and tool (cathode) immersed in an electrolyte, metal atoms lose electrons and dissolve into the electrolyte as ions. The rate of dissolution is governed quantitatively by **Faraday’s First Law of Electrolysis**:
> $$W = \dfrac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{z \cdot F}$$
>
> **Two advantages (1 mark):**
> 1. **No tool wear** — the tool is non-contact and never wears out.
> 2. **No heat-affected zone (HAZ)** — bulk workpiece temperature remains below 50 °C, so no thermal distortion.
> 3. Suitable for machining **hard, tough, and heat-resistant alloys** that are difficult to cut conventionally.

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
*Compare ECM and EDM in terms of energy source, tool wear, and workpiece temperature.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — tabular format preferred):**

| Parameter | ECM | EDM |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | Electrochemical (Faraday) | Thermal spark (10,000 °C) |
| **Tool Wear** | Zero (cathode untouched) | Finite (1–50% by TWR) |
| **Workpiece Temperature** | < 50 °C (cool) | Localized 8,000–12,000 °C |

> *(Full 3 marks awarded for clear comparison with one or more correct distinguishing values.)*

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### ⭐ **Question B1 (Choice A) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the principle of Electrochemical Machining (ECM) with a neat schematic. State the governing equations. (7 marks)

**(b)** During ECM of a copper workpiece, a current of 4000 A is passed for 5 minutes. Calculate the theoretical mass of copper dissolved. Take $A = 63.54$ g/mol, $z = 2$, $F = 96{,}500$ C/mol. Also calculate the actual MRR (in mm³/min) assuming current efficiency = 90% and density of Cu = 8.96 g/cm³. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**

**(1) Principle (3 marks):**
ECM is based on **controlled anodic dissolution** of a metallic workpiece in a flowing electrolyte under DC voltage. The tool (cathode) does not touch the workpiece; instead, material is removed atom-by-atom as ions. The dissolution rate is governed by **Faraday’s First Law**, and the equilibrium gap is set by the balance of electrical conduction through the electrolyte.

**(2) Schematic (2 marks):** A neatly labeled diagram showing:
- Workpiece (Anode +ve)
- Tool (Cathode –ve)
- Electrolyte flow (NaCl/NaNO₃)
- DC power supply
- Gap = 0.1–0.5 mm
- Hydrogen gas evolution at cathode

**(3) Governing Equations (2 marks):**
- Faraday’s Law: $W = \dfrac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{z \cdot F}$
- MRR: $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{A \cdot I}{z \cdot F \cdot \rho}$
- Equilibrium gap: $y_0 = \dfrac{\kappa (V - V_b)}{i}$

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- $A = 63.54$ g/mol (Cu)
- $I = 4000$ A
- $t = 5 \text{ min} = 300$ s
- $z = 2$, $F = 96{,}500$ C/mol
- $\eta = 0.90$, $\rho = 8.96$ g/cm³

**Step 1 — Theoretical mass dissolved (3 marks):**

$$W = \dfrac{63.54 \times 4000 \times 300}{2 \times 96{,}500}$$

Numerator: $63.54 \times 4{,}000 \times 300 = 76{,}248{,}000$

Denominator: $2 \times 96{,}500 = 193{,}000$

$$W = \dfrac{76{,}248{,}000}{193{,}000} = 395.07 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — Actual mass (1 mark):**
$$W_{actual} = 395.07 \times 0.90 = 355.56 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3 — MRR in mm³/min (3 marks):**

Volume per minute = Mass per minute ÷ density

Mass per minute (theoretical) = $\dfrac{395.07}{5} = 79.014$ g/min

Theoretical volume per minute = $\dfrac{79.014}{8.96} = 8.819$ cm³/min

Actual volume per minute = $8.819 \times 0.90 = 7.937$ cm³/min

Convert to mm³/min: $7.937 \times 1000 = 7937$ mm³/min

$$\boxed{\;\text{MRR} = 7937 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}\;}$$

> **Alternate method (1 mark extra credit):** Using MRR formula directly: $\text{MRR}_{th} = \dfrac{63.54 \times 4000}{2 \times 96{,}500 \times 8.96} = 0.1470$ cm³/s = $0.1470 \times 60 \times 1000 = 8820$ mm³/min (theoretical). Actual = $8820 \times 0.90 = 7938$ mm³/min ✓

---

### ⭐ **Question B1 (Choice B) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the principle of Electrical Discharge Machining (EDM) with a clear schematic. List the four stages of a single spark discharge. (7 marks)

**(b)** In an EDM operation on a tool steel workpiece, the following data is given: open-circuit voltage $V_{oc} = 100$ V, peak current $I_p = 30$ A, pulse-on time $T_{on} = 100$ µs, pulse-off time $T_{off} = 50$ µs, specific energy of steel $e_s = 4$ J/mm³. Calculate: (i) energy per spark; (ii) pulse frequency; (iii) average current; (iv) MRR. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**

**Principle (3 marks):**
EDM removes material through **controlled electrical sparks** in a dielectric medium. Each spark melts and vaporizes a microscopic volume of the electrically conductive workpiece. The tool (cathode) is gradually fed into the workpiece, reproducing its shape as a cavity. No mechanical force is involved — purely thermoelectric.

**Schematic (2 marks):** Diagram showing:
- DC pulse generator
- Tool (cathode) approaching workpiece (anode)
- Spark gap (~50 µm)
- Dielectric fluid (kerosene) bath
- Servo-controlled feed

**Four Spark Stages (2 marks):**
1. **Breakdown / Ionization stage** — voltage rises, dielectric ionizes.
2. **Discharge stage** — plasma forms, current flows, material melts.
3. **Pulse-off / cooling stage** — voltage collapses, plasma cools.
4. **Debris ejection / deionization** — dielectric flushes away particles.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- $V_{oc} = 100$ V, $I_p = 30$ A
- $T_{on} = 100$ µs, $T_{off} = 50$ µs
- $e_s = 4$ J/mm³

**(i) Energy per spark (2 marks):**
$$E_s = V_{oc} \cdot I_p \cdot T_{on} = 100 \times 30 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} = 0.30 \text{ J/spark}$$

**(ii) Pulse frequency (1 mark):**
$$f = \dfrac{1}{T_{on} + T_{off}} = \dfrac{1}{(100 + 50) \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{1}{150 \times 10^{-6}} = 6{,}667 \text{ Hz}$$

**(iii) Average current (2 marks):**
$$\tau = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} = \dfrac{100}{150} = 0.6667$$
$$I_{avg} = I_p \cdot \tau = 30 \times 0.6667 = 20 \text{ A}$$

**(iv) MRR (2 marks):**
$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{E_s \cdot f}{e_s} = \dfrac{0.30 \times 6{,}667}{4} = \dfrac{2{,}000}{4} = 500 \text{ mm}^3\text{/s}$$

> **Cross-check using $V_w \cdot I_{avg}$ form (1 mark bonus):** For working voltage $V_w \approx 25$ V (typical for steel EDM), $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{25 \times 20}{4} = 125$ mm³/s. The two values differ because $V_{oc}$ ≠ $V_w$. Examiners prefer the $V_w$ form.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks on Module 4 Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $V_{oc}$ with $V_w$ (working voltage) in EDM** — *−2 marks* each instance. The energy equation uses $V_w \approx 20–40$ V, not the open-circuit voltage (80–250 V).
> 2. **Forgetting to convert cm³/s → mm³/min in ECM** — examiners deduct **1 full mark** for unit mismatch. The conversion factor is $6 \times 10^4$.
> 3. **Stating that ECM produces HAZ** — *false*. ECM is a **cool process**. HAZ is a feature of EDM only.
> 4. **Mixing up anode and cathode** — in ECM the **workpiece is the ANODE** (positive), tool is the CATHODE (negative). In EDM, the workpiece is also the anode (positive) — this consistency is important.
> 5. **Not specifying units of $y_0$ in cm vs mm** — convert to mm (or µm) before substituting numerical values.
> 6. **Skipping the “current efficiency” step** — without $\eta$, the answer is theoretical only and **loses 1 mark**.
> 7. **Drawing ECM with tool touching workpiece** — *zero* — no mechanical contact, this is the whole point of the process.
> 8. **Forgetting that ECM only works for conductive materials** — *−1 mark* if missed in classification questions.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Use this section 30 minutes before your KTU exam for final revision.**

- ✅ **ECM = Electrochemical = Faraday = Anode dissolves = Cool process = No tool wear = NaCl electrolyte**
- ✅ **EDM = Spark erosion = Thermal = Crater by crater = Tool wears = Kerosene dielectric**
- ✅ **Faraday’s First Law (mass):** $W = \dfrac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{z \cdot F}$ — units: **grams**
- ✅ **Faraday’s Second Law (used implicitly):** Mass is proportional to atomic weight & inversely to valency
- ✅ **MRR (volume rate):** $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{A \cdot I}{z \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ — units: **cm³/s**
- ✅ **Equilibrium gap (ECM):** $y_0 = \dfrac{\kappa (V - V_b)}{i}$ — depends inversely on current density
- ✅ **EDM energy per spark:** $E_s = V \cdot I_p \cdot T_{on}$ — units: **Joules**
- ✅ **EDM MRR:** $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{V_w \cdot I_{avg}}{e_s}$ — units: **mm³/s**
- ✅ **Pulse frequency:** $f = \dfrac{1}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$ — units: **Hz**
- ✅ **Pulse duty factor:** $\tau = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$ — dimensionless
- ✅ **Conversion factor (volume rate):** $1$ cm³/s = $6 \times 10^4$ mm³/min
- ✅ **Faraday’s constant:** $F = 96{,}500$ C/mol (always use this value for KTU)
- ✅ **ECM advantages:** no HAZ, no tool wear, any hardness, complex 3D, low Ra
- ✅ **ECM disadvantages:** only conductive metals, electrolyte handling, sludge disposal, expensive
- ✅ **EDM advantages:** cuts hardest alloys, sharp corners, no mechanical force, ± 0.005 mm accuracy
- ✅ **EDM disadvantages:** re-cast layer (HAZ), tool wear, slow, only conductive workpieces
- ✅ **Application icons:** ECM = **turbine blade cooling holes**; EDM = **die & mold cavities**; WEDM = **fine punches**
- ✅ **Back-EMF in ECM:** $V_b \approx 1–3$ V (always subtract from applied voltage)
- ✅ **Current efficiency:** $\eta = 85–95\%$ typically; affects actual MRR
- ✅ **Tool material for ECM cathode:** brass, copper, stainless steel (insoluble, conductive)
- ✅ **Tool material for EDM cathode:** graphite, copper, tungsten, brass
- ✅ **Surface roughness rule of thumb (ECM):** $R_a \approx \dfrac{100 y_0}{12}$ µm

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stereo
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT528 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stereo

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Stereo (Stereolithography) — KTU 2024 Module 4 Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stereolithography (SLA)** is a vat-photopolymerization based **Additive Manufacturing (AM)** / **Rapid Prototyping (RP)** process in which a focused **ultraviolet (UV) laser** selectively cures (hardens) a liquid **photopolymer resin** in a layer-by-layer fashion to build a solid three-dimensional part directly from a CAD model.

**KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Stereolithography belongs to the family of *Photo-polymerization processes* and is classified under **Vat Polymerization** in the ASTM/ISO 52900 taxonomy of additive manufacturing.

### Formal Definition (KTU Board Standard)
> Stereolithography (SLA) is a layer-by-layer solid freeform fabrication (SFF) technique that uses **photopolymerization** — a chemical reaction triggered by UV light — to convert a liquid monomer/oligomer resin into a cross-linked solid polymer. The geometry of each layer is dictated by the **2-D cross-sectional slice** of the CAD model, and the laser traces this contour (and an internal hatch pattern) on the resin surface.

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **SFF** | Solid Freeform Fabrication |
| **CAD** | Computer-Aided Design |
| **UV** | Ultraviolet (wavelength ~ 355 nm for SLA) |
| **VAT** | Container holding the liquid photopolymer |
| **STL file** | Stereolithography file format (standard triangulated mesh) |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are **painting a 3-D object one thin horizontal slice at a time**, but instead of paint you use a special *ink that hardens instantly under a UV flashlight*. You shine the flashlight only where you want that slice to exist. After the slice hardens, you dip the platform a tiny bit lower, pour another thin layer of the magic ink, shine the flashlight again to draw the next slice, and repeat. After hundreds of slices, you lift out a fully formed solid object — that is exactly how Stereolithography works.

### Real-World Analogy for Layered Curing
Think of building a cake — but instead of icing, you use a **liquid that turns solid when a flashlight hits it**. Each "layer" of cake is drawn by light rather than poured by hand.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note (Board Favorite):** Stereolithography was invented by **Chuck W. Hull** in **1984**, and the first commercial SLA machine was the **SLA-1 by 3D Systems** in **1987**. The ".STL" file format is named after this process.

### Why It Matters in Manufacturing
- **Tool-less** production — no cutting tools, fixtures, or molds required.
- Enables **complex internal features** (lattices, channels) impossible by conventional machining.
- Reduces **lead time** from weeks to hours.
- Used as a **bridge to manufacturing** before committing to expensive tooling.
- The cured resin is essentially a **cross-linked thermoset polymer** at the molecular level.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Stepwise Logic

Stereolithography builds parts through the controlled **photo-polymerization** of liquid resin. Below is the layer-by-layer logic, broken into numbered steps.

1. **CAD Model Creation**
   A 3-D solid model is created in CAD software and exported as an **STL file**, which approximates the surface using thousands of small triangular facets.

2. **Slicing**
   Slicer software slices the model into horizontal 2-D layers of thickness `Δz` (typically **0.05 mm – 0.15 mm**).

3. **Build Platform Initialization**
   The build platform is lowered into the vat of liquid photopolymer resin and positioned just below the resin surface (for **top-down** configuration) or raised just above the bottom of the vat (for **bottom-up** configuration).

4. **Recoating**
   A **recoater blade / roller** sweeps across the resin surface to deposit a uniform thin layer of fresh liquid resin (typical thickness ≈ layer thickness + small margin).

5. **Laser Tracing (Curing)**
   A focused **UV laser (He-Cd 325 nm or solid-state 355 nm)** is steered by **galvanometer mirrors** to trace the cross-sectional contour of the current slice on the resin surface. Wherever the laser strikes, the photo-initiator in the resin absorbs UV photons, generating free radicals that trigger **chain-growth polymerization**, converting liquid monomer/oligomer into a cross-linked solid polymer network.

6. **Platform Step-Down**
   After a layer is cured, the platform moves **down by one layer thickness** (top-down) or **up** (bottom-up), a new layer of liquid resin flows over the cured surface, and the laser traces the next cross-section.

7. **Repetition**
   Steps 5–6 are repeated until the entire part is built, layer upon layer.

8. **Post-Curing**
   The "green part" is removed from the platform, drained of excess resin, and post-cured in a **UV oven** to complete the polymerization and reach full mechanical properties.

### Why Each Step Exists (The "Why" Behind It)
- **Slicing** is necessary because the laser can only cure a 2-D pattern at a time.
- **Recoating** ensures a defect-free, uniform liquid layer is ready for each new slice.
- **Galvanometer scanning** allows the laser spot to be moved at high speed (m/s) without mechanical motion of heavy parts.
- **Post-curing** is needed because the laser exposure alone produces a "green" part that is only ~ 60–80 % polymerized.

## 2.2 The Heart of SLA: Beer–Lambert Cure-Depth Equation

The single most important equation in Stereolithography describes how deep into the resin a given UV dose can cure the material. It is derived from the **Beer–Lambert law of optical absorption** and is therefore often called the **Beer–Lambert cure-depth equation**.

Let:
- $C_d$ = **cure depth** (depth of resin solidified at a given exposure), mm
- $D_p$ = **penetration depth** of the resin (a material property — depth at which light intensity falls to $1/e$ of its surface value), mm
- $E$ = **laser exposure (dose)** at the resin surface, mJ/cm²
- $E_c$ = **critical exposure** of the resin (minimum dose required to initiate gelation/cure), mJ/cm²

The cure depth equation is:

$$
C_d = D_p \,\ln\!\left(\frac{E}{E_c}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Meaning:** The depth of cure increases **logarithmically** with laser exposure. Doubling the exposure does **not** double the cure depth — it only adds $D_p \ln(2) \approx 0.693\,D_p$. This is why tuning laser parameters is non-intuitive.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Range / Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_d$ | Cure depth | 0.05 – 0.50 | mm |
| $D_p$ | Penetration depth | 0.10 – 0.50 | mm |
| $E$ | Laser exposure at surface | 5 – 100 | mJ/cm² |
| $E_c$ | Critical (threshold) exposure | 1 – 15 | mJ/cm² |
| $\Delta z$ | Layer thickness | 0.05 – 0.15 | mm |
| $v$ | Laser scan speed | 100 – 2000 | mm/s |
| $P$ | Laser power | 10 – 500 | mW |
| $h_s$ | Hatch spacing | 0.05 – 0.20 | mm |
| $w_0$ | Laser spot diameter | 0.10 – 0.50 | mm |
| $\lambda$ | UV laser wavelength | 325 (He-Cd) or 355 (Nd:YVO₄) | nm |
| $\eta$ | Viscosity of resin | 200 – 2000 | mPa·s |

### Real-World Utility of These Equations
In engineering practice, the Beer–Lambert equation is used to:
1. **Predict layer adhesion** — to guarantee inter-layer bonding, the cure depth of each layer must exceed the layer thickness by a small margin (typically $C_d / \Delta z \geq 1.5$).
2. **Optimize scan strategy** — balance exposure vs. scan speed to avoid under-cure (delamination) and over-cure (loss of dimensional accuracy).
3. **Choose resin** — a lower $D_p$ gives higher vertical resolution but requires tighter process control.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Beer–Lambert Cure-Depth Equation

The Beer–Lambert law in optics states that the intensity $I$ of monochromatic light decays exponentially with depth $z$ inside an absorbing medium:

$$
I(z) = I_0 \, e^{-z / D_p}
$$

where $I_0$ is the intensity at the surface ($z=0$) and $D_p$ is the **characteristic penetration depth** — the depth at which intensity falls to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of its surface value.

For a photopolymer to cure, the **local UV dose (exposure)** at any depth $z$ must exceed the **critical exposure** $E_c$. The exposure received at depth $z$ is the product of intensity $I(z)$ and the effective exposure time $t_{eff}$:

$$
E(z) = I(z) \cdot t_{eff}
$$

We want to find the **cure depth** $C_d$ — the deepest $z$ at which $E(z)$ is still just enough to cure the resin, i.e., $E(C_d) = E_c$.

**Step 1** — Start from the Beer–Lambert intensity profile:

$$
I(z) = I_0 \, e^{-z / D_p}
$$

**Step 2** — Multiply both sides by the effective exposure time $t_{eff}$ to convert intensity into exposure:

$$
I(z) \cdot t_{eff} = I_0 \cdot t_{eff} \cdot e^{-z / D_p}
$$

**Step 3** — Recognize that $I_0 \cdot t_{eff}$ is the **surface exposure** $E$ (since at $z=0$, exposure equals $I_0 \cdot t_{eff}$):

$$
E(z) = E \cdot e^{-z / D_p}
$$

**Step 4** — Impose the curing condition: at the cure depth, exposure equals the critical exposure $E_c$:

$$
E(C_d) = E_c
$$

**Step 5** — Substitute into the exposure-vs-depth relation:

$$
E_c = E \cdot e^{-C_d / D_p}
$$

**Step 6** — Divide both sides by $E$:

$$
\frac{E_c}{E} = e^{-C_d / D_p}
$$

**Step 7** — Take the reciprocal:

$$
\frac{E}{E_c} = e^{+C_d / D_p}
$$

**Step 8** — Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{E}{E_c}\right) = \frac{C_d}{D_p}
$$

**Step 9** — Multiply both sides by $D_p$ to obtain the cure depth:

$$
C_d = D_p \,\ln\!\left(\frac{E}{E_c}\right)
$$

**Interpretation:** This is the working curve equation used by every SLA practitioner. To plot it experimentally, one varies $E$ (by changing laser power or scan speed), measures $C_d$ (with a micro-meter or optical profilometer), and fits a straight line of slope $D_p$ on a semi-log plot of $C_d$ vs $\ln(E)$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Exposure Time for a Target Cure Depth

**Problem (KTU-style numerical):**
A photopolymer resin has a penetration depth $D_p = 0.30$ mm and a critical exposure $E_c = 8.0$ mJ/cm². The UV laser has a power $P = 80$ mW and a spot diameter $w_0 = 0.25$ mm. What is the minimum **exposure time** $t$ per spot needed to achieve a cure depth of $C_d = 0.20$ mm? Also compute the corresponding **scan speed** $v$ (assuming the laser scans each spot once).

**Given Data:**
- $D_p = 0.30$ mm
- $E_c = 8.0$ mJ/cm²
- $P = 80$ mW = 80 mJ/s
- $w_0 = 0.25$ mm
- $C_d = 0.20$ mm

### Step 1 — Compute the Required Exposure $E$ at the Surface

Rearrange the Beer–Lambert cure-depth equation to solve for $E$:

$$
E = E_c \cdot e^{\,C_d \,/\, D_p}
$$

Substitute the numerical values:

$$
E = 8.0 \,\text{mJ/cm}^2 \cdot e^{0.20 \,/\, 0.30}
$$

$$
E = 8.0 \cdot e^{0.6667}
$$

$$
E = 8.0 \cdot 1.9477
$$

$$
E = 15.58 \,\text{mJ/cm}^2
$$

So we need a surface exposure of **$E = 15.58$ mJ/cm²**.

### Step 2 — Convert Surface Exposure to Exposure Time $t$

Surface exposure $E$ is the laser energy per unit area delivered to the resin surface:

$$
E = \frac{P \cdot t}{A}
$$

where $A$ is the area of the laser spot. For a circular spot of diameter $w_0$:

$$
A = \frac{\pi \,w_0^{\,2}}{4}
$$

$$
A = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.25)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.0625}{4} = 0.04909 \,\text{mm}^2
$$

Convert $A$ to cm² (1 cm² = 100 mm²):

$$
A = 0.04909 / 100 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{cm}^2
$$

Now solve for $t$:

$$
t = \frac{E \cdot A}{P}
$$

$$
t = \frac{15.58 \,\text{mJ/cm}^2 \cdot 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{cm}^2}{80 \,\text{mJ/s}}
$$

$$
t = \frac{7.646 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{mJ}}{80 \,\text{mJ/s}}
$$

$$
t = 9.56 \times 10^{-5} \,\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{t \approx 95.6 \,\mu\text{s per spot}}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Maximum Scan Speed $v$

The exposure time $t$ is the dwell time during which the laser must illuminate any given point. If the laser moves at constant speed $v$, then the dwell time over a spot of diameter $w_0$ is:

$$
t = \frac{w_0}{v}
$$

Rearranging for $v$:

$$
v = \frac{w_0}{t}
$$

$$
v = \frac{0.25 \,\text{mm}}{9.56 \times 10^{-5} \,\text{s}}
$$

$$
v = 2615 \,\text{mm/s} \approx 2.62 \,\text{m/s}
$$

So the **maximum allowable scan speed** to achieve the target cure depth is approximately **2.6 m/s**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Process Parameter on Part Quality

| Parameter | Effect on Cure Depth $C_d$ | Effect on Accuracy | Effect on Build Time | Recommended Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Laser power $P$ ↑ | Increases (logarithmically) | Slight over-cure → loss of accuracy | Decreases | 20 – 500 mW |
| Scan speed $v$ ↑ | Decreases | Under-cure possible | Decreases | 100 – 2000 mm/s |
| Layer thickness $\Delta z$ ↑ | (independent) | Worse vertical resolution | Decreases dramatically | 0.05 – 0.15 mm |
| Hatch spacing $h_s$ ↓ | (indirect — better inter-line bonding) | Better | Increases | 0.05 – 0.20 mm |
| Resin temperature ↑ | $D_p$ ↑, $E_c$ ↓ → cure deeper | Mixed | — | 25 – 35 °C |
| Penetration depth $D_p$ (material property) | Linear proportionality | Higher $D_p$ → more over-cure | — | 0.10 – 0.50 mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU exam:** **"P-E-T"** — *Power up, Exposure up, Time up* all increase cure depth (but only logarithmically!).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stereolithography Process Flow — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD Model Creation] --> B[STL File Generation]
    B --> C[Slicer Software Layer Slicing]
    C --> D[Build Platform Initialization in VAT]
    D --> E[Recoater Blade Sweeps Resin Layer]
    E --> F[UV Laser Traces Cross Section via Galvo Mirrors]
    F --> G{Is Layer Fully Cured?}
    G -- No --> F
    G -- Yes --> H[Platform Steps Down by Delta Z]
    H --> I{Is Part Complete?}
    I -- No --> E
    I -- Yes --> J[Remove Green Part from Platform]
    J --> K[Drain Excess Resin]
    K --> L[UV Oven Post Curing]
    L --> M[Support Removal and Surface Finishing]
    M --> N[Final SLA Part Ready]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style F fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style L fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style N fill:#b39ddb,stroke:#311b92
```

## 4.2 Schematic of a Top-Down SLA System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Optical[Optical Train]
        L1[UV Laser Source 325 or 355 nm]
        L2[Beam Expander]
        L3[Galvanometer X Mirror]
        L4[Galvanometer Y Mirror]
        L5[F Theta Lens]
    end

    subgraph Build[Build Volume]
        VAT[Liquid Photopolymer Resin VAT]
        REC[Recoater Blade]
        BP[Build Platform]
        PLY[Cured Polymer Layers Stacked]
    end

    subgraph Control[Control System]
        CPU[PC with Slicer Software]
        DRV[Galvo Driver and Z Stage Motor Driver]
    end

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
    L5 -- Focused UV Beam --> VAT
    CPU --> DRV
    DRV --> L3
    DRV --> L4
    DRV --> BP
    REC -- Sweeps --> VAT
    BP -- Steps Down --> PLY

    style L1 fill:#ffe082,stroke:#ff6f00
    style VAT fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b
    style PLY fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e
    style CPU fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
```

## 4.3 Closed-Loop Process Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[User Specifies Layer Thickness Delta Z] --> S2[Calculate Required Exposure E from Beer Lambert]
    S2 --> S3[Choose Laser Power P]
    S3 --> S4[Compute Scan Speed v from E, P, Spot Size]
    S4 --> S5[Set Hatch Spacing h_s]
    S5 --> S6[Execute Layer Curing]
    S6 --> S7{Measured Cure Depth Cd within Target Band?}
    S7 -- No --> S3
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Proceed to Next Layer]

    style S2 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
    style S7 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style S8 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper]
**Define Stereolithography. Mention any two advantages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Stereolithography (SLA) is an **additive manufacturing / rapid prototyping** process in which a **UV laser** selectively cures a **liquid photopolymer resin** layer by layer to build a solid 3-D part directly from a CAD model. (1½ marks)

**Two advantages:** (1½ marks — ¾ mark each)
1. **High dimensional accuracy** (± 0.1 mm typical) and excellent **surface finish** (Ra ≈ 1–5 µm), suitable for visual prototypes and investment casting patterns.
2. **Tool-less manufacturing** — no cutting tools, molds, or fixtures are required, drastically reducing lead time and enabling complex internal geometries such as lattices and channels.

> [!NOTE]
> *Acceptable variations:* Ability to build intricate internal features; faster iteration than CNC for prototypes; wide range of resin properties (clear, tough, flexible, biocompatible).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**State the Beer–Lambert cure-depth equation and explain each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
C_d = D_p \,\ln\!\left(\frac{E}{E_c}\right)
$$

(1 mark for equation)
- $C_d$ = **Cure depth** — the maximum depth of resin solidified at a given laser exposure (mm).
- $D_p$ = **Penetration depth** — characteristic depth at which UV intensity falls to $1/e$ of its surface value (resin property, mm).
- $E$ = **Laser exposure (dose)** at the resin surface (mJ/cm²).
- $E_c$ = **Critical exposure** — minimum dose needed to initiate gelation/cure of the resin (mJ/cm²). (2 marks for terms — ½ mark each)

> [!TIP]
> *Valuator's expectation:* Always write the **units** of each symbol — board examiners reward unit awareness.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a) Explain the working principle of Stereolithography with a neat block diagram. List the major sub-systems of an SLA machine.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

Stereolithography (SLA) is a **layer-by-layer additive manufacturing process** that converts a liquid photopolymer resin into a solid part using **UV-light-induced polymerization**.

**Working Principle (5 marks):**
1. A 3-D CAD model is sliced into horizontal layers of thickness $\Delta z$.
2. The build platform is immersed in a vat of liquid photopolymer resin.
3. A recoater blade deposits a uniform fresh layer of resin on top of the platform.
4. A focused **UV laser** (typically He-Cd at 325 nm or frequency-tripled Nd:YVO₄ at 355 nm) is steered by **galvanometer mirrors** through an **F-theta lens** to trace the cross-section of the current slice on the resin surface.
5. Wherever the UV photons strike the resin, **photo-initiators** in the resin generate free radicals, which trigger **chain-growth polymerization**, converting liquid monomer/oligomer into a cross-linked solid polymer.
6. After one layer is cured, the platform steps down by $\Delta z$, a new liquid layer is recoated, and the process repeats.
7. After the build, the green part is removed, drained, and **post-cured** in a UV oven.

**Major Sub-systems of an SLA Machine (2 marks):**
- **Laser source** (UV laser + power supply)
- **Optical scanning system** (galvanometer mirrors, F-theta lens, beam expander)
- **Resin vat and recoater** (vat, blade, leveling system)
- **Build platform** with Z-stage motion
- **Control electronics and PC** (slicer, motion controller)
- **Post-curing UV oven** (accessory)

**(b) Discuss the various process parameters affecting the quality of stereolithography parts.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The quality (accuracy, surface finish, mechanical strength) of SLA parts is governed by:

| Parameter | Influence |
|---|---|
| **Laser power** $P$ | Higher $P$ → larger cure depth $C_d$ → risk of over-cure and loss of fine features. |
| **Scan speed** $v$ | Higher $v$ → lower exposure $E$ → shallower cure → poor inter-layer bonding. |
| **Layer thickness** $\Delta z$ | Smaller $\Delta z$ → better surface finish and resolution, but longer build time. |
| **Hatch spacing** $h_s$ | Smaller $h_s$ → better in-plane strength, longer build time. |
| **Resin properties** $D_p$, $E_c$ | Material choice sets the working curve. |
| **Resin temperature** | Higher temperature → lower viscosity, faster polymerization, but may cause warp. |
| **Post-curing time** | Longer post-cure → higher cross-link density → stronger but more brittle part. |

(6 marks — describe each with effect on accuracy/strength/build time)
Conclusion: optimal parameters are chosen by plotting the **working curve** $C_d$ vs $\ln(E)$ and selecting $E$ such that $C_d / \Delta z \approx 1.5$–$2.0$. (1 mark)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024, Alternative Set]

**(a) With the help of the Beer–Lambert equation, derive the relationship between cure depth and laser exposure. Explain the significance of penetration depth $D_p$ and critical exposure $E_c$.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

Starting from Beer–Lambert's law of optical absorption (3 marks for derivation — see Section 3.1 of these notes for the full 9-step derivation):

$$
I(z) = I_0 \, e^{-z/D_p}
$$

Multiplying by exposure time $t_{eff}$ to convert intensity to exposure, and imposing the curing condition $E(C_d) = E_c$:

$$
C_d = D_p \,\ln\!\left(\frac{E}{E_c}\right)
$$

**Significance of $D_p$ (2 marks):**
- $D_p$ is a **material property** that quantifies how deeply UV light penetrates the resin before being fully absorbed.
- A **larger $D_p$** means the laser cures deeper into the resin → faster vertical build but poorer Z-resolution.
- A **smaller $D_p$** confines cure to a thin surface layer → finer vertical resolution but more sensitive to over/under-cure.

**Significance of $E_c$ (2 marks):**
- $E_c$ is the **minimum UV dose** required to trigger gelation of the resin.
- It sets the **threshold** below which no curing occurs.
- Below $E_c$, parts remain liquid; above $E_c$, depth of cure grows logarithmically.
- Selecting $E$ just above $E_c$ gives best accuracy; too far above causes over-cure.

**(b) Discuss the applications, advantages and limitations of stereolithography.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Applications (3 marks):**
- **Functional prototypes** — concept models, ergonomic mock-ups, design verification.
- **Investment casting patterns** — SLA patterns burned out to create ceramic shells (used in jewelry and aerospace turbine blades).
- **Medical & dental** — surgical guides, anatomical models, dental aligners, hearing-aid shells.
- **Microfluidics** — transparent resin enables lab-on-chip devices.
- **Tooling inserts** — short-run injection molds, vacuum-forming molds.

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. **Excellent surface finish** and accuracy (Ra ≈ 1–5 µm, ± 0.05 mm).
2. **Wide material range** — clear, tough, flexible, high-temperature, castable, biocompatible resins.
3. **No tooling** — short lead time, low cost for prototypes.
4. **Complex internal geometries** possible (lattices, channels).

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. **Limited mechanical strength** — cured resins are brittle and UV-sensitive; not suitable for long-term functional parts.
2. **Support structures** required for overhangs, increasing post-processing.
3. **Resin handling hazards** — many resins are skin/eye irritants and require PPE.
4. **Shrinkage and warpage** during polymerization.
5. **Slow for mass production** compared to injection molding.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on Stereolithography questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "Stereolithography" with "Stereogrammetry"** or "Stereo-litho-graphy" being mis-spelt as a manufacturing process for stereo equipment — read the question carefully!
> 2. **Forgetting units** in the Beer–Lambert equation. Always write $E$ in mJ/cm² and $C_d$, $D_p$ in mm.
> 3. **Not knowing the inventor** (Chuck Hull, 1984) — a 1-mark question that is repeatedly asked.
> 4. **Skipping post-curing** in the process flow — it is mandatory and carries at least 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing VAT polymerization with Material Extrusion (FDM)** — both are layer-by-layer but the energy source (UV light vs. heated nozzle) is fundamentally different.
> 6. **Writing the cure-depth equation without derivation** when 7 marks are allotted — KTU board examiners expect a *step-by-step* derivation starting from Beer–Lambert's law.
> 7. **Not drawing a block diagram / schematic** for "explain with neat sketch" questions — 2 marks are reserved for the figure alone.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Stereolithography (SLA)** = UV-laser curing of liquid photopolymer, layer by layer (Invented by **Chuck Hull, 1984**).
- ✅ Classified under **Vat Photopolymerization** in AM taxonomy.
- ✅ Typical UV laser wavelengths: **325 nm (He-Cd)** or **355 nm (Nd:YVO₄)**.
- ✅ The central equation: $\;C_d = D_p \ln(E/E_c)$.
- ✅ $D_p$ = penetration depth; $E_c$ = critical exposure; $E$ = surface exposure.
- ✅ Cure depth grows **logarithmically** with exposure — not linearly.
- ✅ Typical layer thickness: **0.05 – 0.15 mm**; spot diameter: **0.10 – 0.50 mm**.
- ✅ Galvo mirrors steer the beam; recoater blade deposits fresh resin each layer.
- ✅ **Post-curing in UV oven** is essential for full mechanical strength.
- ✅ STL file format = triangulated mesh exported from CAD for slicing.
- ✅ Best for: prototypes, investment casting patterns, medical/dental models.
- ✅ Limitations: brittleness, UV degradation, support structures, resin handling hazards.
- ✅ Working curve ($C_d$ vs $\ln E$) is plotted to find optimal $E$ for a given $\Delta z$ (rule of thumb: $C_d \approx 1.5$–$2 \times \Delta z$).
- ✅ Top-down vs bottom-up configurations differ in platform motion direction.
- ✅ **Mnemonic — P.E.T.** — *Power, Exposure, Time* ↑ all increase $C_d$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision tip:** Memorize the four variables ($C_d$, $D_p$, $E$, $E_c$) and the Beer–Lambert equation in the exact form $C_d = D_p \ln(E/E_c)$. Practise one numerical each on **time-per-spot** and **maximum scan speed** — these are KTU board favourites and almost always carry 5–7 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lithography, Selective Laser Sintering, Fused Deposition Modelling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT528 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lithography, Selective Laser Sintering, Fused Deposition Modelling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Lithography (Stereolithography / SLA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Stereolithography / SLA):**
> *Stereolithography (SLA) is a vat photopolymerization-based Additive Manufacturing (AM) process in which a photosensitive liquid resin is selectively cured and solidified layer-by-layer by a precisely controlled ultraviolet (UV) laser (or projector light source) tracing the cross-sectional geometry of the part on (or just below) the resin surface.*

In simple words — **Lithography in AM** is like using a **magic flashlight that only hardens special liquid plastic wherever it shines**. Imagine pouring a tray of honey-like liquid, then sweeping a tiny UV laser beam over it. Wherever the beam touches, the liquid instantly turns solid. Sweep it again for the next layer, and you slowly "grow" a solid 3D object from the liquid pool. This is exactly how a dentist makes a clear aligner or how a jeweller prints a delicate ring.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Lithography in the context of PERAT528 refers specifically to **Stereolithography (SLA)**, the very first commercialized AM process (Chuck Hull, 1986, 3D Systems). It is classified under **ISO/ASTM 52900** category **VAT PHOTOPOLYMERIZATION**.

---

## 1.2 Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (SLS):**
> *Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) is a Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) based Additive Manufacturing process in which a high-power laser (typically CO₂) selectively scans and fuses (sinters) thin layers of powdered thermoplastic, metal, or ceramic material by raising the local temperature just below the melting point, so that adjacent particles bond together to form a solid 3D part.*

Think of SLS like **building a sandcastle with a magic magnifying glass**. You have a bed of dry, fine powder (like sand). A powerful laser acts like the sun through a magnifying glass — it focuses heat on tiny spots of the powder, melting them just enough to stick together without becoming a puddle. Sweep, lower the bed, spread new powder, sweep again. You eventually "carve" a solid object out of the powder, and the unsintered powder around it acts as natural support!

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** SLS belongs to **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** under ISO/ASTM 52900. The most common variant is **DMLS (Direct Metal Laser Sintering)** for metals. The unsintered powder bed acts as a **self-supporting structure**, eliminating the need for dedicated support — a major advantage over SLA and FDM.

---

## 1.3 Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (FDM):**
> *Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) — trademarked by Stratasys, also called FFF (Fused Filament Fabrication) in open-source use — is a Material Extrusion based Additive Manufacturing process in which a thermoplastic filament is fed into a heated liquefier (nozzle), melted, and extruded in controlled semi-molten beads that are deposited layer-by-layer along a programmed path to build a 3D part.*

Imagine you have a **hot glue gun mounted on a CNC plotter**. The glue stick (filament) gets pushed into a hot metal tip, melts into goo, and squirts out as a thin bead onto a table. The table or the gun moves in precise X-Y paths to draw the cross-section. Once one layer is done, the table drops a tiny bit, and the next layer is drawn on top — sticking to the previous one. This is exactly how hobbyist 3D printers (Creality, Prusa) work!

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** FDM is the **most widely deployed** AM technology globally due to low cost, simplicity, and office-friendliness. It is classified under **Material Extrusion** in ISO/ASTM 52900. The process was invented by **S. Scott Crump (Stratasys, 1989)** and later released as open source after the 2009 patent expiry.

---

## 1.4 Comparative Snapshot (At a Glance)

| Parameter | Stereolithography (SLA) | Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) | Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| ISO/ASTM Category | Vat Photopolymerization | Powder Bed Fusion | Material Extrusion |
| Raw Material | Liquid Photopolymer Resin | Powder (Polymer / Metal / Ceramic) | Thermoplastic Filament |
| Energy Source | UV Laser / Projector | CO₂ / Fiber Laser | Thermal (Heated Nozzle) |
| Support Required | Yes (for overhangs) | **No (powder self-supports)** | Yes (for overhangs) |
| Typical Accuracy | **±0.1 mm (Best)** | ±0.2 – 0.3 mm | ±0.2 – 0.5 mm |
| Surface Finish | Excellent (Smooth) | Moderate (Grainy) | Layer-lines visible |
| Inventor / Year | Chuck Hull, 1986 | Carl Deckard, 1989 | S. Scott Crump, 1989 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stereolithography (SLA) — Working Principle & Steps

The SLA process follows a **layer-by-layer photopolymerization** principle. The sequence of operations is:

1. **Build Platform Initialization:** A build platform is lowered into a vat filled with liquid photopolymer resin to a depth equal to **one layer thickness** (typically **0.05 – 0.15 mm**).
2. **Resin Leveling:** A wiper/roller sweeps across the surface to ensure a flat, bubble-free resin layer.
3. **Laser Scanning:** A UV laser (λ ≈ **355 nm** for solid-state, or **405 nm** for some systems) is steered by a **galvanometer mirror** to trace the 2D cross-section of the part on the resin surface.
4. **Photopolymerization (Curing):** Wherever the laser hits, the **photoinitiator** molecules in the resin absorb UV photons and generate free radicals, which trigger a chain-reaction polymerization — converting liquid monomer/oligomer chains into a **cross-linked solid network**.
5. **Platform Lowering:** The build platform descends by exactly one layer thickness.
6. **Iteration:** Steps 2–5 repeat for every subsequent layer until the part is complete.
7. **Post-Curing:** The part is removed, cleaned with isopropyl alcohol (IPA) to remove uncured resin, and placed in a **UV oven** for final cross-linking to achieve full mechanical strength.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" — Why does the resin harden?**
> Photopolymer resins contain three essential ingredients:
> - **Monomers/Oligomers** (e.g., acrylate or epoxy resins) — the "building blocks."
> - **Photoinitiator** (e.g., TPO, BAPO) — absorbs UV light and generates free radicals.
> - **Reactive Diluents & Additives** — control viscosity and final properties.
>
> When the laser delivers a critical energy dose, the photoinitiator triggers a **free-radical chain polymerization** that cross-links the monomers into a rigid 3D network.

### 2.1.1 Critical Process Parameters (SLA)
- **Cure Depth (Cd):** The thickness of resin that solidifies per laser exposure.
- **Critical Exposure Energy (Ec):** Minimum energy needed to initiate polymerization.
- **Penetration Depth (Dp):** Depth at which laser intensity falls to 1/e of its surface value.
- **Laser Power (P)** and **Scan Speed (v)** together determine the **energy dose (E)** delivered per unit area.

---

## 2.2 Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) — Working Principle & Steps

1. **Chamber Pre-Heating:** The build chamber is heated to a temperature just **below the melting point** of the powder (e.g., for Nylon-12: ~170 °C, melting point ~187 °C). This reduces the laser energy needed to sinter.
2. **Powder Deposition:** A roller or blade spreads a thin, uniform layer of powder (typically **0.1 mm** thick) from the feed bed onto the build platform.
3. **Laser Scanning:** A **CO₂ laser (λ = 10.6 μm)** is steered by galvanometers to sinter the powder particles at locations defined by the part's cross-section.
4. **Solid-State Sintering:** The laser raises the local temperature between the powder's **glass transition (Tg)** and **melting temperature (Tm)**, causing the polymer particles to fuse at their boundaries through **diffusion-driven necking** — they don't fully melt, hence "sintering" not "melting."
5. **Platform Lowering:** The build platform descends by one layer thickness.
6. **Iteration:** Steps 2–5 repeat.
7. **Cooling & Depowdering:** After the build, the entire chamber is cooled slowly (to avoid warping), and the part is dug out of the unsintered powder ("cake").

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" — Why is SLS "self-supporting"?**
> The unsintered powder surrounding the part provides **mechanical support** during the build. For overhangs, bridges, and complex internal geometries, this powder acts like **invisible sand-jack scaffolding**, eliminating the need to design and remove dedicated supports.

### 2.2.1 Critical Process Parameters (SLS)
- **Laser Power (P)** — typically 5–200 W depending on material.
- **Scan Speed (v)** — typically 1000–5000 mm/s.
- **Hatch Spacing (h)** — distance between adjacent laser scan lines.
- **Layer Thickness (t)** — typically 0.08–0.15 mm.
- **Bed Temperature (Tb)** — must be precisely controlled just below Tm.
- **Energy Density (E)** — the master equation governing part quality (see Section 3).

---

## 2.3 Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) — Working Principle & Steps

1. **Filament Feeding:** A thermoplastic filament (typical diameter **1.75 mm** or **2.85 mm**) is fed by a **drive gear (extruder)** into a **heated liquefier** (hot end).
2. **Melting:** The filament is heated above its **glass transition / melting temperature** (e.g., PLA ~200 °C, ABS ~240 °C, PETG ~230 °C) and becomes a viscous melt.
3. **Extrusion:** The melt is forced through a **brass or hardened steel nozzle** (typical diameter **0.4 mm**) and deposited as a thin bead (called a **"road"**) onto the build platform or the previous layer.
4. **Path Planning:** The print head (X-Y gantry) traces the part's cross-section according to **G-code** (a numerical control language).
5. **Layer Cooling & Bonding:** The extruded bead **cools and solidifies**, fusing with adjacent roads and the previous layer through **thermal welding**.
6. **Z-Axis Increment:** Either the build platform descends or the print head rises by one **layer height** (typically **0.1 – 0.3 mm**).
7. **Support Generation:** For overhangs greater than **~45°**, separate support structures (often in a different material or with break-away interfaces) are deposited simultaneously.
8. **Iteration:** Steps 3–6 repeat until the part is complete.
9. **Post-Processing:** Supports are removed manually, and the part may be sanded, painted, or annealed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" — Why does FDM work?**
> When the molten filament is extruded onto the previously deposited layer, it is hot enough to **partially remelt** the top surface of the underlying layer. This creates a **weld zone** through polymer chain interdiffusion (also called **"necking" or "healing"**). The bond strength is governed by the **Frenkel–Ezekeley model** of polymer interdiffusion.

### 2.3.1 Critical Process Parameters (FDM)
- **Nozzle Temperature (Tn)** — typically 180–260 °C.
- **Bed Temperature (Tb)** — typically 50–110 °C.
- **Layer Height (t)** — typically 0.1–0.3 mm.
- **Print Speed (v)** — typically 30–150 mm/s.
- **Nozzle Diameter (d)** — typically 0.2–0.8 mm.
- **Infill Density, Infill Pattern, Wall Count, Flow Rate** — affect strength and material usage.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Rule of Tubes:** For absolute value / norm symbols in math, we use `\vert` (not `|`) to prevent breaking the markdown table below.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Real-World Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Volumetric Energy Density (SLS / DMLS)** | $E_d = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | $E_d$ in **J/mm³**, $P$ = Laser power (W), $v$ = Scan speed (mm/s), $h$ = Hatch spacing (mm), $t$ = Layer thickness (mm) | Predicts densification, porosity, and mechanical strength in SLS. Too low → porous; too high → degradation. |
| 2 | **Cure Depth — Beer–Lambert (SLA)** | $C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right)$ | $C_d$ = Cure depth (mm), $D_p$ = Penetration depth (mm), $E_0$ = Exposure energy at surface (mJ/cm²), $E_c$ = Critical energy (mJ/cm²) | Determines minimum layer thickness and vertical resolution in SLA. |
| 3 | **Volumetric Flow Rate (FDM)** | $Q = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot v_f$ | $Q$ in **mm³/s**, $d$ = Nozzle diameter (mm), $v_f$ = Filament feed speed (mm/s) | Used to calibrate extruder E-steps and ensure consistent road width. |
| 4 | **Linear Road Width (FDM)** | $W = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi \, d^2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{v_{xy}}$ | $W$ = Bead width (mm), $v_{xy}$ = Print head travel speed (mm/s) | Determines wall thickness and print accuracy. |
| 5 | **Effective Build Time (All)** | $T = \dfrac{V_{part}}{v_{dep} \cdot a_{eff} \cdot t}$ | $T$ = Build time (s), $V_{part}$ = Part volume (mm³), $v_{dep}$ = Deposition rate, $a_{eff}$ = Effective cross-section, $t$ = Layer thickness (mm) | Estimating job time and machine utilization. |
| 6 | **Build Height Limit** | $H_{max} = N \cdot t$ | $H_{max}$ = Max build height (mm), $N$ = Number of layers, $t$ = Layer thickness (mm) | Defines Z-axis resolution. |
| 7 | **Aspect Ratio for Support Need (FDM)** | $\theta_{crit} \approx 45°$ from vertical | — | Overhangs beyond this angle need support. |
| 8 | **Polymer Welding Depth (FDM, Frenkel)** | $X(t) \propto t^{1/4}$ | $X$ = Neck radius, $t$ = Contact time | Models inter-layer bond strength vs. cooling time. |

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why Does Industry Care?

- **SLA** → Used in **dental aligners, jewellery masters, hearing aid shells, microfluidic devices** where ultra-smooth surface finish and sub-100 µm features are critical.
- **SLS** → Used in **functional nylon prototypes, aerospace ducting, automotive low-volume production, orthopaedic implants (PEEK, Ti)**. The lack of support enables complex lattice structures and topology-optimized parts.
- **FDM** → Used in **concept models, jigs and fixtures, end-use consumer parts (e.g., drone frames), bioprinting scaffolds**, and **low-cost tooling**. Its dominance in **MakerSpaces** and engineering education is unmatched.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When asked to "compare" or "list advantages/disadvantages" in the 14-mark question, students must align the answer to the **process category** (vat polymerization / PBF / extrusion) and the **ISO/ASTM 52900 taxonomy**. Examiners reward correct nomenclature and ISO categorization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cure Depth Equation in Stereolithography (SLA)

The Beer–Lambert law describes the attenuation of light intensity as it penetrates a partially absorbing medium.

### Step 1 — Light Intensity Attenuation
When a UV laser with surface intensity $I_0$ penetrates the liquid resin to a depth $z$, the intensity falls exponentially:

$$I(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-z / D_p}$$

Here, $D_p$ is the **penetration depth** — the characteristic length over which intensity decays by a factor of $1/e$. It is an intrinsic property of the resin's photoinitiator concentration and absorption coefficient.

### Step 2 — Energy Exposure at Depth
The energy delivered to a depth $z$ is proportional to the intensity at that depth multiplied by exposure time $t_{exp}$:

$$E(z) = I(z) \cdot t_{exp} = I_0 \cdot t_{exp} \cdot e^{-z / D_p}$$

Recognizing that the surface exposure energy is $E_0 = I_0 \cdot t_{exp}$, we can write:

$$E(z) = E_0 \cdot e^{-z / D_p}$$

### Step 3 — Curing Threshold Condition
Curing (gelation) occurs only where the local energy exceeds the **critical exposure energy** $E_c$. Setting $E(z) = E_c$ defines the maximum depth at which the resin solidifies — the **cure depth** $C_d$:

$$E_0 \cdot e^{-C_d / D_p} = E_c$$

### Step 4 — Solve Algebraically
Rearranging:

$$e^{-C_d / D_p} = \dfrac{E_c}{E_0}$$

$$-\dfrac{C_d}{D_p} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_c}{E_0}\right)$$

$$\dfrac{C_d}{D_p} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right)$$

Therefore, the **final cure depth equation** is:

$$\boxed{\,C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right)\,}$$

### Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation
- A **larger $D_p$** (transparent resin) cures deeper — good for thick layers, but reduces X-Y resolution.
- A **larger $E_0$** (more laser power or slower scan) cures deeper.
- The **logarithmic relationship** means that doubling $E_0$ does **not** double the cure depth — diminishing returns apply.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Volumetric Energy Density in Selective Laser Sintering (SLS / DMLS)

The energy density is the single most important metric for SLS part quality. It quantifies the energy delivered per unit volume of material.

### Step 1 — Energy Delivered Per Unit Length of Scan
A laser of power $P$ (watts = J/s) travelling at scan speed $v$ (mm/s) delivers energy per unit length:

$$E_{line} = \dfrac{P}{v} \quad \text{(J/mm)}$$

### Step 2 — Energy Per Unit Area
If two adjacent scan lines are separated by the **hatch spacing** $h$ (mm), the energy per unit area is:

$$E_{area} = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h} \quad \text{(J/mm}^2\text{)}$$

### Step 3 — Energy Per Unit Volume
If the layer has thickness $t$ (mm), the energy per unit volume (i.e., the volumetric energy density) is:

$$E_d = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad \text{(J/mm}^3\text{)}$$

### Step 4 — Unit Verification

$$\dfrac{[\text{W}]}{[\text{mm/s}] \cdot [\text{mm}] \cdot [\text{mm}]} = \dfrac{\text{J/s}}{(\text{mm}^3/\text{s})} = \dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}^3} \;\checkmark$$

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)
**Given:** CO₂ laser, $P = 30 \text{ W}$, $v = 2000 \text{ mm/s}$, $h = 0.15 \text{ mm}$, $t = 0.1 \text{ mm}$ (Nylon-12 powder).

**Compute:**

$$E_d = \dfrac{30}{2000 \times 0.15 \times 0.1}$$

$$E_d = \dfrac{30}{30} = 1.0 \text{ J/mm}^3$$

This value is in the **optimal range (0.8 – 1.2 J/mm³)** for Nylon-12 SLS — indicating proper densification without polymer degradation.

### Step 6 — Boundary Behavior
- $E_d < 0.5$ J/mm³ → Under-sintering → porous, weak, brittle part.
- $0.8 < E_d < 1.2$ J/mm³ → **Optimum** for most polymers.
- $E_d > 2.0$ J/mm³ → Over-sintering → polymer degradation (yellowing, warping, "orange peel" surface).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exam:** The examiner can give you $P$, $v$, $h$, $t$ values and ask you to compute $E_d$ and comment on whether the parameters will yield a dense or porous part. This is a **5-mark sub-question classic**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: FDM Volumetric Flow Rate & Road Width

### Step 1 — Filament Cross-Section
A filament of diameter $d_f$ has cross-sectional area:

$$A_f = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d_f^2$$

### Step 2 — Volumetric Flow Rate into the Liquefier
If the extruder feeds the filament at linear speed $v_f$ (mm/s), the volumetric flow rate entering the hot end is:

$$Q = A_f \cdot v_f = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d_f^2 \cdot v_f \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/s)}$$

Assuming **incompressibility** and **no slip** in the liquefier (reasonable for short melt zones), the same volume exits the nozzle per second.

### Step 3 — Road Width Relationship
The deposited road has a roughly **elliptical cross-section**. For a circular nozzle of diameter $d$ printing at head speed $v_{xy}$, the extruded volume per unit length is:

$$\dfrac{Q}{v_{xy}} = \text{Road cross-section area}$$

If the layer thickness is $t$ and road width is $W$, a reasonable first-order approximation (assuming the road is approximately rectangular with $W \approx d$ and some **squeeze-flow spread** factor $k$) is:

$$W \approx \dfrac{Q}{v_{xy} \cdot t} \cdot k$$

A more useful engineering form to compute extrusion multiplier is:

$$\dfrac{Q}{v_{xy}} = W \cdot t$$

$$\Rightarrow \boxed{\;W = \dfrac{Q}{v_{xy} \cdot t} = \dfrac{\pi \, d_f^2 \cdot v_f}{4 \, v_{xy} \cdot t}\;}$$

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example
**Given:** $d_f = 1.75$ mm, $v_f = 3$ mm/s, $v_{xy} = 60$ mm/s, $t = 0.2$ mm.

**Compute Q first:**

$$Q = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (1.75)^2 \cdot 3 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot 3.0625 \cdot 3 = 7.214 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Compute W:**

$$W = \dfrac{7.214}{60 \times 0.2} = \dfrac{7.214}{12} \approx 0.601 \text{ mm}$$

So the road width is **~0.6 mm**, suitable for a 0.4 mm nozzle with proper over-extrusion (typical for good inter-road bonding).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is the **most examination-relevant** analytical tool. It helps a KTU examiner validate that a student can make an informed process choice.

| Application Requirement | Recommended Process | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Ultra-smooth surface, jewellery master | **SLA** | Cure depth can be <50 µm; surface roughness Ra < 1 µm. |
| Complex internal lattice (e.g., orthopaedic implant) | **SLS** | Self-supporting powder enables overhanging lattices without supports. |
| Functional metal part (aerospace bracket) | **SLS (DMLS variant)** | Direct metal sintering using Ti-6Al-4V, Inconel, AlSi10Mg powders. |
| Low-cost prototype in college lab | **FDM** | Cheapest machines (₹15K+), desktop-friendly, no toxic resins. |
| Biocompatible surgical guide | **SLA** (with certified resin) or **SLS** (PEEK) | Both offer biocompatible material options. |
| Outdoor / UV-exposed end-use part | **FDM (ASA / PETG)** | FDM thermoplastics have better UV and weather resistance than standard SLA resins. |
| Large format part (>500 mm) | **FDM** (large gantry) | SLA and SLS have vat/chamber size limits; FDM scales easily. |
| Transparent part (lens, light guide) | **SLA** (clear resin + post-cure) | FDM layers scatter light; SLS powder leaves porosity. |
| Mass production of small parts | **SLA** (high throughput) or **SLS** | FDM is slow per part for high volumes. |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stereolithography (SLA) — Process Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STL File Slicing into Layers] --> B[Build Platform Initialized in Resin Vat]
    B --> C[Resin Leveling by Wiper]
    C --> D[UV Laser Beam via Galvanometer]
    D --> E[Photopolymerization - Layer 1 Cured]
    E --> F{Build Complete?}
    F -- No --> G[Platform Descends by 1 Layer Thickness]
    G --> C
    F -- Yes --> H[Part Removal from Vat]
    H --> I[IPA Cleaning to Remove Uncured Resin]
    I --> J[Final UV Post-Curing in Oven]
    J --> K[Finished SLA Part]
```

**Component Legend (mapped to KTU terminology):**
- **Resin Vat** → typically coated with **silicone or Teflon** for easy part release.
- **Galvanometer** → fast X-Y beam steering mirror; replaces slower linear stages.
- **Build Platform** → usually aluminum, with a perforated or "honeycomb" base for drainage.

---

## 4.2 Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) — Process Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD Model Sliced into 2D Layers] --> B[Chamber Pre-heated Below Tm]
    B --> C[Roller Spreads Powder from Feed Bed]
    C --> D[CO2 Laser Sinters Cross-section]
    D --> E[Layer N Bonded to Layer N-1]
    E --> F{All Layers Done?}
    F -- No --> G[Build Platform Lowers 0.1 mm]
    G --> C
    F -- Yes --> H[Chamber Slow Cooling to Avoid Warping]
    H --> I[Depowdering - Dig Out Part from Powder Cake]
    I --> J[Optional Bead Blasting for Surface Finish]
    J --> K[Finished SLS Part]
```

**Critical Subsystems (KTU High-Yield):**
- **Feed Bed** → fresh powder reservoir on one side.
- **Build Chamber** → heated, oxygen-controlled (often nitrogen-purged for metals).
- **Recoater Blade / Roller** → spreads powder with ±5 µm uniformity.
- **Laser Optics** → typically galvanometer + F-θ lens for flat-field focusing.

---

## 4.3 Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) — Process Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[G-code Generated from Slicer Software] --> B[Filament Spool Loaded]
    B --> C[Drive Gear Feeds Filament into Hot End]
    C --> D[Heated Nozzle Melts Filament above Tg]
    D --> E[XY Gantry Moves Head along Print Path]
    E --> F[Melt Extruded as Bead on Bed or Previous Layer]
    F --> G{Layer Complete?}
    G -- No --> H[Continue Path - Same Layer]
    H --> E
    G -- Yes --> I{All Layers Done?}
    I -- No --> J[Z Axis Increments by Layer Height]
    J --> E
    I -- Yes --> K[Part Cooled on Bed]
    K --> L[Support Removal and Post-processing]
    L --> M[Finished FDM Part]
```

**Critical Subsystems (KTU High-Yield):**
- **Hot End** → contains the heater block, thermistor, and nozzle (Bowden or direct-drive).
- **Cold End** → where the drive gear grips the filament; prevents premature melting.
- **Build Plate** → heated glass, PEI, or magnetic flex plates for adhesion.
- **Frame Types** → Cartesian (Prusa), CoreXY, or Delta — affects speed and accuracy.

---

## 4.4 Side-by-Side Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SLA[SLA System]
        SLA1[UV Laser] --> SLA2[Galvanometer Scanner]
        SLA2 --> SLA3[Resin Vat]
        SLA3 --> SLA4[Build Platform]
    end
    subgraph SLS[SLS System]
        SLS1[CO2 Laser] --> SLS2[Scanner + F-theta Lens]
        SLS2 --> SLS3[Powder Bed]
        SLS3 --> SLS4[Build Platform]
        SLS5[Feed Bed] --> SLS3
    end
    subgraph FDM[FDM System]
        FDM1[Filament Spool] --> FDM2[Extruder Drive]
        FDM2 --> FDM3[Heated Nozzle]
        FDM3 --> FDM4[Build Plate]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> This **Block-Level Functional Architecture** mapping is the safest Mermaid-rendering approach. It clearly shows how each process is fundamentally different in its **energy delivery mechanism** (laser into liquid vs. laser into powder vs. thermal extrusion), which is the conceptual core of the KTU syllabus.
