# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Stereolithography. List any two advantages of SLA over FDM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Stereolithography (SLA) is a vat photopolymerization-based additive manufacturing process in which a liquid photopolymer resin is selectively cured layer-by-layer using a UV laser to form a solid 3D part, as per ISO/ASTM 52900.

**Two Advantages of SLA over FDM (1 mark — ½ each):**
1. **Higher surface finish and accuracy** — SLA achieves surface roughness Ra as low as 0.5 µm with ±0.05 mm tolerance, while FDM typically shows visible layer lines with Ra > 5 µm.
2. **No thermal issues** — SLA cures resin photochemically at room temperature, eliminating warping, shrinkage, and delamination problems common in FDM due to thermal cycling.
3. *(Optional 3rd)* — SLA can produce thinner features (~50 µm) compared to FDM (~200 µm nozzle limit).

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM)? State two commonly used thermoplastic filaments.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) is a material extrusion-based additive manufacturing process in which a thermoplastic filament is fed into a heated nozzle, melted, and extruded in a controlled manner layer-by-layer to build a 3D part, as classified under ISO/ASTM 52900.

**Two Common Filaments (1 mark — ½ each):**
1. **PLA (Polylactic Acid)** — easy to print, biodegradable, low warping.
2. **ABS (Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene)** — tough, heat-resistant; used in engineering parts.
3. *(Optional 3rd)* **PETG** — combines ease of PLA with strength of ABS; food-safe.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Following the **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, students must answer ONE full question from Part B, which contains a sub-choice. The 14 marks are split equally as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) Explain the working principle of Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) with a neat sketch. List four commonly used materials in SLS. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle (5 marks):**
Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) is a powder bed fusion (PBF) additive manufacturing process in which a high-power CO₂ laser selectively fuses powdered material layer by layer to form a solid 3D part.

The step-by-step process is as follows:
1. **Pre-heating:** The build chamber is heated to a temperature just below the melting point (Tm) of the powder — for example, ~170 °C for Nylon-12 (Tm ≈ 187 °C). This reduces the thermal gradient and minimizes warping.
2. **Powder spreading:** A roller or recoater blade spreads a thin uniform layer (~0.1 mm) of powder from the feed bed onto the build platform.
3. **Laser scanning:** A CO₂ laser (λ = 10.6 µm) is steered by galvanometer mirrors to scan the cross-sectional profile of the part, sintering the powder particles at those locations.
4. **Mechanism of bonding:** The laser raises the local temperature of the powder above its **glass transition temperature (Tg)** but **below its melting point (Tm)**. The polymer particles undergo **solid-state sintering** — surface diffusion and necking occur between adjacent particles, fusing them together without becoming fully molten.
5. **Platform lowering:** The build platform descends by one layer thickness.
6. **Iteration:** Steps 2–5 repeat for every subsequent layer.
7. **Cooling and depowdering:** After the build, the entire powder cake is allowed to cool slowly (often 6–12 hours) to relieve residual stresses. The part is then extracted from the unsintered powder.

**Neat Sketch (refer Section 4.2 flow diagram) (1 mark):** A clear labelled diagram showing the CO₂ laser, scanner, build platform, feed bed, powder roller, and chamber.

**Four Common SLS Materials (1 mark — ¼ each):**
1. **Nylon-12 (PA 2200, PA 2201)** — the most widely used engineering polymer in SLS.
2. **Nylon-11** — tougher and more flexible than Nylon-12; used in automotive.
3. **TPU (Thermoplastic Polyurethane)** — flexible elastomer for gaskets and lattices.
4. **Polypropylene (PP)** — chemical-resistant; used in microfluidics and packaging.
5. *(Optional)* **PEEK** — high-performance thermoplastic for aerospace and medical implants.

---

**(b) With a neat diagram, explain the Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) process. Discuss its advantages and limitations. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle with Diagram (4 marks):**
Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) is a material extrusion-based additive manufacturing process. A thermoplastic filament (typically 1.75 mm or 2.85 mm diameter) is fed by a drive gear into a **heated liquefier (hot end)**, where it is melted to a viscous state and extruded through a nozzle (typically 0.4 mm diameter) as a thin semi-molten bead. The print head, mounted on an X-Y gantry, traces the cross-sectional path of the part layer by layer according to G-code instructions. After each layer is deposited, the Z-axis is incremented by the layer height (0.1–0.3 mm), and the next layer is deposited on top, where it bonds with the previous layer through polymer chain interdiffusion (thermal welding). Once the entire part is built, it is cooled and the support structures (for overhangs) are removed manually.

(Refer to the FDM flow diagram in Section 4.3.)

**Advantages (2 marks — any 4, ½ each):**
1. **Low cost:** The cheapest AM technology; desktop machines available under ₹20,000.
2. **Office-friendly:** No toxic chemicals, no lasers, no powders — easy to operate in college labs.
3. **Wide material range:** PLA, ABS, PETG, TPU, Nylon, PEEK, polycarbonate, fiber-reinforced composites.
4. **Multi-material and multi-colour capability:** Modern FDM printers can deposit 2–4 materials simultaneously.
5. **Scalable:** Large-format printers (1 m³+) are available at relatively low cost.
6. **Simple post-processing:** Supports break away easily; minimal cleanup.

**Limitations (1 mark — any 2, ½ each):**
1. **Layer lines visible:** Stepped surface finish on curved surfaces; Ra > 5 µm.
2. **Limited accuracy:** Typical tolerance ±0.2 mm; nozzle diameter limits minimum feature size.
3. **Anisotropic strength:** Inter-layer bond is weaker than intra-layer; parts split along Z.
4. **Warping & shrinkage:** Especially with ABS; requires heated chamber (enclosed printer).
5. **Slow for high-volume production:** Each part takes hours to print.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Describe the Stereolithography (SLA) process in detail. Explain the photopolymerization reaction and derive the cure depth equation. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Detailed SLA Process Description (4 marks):**
Stereolithography (SLA) was invented by Chuck Hull in 1986 and is the first commercialized additive manufacturing process. It is classified under **VAT Photopolymerization** in ISO/ASTM 52900.

The working sequence is:
1. A build platform is submerged into a vat of liquid photopolymer resin to a depth equal to one layer thickness (typically 0.05–0.15 mm).
2. A wiper levels the resin surface to remove bubbles and ensure uniform thickness.
3. A UV laser (typically solid-state Nd:YAG tripled to 355 nm) is steered by **galvanometer mirrors** to trace the 2D cross-section of the part on the resin surface.
4. Wherever the laser strikes, the photopolymer resin undergoes **free-radical polymerization** and solidifies.
5. The build platform descends by one layer thickness, a fresh resin layer flows in, and the laser scans the next cross-section.
6. This repeats until the entire 3D part is built.
7. The part is removed, washed in isopropyl alcohol to remove uncured resin, and post-cured in a UV oven for final cross-linking.

(Refer to the SLA flow diagram in Section 4.1.)

**Photopolymerization Reaction (2 marks):**
The resin contains three key components: **monomers/oligomers** (e.g., acrylate or epoxy), **photoinitiators** (e.g., TPO, BAPO), and **additives**. When UV photons are absorbed by the photoinitiator, it fragments into **free radicals**:

$$\text{PI} \xrightarrow{h\nu} \text{PI}^\bullet$$

These free radicals attack the C=C double bonds of the acrylate monomers, generating chain radicals:

$$\text{PI}^\bullet + \text{M} \longrightarrow \text{PI–M}^\bullet$$

Chain propagation continues, leading to cross-linked polymer networks. The reaction is:

$$\text{Monomer} \xrightarrow{\text{UV + Photoinitiator}} \text{Cross-linked Polymer}$$

**Derivation of Cure Depth Equation (1 mark):**
Following Beer–Lambert law, intensity at depth $z$ is $I(z) = I_0 e^{-z/D_p}$. The energy exposure at depth is $E(z) = E_0 e^{-z/D_p}$. Setting $E(z) = E_c$ for curing threshold:

$$C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right)$$

---

**(b) Compare SLA, SLS, and FDM in terms of (i) raw material, (ii) energy source, (iii) accuracy, (iv) support requirement, and (v) typical applications. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **SLA** | **SLS** | **FDM** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Raw Material** | Liquid photopolymer resin (acrylate/epoxy) | Powder (Polymer / Metal / Ceramic) | Thermoplastic filament (PLA, ABS, PETG) |
| **(ii) Energy Source** | UV laser (355 nm) or projector (405 nm) | CO₂ laser (10.6 µm) | Thermal (resistive heater) |
| **(iii) Accuracy** | ±0.05–0.1 mm (highest) | ±0.2–0.3 mm | ±0.2–0.5 mm |
| **(iv) Support Requirement** | Yes (mandatory for overhangs) | No (powder self-supports) | Yes (for overhangs > 45°) |
| **(v) Typical Applications** | Dental aligners, jewellery masters, hearing aids, microfluidics | Functional prototypes, aerospace ducting, lattices, orthopaedic implants | Concept models, jigs/fixtures, end-use consumer parts, education |

**Conclusion (½ mark):**
SLA offers the best surface finish, SLS offers the most design freedom (no supports, complex lattices), and FDM offers the lowest cost and widest accessibility. The choice depends on the **application's accuracy, mechanical, and economic requirements**.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic (PERAT528 Module 4):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "SLA" with general "lithography":** Lithography in the AM syllabus specifically refers to **VAT Photopolymerization (Stereolithography)**, not the semiconductor lithography used in chip-making. Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks for confusing these.
>
> 2. **Forgetting ISO/ASTM 52900 categorization:** Every AM process question expects students to mention the process category (Vat Photopolymerization / Powder Bed Fusion / Material Extrusion). Skipping this loses **½–1 mark** consistently.
>
> 3. **Wrong units in energy density formula:** Writing $E_d = P \cdot v \cdot h \cdot t$ (multiplied instead of divided) is a **3-mark blunder**. Always recall $E_d = P / (v \cdot h \cdot t)$.
>
> 4. **Skipping the sketch/diagram in 7-mark sub-questions:** KTU examiners explicitly state *"with a neat sketch"* in the question paper. Even a hand-drawn block diagram is mandatory. Missing it = **−2 to −3 marks**.
>
> 5. **Conflating DMLS with SLM:** DMLS (Direct Metal Laser Sintering) **sinters** (partial melting), while SLM (Selective Laser Melting) **fully melts** the powder. Examiners distinguish these strictly. SLS for polymers is sometimes called "bed sintering" to differentiate from metal variants.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning self-supporting nature of SLS:** When describing SLS advantages, **always** highlight that unsintered powder acts as natural support. This is a **high-yield one-liner** that examiners specifically look for.
>
> 7. **In FDM, calling the filament diameter the "nozzle diameter":** This is a basic but common error. The filament is **1.75 mm**, the nozzle is **0.4 mm** — they are different. The filament is reduced ~4.4× in cross-section as it melts and is squeezed out.
>
> 8. **Not specifying "FDM vs FFF":** Examiners may mark "FDM" as the trademarked Stratasys process and "FFF" as the open-source equivalent. Use the correct terminology.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- **Three AM Processes Covered:**
  - **SLA** → Vat Photopolymerization; UV laser cures liquid resin.
  - **SLS** → Powder Bed Fusion; CO₂ laser sinters powder.
  - **FDM** → Material Extrusion; Hot nozzle extrudes filament.

- **Inventors to Remember (KTU favourite):**
  - SLA → **Chuck Hull, 1986** (3D Systems)
  - SLS → **Carl Deckard, 1989** (DTM Corp → acquired by 3D Systems)
  - FDM → **S. Scott Crump, 1989** (Stratasys)

- **Master Formulas (memorize verbatim):**
  - $C_d = D_p \cdot \ln(E_0 / E_c)$  *(SLA cure depth)*
  - $E_d = P / (v \cdot h \cdot t)$  *(SLS energy density, J/mm³)*
  - $Q = (\pi/4) \, d_f^2 \, v_f$  *(FDM volumetric flow)*

- **Key Numerical Ranges (often asked):**
  - SLA layer thickness: **0.05–0.15 mm**
  - SLS layer thickness: **0.08–0.15 mm**
  - FDM layer thickness: **0.1–0.3 mm**
  - Nylon-12 SLS bed temp: **~170 °C** (below Tm of 187 °C)
  - PLA nozzle temp: **~200 °C**, bed: **~60 °C**
  - ABS nozzle temp: **~240 °C**, bed: **~100 °C**
  - CO₂ laser wavelength: **10.6 µm**
  - UV laser for SLA: **355 nm** (or 405 nm for some)

- **Self-Supporting Property:** Only **SLS** does not require supports due to the surrounding powder bed. **SLA and FDM always need supports** for overhangs.

- **Best Surface Finish:** SLA → SLA → SLS → FDM (ranking from smoothest to roughest).

- **Best for Metals:** SLS (specifically DMLS for Ti, Al, Inconel).

- **Lowest Cost:** FDM (entry-level printers from ₹15,000+).

- **Highest Accuracy:** SLA (±0.05–0.1 mm tolerance possible).

- **Fastest for Mass Production:** SLA (with large build platforms) and SLS.

- **Process Category (ISO/ASTM 52900):**
  - SLA → Vat Photopolymerization
  - SLS → Powder Bed Fusion
  - FDM → Material Extrusion

- **Anisotropy Awareness:** FDM parts are **anisotropic** — weaker in Z (between layers) than in X-Y. SLS is also mildly anisotropic; SLA is more isotropic.

- **Energy Source Distinction:**
  - **SLA** → light (photons)
  - **SLS** → light (laser photons, but thermal effect)
  - **FDM** → thermal (resistive heating, no laser)

- **One-liner for Examiners:** *"SLA hardens liquid with light, SLS fuses powder with light, and FDM extrudes melted plastic."*
